meanings.mnemonic:
The construction radical and the construction kanji are the same!

This kanji also means industry , which is what construction is, if you think about it. It's all part of the construction industry.
meanings.hint: Know your radicals and you'll know this kanji's meaning.

readings.mnemonic:
Every time a kanji uses the こう reading (there are a lot!), we'll use the character こういち . He's one of the people who started this website, so we'll do our best in the mnemonics to embarrass him as much as possible. Like this:

You see some construction in front of you. There, standing shirtless (only suspenders and a hard hat) is こう いち , looking back at you.
readings.hint: If こういち as a construction worker somehow doesn't make your legs tremble, that's okay. Just make sure you take this story to a ridiculous enough level to construct a memory in that brain of yours. Be mad at him for not following construction site regulations — where's his shirt, shoes, and pants, and why is he using the bulldozer like that?!

meanings.mnemonic: This isn't exactly the same as the fins radical, but it's close. Sometimes this will happen (where we have to use a slightly "modified radical" version of the original), so it's good to understand that now, early on. In this case, we have fins. How many fins? Eight fins. How do you know that? Because you caught four fish with two fins each.
meanings.hint: Count the fins out in front of you, all the way from one to eight. It'll help if you actually do this. Now smell each fin, and think "Wow, I have to smell all eight of these?" Yes, you do.

readings.mnemonic: After you count and smell all eight of your fins, it's time to put them away. You open a large hatch ( はち ) and put them in. This is where you keep your fins, always putting in eight at a time because that's what the boss wants.
readings.hint: You've asked him why you can only put eight fins at a time in the hatch, but he never wants to answer. What could be down in the hole that the hatch covers? Why is there a hatch at all? What's with numbers and hatches being mysterious?

meanings.mnemonic: The child radical and the child kanji are exactly the same.
meanings.hint: So, if you know your radicals, you know your kanji as well! This won't last forever (where radicals are exactly the same as the kanji) so enjoy it while the kanji are still small and simple!

readings.mnemonic:
The main reading here is し , so to remember it we'll use this mnemonic:

There is a child standing in front of you. You look closely at the child but something seems wrong. You rub your eyes and when you open them again, there is a shee p ( し ) in front of you. A sheep?! You yelp, blink again, and it's back to being a child. Are… are all children really just sheep?
readings.hint: The child (sheep) runs away and you chase after it. Every time it's about to turn a corner it looks like a sheep again, but whenever you yell and point, it changes back into a child. You're the only one who can see this sheep child!

meanings.mnemonic: The king has a big drop of liquid in his hand... but then he bounces it! The drop was really a ball .
meanings.hint: This kanji looks just like a king bouncing a ball , no? If not, imagine the kanji itself bouncing the drop (the ball ) over and over again. Kanji are surprisingly playful like that.

readings.mnemonic: When he's done playing with it, the king gives his ball to Tama ra ( たま ), the resident ball girl, for safekeeping.
readings.hint: Try to remember Tamara the ball girl. She'll come up anytime the reading for a kanji is たま , and usually she'll be involved with some kind of ball or round object. That's her job, after all.

meanings.mnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for enter first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means enter as well.
meanings.hint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: Turns out, after you enter the tent (remember how the person in the radical is entering one?), you're given a new ( にゅう ) one! Why? No idea, but hey — it's new!
readings.hint: Imagine lights turning on and flashing, and an entire audience inside. You're at a game show, and the announcer announces the neeeeew tent in his announcey voice. Feel the surprise. Feel the happiness. Feel the excitement. A beautiful new tent all for you!

meanings.mnemonic: The river radical and the river kanji are the same as each other!
meanings.hint: So, if you know the radical you know the kanji as well.

readings.mnemonic: You're standing next to a river that's actually used as a ca r wa sh ( かわ ).
readings.hint: Imagine standing by the river, watching all those cars floating by. They start off really dirty, but the river washes them all clean.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same. Both are power .
meanings.hint: If you know the radical, you should be feeling fine… No, you should feel powerful!

readings.mnemonic: Who has the most power of anyone? It's the Power Rangers. They are battling their arch enemy Ricky ( りき ). They defeat him and lock ( りょく ) him up, so that he can't hurt anyone ever again.
readings.hint: Know anyone named Ricky ( りき ), even a famous person like Ricky Ricardo or Ricky Gervais? Imagine that person's face on the monster the Power Rangers are fighting. Now, imagine the Power Rangers lock ing ( りょく ) him up. He looks much less scary once he's locked up. Phew.

meanings.mnemonic: You find a toe on the ground . It's weird, because it's above the ground, not where toes belong.
meanings.hint: Focus your imagination on how body parts should be buried, not above ground like this. Sure, finding a toe is a little strange, but finding a toe above the ground? Downright silly.

readings.mnemonic: Of course when you find a toe above the ground, you want to know where the toe came from. When you examine it, you see a name written on there. This toe belongs to the local clumsy farmhand, Joe ( じょう ).
readings.hint: Try to remember Joe, the local farmhand. He is a recurring character that will come up anytime the reading for a kanji is じょう .

meanings.mnemonic: This cross is the symbol for the number ten . A good way to remember this is to think about all the religions that have crosses and also important sets of tens.
meanings.hint: If you're familiar with Christianity, they have a cross and ten commandments. Cross = ten, you see?

readings.mnemonic: This cross in particular has ten jew els ( じゅう ) on it. Two on each arm and two in the middle. That's ten jewels!
readings.hint: Picture the jewels on this cross. Maybe draw it out on a piece of paper so the visual really sticks. Each cross is really a symbol for different sets of ten things, and in this case, it's ten jewels.

meanings.mnemonic: There's the ground , and there's a toe just below it. Yep, you know this means "below" because the ground has a toe below it in this kanji.
meanings.hint: Where's that toe again? Below the ground. Maybe it's your toe. Imagine digging it out and thinking, Ugh, why does it have to be under the ground like this? So irritating. Will I be able to sew it back on in time to save it?

readings.mnemonic: You keep digging in the ground for the toe below , but instead you hit something hard and metallic. You try to dig around it, but it's huge. Turns out it's a car ( か ). Keep digging until you unearth the car below. (Inside, you find the toe you were looking for.)
readings.hint: There's another useful on'yomi reading that just isn't useful enough for you to learn now. That reading is げ , but we're only going to worry about that much, much later when you have to learn one or two vocab words that read 下 as げ . That's the problem with really common kanji — too many readings.

meanings.mnemonic: Lying on the ground is something that looks just like the ground, the number One . Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.
meanings.hint: To remember the meaning of One , imagine yourself there at the scene of the crime. You grab One in your arms, trying to prop it up, trying to hear its last words. Instead, it just splatters some blood on your face. "Who did this to you?" you ask. The number One points weakly, and you see number Two running off into an alleyway. He's always been jealous of number One and knows he can be number one now that he's taken the real number one out.

readings.mnemonic: As you're sitting there next to One , holding him up, you start feeling a weird sensation all over your skin. From the wound comes a fine powder (obviously coming from the special bullet used to kill One) that causes the person it touches to get extremely itchy ( いち ).
readings.hint: Make sure you feel the ridiculously itchy sensation covering your body. It climbs from your hands, where you're holding the number One up, and then goes through your arms, crawls up your neck, goes down your body, and then covers everything. It becomes uncontrollable, and you're scratching everywhere, writhing on the ground. It's so itchy that it's the most painful thing you've ever experienced (you should imagine this vividly, so you remember the reading of this kanji).

meanings.mnemonic: The radical for woman and the kanji for woman are exactly the same.
meanings.hint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji as well! How swell.

readings.mnemonic: When you think of a woman , think of Jo -Anne ( じょ ), though she usually goes by just "Jo."
readings.hint: Whenever the reading じょ comes up, we will use the character Jo-Anne. She's quite a short woman, which is to help us differentiate じょ from じょう (who is the giant, oafish farmhand Joe). じょ is a smaller lady, じょう is a giant man.

meanings.mnemonic: Nice! This kanji is the same as the radical that looks just like it (down to the meaning, even!). The radical is nine and the kanji is nine , making the meaning of this kanji really easy to remember as long as you know the radical first.
meanings.hint: If you know your radicals well, this kanji will be a breeze!

readings.mnemonic:
Although this kanji has two on'yomi to learn, the pronunciations of those two on'yomi are very similar, so that should make them easier to remember.

When you were nine your favorite foods were coo kies ( く ) and cu cumbers ( きゅう ). Yum yum!
readings.hint: Picture nine-year-old you, lining up nine cookies and nine cucumbers. You dip a cookie in milk and bite into it, then dip a cucumber in and take a bite. Nine bites each until they're all gone. Ah, you miss being nine.

meanings.mnemonic: This kanji is made up of the ground radical (which also happens to be the kanji for "one") and the radical for two. Combine one and two together and what do you get? Three !
meanings.hint: This one should be pretty self explanatory! Even easier, you can look at this kanji and see it has three straight lines. You know what one looks like. You know what two looks like. So... this is three!

readings.mnemonic: Three is the number of chances you get with San ta-san ( さん ). He has a list, and when you get to three "naughty things" lines, that's it. You're on Santa-san's three strikes and you're out list.
readings.hint: Picture Santa-san sitting at his desk at the North Pole, eating cookies, and marking one, two, then finally three lines next to names. Once the third line appears, the kid doesn't get presents anymore.

meanings.mnemonic:
The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for book first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means book as well.

Take note that the second, third, and fourth meanings of this kanji are origin , real , and main . You can figure this out by thinking about how books mainly have their origins in real life.
meanings.hint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo! And good luck with all the meanings (sorry sorry sorry).

readings.mnemonic: Book s are one of the best ways to hone ( ほん ) your intellect, you know?
readings.hint: Just think about books and how you use them to hone your skills. Or read a book about honing things. Like swords.

meanings.mnemonic: Remember how the radical version of this kanji meant person because it looks like a person with their arms down and their legs spread out? Well this is a person too!
meanings.hint: Know the radicals, and you shall know the kanji.

readings.mnemonic: Only one person at Nin tendo ( にん ) complained about the new jean s ( じん ) policy, and that person was you.
readings.hint: Imagine how angry you were about the policy. You'll have to come up with something to be angry about yourself (pick something that would actually make you angry, like maybe you're only allowed to wear cut off short-shorts jeans at Nintendo now), but if you pick something good, you'll definitely remember the two readings for this. Just remember you're working at Nintendo, and the policy has to be something about jeans. Then, imagine yourself as the only person complaining and everyone else in their dumb jeans (or lack-thereof) shaking their head at you because you're being a fool.

meanings.mnemonic: Lucky for you (as is the case with many of the lower-stroke kanji), this kanji is the same as the radical you've already learned. The radical that makes up this kanji is seven and the kanji (which is exactly the same) is also seven . So, if you know the radical, you know the meaning of the kanji as well.
meanings.hint: Make sure you know your radicals well and you'll be A-Okay on this one!

readings.mnemonic:
You know this kanji means seven , so let's use that to remember the reading too. Seven is a lucky number, usually, but this time it isn't so good:

Seven percent is all she got on her test after she chea ted ( しち ) on it.
readings.hint: How unbelievable is it that someone got seven percent on a test, especially if you try to cheat? Imagine the girl you loathe the most from school, and imagine her cheating on the test (it's important it's a girl, because of the "she" part in she chea ted ( しち ). Then, imagine your happiness when she's super upset about getting seven percent on it. Serves her right, obviously.

meanings.mnemonic: The big radical and the big kanji are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: So, if you know the radical, you know the meaning of this kanji as well.

readings.mnemonic: There's this big guy (that's how you remember the radical, remember?), and he's wearing a huge Tie Dye ( たい , だい ) shirt. Because he's so huge, the tie dye shirt is also super huge. Everything about him is pretty big, but the tie dye stands out more than anything.
readings.hint: When you look at the big guy in your head, imagine him covered in tie dye head to toe. Think about how ridiculous it looks on him, especially since he's such a big guy. Shirt. Sweatpants. Hat. Shoes... it goes on and on.

meanings.mnemonic: The mountain radical and the mountain kanji are the same.
meanings.hint: Make sure you're studying your radicals. If you are, then you're studying your kanji as well!

readings.mnemonic: Think about mountains talking to each other, calling each other by their names and adding the Japanese name-ender san ( さん ) to each of their names. "Hello, Everest- san ." "Oh hi, Fuji- san ."
readings.hint: Be sure to imagine this vividly. The more absurdly you can imagine it, the more likely you are to remember the reading, san .

meanings.mnemonic: The mouth radical and the mouth kanji are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: If you learned your radicals — which the WaniKani system forces you to do (unless you're cheating) — you'll already know the meaning of the kanji.

readings.mnemonic:
Do you remember seeing the construction worker こういち earlier in the 工 mnemonic? If you haven't yet, not to worry!

For kanji with こう readings (there are a lot of them!) we will use the character こういち every time. He's one of the people who started this website, so we'll do our best in the mnemonics to embarrass him as much as possible. Anyways, remember that whenever you see こう , think こういち .

Everyone agrees, the mouth is こう いち 's best feature. You stare at it lovingly as he whispers sweet kanji readings to you.
readings.hint: You just can't forget all the cool things about kanji learning he said from that mouth. What a great mouth こういち has. How are the lips of his mouth both so skinny and fat at the same time?

meanings.mnemonic: The kanji two and the radical two are the exact same things. Therefore, you know the kanji for two already!
meanings.hint: More simply put, you can just know that this kanji is the double of one. It's two lines, that's not all that hard to remember!

readings.mnemonic: How do you count to two ? Just use someone's knee ( に ), and then their other knee. There — two knees!
readings.hint: Imagine asking to borrow the two knees of a stranger in the street, just so you can count to two. They're confused at first, but then they present each knee to you in a loving sort of way. Yep, that's two knees alright.

meanings.mnemonic: There is a line on the ground so you stop , making a giant sign pop up that says CORRECT ! You obeyed the rules of the road and did the correct thing. Good on you.
meanings.hint: Imagine walking, staring at the ground until you come across a line that you're supposed to stop at. If you stop, the big "correct" sign appears, chiming a happy tune.

readings.mnemonic: You did the correct thing, so here's your reward: a sa ber ( せい ) from the Shou gun ( しょう ) himself!
readings.hint: Perhaps a slightly unorthodox weapon for a ruler of medieval Japan, but you won't complain about a saber from the Shogun himself!

meanings.mnemonic: The dirt radical and the dirt kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: As long as you know the radical dirt, you know the kanji dirt.

readings.mnemonic: Who spends a lot of time in dirt ? Do gs ( ど ) do! Dogs dig in dirt, they roll in dirt, sometimes they try to eat dirt (which isn't so great).
readings.hint: Dogs and dirt just go together!

meanings.mnemonic: What's something with nine sides? Just add a drop and you'll have something that's almost a circle .
meanings.hint: It's not quite a circle though. You just need to add a drop of liquid to the edges to smooth it out to become a full circle.

readings.mnemonic:
Do you know about the adorable cat Maru? If not, you should check it out . May seem like a distraction, but if you come back knowing the reading for this kanji, then it's totally worthwhile.

When you think about a circle, think about Maru all rolled up in a little circle, being super adorable. Don't tell me it doesn't melt your heart just thinking about it.

Alternatively, you could also look at this kanji and think of Maru inside of a box, with his little tail sticking out of it. Whatever works best for you, just get yourself to "Maru."
readings.hint: Obviously if you "don't know this reading" well enough, you should watch some more Maru videos. I'll understand... just make sure you come back someday, okay?

meanings.mnemonic: The dog radical and dog kanji are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: Soon there will be very few kanji/radical-same combos ... so enjoy it while you can.

readings.mnemonic: Every dog likes to ea t noo dles ( いぬ ). Like that movie where the dogs share a noodle and end up kissing, right? So when you see 犬 , just imagine how those two eat noodles.
readings.hint: P.S. Don't actually let your dog eat noodles, at least not with marinara sauce. Disney didn't show you how that Lady and Tramp date probably ended — unromantically, with indigestion.

meanings.mnemonic: There are two mountains in front of you... and probably a bunch more around you too. You are in a pass, and don't know how to get out. You are frantically looking for the exit before you freeze to death. The most important thing is to find the exit from this mountain range - your life depends on it.
meanings.hint: In your imagination, really feel like your life depends on getting out. The important part is the feeling of "needing to get out of the mountain pass." If you have this feeling, you'll be more likely to remember that this kanji means exit, because that's what you want to do when you see this kanji.

readings.mnemonic: As you're trying to exit this mountain range, you suddenly realize that someone is actually hunting you. What? Make sure to feel that sudden realization. It's crazy. Then, you find the exit and run towards it. Sadly, the hunter is waiting there, and he shoots you ( しゅつ ) through the leg, knocking you down.
readings.hint: Focus on the surprise of realizing you're being hunted while trying to find the exit to the mountain range, then focus on the next part, where you get shot. View it in your head as the hunter shooting you. Powww!

meanings.mnemonic: The evening radical and the evening kanji are the same. If you know the radical you know the meaning of the kanji.
meanings.hint: Do what you're meant to do. Study those radicals!

readings.mnemonic: The evening is when all the you ths ( ゆう ) come outside to play.
readings.hint: Young people love staying up the entire evening doing youth-people things, and it's really obnoxious for us curmudgeonly folk.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical heaven is the same as the kanji heaven , making this an easy kanji to memorize.
meanings.hint: If you know your radicals well (and you should!) you know the meaning of this kanji well, as well!

readings.mnemonic: Heaven ... nowadays, people aren't all that good. Things have gotten bad. That's why the acceptance rate to get into heaven is only ten ( てん ) percent!
readings.hint: Imagine yourself in line to get into heaven. You're super worried, because you're not sure if you'll get in. You heard there was a one in ten chance... those aren't good odds. Feel nervous in your imagination. Feel worried. Feel the pressure as you get closer and closer to the gate... will you make it?? Ten percent!

meanings.mnemonic: The eye radical and eye kanji are the same as each other, so now you know them both.
meanings.hint: Well... you'll know them both as long as you studied your radicals, young grasshopper. If you didn't, then you're just a young grasshopper who needs to study their radicals.

readings.mnemonic: Your eyes are important to you, right? Sadly, one of your eyes is missing right now, because a sheep grabbed it and pulled it out, and is now running away from you, yelling his sheep call.... " Mehhhhhh ( め )!"
readings.hint: Of course, feel the pain of your eye being gone... and struggle to get it back. You eventually give up because the sheep is too fast, but then you come up with an idea. What if I call out back to the sheep in its own language? Go ahead and cover one eye and start making "MEHHH" noises to see if you can get the sheep to come back to you and give you your eye. It's up to you whether it works or not, but be sure to feel frustrated by its reaction, or happy by it. Either one works.

meanings.mnemonic: The fire radical and the fire kanji are the same as each other.
meanings.hint: If you know the radicals then this one is EZPZ.

readings.mnemonic:
Whenever we need to remember か , we use the word car .

For this kanji, when we think fire , we ought to think of something on fire. What's on fire? It's obviously your very own car ( か )! Oh Goddd NOOO!
readings.hint: Oh no... Oh no!! If you don't have a car , imagine what it'd feel like if your car just exploded and burned down. If you do have a car, imagine your own car in this situation. It should be a terrible feeling, if you imagine it well enough. So, when you think fire , remember the fear you had when your car was given to the flames. Now you have to walk everywhere, sad :(

meanings.mnemonic: The west radical and the west kanji are the same. Isn't that nice?
meanings.hint: Make sure you know all those radicals, it will make your life more pleasant.

readings.mnemonic: You go off to the west . It's the Wild West, even. There aren't many people out here, making it easy to find a niche ( にし ) for your snake oil business.
readings.hint: Imagine the dusty, ramshackle towns in the Wild West where you find your niche. You set up shops with big signs that say SNAKE OIL. Congrats, you've cornered the snake oil niche in the Wild West!

meanings.mnemonic: The five radical and the five kanji are the same.
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, friend-o.

readings.mnemonic: Five is the number of buildings Go dzilla ( ご ) will knock down. Oh, look! There he goes now.
readings.hint: One, two, three, four, five—GODZILLA IS HERE GO GO, RUN!

meanings.mnemonic: The hand radical and the hand kanji are identical in looks and meaning! That should make things easy!
meanings.hint: Make sure you're studying your radicals. It won't be forever that radicals and kanji overlap so nicely like this!

readings.mnemonic: To remember the reading て we will use the word "television." You have great hands. Go ahead and look at them. OH MY GOD. You have little te levisions ( て ) on your hands. They are playing the dumbest show you can think of, too, both hands at the same time.
readings.hint: You try to shake your hands off, but it's no good. The televisions are stuck, and the volume won't go down. You have no idea how you got these televisions on your hands. All you know is that you want them off right this instant.

meanings.mnemonic: Imagine contorting yourself in such a way that your mouth now has your legs inside it. You've bent your body in all the wrong ways to achieve this. In fact, if you look at yourself, you look like a square now... You're just a person with four sides.
meanings.hint: If you can see this kanji as a kind of square, you should be able to remember it means "four" as well, since squares always have four sides.

readings.mnemonic: Now that you have four sides, you look like a four-sided animal: the shee p ( し ). Look at yourself in the mirror and see an adorable four-sided sheep looking back at you. Imagine yourself with perfectly square wool. (You can only see four sides because mirrors aren't 3D. So you're a flat, cartoonish sheep.)
readings.hint: What's better than a four-sided sheep?

meanings.mnemonic:
Narwhal mouth s are on the right side of their head. In fact, some people didn't think they had mouths at all because they weren't looking from the right. Rookie mistake.

See how the "mouth" radical is to the right of the "narwhal" radical? That should help you remember that this means right .
meanings.hint: Imagine looking at a narwhal from the front. No mouth. Now look at it from the right. Mouth!

readings.mnemonic: How did we know to look from the right , though? Because the you ths ( ゆう ) figured it out. Youths think in ways that older people usually don't. That's why they can see the swirls on cereal, hear the music behind noise, and of course, look at things from the right side.
readings.hint: Imagine a bunch of youths walking in a group and looking at everything from the right. You remember when you were a youth and you used to look at everything from one side too.

meanings.mnemonic: The blood radical and blood are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
meanings.hint: There'll be some bad blood between us if you're not studying your radicals, especially when they're the same like this kanji!

readings.mnemonic:
To remember the reading けつ , we use the word "ketsup." The perfect mix of ketchup and catsup!

The radical is technically blood , but you know that radical is just a drop (of blood) on a plate. So, you ask yourself... Is that really blood on the plate? Could it be? You decide to take a gamble and lick it off. When you taste it, it's definitely not blood. No, it's ketsup ( けつ ).
readings.hint: Feel the relief that you didn't just lick up someone's blood. Taste the ketsup taste, too. It's pretty good. Not quite ketchup, but not quite catsup. Yum!

meanings.mnemonic: The mouth of the king extends all the way around him (like in this kanji). Where his mouth reaches, that is his country , give or take a few drop s.
meanings.hint: Imagine his talking taking physical form. As he talks, waves extend out from him, and stop right at the border of his country before getting to the next one.

readings.mnemonic: Turns out the king of this country loves drinking Coke ( こく ). Every morning that big mouth of his swallows a whole 2-liter bottle of Coke for breakfast.
readings.hint: Would you drink that much soda for breakfast? Now this king says he wants to pass a law requiring everyone in the country to drink nothing but Coke! That's crazy!

meanings.mnemonic: The radical middle and the kanji middle are both the same. So if you know one, you know the other.
meanings.hint: Make sure you're studying your radicals, otherwise you might find yourself in the middle of a kanji-learning problem.

readings.mnemonic:
To remember the reading for this kanji, we use the word Chewbacca to pull up ちゅう in our memory.

If you remember back to the radical 中 , a stick was stuck in the middle of the mouth of something. Let's say it was your mouth, and when you look up to see who did it, there stands Chew bacca. He's even doing his Chewbacca yell. And it isn't a stick in your mouth, it's an arrow from his bowcaster. It just so happens Chewbacca's bowcaster looks just like this kanji too. Go figure.
readings.hint: Imagine the fear and shock you'd feel after finding out it was Chewbacca who shot the middle of your mouth (and head). Touch his fur. Look really close at his weapon. Give him a sniff, too. Just so that memory never fades away.

meanings.mnemonic:
This is a strange kanji because it doesn't really mean anything at all. In fact, what it does is it repeats the kanji that comes before it. For example: 人々 is actually 人人 . This kanji shows up whenever there's a repeating kanji, because it's often easier to write than writing a more complicated kanji twice. That's why this kanji is the repeater . It repeats what comes before it.

To remember that this kanji is the repeater, though, think of a prison with drop s of water dripping over and over in it. Drip drop drip drop. It does that over and over again, driving the prisoners nuts. But it never stops. It just repeats over and over.
meanings.hint: Since this kanji doesn't really have a meaning as much as it does a function, try to change the way you think about it when you're memorizing it.

readings.mnemonic:
This symbol doesn't really have a name, but it's sometimes called the のま because it looks like the katakana characters ノ and マ put together. If you know katakana then this will be easy to read. If you don't, think of it this way:

Your mom is the repeater . She is trying to force feed you over and over again until you explode (literally). You keep yelling " No ma ( のま )!" but she doesn't stop. She's on a repeating track, never stopping, repeating forever.
readings.hint: If you feel like you're so full you're about to burst, and if you imagine your mom doing this to you (how could you, ma?), you should be able to remember the reading of this kanji. That being said, the reading is mostly only useful in the event that you have to identify a 々 — otherwise, you won't see it much.

meanings.mnemonic: If you loiter around to poop in winter you never know what could be happening behind you. After you're done you could turn back to see something really crazy that you missed because you took so long to poop in the cold.
meanings.hint: Imagine trying to loiter while you poop in the winter. You're just trying to take your time as you squat over the snow, but soon you hear things behind you.

readings.mnemonic: You finally look behind you and see Go dzilla ( ご ) and a giant こう いち having a violent battle back there!
readings.hint: Godzilla and こういち were going at it behind you the entire time you were just lazily taking a poop in the snow?! Ahh, that's so embarrassing! And also dangerous, they could have crushed you! You should look behind you more.

meanings.mnemonic: The rice paddy radical and the rice paddy kanji are the same, making this one super easy to remember, at least meaning-wise.
meanings.hint: Just know your radicals, and you will know your kanji as well.

readings.mnemonic:
To remember the reading た , we use the word "taco."

You're in the rice field... but when you lean down to examine the rice more closely, you see that it's not rice that's growing, it's a bunch of little ta cos ( た ), blossoming out of the ground.
readings.hint: Smell the tacos. Touch the tacos. Taste the tacos. This is definitely a very strange rice field.

meanings.mnemonic: The hat crowns the king , because all kings must have one.
meanings.hint: Think of as many kings as you can think of. Guess what? Literally all of them wear hats! Think of strange hats, funny hats, normal hats, and crowns. Just make sure you're amazed by the fact they're all wearing them.

readings.mnemonic: But, as you're going through all the kings, it turns out not all of them have hats after all. The last one you come to is hatless. He's the king of Zen ( ぜん ). He has simple clothes and a shaved head, unlike all the other fancy kings standing around nearby.
readings.hint: To add to this story and make it more memorable, you can imagine the other kings getting upset, saying things like "All kings must wear hats! You are no king!" The king of Zen Buddhism just ignores them and begins to meditate.

meanings.mnemonic: The king radical and the king kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Hopefully you're a king of radicals... because if you are, this kanji was super easy for you!

readings.mnemonic: When you see the king you need to say, " Oh ( おう ) King! Oh King, oh no, oh why are you looking at me like that? Oh King... you aren't going to chop off my head are you? OH NO!"
readings.hint: You need to be polite when you talk to the king and add "Oh" to the beginning, otherwise the king will be pretty angry and bad things might happen.

meanings.mnemonic: The moon kanji is made up of the moon radical. This kanji is also used to mean month .
meanings.hint: This should be EZPZ as long as you know the radicals already!

readings.mnemonic: The moon really gets ( げつ ) to your guts ( がつ ). Staring at the moon for too long will give you terrible indigestion, so be careful!
readings.hint: Use the げつ reading when this kanji means "moon", in words like "moonlight". Use げつ for counting months, and saying things like "last month and "next month", too. Go for がつ in the names of months, like "January".

meanings.mnemonic: Narwhal s do construction with their left hands (or flippers, I guess). That's because all narwhals are left-handed!
meanings.hint: Picture a bunch of narwhals constructing an underwater ice village. They're all working away with their left hands.

readings.mnemonic: In their left hands, the narwhals all hold saw s ( さ ). That's because the ice for their homes is already there, so they just need to saw away at them to make the shapes they want. Narwhal construction is actually very simple.
readings.hint: If you're right-handed, imagine trying to use these left-handed saws. It flips and flops all over until you drop it. But if you're left-handed it feels just right!

meanings.mnemonic: A leader with a spoon is a powerful thing. Even something as ordinary as a spoon can create great change in the world when given to the right person.
meanings.hint: Imagine your leader-hero holding a spoon out, then doing something awesome with it to totally change everything for the better. It's amazing the change this person can bring about with such a simple, everyday object!

readings.mnemonic: Then, this hero of yours takes their spoon and says some magic words into it, changing it into a car ( か ).
readings.hint: Of course, doing something like this is utterly amazing. Feel amazed in your imagination, because if you don't, then it'll be hard to remember the reading for this kanji. If you are amazed, you'll know that spoons going to cars = the reading is か , so make sure you take the time to be amazed.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical death and the kanji death are the same.
meanings.hint: This thing is deceased. It ain't alive anymore.

readings.mnemonic: Your death will be because of a bow ling ( ぼう ) accident. Oh, you didn't know? You're going to be hit by a giant bowling ball and crushed flat.
readings.hint: You didn't want to know what would cause your death, you say? Well that's a shame. I guess you could try to avoid bowling from now on, but that won't do any good. Mark my words, your death will be bowling-related.

meanings.mnemonic: As you dig under the ground you discover a head resting near a mountain , which are both strange things to find beneath the earth.
meanings.hint: You'll want to focus on the part where you find both, not so much the things themselves. Think about how you can't believe you did so well finding both things at once. Usually you only find one or the other when you're searching.

readings.mnemonic: But, you dig too deep and water flows into where you are. Your only hope is a row ( りょう ) boat floating off in the distance.
readings.hint: Also think... "What's that row boat doing here?" But, it's your only hope, so you have to focus on it really hard.

meanings.mnemonic: This is the same as the radical for east , so it means east too.
meanings.hint: Remember the radical mnemonic? The sun rises behind that tree in the east!

readings.mnemonic: You see a man walk straight toward the sun rising in the east . It's so bright he can't see anything, so he trips and he gash es ( ひがし ) his knee on a rock. Yikes… But he gets back up, determined to head east, and, oh no, he gashes his other knee! OK, he should probably give up on going east.
readings.hint: Imagine the secondhand pain you'd feel watching him as he gashes his knee. Ow… Surely nothing in the east can be worth that much suffering?

meanings.mnemonic: If the yakuza hands you a spoon , death is coming for you. It's the death spoon, a sign that the yakuza is coming to cause your death.
meanings.hint: This spoon isn't for pudding, it's for death!

readings.mnemonic: Death comes in the form of a shee p ( し ). The death sheep works for the yakuza, and kills anyone who has their spoon.
readings.hint: Picture the death sheep. He looks like a black shadow, and he has a scar on his face. He's holding a bunch of spoons, from all the other people the yakuza marked for death!

meanings.mnemonic: The radical for eat and the kanji for eat are the same! Lucky you.
meanings.hint: If you know the radical, you basically know the meaning(s) of this kanji. There is a second meaning added to it, though, and that is meal . Just think about the things you eat? What are they? In the simplest form, they are just meals. So, if you know "eat" you should be able to remember "meal" as well.

readings.mnemonic: Thank goodness, you finally get to eat your meal . You're so hungry. Someone has been starving you and now they've finally given you food. Unfortunately, though, every time you take a bite, you get a shock ( しょく ) from somewhere.
readings.hint: So, do you keep eating and keep getting shocked? Or do you not eat and continue to starve? What is worse? Obviously you're in one of the Saw movies, or something, too, so feel the fear. What will you choose? Life with shocks or death from hunger?

meanings.mnemonic: A narwhal and a moon is all you have . Imagine one of each under your arms.
meanings.hint: You don't have anything else. This is it. All you have.

readings.mnemonic: Everything you have (except the narwhal and the moon) were taken from you by a roving gang of you ths ( ゆう ).
readings.hint: They came and took your things. Now they have all your stuff, and you only have the moon and a narwhal. You don't have much anymore, but at least you have these things still.

meanings.mnemonic:
A dirt alligator lives in the earth , not the water. They can swim through the earth itself! Which is pretty scary.

You can think of this word as ground too, because that's kind of what earth is made of.
meanings.hint: Imagine seeing the earth moving as a dirt alligator rises out from the ground. You should run.

readings.mnemonic: The only way to keep this dirt alligator from dragging you under the earth and eating you is to feed it chee se ( ち ). That way it won't lead you to a horrific death under the earth by dragging you under and spinning you around until you eat dirt (literally).
readings.hint: If you ever see someone throwing cheese on the ground, now you know why. They aren't crazy, they're saving us humans from what's lurking under the earth.

meanings.mnemonic: The house radical makes up the house kanji! Pretty easy. This kanji is also used to mean home , so try to remember that too.
meanings.hint: I guess not all houses are homes, and not all homes are houses. But there's a pretty big overlap, right? And in most situations, this kanji's got you covered.

readings.mnemonic: What do you do in your house ? You ea t e ggs ( いえ ), that's what. You eat eggs three times a day, every day. You really love eating eggs in your home !
readings.hint: Imagine all the eggs you've got in your house. Whole fridgefuls! It's not a normal house, but that's okay. If you couldn't eat eggs, it wouldn't feel like home.

meanings.mnemonic:
There are some horns on the ground and a moon knife in front of you. When you try to move, they always rotate around with you, staying right in front. You can't escape these objects, for some reason.

This kanji means before too. Something that's in front of you is before you, after all.
meanings.hint: Perhaps it's some kind of magic, but you don't care. Why are these strange objects always in front of me? Why won't they stay put? This will get pretty freaky after a while. Try to run. Try to hide. Spin around. Do whatever you need to do. No matter what, these objects are always in front.

readings.mnemonic: You're going crazy with these things in front of you all the time. You finally seek the help of a Zen ( ぜん ) master, who tells you that meditation will save you from this curse. You try it out, and sure enough, the objects are gone! It worked!
readings.hint: Go ahead and do some Zen Buddhist meditation. Maybe it'll help you to remember this kanji's reading better (and calm your mind so you're ready for the next kanji!).

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami of tongue s is coming at you. As it washes over you, you feel each individual tongue moving around on your body, licking you. They make you feel so... lively !
meanings.hint: Your entire body wakes up, you feel so lively! The tongues are making you more aware of yourself and your surroundings than you've ever been. You want to dance, you want to sing, you want to live your life.

readings.mnemonic: This lively feeling isn't just happening to you, the tongue tsunami is licking it into all of the cats ( かつ ) in your neighborhood too!
readings.hint: You and the cats are running around playing together, because you can't seem to get the lively feelings to stop. You have to get the energy out, but the cats have sharp claws and they're scratching you.

meanings.mnemonic:
Your private grain s are meant to stay private ! You shouldn't be sharing them with the whole town, they're just for you.

This kanji also means I . Why? Because what's more private than you? I mean, I! Basically, some things are only the business of you (I) and those things should be private.
meanings.hint: Look at your private grains. Who are those for? Why, they're for I!

readings.mnemonic: You're getting into the zone, thinking about yourself ( I ) and how much you love having things that are private and just yours, when you realize there is a shee p ( し ) in this grain bag. And it's eating all of your (I's) private grains. No!
readings.hint: The moral of the story is: don't let a sheep sneak into your private stash of things just for you (I). I'm sure this is a metaphor for something important...

meanings.mnemonic:
The long radical and the long kanji are the same!

This kanji also means leader , because the longer a person is, the better a leader they'll be. Or maybe it was... the longer they've been somewhere? Whatever, longness = leaderness, you know?
meanings.hint: As long as you know the radical long, you know the kanji long as well.

readings.mnemonic: You see a very long person. It's Mrs. Chou ( ちょう ), who has made herself long, like her street. "I made myself long so I can watch more of the street, my domain. I'm the leader of these streets. Watch yourself not-long person," says Mrs. Chou.
readings.hint: Long isn't an idea that's easy to imagine in any specific sense, but you can try some other things too, as long as it gets associated with Mrs. Chou . For the most part, when you see a kanji with the radical 長 the reading will be ちょう .

meanings.mnemonic: Your fortune boil s down to a single point in time. One point in time determines how the rest of your future will go.
meanings.hint: Imagine actually going somewhere to get your fortune told and watching a big boiling pot of goo. As it boils, it melts down to a single point at the very bottom. Where this point is determines your future.

readings.mnemonic: As you stare at the point , you watch in awe as it moves to the ten ( てん ) spot in a long series of numbers.
readings.hint: Is the ten spot good? Are you going to live a long life or does this point mean you're only going to live ten more years?! Ahh!

meanings.mnemonic: You take an arrow to the mouth because you know too much. You know some very sensitive information, and someone's sent a bow-slinging enforcer to pin your big blabbermouth shut with arrows.
meanings.hint: Think of all the important things you know that you were hoping to blab about: JFK's real killer, the truth about Area 51, Koichi's shocking connections to the Illuminati. You better hurry and get this arrow out of your mouth so you can tell the world what you know!

readings.mnemonic: One of the things you know too much about is the shee p ( し ) plan for world domination! Yes, you know that sheep are in fact plotting to overthrow humanity and take over the world.
readings.hint: Take a closer look at that bow-slinging thug who fired the arrow into your mouth. Their shadowy silhouette looks awfully… woolly, doesn't it? It's undeniable: the sheep are trying to shut you up!

meanings.mnemonic: The only woman in this city is your older sister . She's the only woman surrounded by a bunch of men, but she's following her dreams in the big city.
meanings.hint: You want to go to the city someday, but you're not old enough yet, like your older sister is.

readings.mnemonic: Your older sister may have moved to the city, but she kept her pet shee p ( し ) with her. Now the only woman in the city is your older sister, and the only sheep is her pet.
readings.hint: Picture your older sister and her sheep walking through the city. Everyone turns to stare at them, because they're the only ones of their kind.

meanings.mnemonic: There are mouth s, twenty of them, singing beautiful music together. The music seems to bend toward you through the air.
meanings.hint: Listen to the mouths. All twenty of them. They sound great. Have them sing your favorite music. Imagine the music coming at you, bending all around in strange shapes through the air.

readings.mnemonic: The twenty mouths make so much music that they literally cloak you ( きょく ) with it. They cloak you with the music.
readings.hint: Imagine how you feel as they cloak you with music. Feel the music all around you, enveloping you. Become the music itself.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical neck and the kanji neck are exactly the same!

readings.mnemonic: The only thing you do with your neck is use it to swallow lots of coo l bea ns ( くび ). Mm mm. Feel those cool, cool beans sliding down your neck. You don't really taste them, but you know they're there.
readings.hint: Look at yourself in a mirror as the cool beans make their way down your neck. You can see them, like little ants marching all the way down to your tummy.

meanings.mnemonic: With a roof over her head, a woman can relax . She doesn't have to worry about rain or snow or finding a place to sleep. The roof also happened to be super cheap , so she can even relax about her finances.
meanings.hint: Picture the super relaxed woman lounging under her cheap but sturdy roof. Ah, she doesn't have a care in the world!

readings.mnemonic: As she relax es, she pulls out the cheap est, most relaxing food in the world: an chovies ( あん ).
readings.hint: Think about it: anchovies cost next to nothing, they come in cans, you don't have to cook them or cut them up. You just munch them down. There's nothing more cheap and relaxing than eating anchovies under your own roof.

meanings.mnemonic: The sheep radical and the sheep kanji are the same in looks as well in meaning.
meanings.hint: You are no sheep—you study your radicals, unlike most Japanese learners. You'll learn sooo much faster!

readings.mnemonic: To remember よう , we use the word "Yogurt." The sheep are after you. There are too many of them, and they're chasing you down, stalking you... because you stole their sheep's milk yo gurt ( よう ). Now you're running and crying and trying to eat the yogurt before the sheep catch up and stomp you down with their hooves.
readings.hint: Imagine the taste of this yogurt. You're scared and tired from running, but it tastes great!

meanings.mnemonic: You put a roof over the ground to make your own private patch of dirt . This is your room ! That's really all a room is, you know? A covered, private place where you can have some time to yourself.
meanings.hint: Imagine the room you live in now. Take away all the stuff and you're left with a roof above the ground. Your own little private place atop the dirt.

readings.mnemonic: The only problem with your room is that you can't always be alone. Because of your dad's shih tzu ( しつ ). The shih tzu likes to come into your room and pee everywhere. Ruining your privacy completely.
readings.hint: Imagine watching the little shih tzu come into your room and lift its leg to all your things. This isn't fair. The shih tzu has its own room to pee in. Why does it have to ruin yours?

meanings.mnemonic: You scoop some grain s into a ladle to do science . You're doing experiments on these grains in your science course .
meanings.hint: Imagine carefully ladling grains into different piles so you can do science experiments on them. This course is going to make you an expert. You'll be the best in this department!

readings.mnemonic: Using your science skills, the right temperatures and measurements, you manage to get these grains to grow into a ca r ( か ). This is the science department 's special car now!
readings.hint: From your science meddling, these grains grow huge, red, and sprout sports cars when they're ripe. This is the true power of science!

meanings.mnemonic: Think about Japan. Japan is a country made up of islands. They're basically three drop drop drop s in the large river that is the Pacific Ocean. These are Japan's states or province s.
meanings.hint: Also, another way to think of this is that the river radical is actually just three rivers, separating drops, which are capitals of various states. Rivers are usually great ways to divide up a country, and states are one way to do it!

readings.mnemonic: Each state has its own shoe ( しゅう ) to represent it. The bottom of each shoe is the shape of the state, so when you step it leaves a state-shaped imprint in the ground.
readings.hint: The state shoes also have things like the state birds, state flowers, and state fish printed on each of them. They're quite collectable, you should buy a pair for your favorite state!

meanings.mnemonic: There's an ear just sitting on a stool . Nobody's watching, so, you take it.
meanings.hint: Feel the rush of stealing something (even though it's a weird something, admittedly). Start walking away quickly. You don't want to get caught!

readings.mnemonic: You're the kind of person who likes to take things. "What things?" you ask. Little toe s ( と ). Because that isn't creepy.
readings.hint: When someone asks you "Do you like to take little toes?" your answer is always "Toetally."

meanings.mnemonic: A long time ago, someone put up a canopy over their fortune telling booth and created the first shop . This store was fortune-based, but since then lots of different stores have popped up!
meanings.hint: Picture the very first store in history. A fortune teller decided enough was enough, they should be in some kind of structure where it's safe, official looking, and more permanent.

readings.mnemonic: This shop quickly turned into ten ( てん ) shops! It was a good idea, after all, so all of the other people selling goods and services decided to copy it and create their own shops. The next day there were ten more.
readings.hint: Picture ten shops, all made up of someone selling something under a canopy. The first one is the fortune teller, but the nine others are whatever you want them to be!

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami is coming, so you point your gun out the window at it. "You better turn back, sea , or I'll shoot!"
meanings.hint: You'll have to think of the "sea" as its own entity, controlling the tsunami, otherwise you might confuse and combine the two. So when you point your gun out the window, aim it at the "sea," not the "tsunami." It's like aiming for the head of a zombie and not the arms or legs.

readings.mnemonic: The sea decides it doesn't want to die today, so it turns its tsunami around and goes the other way. You hop in your kay ak ( かい ) and chase after it with your gun, to make sure it actually returns to the sea and doesn't just drown someone else.
readings.hint: Imagine paddling in your kayak, your gun still in one hand, as you chase the sea back to its home.

meanings.mnemonic: In a rice paddy you find a heart and it makes you think . You think a lot of thought s like, "Whose heart is this? Why is it in a rice paddy? Why am I thinking out loud?"
meanings.hint: Think, think, think. Think about this rice paddy heart.

readings.mnemonic: As you are think ing these thought s, you spot someone on the edge of the rice field. He really reminds you of someone. But who? That's it! He reminds you of that bartender on The Simpsons. What's his name? You think some more. What is it? Oh! Moe ( おも ) is his name!
readings.hint: If you don't know Moe from the Simpsons use a different Moe, perhaps the one from the Three Stooges? You should be able to come up with one. Think really hard about the Moe you know best and imagine them standing watching you from the edge of the rice field.

meanings.mnemonic: You stop at a small slide . What were you doing before you stopped here? You were walk ing.
meanings.hint: Question is, what made you stop? Imagine yourself walking at a nice casual pace... then imagine something that would make you stop that's incredibly interesting and strange. Maybe an alien is going down the slide? He would say "keep walking, friend."

readings.mnemonic: So, you're walk ing around, minding your own business, when suddenly something flips up and whacks you in the face. You stepped on the head of a hoe ( ほ ) and it caused it to swing up from the ground and hit you.
readings.hint: Of course, imagine the pain of your face. Did your nose break? After bringing yourself together, look down at what hit you. It's a hoe! How did you manage to step on just the right spot to flip it up on you like that? Examine the hoe. Pay close attention to it. Whose hoe is this? etc.

meanings.mnemonic: If you can steal the flowers that adorn a hat worn by an angel , you can use them to make delicious tea . Naturally, angels decorate their hats with only the most aromatic and delicious flowers fit for brewing.
meanings.hint: Imagine wrestling with an angel for its flowery hat, then running home with it. Success! Oh yes, this tea is going to taste… heavenly . *rimshot*

readings.mnemonic: After brewing the tea , you look at the hat again and find a nametag inside that says Cha d ( ちゃ ). You decide that from now on this tea variety will be known as Chad tea, in honor of Chad.
readings.hint: For some reason knowing the angel's name makes you feel extra guilty. Chad the angel, flowerless and hatless... Oh well. At least its name lives on in this heavenly tea.

meanings.mnemonic: The sun that gives life is our very own star (which we happen to call the sun). Of course, all the other stars are probably giving life to other planets as well.
meanings.hint: Imagine all sorts of stars all around the universe giving life to planets as suns. These are all the stars you see in the sky and there are billions of them. I hope you don't feel overwhelmed by how minuscule we really are when you look up at all of them.

readings.mnemonic: "All these life-giving star s need a protector," you say, pulling out your sa ber ( せい ). It's a big responsibility, but with your trusty saber in hand, you're sure you can defend our star system.
readings.hint: Now you're soaring amongst the stars, swinging your saber at all manner of cosmic foes, and... wait. No, that was a dream. You roll over and pat your saber. The two of you are ready just in case the stars need your help.

meanings.mnemonic: While riding your scooter , you take your sword and cut out an area on the ground.  You carefully cut all the side s as you go. This area's all yours now!
meanings.hint: Imagine riding your scooter around in a really large area, pressing your sword into the ground as you do. Once you've finished carving out the sides of the entire area, you claim to everyone around that this is YOUR area, and they aren't allowed in.

readings.mnemonic: As you look down at your area with its sword-carved side s, you suddenly realize there is a hen ( へん ) pecking at the ground in there. "Um, excuse me, hen, I believe I said this is MY area?!" You try to kick it out, but it hops over your leg.
readings.hint: Imagine doing everything you can to get this hen out of your area, but it manages to dodge and outwit you at every turn. You even slash at it with your sword and try to run it over with your scooter, but the hen can't be stopped. This is the hen's area now. You have been defeated.

meanings.mnemonic: Who's that woman pretending to be a jet ? That's my younger sister . *Sigh*
meanings.hint: Imagine a little girl (your younger sister) running around with her arms out, pretending to be a jet, too. Prwoooooo!

readings.mnemonic: Your younger sister is giving some mi ce ( まい ) a ride on her back like a jet.
readings.hint: Ew, gross. Your younger sister is such a weirdo! Why does she have mice on her back while pretending to be a jet?

meanings.mnemonic: A drop hits your mustache . Where did it come from? "Ah, it came from yonder ," you say, facing that way.
meanings.hint: Yonder is a bit of a weird word, but it fits the meaning of this kanji quite well. You'll have to trust me that it's more useful in Japanese than it is in English. When you imagine through this mnemonic, make sure the drop comes from the side and hits you in the mustache with quite a bit of force. That means it came from a far distance thus making you look over "yonder."

readings.mnemonic: Who shot this drop at you from yonder ? You look over yonder, and see a figure. It is こう いち .
readings.hint: "Who is that over yonder!" you yell. "It is I, ( こういち )," he yells back. "I shot at you over yonder because you weren't doing your kanji reviews often enough and you're taking up space on our database." "But I'm paying you!" "Oh, that's true. Well, I'm going to walk over yonder and leave you alone, then."

meanings.mnemonic: The radical black and the kanji black are exactly the same.

readings.mnemonic: You're in a completely black room. There's no way out, and you can't see. The room starts to fill with liquid. It gets in your mouth. You taste it. It's Coke ( こく )! Mm, tasty Coke.
readings.hint: You need to drink all this Coke, otherwise you're going to drown. Drink this Coke and keep tasting its cold sweetness as you drink it all to survive.

meanings.mnemonic: This tree has an umbrella , which it's holding over its head. Strange, because trees normally like water… Turns out, this tree wasn't getting showered in water, but in dollar bill s instead.
meanings.hint: The sick part is that all those bills were once this tree's friends. Picture the tree sobbing uncontrollably, as pieces of its former friends fall everywhere around it. No wonder it needs that umbrella.

readings.mnemonic: And the person who gave the tree an umbrella to protect it from all those bills ? Satsu ki ( さつ ), a little girl famous for… handing out umbrellas, I guess. Makes sense that Satsuki would take care of these bills then.
readings.hint: Try to remember this little girl named Satsuki, since she'll come up whenever the reading for a kanji is さつ (there's a good handful of them). Picture her in your mind, with details like her hair and clothes. Maybe even holding an umbrella or standing by a tree? You should also know she's kind of a bookworm, I guess, in terms of personality. If you can imagine her clearly now, it'll be easy to remember さつ kanji later on.

meanings.mnemonic: The bird radical and the bird kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: As long as you know the radical bird you know the kanji bird.

readings.mnemonic: Think of a run-of-the-mill bird , whatever comes to mind for you first is fine. Then, your lovely bird swells. The shape of a face, then a hand, start stretching the bird apart until it explodes outward. Covered in blood and feathers is Mrs. Chou ( ちょう ).
readings.hint: After bursting out of this bird, she turns her head sharply in your direction. "I don't just own the streets, I own the birds too. I'm inside every bird, and I, Mrs. Chou, can burst out at any time."

meanings.mnemonic: Your boat has fins and a mouth ! It's still a boat though.
meanings.hint: Picture this boat. It's still a boat, but there is a big mouth on the front and it has fins that it paddles forward with.

readings.mnemonic: This boat belongs to the cen taurs ( せん ). That's probably why you've never seen a boat like it before.
readings.hint: Picture this mouth and boat with a bunch of centaurs riding in it. Centaurs have a lot more parts than humans, so it makes sense that their boats have more parts (animal ones) too.

meanings.mnemonic: Your heart has a slide running through it because you want to be certain that the blood is flowing in and out of it smoothly. Given how important this is, you want to be absolutely certain .
meanings.hint: Implanting a slide into your heart is certain to improve your cardiovascular health.

readings.mnemonic: To be certain that the heart-slide is working, the doctor who put it there hits ( ひつ ) you in the chest a few times as a test.
readings.hint: Feel the impact as the doctor hits you again and again. You're not certain that this is safe, but you do feel certain you've got a terrible doctor.

meanings.mnemonic: When you take your leader 's measurement s, you realize they're too short, so you attach things to them to make the measurements more impressive. Attach some heels to the shoes, then attach another head on top of theirs. There — perfect measurements thanks to these attachments.
meanings.hint: Imagine guiding your leader's dainty feet into the heeled shoes, then attaching the weird head replica. Now picture yourself trying to keep a straight face as you tell them they totally look tall enough.

readings.mnemonic: You have to remember the kun'yomi reading of this kanji, and the part you need to remember is つ . So, think of how you would attach something to something else. What would you need? You'd need two ( つ ) things to put together!
readings.hint: Just focus on how attaching requires two things to remember this reading.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical fish and the kanji fish are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: Nothing fishy about that, right?

readings.mnemonic: You catch a fish . As you pull it out of the water, it starts yelling and pointing: " GO !" ( ぎょ ). "GO GYO GYO GYO!" There's a huge cruise ship coming right towards your little fishing boat. No wonder the fish was yelling GO at you.
readings.hint: Have him continue to scream "GO GYO GYO GYO!" as you try to get your engine started and attempt to flee your impending doom. The fish helps too, because he's pretty worried as well.

meanings.mnemonic: That slide is your husband 's only fault . He loves that slide, more than you, and it's the only thing you don't like about him.
meanings.hint: Imagine looking out the window of your house and watching your husband slide down his slide over and over. He's not even good at it, he falls off the side halfway down most of the time. You want to complain, but he's pretty decent otherwise. This is his only fault.

readings.mnemonic: You try to come up with another fault of his so you can complain, when you see a tiny shih tzu ( しつ ) trotting by. Oh no! Before you can call out, your husband has fallen off the slide and crushed the poor thing. You hear a sharp yelp and then nothing.
readings.hint: You run outside and yell, "AHA! It's your fault that shih tzu died! You and that stupid slide!"

meanings.mnemonic: The elephant radical and the elephant kanji are the same. But! This kanji has another meaning: phenomenon . If you were the first person to see an elephant, you'd call it that too.
meanings.hint: Picture the first Japanese person to see an elephant. He stops dead in his tracks, points and screams out, "What a phenomenon!"

readings.mnemonic:
Elephant s are great and all, except that when they die they turn into elephant zo mbies ( ぞう ). But you never see these zombies because the shou gun ( しょう ) has a special project in place that destroys all elephant bodies when they die.

Hint: The readings and meanings for this kanji match up really nicely. ぞう is always for elephant and しょう is always for phenomenon. Remember it like this, the elephant is the zombie and the shougun keeps everyone from finding out about this phenomenon.
readings.hint: Picture the shougun standing over a pit of zombie elephants. None of these are getting out on his watch, letting people believe in such crazy phenomenons.

meanings.mnemonic: This kanji uses a modified jet radical. The front area of the wing is wider than the middle area of the wing. This may seem counterintuitive, but by doing this they can fit so much more jet fuel. The jet zooms off and keeps going for a long, long time, until it reaches the end of the earth.
meanings.hint: Imagine the end of the earth being like how they imagined it pre-Columbus era. Just a big drop going off into space, giant waterfalls plummeting down. Your crazy jet has made it to the very end! Congratulations!

readings.mnemonic: When you get to the end of the world, you land your jet and put out a few mats ( まつ ) to sit on so you can view the sights.
readings.hint: It's surprisingly dirty down here, because all the water takes the garbage this way. That's why you sit on these mats you brought.

meanings.mnemonic: Under the canopy , you use a tree for your floor . It also becomes your bed , because hey, what is a floor but the original bed?
meanings.hint: You lay it down, cut it into slats, and voila! A floor! And a bed!

readings.mnemonic: It's great having a floor or bed , but whenever you use it you say yucka ( ゆか ) because it just gets so dirty. That's the one flaw of using a floor as a bed. In fact, it's the dirtiest floor you've ever seen, because it's covered in yucca ( ゆか ) leaves. You're sleeping on a yucca floor. Ew!
readings.hint: You can imagine how comfortable it is to sleep on a floor of yucca leaves, but at the same time how horrible the smell must be when they start to rot.

meanings.mnemonic: Sew thread through your top hat and to the top hats of other people you know and you'll turn into a group of top hat wearing people. You're all connected by the thread now, so everyone will assume you're a group, even if that's all you guys have going for yourselves.
meanings.hint: Picture a group of people threaded together by their top hats. Don't you want to be part of a cool group like that? Your own top hat team.

readings.mnemonic: Everyone in this group loves cumi n ( くみ ). You know, the spice or seed. The group take off their top hats and fill them with cumin. You're officially a group for lovers of cumin.
readings.hint: What do you do in the cumin group? You smell the cumin. Cook with it it. Taste it. This group lives and breathes cumin!

meanings.mnemonic: Your clan is where you get your name. But not your given name, your family name . That's why people have names that mean "in a rice paddy" and "near the ferry crossing." The location of your clan determined your surname ! Hope your ancestors didn't live on "Poo Lake" or something.
meanings.hint: Think about your own surname in your language. Where did it come from? Were you a clan of village dwellers? Maybe you made shoes! You should look it up and find out!

readings.mnemonic: Don't worry, I'll tell you where your family name came from. Your family used to herd shee p ( し ). You read that right, your family was of the sheep clan because you took care of sheep all day.
readings.hint: Imagine your ancestors, herding sheep. Thanks to them your name means "sheep people." Thanks, great-great-great-great-great-grandpappy.

meanings.mnemonic: You go down a street that cuts through a rice paddy and leads to a box . When you open the box at the end of the street, you see it's full of image s. Image after image. Drawing , painting , you name it. Someone must have left this box of images here.
meanings.hint: What are the images of, anyway? Well, it's better not to say out loud. Just think of the kind of image you enjoy looking at most. That's what's in the box at the end of the street. Aren't you the lucky one?

readings.mnemonic: Okay, I didn't want to tell you this, but the image s are all drawing s and painting s of ga rgoyles ( が ) and cock s ( かく ). Male chickens, that is. Turns out that's what you wanted to see most. Don't worry, no one is judging you.
readings.hint: Try to see the images of the gargoyles. Winged, stony monsters. Very cool. And the roosters? Colorful little dinosaurs. What's not to like?

meanings.mnemonic: There's a scooter with your mama carrying a task she needs to get done. You see her pass through , hurrying by to get that task done.
meanings.hint: Imagine your mama passing through your house on a scooter, not stopping because she needs to get her task done urgently.

readings.mnemonic: As you watch your mama pass through the house, she yells, "YOU HAVE TO DO THIS TASK TOO ( つう )!" Too?! Oh no, now you have to do it too. Hurry up!
readings.hint: Why do you have to do the task too? Because there are two tasks, so the two of you have to do them.

meanings.mnemonic: The lion drops a drop of saliva onto this person from above. The person is in a much worse place compared with where they were before, since they only just recently fell into this pit.
meanings.hint: Picture the person, the lion is leaning over them, the drop of spit dangling from their mouth. "I-I want to go back where I came from," the person gasps, shaking.

readings.mnemonic: From above there is a loud caw, and a giant ea gle ( い ) swoops down, forcing the lion to back off. The eagle grabs the person with its talons, bringing him into the air! But now he's... in the air being carried by a giant eagle, compared with where he was before, is this really better?!
readings.hint: The eagle came from the sky and technically rescued him, but compared to where he was before, it may not be a better situation. I think this person is going to be eaten either way.

meanings.mnemonic: You're flying in a jet . Even though it's one of the fastest things on earth, your child is sitting in the back asking, "Are we there yet? Are we there yet?" You have to keep responding. " Not yet ! Not yet! NOT YET!"
meanings.hint: Feel the irritation of this. How irritating is this kid going to be the whole flight over? The jet is going as fast as it can!

readings.mnemonic: What a selfish kid, right? Well, all he thinks about is " Me ( み ) me me me me me me me me me me." Hate kids like that.
readings.hint: Go ahead and pretend to listen to the kid asking if we're there yet. Then in your mind go "me me me me me me me" quietly.

meanings.mnemonic: This kanji is the same as the radical it comes from. Lack becomes Lack !
meanings.hint: If you know the radical you know this.

readings.mnemonic:
To remember けつ , we use the word "ketsup." It's the perfect blend of ketchup and catsup!

You're lack ing something in your fridge. You look around, trying to find the ketsu p ( けつ ) you lack. You can't find it, because you lack it.
readings.hint: Imagine having to use regular, ordinary ketchup, or just old, boring catsup on your burger. Or even worse, an actual tomato. BLEH!

meanings.mnemonic: The thread that ties you to your clan is paper . Your papers, that is.
meanings.hint: The paper itself was also made by your clan using thread woven really tightly together.

readings.mnemonic: You need this specific paper to prove that you aren't a Commi ( かみ ), aka a Communist. Or that you are. It really depends on where you want to go and what decade this is.
readings.hint: Imagine a big guard coming up to you and asking to see your papers so they can see what clan you belong to. Well you're from the Commi clan, so you really hope this guard is okay with that.

meanings.mnemonic: The mouth clan are a group of people . They are the people of a nation .
meanings.hint: The mouth clan are distinct peoples. They do, of course, use their mouths.

readings.mnemonic: The mouth clan peoples are a nation of people who use their mouths to say mean ( みん ) things to other peoples.
readings.hint: That's why they're the mouth clan. This nation of people are known for mouthing off and saying mean things. Imagine them, as a nation, saying mean things to you. How awful! All being from the same place, they're all into it too. In a way, they're not the mouth clan, they're the mean clan.

meanings.mnemonic: The king 's village is the reason he's king. Without the village there would be no reason to have a king in the first place.
meanings.hint: If a king has no one to rule then he has no reason to exist.

readings.mnemonic: You can't have reason without re ( り ).
readings.hint: Just like you can't have a king without a village, you can't have reason without re.

meanings.mnemonic: A giant cross with a mouth stands above you. "I am reason !" it says to you. It is the Cross of Reason.
meanings.hint: Because it's a cross with a mouth, it thinks that it is reason in all the chaos.

readings.mnemonic: The reason that this Cross of Reason exists is to combat the you ths ( ゆう ) of the world, who just bring chaos into everything.
readings.hint: "Those youths just do things for no reason! That's why I, the Cross of Reason, am here! I will knock some reason into those youths."

meanings.mnemonic: The moon shines down on a stool and a jackhammer which are there for the upcoming festival . Festivals need activities, and at this festival, you can sit on a stool in the moonlight and use a jackhammer to make a lot of noise. Perfect for a festival!
meanings.hint: Imagine being at a moonlit festival and waiting in line for the stool and jackhammer. You don't want to eat candy apples or ride rides, you want to wait your turn to try out the jackhammer (while comfortably seated on a stool, of course).

readings.mnemonic: This festival is for cy borgs ( さい ) only. Picture the cyborg you made getting excited that you're taking it to the festival. That's why all of the activities have to do with heavy machinery. The rides are mostly just cyborgs letting other people ride them, but it's still pretty fun.
readings.hint: Imagine your cyborg going to this festival. Everyone here has some type of cyborg enhancement, like robotic arms, legs, or braces. Your cyborg is really excited to be around its people and play with jackhammers.

meanings.mnemonic: You'll have to excuse me, having all this power puts a lot of strain and exertion on my body. My power comes from exertion of muscle, so please excuse me if I start to perspire.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself under great exertion. You're attempting to use your power to move something and it's really requiring a lot of exertion to move. Picture yourself doing this action and imagine how difficult it is.

readings.mnemonic: Excuse me, I don’t think I’ve introduced myself yet. My name is Ben ( べん ) and I use powerful muscle exertion to accomplish every task. Ben knows what Ben wants and how to get it. Remember that, kids.
readings.hint: Picture this Ben in your head. Ben always uses lots of exertion to do tasks. Imagine him using an unnecessary amount of power and exertion in carrying out normal everyday tasks.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a tsunami coming and you want to be together with it by yourself (oneself) so you go to the harbor .
meanings.hint: Picture the harbor. You're the only one there. The wind is whipping violently and all of the boats docked around you are being bounced around. You can see the tsunami rising out of the ocean and coming for you and the harbor.

readings.mnemonic: As you look around at the harbor in your last moments you suddenly see someone in the water. It's こう いち ! He's sitting in an inner tube in the harbor waiting for the tsunami too.
readings.hint: Well this sucks. You wanted to enjoy being alone together with the tsunami and the harbor, but now こういち is here ruining your peace. He's floating around in the harbor like everything isn't about to be destroyed, sipping on a fancy drink in his inner tube.

meanings.mnemonic:
If the sun is on someone , they will get hot . That's just how it is. Someone standing out in the sun for a long time will inevitably get hot. The sun is hot, and it makes you get hot too.

Note that this usually refers to hot weather or the heat of an area you're in and doesn't refer to a hot "thing."
meanings.hint: Imagine you're this someone, standing out in the hot sun. Feel the hot rays beating down on you. You're getting all sweaty and sticky from the heat now, ugh.

readings.mnemonic: One sun was hot enough, but then a second one shows up. " Ah, two ( あつ )!" you scream. If you were sweaty and sticky from the heat of just one sun, imagine what this second one is going to do to you. Ah, two hot suns!
readings.hint: Feel the hot rays of sunlight beating down on you from two directions. "Ah, two hot suns is more than I can take," you gasp as your body slowly melts into a human puddle of sweat.

meanings.mnemonic: A leader of business ? Ha! That's me ! That's I !
meanings.hint: This version of I is more boyish. It's a sort of young man/boy way of saying "I," though you'll hear women use it too sometimes (it's a teensy bit tomboyish though).

readings.mnemonic: You use the word I a lot. You say it so much, you just start referring to yourself with the french beaucoup ( ぼく ) for a lot. Because you love yourself a lot.
readings.hint: I am beaucoup I am. (Is that a pun? Did I do it right?) I dunno. Just say ぼく a lot and try to connect all of your approximate knowledges of languages together. Merci ぼく from ぼく .

meanings.mnemonic: Those who stand on top of a village are juvenile . Imagine a bunch of young people standing on the roofs of buildings in the village. That's a juvenile thing to do.
meanings.hint: Picture all those juveniles standing on top of buildings in your village, being bored, and doing juvenile things.
