readings.mnemonic: Mostly, though, the juvenile s are standing on top of village buildings, eating dou ghnuts ( どう ).
readings.hint: Imagine looking up to the top of the buildings in your village. Thousands of juveniles, staring down at you and everyone else, eating doughnuts. The crumbs falling down on top of you. But, the doughnuts smell good, at least. Maybe you try to catch some crumbs.

meanings.mnemonic: If there's a tsunami it's easy to get hot water . Just stick your hand out the window as the tsunami goes by and poof! That water's HOT.
meanings.hint: Picture the boiling hot tsunami water as it comes for you. Psh, this will be easy.

readings.mnemonic: As the hot water comes, you just lift up the bottom of the yu rt ( ゆ ) you live in, and it comes flowing in. All of the cups, pots, and barrels you laid on your floor are now full of hot water.
readings.hint: You decided to live in a yurt because of the easy access it provides to hot water. They're so mobile!

meanings.mnemonic: A long time ago a huge tsunami swept a triceratops up into the moon and extinguish ed its life.
meanings.hint: I’m sure you’ve heard many theories on how dinosaurs went extinct. Well, this is the real story. Imagine this poor triceratops and all his friends being extinguished by this powerful tsunami, sweeping them up into the moon where there's no air to breathe or leaves to eat.

readings.mnemonic: This famous event where almost all life was extinguish ed is reenacted every year for the Shou gun ( しょう ). He's got a bit of an unhealthy obsession with extinguishing life...
readings.hint: Watch the Shougun's eyes light up as an actor dressed like a tsunami crashes into an actor dressed as a triceratops and all of the lights on the stage are extinguished. Spooky...

meanings.mnemonic: There's a canopy and a man called the Yoga King standing in the middle of your garden .
meanings.hint: Somehow your little garden is now being used by the Yoga King to teach yoga to a whole group of random people under this canopy... you have no idea how they got back here.

readings.mnemonic: Not only is this Yoga King taking over your garden , but he's ordering you to serve food to his class. "You there, peasant, fetch these people your freshest ta ter ( てい ) tots from this puny garden to give them strength."
readings.hint: You're not sure what else to do, so you end up serving tater tots in your garden all day to stretchy strangers.

meanings.mnemonic: A bird sits upon a mountain . If you look at the radicals, the bird is quite large and the mountain quite small. Think of this in the mnemonic as well. The reason the mountain is small is because you can only see the top of it, sticking out of the water. On it is the bird. This small mountain with the bird on it is an island .
meanings.hint: Imagine the mountain as small as you'd like. Maybe it's only barely sticking out of the sea, and the bird is having trouble standing on it. Whatever you do, make sure you think of it as an island, possibly owned by the bird.

readings.mnemonic: But this island doesn't only belong to the bird. In fact, the bird shouldn't be on this island, because it belongs to the She ep Ma ma ( しま ), who is like Mother Nature but for sheep. The all-powerful Sheep Mama comes to kick the bird off the island, because this place is only for sheep! All hail the Sheep Mama.
readings.hint: The Sheep Mama is a huge, majestic sheep with magical powers. She kicks the bird off the island with her hooves, and the bird squawks and flies away as fast as it can.

meanings.mnemonic: A stand ing mustache with legs is able to sell merchandise . Most mustaches can’t stand because they don’t have legs. This mustache, however, does. It also has a keen business sense, which is why it’s selling merchandise.
meanings.hint: Imagine this mustache entrepreneur, standing at his merchandise stand, selling his wares. He looks quite dignified, legs and all.

readings.mnemonic: The mustache is selling his merchandise so well that even the Shou gun ( しょう ) notices. He leans down really close to the standing mustache man's merchandise and stays very still. Then he snaps his fingers and his servants come rushing out to buy it all!
readings.hint: Now all of the merchandise belongs to the Shougun. The mustache has nothing left to sell, but at least he's rich!

meanings.mnemonic: Even a mouth -bearing shellfish is a member of the invertebrate family. Yes, shellfish do have mouths, look it up!
meanings.hint: Imagine a shellfish opening and closing its mouth, talking to you and telling you what a proud member of its family it is. It's a strange sight, but picture it in your mind anyway.

readings.mnemonic: If you were a member of the same shellfish family, you'd be able to go to the special shellfish inn ( いん ). Every species has a members-only inn.
readings.hint: Imagine trying to go to the shellfish inn. Imagine all the members-only signs telling you that you can't come in. Womp womp.

meanings.mnemonic: If you loiter around my skin , he will get angry at you. He's my very protective friend, and you don't wanna make him angry!
meanings.hint: He's already eyeing you down, loitering this close to my skin. Might wanna... back up a little.

readings.mnemonic: He may get grumpy if you loiter around my skin, but a well-made curry ( かれ ) always brightens his mood. So give him some curry.
readings.hint: Imagine him turning from a frown to a broad smile as he smells the fragrant curry you've brought.

meanings.mnemonic: Someone s and building s, someones and buildings, someones and buildings. So many someones and buildings. With so many someones and buildings, we have ourselves a metropolis .
meanings.hint: Imagine just a ton of someones and buildings all packed together in a small area. More than a village or town or city. With enough someones and buildings, you end up with a metropolis.

readings.mnemonic: In this metropolis everything is a toe ( と ). The someones are toes, walking around. The buildings are toes, too.
readings.hint: Imagine this huge metropolis. Now imagine every building just being a toe. Tall skyscraper toes. Smaller house toes. Wide apartment building toes. And then the people walking around. They are toes, too. Toes walking around and going into toe buildings, with toe cars and toe dogs. If you imagine the toe metropolis deeply enough, it should be weird enough to burn into your memory.

meanings.mnemonic: If you want a tree to grow straight , you have to plant it properly. Everyone knows that poorly planted trees grow all crooked and twisted. That's just how plants work. If your tree's not straight, start over and plant it again.
meanings.hint: After four or five attempts, you've finally planted a perfectly straight tree. Pat yourself on the back for a job well done.

readings.mnemonic: You plant and re-plant your tree over and over, utterly shock ed ( しょく ) at how hard it is to plant it right. You thought planting trees was easy, so this is a real shock to you.
readings.hint: After planting your tree and failing again for the 100th time, you stand there in total shock. How could it be this difficult?

meanings.mnemonic: Bamboo 's younger brother is counted with an ordinal number prefix . An ordinal number is one that represents a position or rank in sequential order (like first, second, third, etc.). We use this ordinal number prefix before counting these things in Japanese sometimes. Like with bamboo, going down in order toward the youngest.
meanings.hint: Imagine a line of bamboo shoots arranged from oldest to youngest. They look like pipe organ pipes, or a xylophone, and now you need to count them.

readings.mnemonic: To remember you need to use the ordinal number prefix before you count these bamboo, you dye ( だい ) them.
readings.hint: Now they're in descending order already, and you can count them nice and easy thanks to your dye method.

meanings.mnemonic: When something is heavy , you need a lot of power to move it. It’s not going to move unless you use a lot of power. That thing is heavy and unless you use all the power you got, it’s not moving.
meanings.hint: Picture a massive 1 ton weight. It’s heavy. You can’t even begin to move it without some sort of powerful machine to help you. Imagine yourself trying to move it with just your bare hands.

readings.mnemonic: Remember that 1 ton weight? Well, actually it’s a dou ghnut ( どう ). A huge, massive, 1 ton doughnut. It’s super heavy and hard to move, but it’s delicious.
readings.hint: Okay, so you don’t have enough power to move this doughnut as is. In order to move it, you’re going to have to use your superhuman power of eating. Once you eat enough of it, the remainder will be easy to move. Imagine yourself digging into this massive doughnut and taste its fresh baked goodness on your tongue. It’s mighty tasty.

meanings.mnemonic: The king request s a sphere !
meanings.hint: Imagine the king holding a sphere in his hand, and looking at it. It's a green sphere, and that is because...

readings.mnemonic: The sphere is a cu cumber ( きゅう ) sphere. A strange sphere shaped cucumber.
readings.hint: Now imagine the king requesting a cucumber sphere, and holding it up to look at it. If you want, have him take a bite out of the sphere, and say "this sphere is a cucumber?" Imagine the taste of it. Imagine the smell, and its bumpy cucumber skin.

meanings.mnemonic: A " crab moon " is a specific period of time .
meanings.hint: Imagine looking at the moon and seeing a crab on it and thinking: "oh, it's the crab moon time period ."

readings.mnemonic: When the crab moon period of time is upon us, we must take a special key ( き ) and lock everything in such a way that crabs cannot get in.
readings.hint: Imagine a key with a little crab moon on it, denoting the Crab Moon period of time. Then, go around and lock everything with it and watch thousands of crabs trying to get into everything. Luckily, they can't, because you have a special key that locks things so crabs can't get in. When the Crab Moon time period is over, you can use your key to unlock everything.

meanings.mnemonic: White water shoots out from a spring in the ground. Due to the force the water shoots from the spring, the water looks white with all the air bubbles.
meanings.hint: Imagine these geysers and the white water shooting from the springs under the ground. Now imagine yourself standing over one when the water shoots out. You’d get super wet and probably knocked over due to the force of the spring water. Imagine this happening to yourself.

readings.mnemonic: From the spring , a cen taur ( せん ) emerges.
readings.hint: Imagine this centaur pushing its way out from the hole in the ground, plugging the water as it squeezes through the hole. Why was there a centaur in the spring? Once it gets out, imagine the centaur standing in front of the spring as it bubbles up and starts shooting water out again. This spring belongs to the centaur.

meanings.mnemonic: Big products need to be put in a big container . A small container just won’t do. These products are big and they need a big container to be contained in.
meanings.hint: Imagine having all these big products. How will you contain them all!? Imagine yourself stuffing all of these big products into their respective containers.

readings.mnemonic: Once you get the big products into a container , you then lock it with a key ( き ). You wouldn’t want these products to bust out of their containers, so it’s best to lock them up with a key.
readings.hint: Imagine the size of these big keys you have to use to lock these containers. It’s a really big key. Picture yourself straining to turn the key in the lock.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical can is the same as the kanji can . Usually when we talk about a can with this kanji we mean a tin can , though.
meanings.hint: Picture a tin can in your mind as clearly as you can.

readings.mnemonic:
The reading for a can is かん , which seems impossibly easy. If that isn't close enough for you, picture this:

Genghis Khan ( かん ) is drinking out of a tin can. He glug glug glugs, gasps, and wipes his face. He holds out the can, "Khan made cans," and winks.
readings.hint: かん is can, it's really easy, come on. Just take this one.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami lifts all the trash out of the river and it stream s out onto the shores.
meanings.hint: As the tsunami recedes, watch as all of the trash comes streaming out of the river and crowds the bank.

readings.mnemonic: Next to the trash stream you suddenly see Ryu ( りゅう ) from Street Fighter and he looks pissed. He's furious that so many people throw their trash in the river and it causes streams like this.
readings.hint: Now Ryu is punching and kicking and tatsumaki senpukyaku-ing all of the trash into burnable and non-burnable piles. But the stream doesn't end. Now he's trying to get his other Street Fighter pals to help him beat it back!

meanings.mnemonic: Stop ! That box of rice is actually full of teeth ! I know it says "rice" on the cover, but I promise you those are all teeth and if you bite into them you'll break a tooth of your own.
meanings.hint: Stop what you're doing and look into the rice box. Nope, it's definitely full of teeth. There's a grain or two of rice, but it's like 98% teeth.

readings.mnemonic: Why do I have a rice box full of teeth ? Ha ( は )! Wouldn't you like to know. Ha! I definitely have an answer that isn't weird or creepy. Ha... ha ha...
readings.hint: Ha! Wouldn't you like to know why there are teeth in there? Ha. Ha ha. HA HA HA HA!

meanings.mnemonic: People who loiter around the temple are probably just trying to wait . They don't mean to look like they're up to no good, but it's hard to wait without looking suspicious when you're waiting outside a temple.
meanings.hint: Imagine waiting outside a temple. People are giving you looks. People think you're loitering, not just waiting innocently.

readings.mnemonic: As you wait outside the temple, you can't help but adjust your tie ( たい ). When you wait you get nervous, and when you get nervous you fidget. So now you're messing with your tie a whole bunch.
readings.hint: Imagine adjusting your tie as you wait. Oh no, now even more people are staring at you. The tie thing is making you look even more suspicious!

meanings.mnemonic: You have a rice ladle that's specifically for paying the rice fee . Each scoop of something has an extra fee tacked on, so when you use a material , there's a fee you have to pay in that material as well.
meanings.hint: Imagine ladling up a nice big scoop of rice and then seeing the little fee ladle next to it. Ugh. The material fee.

readings.mnemonic: One day, the fee ladle is suddenly the size of row ( りょう ) boat oars! What! You're supposed to pay a row boat sized fee now?!
readings.hint: That's a lot of material you have to give up every time you want to eat some rice. Maybe you should take this row boat oar ladle and row out into the ocean where no one can tax you.

meanings.mnemonic: If you compare white light to the other colors you'll realize... white consists of all the colors in one. It contains all the colors in the spectrum in one color. It is the one color that is everything .
meanings.hint: Just look at the color white. Compare it to other colors. Now look at white again. Split it up into all the colors, creating a beautiful spectrum. Wow, it's all the colors!

readings.mnemonic: You decide white light is the ultimate color. You decide you want to put it on all things to make everything white. "What a great idea," you think. You try to put it on everything and you nearly succeed... except, the miners ( みな ). They keep getting dirty doing all their mining things. NOOO, your plan is ruined!
readings.hint: Imagine everything covered in white. Everything in the world. Then, you see a dark splotch. It's the miners, they're all covered in coal, ruining all your ambitions. Walk up to them and see all the dirt on the perfectly white uniforms you gave them just a few minutes earlier. Feel enraged at them. How could they ruin all this white you made for all the people of the world!?

meanings.mnemonic: You want to get a scooter , so you have one self ( oneself ) and another self working together to choose . The two tiny yous sit on each of your shoulders, like angel and devil, and argue about which scooter you should choose.
meanings.hint: Your angel self says you should choose the safest scooter. The devil self says safety is for suckers, and you should buy one that's loud and dangerous and covered in flames.

readings.mnemonic: Suddenly your selfs both gasp and choose a scooter in front of you that looks like a cen taur ( せん ). The scooter is designed like a human on the top, the bars are its arms, and the bottom with the seat and the wheels are all horse. This centaur scooter is what you choose to ride.
readings.hint: The angel self says, "I choose this centaur scooter because of its strong arms." The devil self says, "I choose this scooter because it looks like a badass horse monster, wooh!"

meanings.mnemonic: A bundle in winter is the correct way to arrange things. Just make sure you organize it all before winter comes, or it'll be too late.
meanings.hint: Picture all the things you've arranged correctly ahead of winter — logs bundled for the fire, blankets for the night, and yourself, all bundled up in your neatly organized home — just as the first snowflakes begin to fall.

readings.mnemonic: But the most important things to arrange and organize are your sa bers ( せい ). You have an extensive saber collection that you simply must organize before winter arrives.
readings.hint: Last winter you tripped and fell on a saber you'd left lying around. Ouch, lesson learned. That's when your passion for organizing things in bundles started. You know better than anyone the value of arranging your sabers as safely and tidily as possible.

meanings.mnemonic: If someone is headed in your direction with a gun , nock an arrow in your bow and gather your tribe behind you because it’s about to get real! This man with a gun is headed your direction and he doesn’t even know what you and your tribe are gonna do to him. If he gets any closer, you’re likely to shoot that arrow right at him.
meanings.hint: This guy headed your direction with his gun doesn’t look friendly at all. Hear the sound of the bowstring as you nock your arrow and prepare your shot to defend yourself. Then imagine the sound of everyone in your tribe doing the same.

readings.mnemonic: When he starts to get closer, you notice that this guy is a boso zoku ( ぞく ) bike gang member. Bosozoku means “running tribe ” and this guy is running right at you. With a gun. So gather your own tribe behind you and prepare to defend yourself against this bosozoku member.
readings.hint: You remember what a bosozoku is, right? We wrote about them on Tofugu a while ago. They’re bike gang members and the “zoku” in bosozoku means tribe. Remember that article when you see this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: If the sun starts to make a sound , you know it's about to go dark . The sun isn't supposed to make a sound, so if you can hear it, it's probably about to fizzle out or something. After it's gone, obviously everything will be pitch black. Get ready to be in the dark!
meanings.hint: Imagine a weird sound coming from the sun. Maybe a rumble, or something like a balloon deflating? Then everything goes totally dark. Welp, guess you'll be living in the dark for the rest of your life.

readings.mnemonic: Now wandering around your house in the dark you start to feel hungry, so you reach blindly for some food and take a bite. Yuck! It's an chovies ( あん ). You don't even like anchovies, how did they get in your house? Hmm… Dark forces are at work here…
readings.hint: And because it's so dark, the anchovy taste is all you can concentrate on. Imagine that fishy, salty taste on your tongue, the little softened bones sticking between your teeth. Ugh.

meanings.mnemonic: There are sheep right before your eye s, because they just love what you wear . It's a little known fact that sheep are fashionistas, and they arrive daily to admire your style. These sheep are so stylish they wear their tail to one side - in the latest fashion (check out the sheep radical - the tail is sweeping stylishly to the left!).
meanings.hint: Imagine all the chic sheep lining up to check out your style, with their carefully coiffed left-hanging tails!

readings.mnemonic: More sheep arrive every day to admire the clothes you're wear ing. You use a chalk ( ちゃく ) board to keep count of how many sheep come by each day.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself chalking up a line on a chalk board for each sheep that comes to admire you.

meanings.mnemonic: If you use an arrow to shoot a stalk of beans , the bean plant will end up short . When the beans are hit with that arrow, it severs the plant in two, and you are left with a stalk of beans that is much shorter than before.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself shooting this arrow. Imagine yourself nocking the arrow to the bow, and pulling the string back. That bean plant is not short enough, and you're going to do something about it.

readings.mnemonic: All this shooting of arrows to make the bean plants short is taking a long time. Since you're out in the sun, you're starting to get a tan ( たん ). After the day is finally over, your tan is really, really dark.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself being out in the sun for so long that you get an amazing tan. Your skin feels warm all over from all the sun you were getting throughout the day. Feel the warmth coming off of your tanned skin.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a tsunami which washes a plate out into the sun . Because it's in the sun, it becomes warm .
meanings.hint: Right after the tsunami, this plate was wet, but the sun is warming and drying the plate. Imagine looking at the plate and seeing the beads of water evaporate off it as it warms up. Feel the warm steam on your face as this happens.

readings.mnemonic: The plate starts to get so warm that you could fry an on ion ( おん ) on it. Who doesn’t love fried onions? That plate sure is warm enough, so start cookin’ ‘em!
readings.hint: Imagine yourself frying onions on this plate warmed by the sun. Smell the onion smell as the onion fumes get into your eyes and your sinuses. It makes your eyes water as you are very sensitive to onions.

meanings.mnemonic: I have sick in my skin . Just look at it, it doesn't look too good. Whenever I have sickness in my skin, I know that I'm probably tired .
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself being really, really tired. Then look at your skin. It's all blotchy and sickly looking.

readings.mnemonic: Why so tired ? Your body is hea ted ( ひ ) up from exercising way too much. You should let that heat cool down so your skin starts looking better.
readings.hint: Feel all the heat ( ひ ) emanating from your body. It makes you realize how very, very tired you are.

meanings.mnemonic: You thought it would be a good idea to say the body measurement s of everyone in your village and now you have to apologize .
meanings.hint: When you do a bad thing you should apologize. And saying everyone's private body measurements is definitely something you should not have done. So it's apology time.

readings.mnemonic: You were told you had to apologize by the village sha man ( しゃ ). At first you didn't even know why everyone was glaring at you, but the shaman took you aside and explained. And now she's standing behind you with her arms crossed as you apologize to everybody.
readings.hint: "What do we say?" the shaman says, poking you in the back. "Ugh, I'm sorry I told everyone how tiny your socks are, I guess." "And?" "Annnddd I'll never do it again."

meanings.mnemonic: The direction your gun is facing leads straight to a block of tofu . This might be a sign you need to take a trip . If you're out there pointing guns at tofu, you're probably a little… tired… and in need of a change of scenery. Take a trip!
meanings.hint: Imagine coming to your senses after shooting up a block of tofu for stress relief or whatever you were doing. Yeah, you gotta get away from tofu for a while… It's definitely time for a relaxing trip.

readings.mnemonic: So, where do you go on your trip ? To Rio ( りょ ). You had to go way down south to get as far away as possible from tofu. Ahh, nothing like a nice trip to Rio!
readings.hint: Imagine soaking up the sun on the beaches of Rio de Janeiro. Rio has its fair share of guns, but at least there's a lot less tofu around these parts.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical suit and the kanji suit are exactly the same! This kanji can also mean join which makes sense. When something suits you well, like a hat or something, it joins together with you quite well too. Join me, my hat! You suit my head well. It just fit s, you know?
meanings.hint: Just remember that this "suit" means "to suit you" - not the thing you wear, okay!

readings.mnemonic:
You need someone who will suit you. Someone who can join you on your life journey. So you call Grandpa ごう いち .

Note: For the ごう reading mnemonics, we use Grandpa ごういち because こういち is used for the こう reading. So just remember that ごう comes from Koichi's grandpa ごういち .
readings.hint: Imagine you two standing next to each other, posing for a photograph. You two suit each other well. Grandpa ごういち with his gray beard, and you with your [well, you have to fill in this part by yourself].

meanings.mnemonic: A tree root is still just a root !
meanings.hint: Roots are roots, ね .

readings.mnemonic: Tree root s are the perfect roosting place for con dors ( こん ). Unfortunately, living on roots is also why they're always endangered. Condors can be killed pretty easily living on the ground on roots like this.
readings.hint: Picture a family of condors nestled at the bottom of a tree in its roots. Then a huge machine comes over and mows them all down as it cuts all the trees off their roots. Darn you humans! Do you have to ruin everything?!

meanings.mnemonic: This tree must've been sent from heaven , because it has a mouth and a mustache . Like some kind of special heaven tree that talks. And why was it sent? To be a bridge . To be the great heavenly bridge that we all, someday, must cross.
meanings.hint: Picture this majestic tree bridge laid on its side, beckoning you into heaven with its mustachioed mouth. "Cross over!" it says in its bridge voice. Do… do you just step over the mouth? Can you wipe your shoes on the mustache, like it's a bristly doormat to heaven?

readings.mnemonic: Actually, to get across this bridge you have to give it some hash ( はし ) browns. That's what the bridge's mouth is for. Hash browns to get across the heaven bridge? Small price to pay, really.
readings.hint: Hear the bridge shouting "HASH BROWNS" in its bridge voice. You stuff some hash into its mouth and it starts to chew loudly. Oh, ew… Little hash brown bits are getting stuck in its mustache. Fortunately, the bridge lets you across. Unfortunately, it turns out all of heaven smells like hash browns.

meanings.mnemonic: You reach for a spoon to start eating, but your mama hands you an arrow instead and points towards the coat rack . Hm, that's not like her… Suddenly you're filled with doubt . Is this really your mama? You doubt it.
meanings.hint: She's just acting so weird. You doubt she would ever make you hunt for your own meal like this. What are you going to do?

readings.mnemonic: Now full of doubt , you decide to test your mama by putting her in a gui llotine ( ぎ ). It sounds harsh, but the guillotine is the only way to clear up your doubts.
readings.hint: With her head in the guillotine, it's your mama who starts to look doubtful now. "How could you ever doubt me?" she cries. Ugh, now you're really not sure. This guillotine thing has just compounded your doubts.

meanings.mnemonic: If you tie a thread around Wolverine and dip him in water , he'll turn green , like the Hulk. Some kind of weird chemical reaction. But I… I wouldn't try it if I were you. You wouldn't like him when he's green.
meanings.hint: Still, imagine pulling Wolverine from X-Men out of the water with that thread, and seeing him looking kinda green. Pretty green, but he could be greener. We have to go greener! We have to make the Hulk green with envy! So you put him back in the water, again and again…

readings.mnemonic: As the flailing Wolverine begins to turn even more green , he calls out, "HELP ME DORY ( みどり )!" Me, Dory? No, not you. He's calling out to that fish from Finding Nemo, another Disney IP. "Help me Dory, I'm green!" Then Dory leaps out of the water to bite through the thread and save Wolverine… THE END. Fade to green.
readings.hint: These Marvel crossovers are getting ridiculous.

meanings.mnemonic: When a canine puts its mouth on your heart , it's a pretty unforgettable feeling .
meanings.hint: The feeling is kind of hard to describe. On the one hand it's warm and loving, but on the other hand it's also kind of wet and slobbery.

readings.mnemonic: Overall, though, it's a good feeling — so good that you feel like Genghis Khan ( かん )! With this canine lovingly slobbering on your heart, you feel like you could go out and conquer the world!
readings.hint: Just be careful not to let in all the Genghis Khan feelings. The confidence and motivation are good, but the feeling of wanting to wantonly slaughter your enemies is probably one you ought to repress.

meanings.mnemonic: Your soul is blue . As in, it's sad and not feeling well. This is because your soul changes colors depending on your feeling s or what emotion s you're expressing.
meanings.hint: Start with a blue soul. What feeling is that? Now a red soul. What emotion is that? Go through as many colors as you can think of, focusing on the fact that each color represents a different emotion or feeling.

readings.mnemonic: Why is your soul feeling blue? Because Joe ( じょう ), the big local farmhand, was extremely mean to you.
readings.hint: Maybe he was just having a bad day, but that still really hurt… and now your soul’s all blue too. Seems you’ll have to walk around with a blue soul for the next few days.

meanings.mnemonic:
By a tree , there's a sheep in the water with a formal name ender . That means everyone calls him Sheep-sama. No one calls him just "Sheep," since he's kind of a big deal. He's actually like sheep royalty. When he's not in his castle, Sheep-sama enjoys playing in the water by the trees.

This can also mean manner , as in your manner of doing things. I guess someone with a formal name ender has a different manner of doing things.
meanings.hint: Imagine what Sheep-sama must have done to become such important and distinguished sheep royalty. Some pretty amazing things, probably, especially for a sheep.

readings.mnemonic: This sheep with the formal name ender only likes to play outside in the water during the summer ( さま ). Summer is the best season for this fancy sheep. The other seasons just can't compare.
readings.hint: Imagine playing in the water with Sheep-sama in the summer. It's crazy hot outside in the summer, so going in the water feels nice. Imagine the coolness of the refreshing water envelop your body.

meanings.mnemonic: Sheep fins are good for foster children. The sheep have been genetically combined with fish, so the sheep have fins (and they've lost their tails — which is why the tail part of the sheep radical is missing in this kanji). These sheep fins are rich in nutrients, so they're good for fostering big, strong foster children.
meanings.hint: Imagine eating a fish fin with sheep wool on it. It tastes fishy like the ocean but the wool tastes like clothes. It's probably the worst thing ever, but try to imagine you're tasting it right now.

readings.mnemonic: The best way to foster a better relationship with these foster kids would be to give them some yo gurt ( よう ). Maybe they can dip the sheep fins in the yogurt to help mask the strange taste. Yogurt is also very nutritious, so it's a good supplement to the foster kids' diet.
readings.hint: Remember how bad those sheep fins tasted? Now imagine eating your favorite yogurt flavor instead. This yogurt is delicious.

meanings.mnemonic: Your womb spins in a circle and starts to boil with heat . Uh oh, I think you have a fever .
meanings.hint: Even if you don't have a womb, just picture one anyway: it's turning around in a circle faster and faster, as the heat bubbles and boils in you. You can feel this heat spread through your entire body as you burn up with a fever.

readings.mnemonic: The heat from your fever is making your insides and your head and your entire body spin and boil, until your doctor throws some nets ( ねつ ) around you. You were flailing around and they couldn't get you to stop, so they had to catch you in a series of nets.
readings.hint: These nets aren't making the fever heat go away, but they have made the spinning stop at least.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of a big rice paddy is standing in the middle of said rice paddy with an umbrella . Who is this wacky leader? Why, it is I . It is me .
meanings.hint: Imagine that this leader is you. Be rough about it, because 俺 is the "rougher" version of "I." You should only use it in casual situations or if you're a badass.

readings.mnemonic: Why am I standing in the middle of this rice paddy with an umbrella? I'm digging for ore ( おれ ).
readings.hint: More accurately, I'm standing here with an umbrella so I don't get wet. My minions are doing the ore digging, but I am watching to make sure they don't slack off.

meanings.mnemonic: The small radical and the small kanji are exactly the same.
meanings.hint: Knowing radicals sure is nice, huh?

readings.mnemonic: This small kanji would be the perfect gift to give to the Shou gun ( しょう ). The Shougun loves little things, and the little kanji represents all of that.
readings.hint: You read that right, shougun is written with a long う sound. Make sure you spell it right from now on!

meanings.mnemonic: A cult leader has a great idea to recruit a hundred million new members. Since he’s the guy with all the ideas, he is the leader of the cult. His idea is to give every new member a free puppy. He’ll have a hundred million new recruits in no time.
meanings.hint: Imagine a hundred million free puppies. That’s a lot. Imagine playing with all of them. You’d be pretty happy. Unless you hate puppies. Then you’d be pretty unhappy, I imagine.

readings.mnemonic: The cult leader has his headquarters in Ok lahoma ( おく ). Are there a hundred million people in Oklahoma? No, probably not. But hey, this leader is shooting for the stars. First Oklahoma, then the universe!
readings.hint: Picture this cult leader gathering a hundred million people into Oklahoma. It’d be awfully crowded. Imagine yourself being stifled by the bodies of a hundred million other people.

meanings.mnemonic: Pointing a thread to the east is what you practice every day. You're incredibly passionate about eastward thread-pointing, so you make sure to practice every day.
meanings.hint: You're absolutely obsessed with pointing your thread towards the east, so all you do is practice. Practice, practice, practice.

readings.mnemonic: You do all of this practice to try and afford all of your ren tals ( れん ). One day, all of this practice will pay for all of your rentals.
readings.hint: Everything in your life is a rental, so you better get to practicing. Your house, your car, your friends — all of these rentals need to be paid off!

meanings.mnemonic: Only a tree with eye s and a heart has the ability to imagine and to contemplate . For a tree to imagine and contemplate things, it needs eyes to see and a heart to feel.
meanings.hint: Can you picture a tree without eyes and a heart imagining something? Contemplating? Probably not. To imagine or contemplate, a tree definitely needs eyes to observe and a heart to experience feelings.

readings.mnemonic: If a tree can imagine , it likely has a soul ( そう ). Its soul is what makes it possible for the tree to contemplate things, you see?
readings.hint: Use your soul to imagine what this tree is imagining with its soul. Is it contemplating the deep mysteries of the universe? Is it imagining where that squirrel has gone off to?

meanings.mnemonic: If you run into a gate mouth first, you’re going to have a problem . Smashing your mouth into a gate is going to hurt. A lot. The problem you’re now in is one of getting to a hospital and having your mouth sewn back on. The question is, how are you going to get to the hospital?
meanings.hint: Imagine having your mouth bashed into a gate. It’s going to hurt. Imagine how painful this would be and how problematic it is. Imagine that the only question in your mind is how to get to the hospital as quickly as you possibly can.

readings.mnemonic: The reason you ran mouth first into this gate was because you were being chased by a mon ster ( もん ). A monster in and of itself is a big enough problem as is, let alone smashing your mouth into a gate. You poor, hapless soul.
readings.hint: Monsters are scary. Maybe you’ve had a dream where you were being chased by one. Remember how scary that is when you see this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: There's treasure in this tree so you take out your weapon and kill it.
meanings.hint: You didn't just cut the tree down, you stabbed it and sliced it and killed it to death.

readings.mnemonic: But after the kill , Satsu ki ( さつ ) appears and yells at you. She likes trees — remember how she protected one in the 札 mnemonic? — so Satsuki's pretty upset you'd kill one like that.
readings.hint: Here's that little girl Satsuki again. Remember how you imagined her last time? Try to have a picture of her in your mind so these さつ kanji are easier to remember.

meanings.mnemonic: The roof under which the leader can house one hundred of his people is at the local lodge .
meanings.hint: Back in the old days a lodge had to be huuuuge so when a leader brought a hundred people through town they'd have a place to stay.

readings.mnemonic: The lodge shook ( しゅく ) as the hundred people came tumbling in through the door. It shook, but it didn't fall over. The roof stayed firmly in place, and everyone got to sleep under it okay.
readings.hint: Imagine being someone staying at the lodge as they came in and everything shook around you. This lodge would probably last through an earthquake too!

meanings.mnemonic: Under a misshapen crescent moon , a dog boil s as much nature as it can find.
meanings.hint: Picture a weird-looking, misshapen crescent moon casting its light onto the scene of a dog boiling up everything you'd find in nature. Trees, sticks, mud, little animals. All of that is being boiled by this dog by the light of a weird-looking moon.

readings.mnemonic: Destroying nature like this is the dog's way of practicing Zen ( ぜん ) Buddhism. As it breathes in the fumes from all of the different pieces of nature being boiled down, the dog gets really zen and chill.
readings.hint: Picture the dog kneeling in front of the pot of concentrated nature and breathing deeply as it practiced Zen. You kind of wish you could join it, considering how peaceful it looks.

meanings.mnemonic: Gold reveals itself when you stand and see into this mirror . Yes, even you are made of gold. You're so great and valuable, standing in front of that mirror. It's not hard to see, especially in a mirror – you're golden.
meanings.hint: Imagine looking at yourself in the mirror only to see you're made of gold. It would be pretty shocking. Imagine how surprised you would be.

readings.mnemonic: As you look into the mirror you think, " Kaga … me ( かがみ )?" You always forget that you're Chairman Kaga from the Iron Chef TV show. That's why you say "Kaga… me?" every time you look in the mirror.
readings.hint: Picture yourself in front of a mirror, except your reflection is Chairman Kaga Takeshi. (Look up his picture if it helps.) Imagine not recognizing yourself at first, how weird that would feel, then suddenly remembering: "Kaga… me!"

meanings.mnemonic: The Viking opens his mouth , and below it, a shellfish sits. Grab that shellfish and throw it into his mouth to win a prize !
meanings.hint: Imagine a game at a fair where you have to throw a shellfish into the mouth of a hungry Viking. You want this prize just as much as the Viking wants his shellfish.

readings.mnemonic: The prize you're working so hard to win is lunch with the Shou gun ( しょう ). This is a pretty huge prize, considering all you're doing is knocking something over.
readings.hint: You want this prize. You want to meet the Shougun. Soon you'll both be eating shellfish together!

meanings.mnemonic: Just imagine a beast with a lid for a head and fins for feet, running around looking as insane as possible (really imagine it!). When it runs up to you, it shows you the top of its lid-head. On it is written the numbers 666. Why? Because it's a spawn of the devil, and the number six is the devil's number.
meanings.hint: Imagine this beast doing various crazy things, the crazier the better (to help you remember). Make sure all these things are associated with the devil and the devil's number ( six ), so you can remember the meaning of this kanji.

readings.mnemonic:
Whenever we need to remember the reading ろく we just use the word rock .

So, think about the devil and the devil's number. What is often associated with those things? Why, rock ( ろく ) and roll is. Imagine the lid-head fin-legged beast suddenly blasting out rock music in the middle of his destructive mayhem.
readings.hint: Sing along with the monster, if it helps you to remember the reading of this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: What you say at a temple is a poem . It has to be a poem, because if you say anything else it's just plain disrespectful. All of the monks and priests at this temple have to read and recite poems all day, and if they can do it forever, you can while you're visiting.
meanings.hint: Picture all of the people at the temple reciting poems together. You join in too, and it's a really powerful moment!

readings.mnemonic: As you recite a long poem at the temple you see a shee p ( し ) in the crowd. It's opening and closing its mouth like it wants to say the poem too.
readings.hint: You turn to the person next to you and realize you have to say everything in poem form.

meanings.mnemonic: The kanji and the radical that look like this are exactly the same. They are both street .
meanings.hint: As long as you know the radical street, you know the kanji street.

readings.mnemonic: So you're walking down this street , and off in the distance you see the silhouette of somebody. A lady. Big, black curly hair and red eyes. It is the street-woman, Mrs. Chou ( ちょう ).
readings.hint: Mrs. Chou is a character you'll see a lot throughout WaniKani, anytime the reading is ちょう like this one. A lore will develop around her, so the more vividly you can imagine your Mrs. Chou right now, the easier things will get later. In general, she's a mean, evil woman, so as long as you imagine her within those parameters, you're good. And, when I say she is a "street-woman" in the mnemonic, I don't mean she is homeless. She has a nice, middle-class home that's not outside of her financial means. What I'm saying is she wanders the streets looking for evil things to do. These are HER streets. So whenever you see the street radical, know that Mrs. Chou is probably going to show up!

meanings.mnemonic: The radical stand and the kanji stand are the same thing, so now you know them both.
meanings.hint: If you're keeping up with your radical studies, you're also keeping up with your kanji studies!

readings.mnemonic: Think of someone who has to stand up straight for you. These people are pros in hospitality. These are the doormen at the Ritz ( りつ ).
readings.hint: Imagine the doormen being extra straight… too straight in their standing. On their uniform is “The Ritz” written in fine lettering, and as you walk past them, they stand up straight and say “Welcome to the Ritz!” Whatever you do, while you’re thinking about it, make sure you think about how they stand up so straight, and associate it with these doormen, who work, specifically, at the Ritz. If you effectively imagine all this, you should be able to remember this on’yomi. Go through it like it’s a movie in your head!

meanings.mnemonic: The sun radical and sun kanji are the same, huzzah! Also, if you want to remember day as well, you can just think about how a day is the time it takes for the sun to cross the sky.
meanings.hint: The radical for this one is easy too, making the meaning for this kanji super easy as well!

readings.mnemonic:
There are so many on'yomi for this kanji, but since this kanji is so important and common, you'll end up learning them all eventually whether you want to or not. For now, you're only going to have to learn one of the readings, and that one is にち .

The sun is so hot. It focused its rays to take out one man... and that man is Friedrich Nietzsche ( にち ), famous philosopher. He philosophized one too many things about the sun, so the sun decided it was time he paid.
readings.hint: This reading will be easier to remember if you know of Friedrich Nietzsche... so do your research and learn about him if you don't. He's a pretty historical dude. Imagine the sun totally pissed off, too. It makes a grunting noise as it builds up a sun beam to blast him off the face of the earth.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are almost the same! The radical is sword , but we're learning Japanese, so can you name a Japanese sword ? That's right, the most common is a katana , which happens to be the meaning of this kanji as well.
meanings.hint: This kanji also looks a lot like the kanji 力 . To avoid getting them confused, remember that this one is missing the little nubbin at the top, because 90% of the power comes from the sword to cut your enemies down (which is why the sword kanji is 90% of the power kanji).

readings.mnemonic: So now you have this sweet katana in your hands and you're swinging it around like an idiot. Feel free to make whooshing noises as you swing it around. To become a master katana wielder, you decide to travel to とう きょう (Tokyo). とうきょう has the best sword and katana schools in the world, so it makes sense to travel to とうきょう .
readings.hint: Imagine trying to travel to とうきょう with your katana. Airport security is not happy with you trying to take a katana on the plane, so you get a full cavity search. I hope you don't have any other swords hidden anywhere.

meanings.mnemonic: On the ground you put barb s at the bottom of a slide . This is your special talent . When you go down the slide, you fly right past the barbs like some kind of daredevil. Any normal kid would land on the barbs and get hurt, but you have this talent.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself placing the barbs in front of the slide, thinking about how talented you are. It took years of training to perfect this talent. But it was worth it. Everyone on the playground respects you now.

readings.mnemonic: But then people start to say that no human could have so much talent , that you must be a cy borg ( さい ). Are you really a talented cyborg?
readings.hint: Cyborgs have brain implants and cool bionic limbs, so they're basically the most talented people around.

meanings.mnemonic: This kanji is the same as the radical you learned, so it means thousand .
meanings.hint: As long as you know the "thousand" radical, you'll know this kanji as well!

readings.mnemonic: Remember the thousand people going down the slide on a cross in the radical 千 ? Turns out each one of them was carrying a thousand cen ts ( せん ). Go ahead and try to calculate what a thousand times a thousand cents would come out to. It's quite a lot, actually.
readings.hint: Problem is, as they all tumble down the slide the change falls out of their pockets, so there's a thousand times a thousand cents scattered all over on the floor under the slide.

meanings.mnemonic: The water radical and the water kanji are the same.
meanings.hint: So as long as you know the radicals, you know the kanji.

readings.mnemonic: Imagine a lake (i.e. a whole bunch of water ). When you think of water, you should try to think of this beautiful lake. It’s sunny, the trees smell nice (imagine a tree smell if you can – the more senses you use the better!) and on that tree there is an awesome tire swi ng ( すい ). You get onto the tire swing and you are swinging back and forth. The water is right in front of you, and you are able to use the swing to throw yourself into the water. It’s a lot colder than you expected, and you come back to the surface, gasping for breath. The water gets into your mouth and you taste it. How strange, it’s very, very swee t. Imagine the sweetness of the water and how weird it is (really really sweet, this water is).
readings.hint: I know that was a long one, but try to imagine all the different bits. Try to feel the feelings (cold, smell, etc) too. The more you create in this fake memory (which you’re using to remember and associate water with すい (swing)) the more likely you are to end up remembering enough to make it happen. Especially when it comes to the senses part, the more vividly you imagined it, the more likely you are to remember the on’yomi.

meanings.mnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for writing first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means writing as well.
meanings.hint: If you can't remember the radical for writing, maybe you could try… writing it down?

readings.mnemonic:
This kanji has more than one important reading you need to learn! But don't worry, here's a mnemonic to help you:

You always put writing on your bun s ( ぶん ) to keep them from getting eaten by the bun mon ster ( もん ). What kind of writing? Usually it's just an X to show the monster these buns are off-limits. (In fact, it kind of looks like the X part in 文 . Hmm…)
readings.hint: Imagine writing a big X (or even a big 文 ) on all your buns, then showing them to the bun monster. Watch as he turns and goes at the sight of the writing, all disappointed. Not today, bun monster.

meanings.mnemonic: Here we have a head with a lid in it, near the top. Imagine the top of someone's head being closed by a lid. Open that lid up and peer inside... it's full of yen coins, the currency of Japan. Jackpot! And all those yen coins have a nice round shape, naturally.
meanings.hint: This kanji can mean both "yen" and "round"! It's OK if you can't remember both meanings. But it might help to think about how round the yen coins are inside this person's head, which can also be pretty round…

readings.mnemonic: What does the word yen sound like? It sounds like えん . Why? because back in the day, Japanese used to have a "ye" sound, but now it doesn't. Instead, it's an "e" sound, which is why yen = えん !
readings.hint: Picture these round えん in your mind. Maybe look some up online too, just to be safe. See how round those yen ( えん ) are?

meanings.mnemonic: The arrow radical and the arrow kanji are the same. Sort of a "straight" shot to memorizing this kanji, you know?
meanings.hint: Just know your radicals and you'll know this kanji!

readings.mnemonic:
To remember the reading や we use the word “Yankees” (like the evil baseball team, go M’s!).

What is this arrow doing? It’s following around all the Ya nkee ( や ) baseball players, stalking them, waiting to make its move (I’ll let you decide what its “move” is).
readings.hint: Imagine the arrow as a human-sized living thing with legs, arms, and clothes, though. Strangely, it’s able to walk around even though it's an arrow. It has some kind of agenda. Make it a bit evil, if you can. What a strange thing…

meanings.mnemonic: The radical white and the kanji white are the same.
meanings.hint: Study your radicals hard! Most important thing!

readings.mnemonic: The white team was the first to be able to hack ( はく ) the white iPhone.
readings.hint: Imagine two teams (black and white ), wearing their team colors, working really hard on hacking the iPhone. It is a huge contest, and people are watching with a lot of tension, to see who will hack the white iPhone first. Feel that tension, and see the colors separating them so vividly. The colors are important, and the excitement of the winner holding up the hack ed iPhone is too.

meanings.mnemonic: This is another kanji that is the same as the radical. Both tree and tree are the same in looks and meaning.
meanings.hint: So, how are those radicals doing? Do you know them well?

readings.mnemonic: You’re walking through the trees, looking at them. They look ominous. Suddenly, you trip and fall. The trees start to mock ( もく ) you. Even the mocking birds mock you. It’s terrible. It’s painful. You’re being mocked as you pull yourself up off the ground.
readings.hint: Feel the shame, feel the irritation. Make yourself want to run away from those dumb trees that are mocking you.

meanings.mnemonic: You see a manhole lid and a towel , both with the name of a city on them. Decorative manhole lids and souvenir towels — two things cities love to slap their names on, especially in Japan.
meanings.hint: Picture this towel with the name of a city on it. Any city you like. Maybe it's your city!

readings.mnemonic: This city must have a lot of shee p ( し ) because, besides the manhole lid and the towel, all you can see are sheep. There are sheep everywhere. It's a sheep city.
readings.hint: Imagine a city that has a sheep everywhere you look, as far as the eye can see. This city was built on sheep, don't you say a bad word about sheep when you're there.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical and the kanji for cow are exactly the same! That makes it easy to remember, so long as you know your radicals well enough.
meanings.hint: To distinguish this from the "noon" kanji ( 午 ), try thinking of the bit poking out of the top of this kanji as a tuft of hair on the cow's head — a cow lick, if you like.

readings.mnemonic: Have you ever heard of わ ぎゅう (wagyu) beef? That ぎゅう is this ぎゅう .
readings.hint: I hope you know what わぎゅう beef is, otherwise this will be a difficult one to learn.

meanings.mnemonic: The bright radical and the bright kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: If you already know the radical, this one's a freebie. That should make your day a little brighter, hm?

readings.mnemonic: What do you say when you see something bright ? " Ah ( あ )!" Like when you open the curtain and the sunshine hits your eyes, or someone shines a flashlight right into your face. Ahhhh, so bright!
readings.hint: You might also say "Ah!" when you come up with a bright idea, like in those light bulb moments.

meanings.mnemonic: If you have seven swords , there's only so much you can do with them. The main thing? Probably using them to cut something.
meanings.hint: Take these seven swords and swing them around. Cut everything in your path, including yourself. Then, think about how dumb it was to try to use seven swords at once, and then feel the pain of the cut you gave yourself. Ouch, ouch ouch.

readings.mnemonic: Because having seven swords for yourself is too much, you decide to take the swords and divide them up amongst pairs of people. You cut them into pairs, and give sets ( せつ ) to everyone who needs them in the village.
readings.hint: Just make sure you think about how you need to give them away in sets , and not anything else. This is the most important thing to think about in your head as you're trying to establish the mnemonic for your kanji reading memory. It's gotta be sets.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical and the kanji for door are the same!
meanings.hint: Time to open the door to the reading part of this mnemonic.

readings.mnemonic: You open the door and immediately get it closed back on your toe ( と ), literally causing your toe to explode.
readings.hint: Feel the pain of your toe going away. Think about the other side of the door , where your toe is probably rolling around after it blasted off your foot.

meanings.mnemonic:
The radical and the kanji for direction are the same! Huzzah!

There is also another meaning too, though. This can also mean way as well. Think of it this way: When you move in one direction in your life, you are developing your own way. "The way of [your name here]."
meanings.hint: The only direction you need to go in is forward, it's always the right way.

readings.mnemonic: What direction do you always know? Which direction do you always want to go? Ho me ( ほう ). Something about your home always stays with you, no matter how far away you go. So if you're lost and you don't know which direction to go, just think of home!
readings.hint: Close your eyes and picture your home in your mind. Can you tell which direction it is from where you are?

meanings.mnemonic: The kanji is the same as the radical, so this means fat .
meanings.hint: We've fattened you up with lots of juicy radical knowledge, so this one should be pretty easy.

readings.mnemonic: Imagine a fat person who loves to sleep on futo n ( ふと ).
readings.hint: Picture this fat person curled up all cozy-like on their Japanese futon. They look so content! Have you ever slept on a proper Japanese futon, by the way? We're talking premium, cloud-grade comfort — luxurious enough to make you feel like a real fat cat.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical and the kanji for father are the same.
meanings.hint: No, I am not your father... but this kanji / radical set is!

readings.mnemonic: Imagine your father is Cheech ( ちち ) from Cheech and Chong.
readings.hint: Hopefully you know who they are. If not, think about your father laughing like this: ちちちちちちちち〜！

meanings.mnemonic: You have a small slide . The thing about small slides is that few people can use them. Not everyone can fit their bum in a small slide — only a few small ones can make it down.
meanings.hint: Imagine a small slide (how could you not, after you see the radicals?). So few people can use this thing, really, but there are a few little ones who can fit on it at least.

readings.mnemonic: Another reason so few people can use it is that the Sho gun ( しょう ) is trying to go down the slide. We're talking a grown man who's way too big for this little slide, so a few people have to wait behind him — it's a slide traffic jam.
readings.hint: Imagine this in your head. Even the few people who fit on the slide are struggling to get past the Shogun. Meanwhile the Japanese secret service is trying to make sure no one gets close to the shogun while he slides. A few people are crying. They want to slide, they want to leave… It's chaos.

meanings.mnemonic: A narwhal sitting on a stool is your friend .
meanings.hint: Imagine that narwhal sitting there. It waves its flipper and says "hi friend" to you.

readings.mnemonic: "Hi friend ," says the narwhal. "I must warn you about all the you ths." ( ゆう )
readings.hint: Your narwhal friend goes on to warn you about all those youths, and how they will try to take away your friendship with their good looks and young ages.

meanings.mnemonic: Fur and fur are the same as each other, so the meaning of this kanji should be simple. This can also mean hair , too, though more like furry hair.
meanings.hint: Just make sure you're studying your radicals, otherwise it won't be as simple as I'd hoped.

readings.mnemonic: With fur , whisk your imagination to a land where there are stalks of fur all around, just like a big tall grassy field (except instead of grass, it's fur). You're surrounded by the black fur, the wind is blowing. Imagine yourself touching the long fur and smelling it. What does it feel and smell like? Now it's time to get rid of it. There's just too much hair ! To get rid of it, you'll need to mow ( もう ) it down with a lawn mower. Imagine yourself starting up the lawn mower, and taking it around, mowing down the hair. Hear the sound of the mower in your head (they're loud and distinct). The important thing is to imagine yourself mowing everything. Key is to think "fur → in the fur → get rid of the fur → mow the fur," in that order. That'll get you to "mow," which will help you to remember the on’yomi.
readings.hint: Smell the fur as you mow it down. Feel it flying everywhere. It's giant, so it must be pretty intense. Make it more intense to help yourself to remember the reading of this kanji better.

meanings.mnemonic: A triceratops is lying out to dry . But it's so big, and it's lying on its side, so only half of it gets dry.
meanings.hint: Imagine the triceratops on its side, then imagine it getting up. When it stands up normally, half of its body is soaking wet still. It's a half-n-half triceratops right now.

readings.mnemonic: "You're half wet and half dry! I will cut you in half with my laser blaster so you're proper again," a voice says. It is Han Solo ( はん ), the anti-establishment guy who hates when things aren't even.
readings.hint: Imagine him using his blaster to shoot the poor triceratops in half, perfectly down the wet-dry line of its body.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical heart and the kanji heart are the same. Rejoice and let your heart be free!
meanings.hint: Also, make sure you know the radicals by heart, it'll help you to learn more effectively, just like with this kanji.

readings.mnemonic: The heart , where is it stored in your body? Most people thought it was in the chest, but in reality, your heart is stored in your shin ( しん ).
readings.hint: This is when I start kicking you in the shin and you imagine feeling the pain of getting kicked there. As I kick you in the shin, the stress is getting too much for your poor heart inside your shin. Poor shin, and poor heart.

meanings.mnemonic: Your head has a person inside it!
meanings.hint: And, you have no idea how to get them out. They're inside for good, and it's freaking you out. As in, there's a little person in there, running around, controlling your brain. People think you're crazy, but you know better... He's inside there, doing evil things.

readings.mnemonic: You know this kanji means inside , so you can keep thinking about the person that's inside you, running around. You finally discover him, and try to take him out, but before you can, he pulls out a kni fe ( ない ) and threatens to cut you if you do anything irrational.
readings.hint: Imagine him holding the knife out, threatening to stab your brain. You’re in a conundrum here. If you don’t take him out, you’re in trouble, but if you do try to take him out, he might stab you in the brain with his knife. Try to think a way around this situation, focusing mainly on his knife.

meanings.mnemonic: The roof child stands upon the roof, holding up a single letter , a single character , a single symbol for the world to see.
meanings.hint: More people can see this character since the kid is on the roof.

readings.mnemonic: The character that is being shown is "J," which stands for Je sus ( じ ). The kid wants everyone to see this letter and symbol .
readings.hint: Imagine the child holding up the letter yelling at everyone who passes that the "J" stands for "Jesus." And yes, this kid just loves the Big Lebowski.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a private mouth that's a machine . But you have to keep your machine mouth private because it would scare all the non-machine people around you.
meanings.hint: Doesn't this kanji look like a machine mouth? Now wouldn't you want to keep that private from the people you know?

readings.mnemonic: To make sure they can't tell what's machine and what's human, you dye ( だい ) the mouth to make it look like skin. You find dye the same shade as your normal mouth and just slap it on there.
readings.hint: Imagine being so afraid that people will find out about your machine mouth that you have to dye it to match a normal human mouth. Oh, when will this discrimination against machine-kind end?!

meanings.mnemonic: Someone breaks part of your window . Who did this? It was your mother !
meanings.hint: See how the vertical bar is broken and your window frame is all messed up now? Why would your mother do such a thing?! Her hand is bleeding too…

readings.mnemonic: Suddenly your mother points her bloodied finger at you and laughs, " Haha ( はは )!" She keeps laughing, haha haha haha, over and over. You feel even more alarmed.
readings.hint: You thought your mother was going to explain or apologize, but instead she's pointing and saying, "Haha," over and over.

meanings.mnemonic: The life radical and the life kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.
meanings.hint: Study your radicals and you'll read good.

readings.mnemonic: You can save life or take it away with your new sa ber ( せい ). This is the saber you got from doing the correct thing (remember?), and now you need to figure out how to use it with this life. So what do you do?
readings.hint: Imagine having someone's life in the palm of your hands. It's up to you whether you protect it with your saber or... well... do the other thing sabers are for.

meanings.mnemonic: The slide is dry at noon . The sun is brightest at noon, so it dries the morning dew off the slide, making noon the perfect time to slide.
meanings.hint: Picture all the kids out sliding on the slide at noon during their lunch break. It's hot and dry and they're very happy.

readings.mnemonic: Noon also happens to be the time Go dzilla ( ご ) decided to attack for the same reasons: it's dry and kids are using the slide.
readings.hint: Watch in horror as Godzilla's giant foot crushes all the kids and their slide at exactly noon. You'd better go...

meanings.mnemonic:
The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for old first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means old as well.

Note: This "old" does not refer to people. Think of it as "old things" instead.
meanings.hint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic:
Old things are misunderstood by the 子 s ( こ ) of the world.

*Note:* Come up with a 子 character you can use for all the こ reading mnemonics going forward. This child will show up many times throughout your mnemonic journey, and it will help if you imagine the same character the whole time.
readings.hint: Imagine a 子 looking at old things. A record player. A Game Boy. A cassette player. Kids just don't get old things.

meanings.mnemonic: You're known to get the ailment evening toe , which is when your toe catches on fire after getting really, really hot (imagine this happening, ouch!). The only way to fix it is to cool your toe down, and the best way to do that is to go outside .
meanings.hint: After imagining your evening toe overheating, rush outside to fix it. It's the best way to prevent fire toe, which is way worse. In the evening it's nice and cold outside too, so feel this on your skin, and feel the cold air on your toe, which is starting to get some relief.

readings.mnemonic: You go outside (you’re not supposed to be out there at night!) and you’re accosted by a couple of guys. Then, all of a sudden, Guy ( がい ) Fawkes jumps from the roof above you and takes them out, then starts running. “Guy Fawkes!” you yell after him!
readings.hint: If you don’t know Guy Fawkes, you should do a little reading up on him to make this mnemonic make sense. Also, Anonymous People wear a Guy Fawkes mask, if that helps any.

meanings.mnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for now first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means now as well.
meanings.hint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: Right now the con dors ( こん ) are in danger! Not next week, not next year, right now! Haven't you heard that deforestation and pollution are killing all of the condors?!
readings.hint: Go outside right now and do something to save the condors. If you don't do something right now there won't be enough time left!

meanings.mnemonic: The kanji is the same as the radical — it means north .
meanings.hint: Hold on to that radical knowledge! It'll be the north star that guides you through your studies.

readings.mnemonic: You go north , and notice that everyone has a keytar ( きた ). You know, those keyboards that people hold and play like a guitar?
readings.hint: Yep, those things are called keytars. And for some reason, in the north, everyone has a keytar. They're basically obsessed with them. North = keytar. Quite simple.

meanings.mnemonic: If you have a bow and a stick you need to pull the two back together to shoot it. If you can't pull it, you won't be shooting anything.
meanings.hint: Imagine taking a stick and pulling it back into your bow. Imagine how hard you have to pull. This thing is really strong!

readings.mnemonic: When you pull the bow back, your body strains and you feel an immense he at ( ひ ) rise inside of you. You keep pulling and the heat grows hotter and hotter.
readings.hint: Imagine using all your strength to pull on this bow. Imagine the heat rising in your body. There's sweat dripping down your face. It's so hot, but you have to keep pulling!

meanings.mnemonic: The fins that fell onto my sword were sliced into many part s. Following this logic, this also means minute , which is part of an hour, and divide , which is the action of splitting something into many parts.
meanings.hint: Picture the fins falling onto the sword, and being divided into many parts. And if those fins represented hours, they’d be divided into minutes. Makes sense, right?

readings.mnemonic: Now that the fins have been cut into neat part s, it's time to cut something else: bun s ( ぶん ).
readings.hint: This is how you feed your people: neatly divided, delicious buns. Picture these delicious buns divided into perfectly shaped parts. Yummy.

meanings.mnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for task first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means task as well.
meanings.hint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: Today, your task is to eat yo gurt ( よう ). It's nutritious, it's delicious, and I'm telling you to do it, so you have to. A task is basically just something you have to do, so that's what this is.
readings.hint: Eat your yogurt. (Gogurt doesn't count, don't @ me.)

meanings.mnemonic: You cut the leaf with your sword into ten thousand pieces!
meanings.hint: If you can get to ten thousand different pieces, then that means your sword is absolutely perfect. If your sword dulls before you get to ten thousand, you have to throw it away.

readings.mnemonic: Now that you have ten thousand pieces of tiny leaf paper, you decide to turn them into a man ga ( まん )! Each little leaf piece will become a panel.
readings.hint: Carefully draw your manga onto these ten thousand leaf pieces. Maybe you even use your sword again to give them some textured details. You're so creative and talented!

meanings.mnemonic: The stop radical is the same as the stop kanji.
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: You have to stop because there is a shee p ( し ) in front of you. You try to walk around the sheep, but it moves to stop in front of you again. Every time the sheep stops you stop too.
readings.hint: Imagine the fluffy wool of the sheep, stopping you from moving forward. You can't push past it, no matter how hard you try.

meanings.mnemonic: You’ve got a winter with lots of ice . But doesn't any winter have lots of ice? Exactly. That's why this is just winter .
meanings.hint: You go outside. There's ice everywhere. It's freezing cold. Yep, this is definitely winter.

readings.mnemonic: Winter is also the time of year you give out presents: "One fu' you, one fu' you and… one fu' you ( ふゆ )!"
readings.hint: Your pronunciation might be a little weird. But that’s okay. People still love your presents, and the way you say "fu’ you!"

meanings.mnemonic: The canopy you use in private is very wide .
meanings.hint: You want your private canopy to be wide so you can sprawl out under it as much as you want. No one's going to come in and tell you to close your legs or sit up straight. So it can be as wide as you like!

readings.mnemonic: You like being wide because it makes you feel like a different person. But not just any person, like a super hero ( ひろ )!
readings.hint: Picture yourself lying under your wide, private canopy pretending to be a super hero on the ground. You're stretching a nice, wide super hero pose, like you're flying through the sky.

meanings.mnemonic: The bamboo radical and the bamboo kanji are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: Just make sure you know your radicals!

readings.mnemonic: Today is free bamboo day, so just take ( たけ ) all the bamboo you want.
readings.hint: Really take as much bamboo as you could possibly hold. Take some bamboo, then take some more, and take more… It’s free after all.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical car and the kanji car are exactly the same.
meanings.hint: Make sure you study your radicals... though don't do it while driving.

readings.mnemonic:
When we need to remember the reading しゃ , we use "shaman".

There's a car . It's the nicest car you've ever seen. Exquisite, beautiful. Wouldn't you be surprised to learn it belongs to a sha man ( しゃ )? Well it does. This shaman charges a premium for his shaman services so he can afford fancy cars like this.
readings.hint: The more fantastic you make this car, the better. Also, feel the surprise of a shaman getting out of this car. What a cool dude that shaman is.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical for center and the kanji for center are exactly the same.
meanings.hint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji as well! How swell.

readings.mnemonic: Remember that guy from the 央 radical fastening his belt across the center of his body? This guy always secures his belt at the center — not slightly to the left, not slightly to the right, but dead center. His precision is so mind-blowing that everyone watching can't help but say, " Ohh !"( おう ).
readings.hint: Next time you put a belt on, make sure it's in the exact center of your body. Heck, try it now. And don't forget to say "Ohh!" as you do it. It'll keep the reading for this kanji front and center in your mind.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader samurai didn't become leader by sitting around not taking action. He got to where he is by doing .
meanings.hint: Imagine the leader samurai doing things. He's doing more than anyone else, no one can keep up with what he's doing.

readings.mnemonic: He was doing things with shee p ( し ). He is the leader of the sheep clan, and he was able to do more with sheep than anyone else. That's why he's the leader.
readings.hint: Picture the leader samurai doing lots of sheep things. He's shearing sheep. He's feeding sheep. He's doing everything you could possibly do, because he's the sheep samurai.

meanings.mnemonic: The forehead of this beggar is on the ground . Except it's not exactly the ground, it's a giant copy machine, and he's making copies of his forehead!
meanings.hint: Picture how ridiculous this beggar looks, taking copies of his forehead with the ground copy machine.

readings.mnemonic: This ground copy machine can only be the work of one person: a sha man ( しゃ )!
readings.hint: This particular shaman's specialty is being able to turn any surface into a copy machine at a swirl of his little shaman wand. Picture him running around in circles, doing a little dance and chant, and then the ground suddenly turning into one giant copy machine.

meanings.mnemonic: When the sun is above the cross , it's time for some people to go church — which is always way too early on a Sunday, am I right? And if you want to get to church early, you need to be fast , otherwise you'll be too late.
meanings.hint: Imagine the sun coming up slowly, over the horizon. It then lines up with the cross, and at that moment, your alarm goes off, waking you up. You check the alarm clock, and instead of displaying the time, it just says EARLY and FAST on it, flashing in big red letters and alternating.

readings.mnemonic:
To remember the そう reading we use the word "soul."

When you're forced to get up fast and early you can feel it in your sou l ( そう ). You're so tired and it's so early, your soul cries out, "Please don't make me go this fast this early. I'm gonna leave your body if you keep this up!"
readings.hint: But you get your soul up early and head to the church fast anyway, because you're doing all of this for your soul in the first place!

meanings.mnemonic: The ear radical and ear kanji are the same in looks as well as meaning.
meanings.hint: Study those radicals hard to make sure you know your kanji as well!

readings.mnemonic: Put your hand to your ear and sing... " Mimi mimimimiiiii!" ( みみ ) and listen to your tone with your ear.
readings.hint: This is sort of a stereotypical singer sort of image. Make sure you concentrate a lot when you're listening to your mimimimiiii tone. In fact, why don't you do it out loud right now - make a little fool of yourself. It's fun, and it'll help you to remember!

meanings.mnemonic: If you want to dry your horns you need to lay them flat . If they aren't flat they'll always be a little damp somewhere and you'll never have a moment's peace .
meanings.hint: Imagine carefully taking off your horns and laying them flat on a paper towel. You have to smooth them out so they're as flat as possible.

readings.mnemonic: Being flat isn't enough, so you cover the whole thing in hay ( へい ). Hay absorbs water, so you add a flat layer of it.
readings.hint: Imagine carefully laying a flat layer of hay on top of your flat horns. You feel really at peace and... you're tired now. You decided to lie on top of the hay and take a nap while you wait for your horns to dry.

meanings.mnemonic: All these flowers were planted by your leader with nothing but a spoon . Now the whole place is absolutely full with beautiful flower s. Flowers everywhere.
meanings.hint: Your leader is a nice guy who wants there to be flowers for even future generations to enjoy. That’s why he’s painstakingly planting all these flowers with his spoon. Imagine watching him dig hole after hole with his little spoon just to plant some flowers for everyone.

readings.mnemonic: But for some reason, the leader just can't stop planting flower s... He's going crazy. Maybe it's the pollen? Now there’s only one person that can save him: Hannah ( はな ).
readings.hint: Hannah is the most reliable person you know. Whenever someone’s in any trouble, Hannah shows up and sets things right. Everyone needs a person like Hannah in their life. Especially the leader…

meanings.mnemonic: These days energy is the greatest treasure . Not gold, money, or anything like that. Imagine finding some in a literal treasure chest. As soon as you open it, out bursts the energy in a kind of spirit ball.
meanings.hint: Touch the energy, catch the spirit ball. Is it solid or gaseous? Does it tingle or burn? Try to imagine it out. Then, yes, go ahead and sell that valuable energy treasure to a power company or something.

readings.mnemonic: As you reach inside the energy spirit ball, you feel something hard inside. It's a large key ( き ) that gives you access to another treasure.
readings.hint: You take the key and open another treasure that's nearby. Open it up and see what's inside. Imagine something spectacular. Thanks, energy key.

meanings.mnemonic:
The radical for foot and the kanji for foot are exactly the same.
