That being said, there's also another meaning you should keep in mind, which is sufficient . If you think about it, one foot should be sufficient enough to walk around with if you hop.
meanings.hint: Same kanjis and radicals make Jack a happy boy. You should also learn the other meaning as well, though, if you can.

readings.mnemonic: What do you wear on your foot ? You wear a sock ( そく ).
readings.hint: Just think about your bare feet. Think about them too much. Feel how cold it is on your feet. Now, put on a sock to make it feel better. Ahhh, so nice!

meanings.mnemonic: Take your fingers to the street to hit some people! Street fingers are for curling into a fist. And that street fist wants to do some hitting!
meanings.hint: Imagine: it's just you, your fingers, and the wild street. It's hitting time!

readings.mnemonic: As you hit one chump after another, suddenly your da d ( だ ) comes marching out of your house. Uh oh. Dad caught you hitting, which, honestly, isn't a very nice thing to do, so he has every right to stop you.
readings.hint: Now imagine your dad saying, "Don't do a hit!" over and over at you. "I'm sorry dad! But the streets were calling me!"

meanings.mnemonic: The hole radical and the hole kanji are exactly the same! Spectacular!

readings.mnemonic: Ana ( あな ) fell into a hole . Laugh at Ana and her unfortunate misfortune of having fallen into a hole like a ninny. Haha, Ana – you’re stupid and clumsy!
readings.hint: Imagine yourself laughing at Ana while you point at her, continuing to jeer as Ana struggles to escape the hole. You’d help Ana out of the hole, but you’re too busy laughing at her.

meanings.mnemonic: Add a drop of something magical to water and suddenly it turns into big block of ice , just like that.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself adding that drop. What is it exactly? Something pretty potent, that's for sure. Now go ahead and touch the block of ice... wow, it's really cold — and only from one drop, too. Feel the burn on your finger from where you touched the ice .

readings.mnemonic: When you think of ice , I want you to think of someone frozen with ice. Do you know anyone named Corey ( こおり )? Anyone famous named Corey, perhaps Corey Feldman? Imagine this Corey person frozen solid with ice.
readings.hint: How did this happen? Was it your fault? Imagine the situation that would get Corey frozen like this.

meanings.mnemonic: There is a leaf growing out of the sun ! You fly out into space to check it out and when you get closer you see that it isn't just one leaf, it's a hundred ! That makes sense, you wouldn't be able to see just one leaf on the sun from where you were on earth.
meanings.hint: Imagine flying toward the sun to see a hundred leaves growing together in the shape of one big leaf. You want to go closer!

readings.mnemonic: You fly closer, counting all one hundred leaves when you hear a strange sound. You take off your space helmet and hear, "GUH HYUCK ( ひゃく )!" You know that laugh... it's Goofy! On top of the hundredth leaf is Goofy laughing and waving around. He must have built this leaf tower to get your attention.
readings.hint: Imagine hearing Goofy guh-hyuck over and over after you save him from being stranded on the sun. How would you feel being stuck in a cramped ship with laughing Goofy and his hundred leaves? Oh, you thought he would leave those behind? Nope, they saved him so you have to take them back too. Happy travels!

meanings.mnemonic: The insect radical and insect are the same in looks as well as and meaning.
meanings.hint: It's times like these when you gotta hope you're studying your radicals!

readings.mnemonic: It's an insect ! Get it! You squish it with your foot - what sound does it make? A mush ( むし ) sound.
readings.hint: Imagine little words that say "mush" flying out from under your foot as you mush the poor thing. What does a "mush" sound sound like? You'll have to come up with something unique to yourself, and focus on it to remember this reading.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical for man and the kanji for man are exactly the same.
meanings.hint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji too! Man, kanji are easy sometimes.

readings.mnemonic: When you think of a man , think of dawn ( だん ), because that man has to get up at dawn to power his rice paddy every day.
readings.hint: Picture the man watching as the first light of dawn tints the sky pink and orange. Time to get to work, man!

meanings.mnemonic:
The radical master and the kanji master have the same meanings!

This kanji also means main because the master is the main person of a dojo or household or business.

readings.mnemonic: Do you remember back to the radical mnemonic, where the master is floating a water drop above his head? Imagine back to there. Once you do, the most amazing thing happens. The master sends the drop of water into a chu te ( しゅ ) and it goes careening through an intricate series of them.
readings.hint: Picture this master of water controlling the droplet as it flies around inside these chutes, like he's solving a difficult puzzle with it. Astounding!

meanings.mnemonic: What is the evening mouth ? Imagine a mouth that’s up in the night sky. It’s just there, talking, and only comes out at night. It only appears in one area of the world (what area would you choose? Imagine yourself going to that place to see the “night mouth.”), and appears only one month out of the year (more on this in a second). The “evening mouth” is the most famous thing in the _____ area (this is the area you choose). He’s so famous that everybody knows his name (“Evening Mouth.”).
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself going to the area you chose, and watching “Evening Mouth” speak. There are thousands of others there, wanting to see the giant floating mouth in the night sky. “Evening Mouth” then says “Everyone, what’s my name !?” and everyone shouts back “Evening Mouth!” in unison. “Evening Mouth” has certainly gotten really famous .

readings.mnemonic: So, when does “Evening Mouth” appear? Turns out, “Evening Mouth” only comes out to talk in May ( めい ). You know this because it’s just starting to be spring, and when you look around (you’re watching “Evening Mouth” again) you see some spring flowers come up.
readings.hint: You know what they say. April showers bring May Flowers. What do May Flowers bring? They bring “Evening Mouth,” baby.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical not and the kanji not are the same thing! That makes it easy... Not!
meanings.hint: Actually, it does make it easy — the radicals make kanji wayyy easier, no?

readings.mnemonic:
To remember ふ we'll use the word "fool."

Think of those jokes, where someone says something, then ends with a loud " NOTTTT !" Someone is doing that to you, over and over. What is that person? That person is a foo l ( ふ ).
readings.hint: Be sure to feel irritated at that person. Think in your head as they're telling these dumb "not" jokes how much of a fool they are... and how much of a fool you are for sitting here and listening to them.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of alligators ... she's no ordinary leader, that's for sure. She's not a politician.. she's not a queen... She's one of the Other Ones, a group of strange, eccentric people who become leaders of animals.
meanings.hint: Imagine different groups of leaders - they're all dressed nice and normal. Then, off to the side are the leaders who are leaders of bears, cats, alligators, and narwhals... they're the other ones, no matter how you look at it. They are in their own group, and they are considered "others" because society won't accept them :(

readings.mnemonic:
To remember the reading た , we use the word "Taco"

Then there's the other s of the others. These are the ta co ( た ) worshipers. They're really strange.
readings.hint: Just imagine the "others" shaking their heads at these taco worshipers. Seriously, what the heck?

meanings.mnemonic: There's a mouth in the ground (in our case, it's a hatch), and inside is some kind of prison you can't quite get to. If you've ever seen Lost, this will sound familiar... on the hatch, there's actually a series of number s that you don't understand.
meanings.hint: What are these numbers? What do they mean? Think over it for a while, get frustrated, and try to figure it out. You can't, though, so you probably won't ever get inside.

readings.mnemonic:
"You want to know the number to the hatch?" yells an old voice from inside. You say "yes," and the voice gives you the numbers and you're in! You go down into the prison, where there is one prisoner: Grandpa ごう いち .

Note: For the ごう reading mnemonics, we use Grandpa ごういち because こういち is used for the こう reading. So just remember that ごう comes from Koichi's grandpa ごういち .
readings.hint: Grandpa ごういち keeps saying numbers as you free him from the prison. Just one after another. Numbers numbers numbers. Hopefully old man ごういち isn't crazy.

meanings.mnemonic: If someone tries to dig up dirt on your private life, tell them no thanks, that stuff is in the past . Some things should stay in the past. Not everyone needs to know everything about your life and your past.
meanings.hint: Keep your private dirt in the past. It's better for everyone.

readings.mnemonic: To remember the reading きょ , we'll use "Little Kyoto." This will differentiate きょ from きょう , which is regular-sized Kyoto. Get it? You decide to bury all this private dirt from your past in Little Kyo to ( きょ ). That stuff is from when you were just a little 子 ( こ ) and you don't like to think about it.
readings.hint: Imagine physically, literally burying your past 子 self. If you hide it in Little Kyoto, no one will ever be able to dig it up.

meanings.mnemonic: The origin radical and the origin kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals!

readings.mnemonic:
The origin of everything can be found in Gen ji ( げん ). Genji is the name of the main character in the world's first ever novel, "The Tale of Genji."

Picture him as a rich, pretty boy who cares a ton about his looks and gets along really well with all the ladies. He's also really great at poetry, which helps the lady situation.
readings.hint: Genji's origin story is pretty important when it comes to mnemonics and Japanese literature. He's going to keep popping up on your kanji journey, so make sure you remember him!

meanings.mnemonic: If you go down a slide and get dirt on your legs , it must have been the fault of the previous person, aka the person who went down the slide ahead of you. They should've cleaned off their dirty legs before they went down the slide, but they didn't, and now you've got dirt all over your legs too.
meanings.hint: Try to imagine the previous person — they must have been absolutely filthy to leave all this dirt behind. Why'd they have to go down the slide ahead of you?! If only you could've gotten here before them…

readings.mnemonic: Then you realize: the previous person on the slide must've been a cen taur ( せん ). Centaurs track dirt everywhere 'cause they're always galloping around in the wilderness.
readings.hint: If you look really far off into the distance ahead of you, you might still be able to spot this dirty, slide-ruining centaur. Hurry up and go after him before he gets on another slide!

meanings.mnemonic: The plate kanji and the plate radical are exactly the same, like two matching pieces of china.
meanings.hint: If you know your radicals, you know your kanji too.

readings.mnemonic: The person washing all the dish es was named Sarah ( さら ). She was named that because of her large head, which was shaped like Sarah Jessica Parker’s head (very big).
readings.hint: Imagine someone with a huge head, washing dishes. When I say huge, I mean huge. She turns towards you with her big head and says “Hi, I’m Sarah” and you see her name tag, which also says Sarah. She is Sarah, the dish washer. The plates she holds up next to her head pale in comparison, even though they're the biggest pieces.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical for red and the kanji for red are the same thing!
meanings.hint: What a lucky ducky — as long as you know your radicals, you'll know this kanji's meaning as well!

readings.mnemonic: This kanji means red , AKA , also known as, あか .
readings.hint: Think of あか as being some sort of alias for "red." That's why it's pronounced あか in Japanese, AKA "red."

meanings.mnemonic: The radical see and the kanji see are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: Make sure you're studying your radicals. If you do, you'll know a lot of kanji as well, almost magically!

readings.mnemonic: You see something. It's walking towards you, but you need to see better. When the person comes close, you realize "That person is me ( み )!"
readings.hint: The struggling to see bit is pretty important — make sure that you slowly see a little better and better as the person comes closer, but it's difficult the whole time (struggles are good for memory). Then, it starts to become more and more clear, and you start saying "that can't be me . No way it's me..." When the person comes up to you (the person, being you) you learn about parallel universes and other fabulous things. Still, the whole time you can't stop thinking about how "this person looks just like me... no, this person is me!"

meanings.mnemonic: The leader is next to the tree . What is he doing? He's leaning against it, rest ing.
meanings.hint: The best thing to do with a tree is rest under it. Picture the leader resting under the tree with his eyes half closed.

readings.mnemonic: As he rest s, he suddenly gets hungry and pulls a cu cumber ( きゅう ) off the tree and bites into it. A good rest isn't complete without some crunchy tree cucumbers.
readings.hint: Don't email me saying cucumbers don't grow on trees. Cucumber trees exist. Look them up and then rest easy knowing I'd never lie to you.

meanings.mnemonic: The shellfish radical and the shellfish kanji are the same! It can be just the shell part too.
meanings.hint: Shellfish are shellfish, you know?

readings.mnemonic: To get some shellfish , all you have to do is hop in your kay ak ( かい ) and get some. Shellfish live in the water.
readings.hint: Imagine rowing your kayak out into the ocean to collect shellfish. Imagine the color and shape of the kayak. Feel the spray of the salt water and the taste of some fresh shellfish, right out of the ocean.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a cross inside your mouth , so the things you say are very pious. Everything you say, you say humbly .
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself with a cross inside your mouth, saying all kinds of humble things. "Oh no, I'm not that good." "Oh no, my mustache doesn't look that great."

readings.mnemonic: As you're saying all these humble things, you're also mow ing ( もう ) your neighbor's lawn. Your neighbors love you so much, because you're so humble, and you mow their lawn for them.
readings.hint: Imagine mowing your neighbor's lawn while you say humbly how bad you are at mowing lawns. What a good neighbor you are.

meanings.mnemonic: The stone radical and stone kanji are the same thing!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals and therefore know your kanji. It's just that simple.

readings.mnemonic: You throw a stone through a window... and, now you can see something very sexy ( せき ).
readings.hint: Whoops, maybe you shouldn't throw stones through windows or you'll see people's sexy times.

meanings.mnemonic: What do you get if you lend a spirit your umbrella ? Thanks , of course! Especially if that spirit is Totoro, the spirit of the forest.
meanings.hint: It's raining, so you lend Totoro your umbrella. As a thanks, he gives you a present wrapped in bamboo leaves.

readings.mnemonic: So what did you receive as a thanks ? You open it up, and there's a ray ( れい ) gun inside!
readings.hint: Thanks for the new ray gun, Totoro!

meanings.mnemonic: The thread radical and thread are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
meanings.hint: This is why it's good to make sure you know your radicals.

readings.mnemonic: You have some thread ... what's the first thing you do with it? You take it and put it in your mouth, and start sucking it down like spaghetti. You have decided to eat ( いと ) the thread.
readings.hint: Think about how it tastes. Think about how it feels in your throat. Let it get caught on your teeth and around your tongue. It's a weird feeling having thread in your mouth, and you want to use that weird feeling to remember you're going to eat it.

meanings.mnemonic: When you see both a rice paddy and a street in the same place, you have the makings of a whole town !
meanings.hint: Think about it, a single farm wouldn't need a whole street. Just paths would do. There is a street next to this rice paddy, so there must be other people, which makes this a town .

readings.mnemonic: You go to visit this nice little town . But then, there she is... Every time you walk down the street, the street-woman Mrs. Chou ( ちょう ) arrives to ruin your day.
readings.hint: Remember imagining Mrs. Chou in great detail in level 2? Time for some of that work to come to fruition. Imagine Mrs. Chou walking down the street of the town towards you, ready to make your day bad. She breaks things as she walks towards you, then knocks you down for walking around in her town. She says, "Wherever there's a street, you'll find Mrs. Chou." Then spits on your back as she walks away.

meanings.mnemonic: Under his roof , the king stares into a drop of water containing his greatest treasure . Just kidding, the king has way more treasure than that. The drop of water is just reflecting what's around him: mounds and mounds of treasure, stacked all the way up to the roof.
meanings.hint: Picture the treasure reflected in the drop of water. It's marvelous!

readings.mnemonic: All of this treasure is kept in the king's ho me ( ほう ). He can't let other people see how much treasure he has, or they'll get mad. Or worse, ask him to share some with his people. Yuck. The treasure stays at home.
readings.hint: Picture his home from the outside. It looks like a normal, small castle. But then you walk in and it's a treasure trove of... well, treasure!

meanings.mnemonic: Someone's taking tree measurements … Why are they taking measurements of trees? Because they're planning to cut them down to make their village .
meanings.hint: There is a whole group of people out there who are measuring these trees. Imagine them asking each other "do you think this will be a good tree for our village center?" and other people nodding in agreement or disagreement as they measure.

readings.mnemonic: They take all the wood back to where they're building their village , and once they construct the village center they paint a mura l ( むら ) on the side of it. The mural is of the forest where they got the trees from, to honor the village's origins.
readings.hint: Look at the mural on the side of this newly constructed village center. So many trees! Visualize the whole process of the tree becoming a building, as you reflect on the message of the mural.

meanings.mnemonic:
The world radical and the world kanji are the same, but there is another really important meaning you need to know: generation .

Think of it this way: the world is just made up of many different generations of people, plants, and animals all getting along together.
meanings.hint: It's all these generational differences that make the world go 'round!

readings.mnemonic: It's your job to protect the world and every generation with your special sa ber ( せい ). It was given to you to uphold correctness, and you'll pass it on to the next generation when it's time. Then they'll watch out for the world when you're gone.
readings.hint: Hold the saber up high and pledge to protect the world for all of the generations to come! Then swish it around and make buzzing sounds with your mouth.

meanings.mnemonic:
The blue radical and the blue kanji are exactly the same.

Additionally, this can also mean young . You can remember this because young people have potential as limitless as the blue sky!
meanings.hint: Don't feel blue, you should know this kanji automagically!

readings.mnemonic: You've been feeling really blue . Mostly because your skin, hair, and eyes have all turned blue. It was an out-of-the-blue transformation! But now someone places his hand on your shoulder and points at your sa ber ( せい ). It's blue too! You aren't alone!
readings.hint: You aren't sure how you or the saber turned blue, but at least you and your saber are a good match now, I guess.

meanings.mnemonic: Plant a spirit in the dirt before you start your company and it will be successful. That's what all the great, old Japanese companies did back in the olden days. That's why companies like Mitsubishi have been around for hundreds of years. They planted a spirit in the dirt to help their company grow, and grow it did!
meanings.hint: Picture a young person way back in old-timey history times burying the spirit of one of their ancestors in the dirt. Now watch as an old-timey time lapse plays out in front of your eyes and a company is born!

readings.mnemonic: Where did you learn about this method of company growing? From a sha man ( しゃ ), of course. Shamans aren't just spouting nonsense, they know what they're talking about. And spirits are right up their shaman alley.
readings.hint: Imagine going to a shaman and having them tell you that for your company to be successful you need to bury a spirit in the dirt so it can help the company grow. You should probably listen.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader has a book all about the human body . Knowing about bodies is a privilege. One that only leaders get—it helps them control all their subjects, like you.
meanings.hint: Imagine looking down at your body and thinking, huh, I wonder what all that junk is? Oh well, I'm sure the leader has it all figured out in that book of hers.

readings.mnemonic: The leader's body book also taught her about something very body specific: how to tie a tie ( たい ). You can't have a tie on your body, no no. Those are for the leader and her smart body.
readings.hint: Picture the leader, reading a book, putting a tie on her body. Look around at you and your family and your neighbors. You don't know what your body meat-bags do and you don't know how to tie a tie. Womp womp.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a gun and you see some windows . What do you think you're going to do with this gun? Of course, you're going to shoot the windows... every single one of them. Each and every one.
meanings.hint: Make sure you get every one, and don't miss any of them. Actually search around in your imagination for any more windows, because you want to stress it's important you hit each and every window with your gun.

readings.mnemonic: Now that every window is broken, you feel pretty stupid because mi ce ( まい ) are pouring in through each one. You've made a way for mice to get into your house. That wasn't smart.
readings.hint: Now you need to shoot every one of the mice that came through those broken windows. You can't let any survive. You have to get every last one out of here!!

meanings.mnemonic: The ground is scattered with rice , which will cause the Predator to come because he loves rice. Then once he's gone, the next Predator will come to eat.
meanings.hint: This kanji kind of looks like a Predator face, so just imagine Predators come whenever the ground is scattered with rice. They come from all around to grab handfuls of it. Who's next, then? Come forward!

readings.mnemonic: When they come, though, they're all disappointed. It turns out it's rye ( らい ) grains, and rye grains taste terrible. Yuck.
readings.hint: If you love rye grains, on the other hand, imagine them being really happy that they came to get the rice. They're so lucky it's rye!

meanings.mnemonic: The color radical and color are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
meanings.hint: Color me pink! This kanji's super easy if you know your radicals.

readings.mnemonic: You already have color on the mind because you know the meaning of this kanji, so think of something colorful about yourself. Imagine your ear ( いろ ) being a weird color... green, purple, red, whatever. Just make it really bright and standing out.
readings.hint: You can even have someone ask you about your ear... "Is your ear okay? I think it's the color blue..."

meanings.mnemonic: The axe radical and the axe kanji are exactly the same, with one minor difference. This kanji is also used as the bread loaf counter , probably because bread was so big and hard in the past that they had to use axes to slice them.
meanings.hint: Imagine you've gone back in time a good hundred years, and you're trying to count loaves of bread after each swing of your axe. Axes and bread loaves will become intertwined in your brain.

readings.mnemonic: You imagined being back in time too good and now you're stuck using an axe to be a bread loaf counter for the Kin g ( きん ) of England. You aren't sure which king he is or when in time you are, but he's mean and keeps eating all the bread you cut up with your axe.
readings.hint: Imagine being stuck using an axe on bread while the King of England glares down at you, munching and spilling crumbs everywhere.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a tree and another tree . You basically have yourself a forest !
meanings.hint: Follow the logic of this one and you should be okay.

readings.mnemonic: So, now you have this forest and you want to cut it down (so much money!). But, you don't have any tools, only your hands. You think... I've done karate for a while, so I can probably chop it down that way. You get in a chopping stance, then do your battle cry and hit: " HAYA ... .SHI****** !" ( はやし )
readings.hint: Obviously that didn't work well - you'll want to focus on your battle cry plus indecent swearing right afterwards to remember this reading.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a mouth full of two massive drop s and… treasure ?! Upon closer inspection, they appear to form some sort of diagram !
meanings.hint: Scientists have spent decades trying to figure out what the diagram of two massive drops and treasure in your mouth could mean, but to no avail. Imagine the pain of having to spend your entire life with a mouth full of two massive drops and treasure… poor you.

readings.mnemonic: After decades of analyzing the diagram , scientists found out it leads to… a zoo ( ず )?!
readings.hint: Now you’re stuck at this zoo with your diagram mouth full of two massive drops and treasure. Scientists think this can't be it, so further research will be required. Your diagram mouth of drops and treasure isn't off the hook quite yet. Just a few more years, spent at this zoo! I promise!

meanings.mnemonic: You see a triceratops in the distance. But, it's odd. It's perfectly pi shaped (that is, circular, like a pie). Some kind of piceratops, if you will. The reason they are this shape is because they want to absorb as much light as possible. They evolved to be round so they don't have any corners casting a shadow.
meanings.hint: Absorbing light is natural for reptiles, and a round one can absorb even more. Imagine this strange, round piceratops basking in the warm rays of light shining down from the sky. What a rare sight you've come across.

readings.mnemonic: As you watch this majestic beast absorbing light , suddenly several dozen feral こう いち s burst out from the wilderness, spears in hand. Oh no! Those こういち s are hunting this rare piceratops!
readings.hint: Imagine many different (but also kind of the same) こういち s attacking and throwing spears at the piceratops. Little do they know that it's fully charged up after absorbing all that light, and it unleashes a bright flash of energy, rendering all the こういち s momentarily blind and allowing it to escape.

meanings.mnemonic: The go radical and the go kanji are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: If you know your radicals well enough (and you do), you'll know this kanji.

readings.mnemonic: "Oh, did he go again?" When こう いち is not around, that's what people often say because he goes to the gyo za ( ぎょう ) restaurant almost every day. こういち just loves gyoza so much, you always know where he is when he is not home.
readings.hint: Remember, whenever we see the reading こう , think of こういち , a founder of WaniKani (who loves gyoza). We'll keep it consistent, so over time you should develop a character around こういち that you learn to love or hate.

meanings.mnemonic: What superhero is most like a triceratops ? …If you said Wolverine , you're correct ! Why is that the right answer? Because the triceratops has three horns, and Wolverine has three blades coming out of his hands. It's obvious, isn't it? But you knew that of course.
meanings.hint: Picture a triceratops. Now picture Wolverine. An equals sign between them. A little arrow pointing at the horns, an arrow pointing at the blades. And a big flashing CORRECT sign above them. Need I say more?

readings.mnemonic: Because you got the answer correct , you win a free trip to とう きょう . Congrats!
readings.hint: If you're lucky, you might see Wolverine fighting a triceratops in the middle of Shibuya Crossing in Tokyo. Heroes and monsters are always fighting in Tokyo, right? At least that's what my anime tells me.

meanings.mnemonic: The feathers radical and feather kanji are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
meanings.hint: Have you been studying your radicals? If so, this'll be easy!

readings.mnemonic: You've gathered feather after feather, like some kind of wannabe birdman or birdwoman, and now you're ready to fly. But as soon as you're about to take off, someone dumps honey ( はね ) on you, making your feathers all goopy.
readings.hint: Feel the honey in each feather, taste it trickling over your lips... And go ahead and plummet towards the ground (because you were jumping off a building to fly when someone doused you with honey). At least it's a tasty way to go, no?

meanings.mnemonic: You plant your flowers in the light of the sun in the shape of a cross , but it's too hot—the sun is too strong—and you end up with grass . Flowers can't grow in these conditions.
meanings.hint: Think of how disappointed you are. You wanted a beautiful cross of flowers, and now you just have a big overgrown patch of thick grass. It isn't even pretty green grass, it's a little brown and damaged from the sun.

readings.mnemonic: The grass on top is brown and burned from the sun, so you need to take a cool saw ( くさ ) and hack the tops off. The saw is actually physically cool to the touch, to help heal the grass' sunburned bits. It just happens to look cool too.
readings.hint: Imagine using your cool saw to saw off the heads of all of this grass. You made the cross pretty big, so there is a ton of grass to get through. The sun is beating down on you and the grass too, which isn't making it any easier.

meanings.mnemonic: The meat radical and meat are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
meanings.hint: Study those radicals, and perhaps we'll eat some meat tonight.

readings.mnemonic: You know who loves meat ? Nick ( にく ). You probably know a Nick — imagine him eating some succulent meat right now.
readings.hint: If you don't know anyone named Nick, then just imagine it's Nic Cage. Picture him as he chews that succulent meat, maybe even closing his eyes in gastronomic ecstasy. Yeah. Go for it, Nic. Enjoy that meat.

meanings.mnemonic: The meet radical and the meet kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: What better way to meet new people than to start kay aking ( かい )! Get into your kayak and just float out into the water. You'll eventually bump into someone else in a kayak and boom, you're meeting someone new!
readings.hint: "I met my soulmate in a kayak," said Koichi.

meanings.mnemonic: You put a lid on your father . But, he won't taste good alone, so you mix in some other ingredients. They mingle inside the pot with him.
meanings.hint: This may seem traumatic, but actually your father is surprisingly into it. Imagine him mixed in with all kinds of other things. It's important to note that this kanji is about things being mixed in, not so much about the action of mixing. That's another kanji.

readings.mnemonic: With a mix of your father and other ingredients mingl ing in your cooking pot, you lift up the lid... your father pops up. Your father is こう いち . He stares back at you with a big smile.
readings.hint: "I love mixing in with potatoes and tomatoes," your father こういち states. "Turn up that temperature. I like to mingle with a bit more tingle."

meanings.mnemonic: The village you always think of first is your hometown .
meanings.hint: Why would you ever talk about the village where someone else was born? Answer: you wouldn't. Everything is always about you.

readings.mnemonic: Your hometown is where you sat ( さと ), nostalgia tugging at your heartstrings amid the endless rice fields and dirt.
readings.hint: Visualize yourself sitting in your hometown, the village's rustic charm evoking nostalgia.

meanings.mnemonic: The night radical and the night kanji are exactly the same.
meanings.hint: All those late nights studying radicals really pay off, you know.

readings.mnemonic: You call your friend at night and say " Yo, Lou ( よる )!"
readings.hint: Lou is a total night owl, so you only hit him up at night.

meanings.mnemonic: The gold radical and the gold kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Just know your radicals and you'll be golden, so to speak.

readings.mnemonic: The most gold in the land belongs to the Kin g ( きん ) of England. In fact, you've never actually met someone with gold, it all goes straight to the King whenever someone finds some. You just have a few pennies to work with.
readings.hint: Picture the King on a huge pile of gold. Gold coins, gold candelabras, gold ponies. He's laughing and pointing at all of it while making very strong eye contact with you.

meanings.mnemonic: How many evenings are there? There are many .
meanings.hint: If you accidentally think "a lot" or something similar, that's okay too. It's the basic meaning that counts, and all these things are pretty close, though eventually you want to get to the point where you know that this kanji does in fact mean many .

readings.mnemonic:
To remember the reading た we use the word "taco."

Let's start with the kanji meaning: many . You go outside in the evening (because that's what this kanji is made up of) and are surprised when you're hit by many ta cos ( た ) flying from the sky. For some reason it's raining tacos, and of course you have no idea why.
readings.hint: The tacos pelt you and knock you off your feet. Hard shell, soft shell, meat, lettuce, tomatoes, cheese... it's terrible. And there are so many of them, flying from the sky to drown you in tacos. This is absolutely absurd (and you should think that too, so you remember the reading).

meanings.mnemonic: When the moon is bright, it's easy to see that a pig is indeed a pig .
meanings.hint: Imagine walking outside at night and seeing a strange oinking animal. You think it's a pig, but just to be sure you grab that thing and hold it up to the moonlight. Yes, definitely a pig! Now you know.

readings.mnemonic: Legend has it that if you catch a pig in moonlight, it will tell you its secrets. Sure enough, this pig tells you it's from Bhuta n ( ぶた ).
readings.hint: A pig from Bhutan?! Yes, all pigs are actually from Bhutan, it whispers. Then this Bhutan pig escapes your grip and trots off into the moonlight.

meanings.mnemonic: Who's someone who's just a mouth with legs ? That's your know-it-all older brother , who won't shut up about anything.
meanings.hint: To help this mnemonic along, literally imagine your older brother as someone that's just a giant mouth with a pair of legs walking around. It talks and talks and talks, too, irritating you. Feel the irritation specifically at your older brother. He's older than you, so you can't beat him up... yet.

readings.mnemonic: You wish you could box your loud-mouth older brother up and send him far away to きょう と . きょうと is super far away from where you currently live, so just ship your older bro off so he can never bother you again.
readings.hint: Hmm... don't you want to go to きょうと someday? Maybe you should have sent your brother somewhere less cool.

meanings.mnemonic: Someone on a scooter has an axe , and they're getting near to you really fast! Oh no, they're so close now, you need to get out of here!
meanings.hint: Picture a scooter coming toward you in the dark. You see the gleam of the axe as it looms nearer and nearer.

readings.mnemonic: As they draw near , you recognize who is riding the scooter: the Kin g ( きん ) of England! Behind him, a bunch of his guards also on scooters and wielding axes appear and move close to you.
readings.hint: The King is now so near to you his nose and mustache are tickling your face. He's holding the axe high above his head and saying, "I can get as close to you as I want and you can't stop me because I'm the King. I can get sooooo close. Look, I'm not touching you though!"

meanings.mnemonic: The leader 's key is the key that make s all the other keys. It's sort of like the one ring to rule them all, the one ring to bind them... except with keys.
meanings.hint: Imagine the leader taking his key to open up a safe that has a button to make other keys for the underlings. No key is as great as this key, though.

readings.mnemonic: The leader make s all the keys and puts them in a sack ( さく ) to distribute them to the underlings, who then cut them into shape with a saw ( さ ).
readings.hint: Imagine the leader making the keys and dropping them one by one into a big sack. He's always walking around carrying a big sack over his shoulder, distributing the keys to his underlings and watching them saw them into shape.

meanings.mnemonic: The rice radical and rice kanji are the same in looks and meaning. This kanji can also mean America , maybe because America is covered in rice or fields or something?
meanings.hint: Aren't radicals nice? As nice and as fundamental as… rice. They make things so much easier, if you've been studying them.

readings.mnemonic: A ton of rice is grown in America . And yet you never see rice in American cuisine. So where's all that unused rice going? Actually, it all gets dumped into a big bay ( べい ). They have too much rice and no place to store it, so they've started using bays to store it.
readings.hint: Imagine helping to bulldoze the rice into the bay, watching it fill it up, then pile high. A bay isn't the best space for rice storage, but it'll do. The important thing is that you think you have way too much of the stuff, so the only way to get rid of it is to dump it in a large body of water, and that large body of water is a bay.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical for rain and the kanji for rain are the same thing.
meanings.hint: Make sure you know your radicals!

readings.mnemonic: What if the rain actually tried to aim ( あめ ) for you? As in, drops would move out of their normal flight path just to hit you on your head? You'd be a lot wetter, that's for sure.
readings.hint: Imagine a world where each rain drop had to choose a target to aim for. How strange would rainfall be? Also, あめ and "aim" don't really sound too much alike, but the goal is to use "aim" to get to あめ , so make sure to take note of that in your mind as you learn the reading.

meanings.mnemonic: The say radical and the say kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: If you aren't sure what to say , just think about Gen ji ( げん ) and ask yourself, "What would Genji say?"
readings.hint: Genji is a real smooth talker. In fact, he can talk his way out of anything. If you can just stay calm and think "What would Genji say?" before saying anything, you'll probably end up saying something cool instead of something lame.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical run and the kanji run are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: Make sure you know your radicals!

readings.mnemonic: You run and run and run and suddenly you feel your sou l ( そう ) leave your body. Still running, you turn around and see your soul running a few steps behind you.
readings.hint: Running really takes it out of you. In this case, it takes your soul out of you. Don't worry, when you stop your soul will collide with you again and everything will be okay. (Probably.)

meanings.mnemonic: There's a mustache on the ground . Upon closer inspection, you see that the mustache on the ground is exactly the same as yours!
meanings.hint: Pick up the mustache, hold it up next to your face in front of a mirror. It's unbelievable, but it's the same in every way, down to the last hair.

readings.mnemonic:
When we need to remember the reading どう , we use the word "doughnut".

While this is already strange enough, you notice something stranger. Over a couple feet away is a dou ghnut ( どう ) who also has the same mustache as you (and the one that fell on the ground). The doughnut looks at you for a moment, then scampers off. This doughnut must be a mutant because normally doughnuts don't move.
readings.hint: If it helps, imagine hundreds or thousands of doughnuts all around you with the same exact mustache. First of all, it's terrifying to be surrounded by thousands of mutant doughnuts. Secondly, it's really weird they all have the same mustache as you.

meanings.mnemonic: The self radical and self are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
meanings.hint: Radicals have self. Kanji have self. Kanji and radicals can sometimes be the same thing... so do they really have "self"? Only way to find out is to study both of them hard!

readings.mnemonic:
When we need to remember じ we use the word "Jesus."

Who said you should always look at your self before tossing stones (or whatever) at others? Je sus ( じ ) did. So, make sure you look at your own self before being a jerk.
readings.hint: You can feel free to do some introspection here.

meanings.mnemonic: You're walking at night when you discover a lantern covered entirely in hair , making it a very strange shape . In fact, this is a form you'd never expect a lantern to take.
meanings.hint: Picture all the different shapes this hairy lantern could take in your mind.

readings.mnemonic: You stare at the shape until you finally recognize it: ca ke ( けい )! Well, it's hairy, glowing cake, but it's still shaped like a big slice of cake.
readings.hint: Picture the shape of this hairy, glowing cake.

meanings.mnemonic: What was the biggest hole in the construction of the world? The sky . If you think about it, the sky is like a big hole someone forgot to fill in. That's why this kanji also means empty ! Makes sense — nothing is more empty than the sky.
meanings.hint: Next time you look up at the sky, try to imagine it's a big empty hole you're stuck at the bottom of.

readings.mnemonic: The sky is usually empty until clouds stage a coup ( くう ). Basically you can think of the sky as the site for one coup after another as the weather changes.
readings.hint: First the sky's cloudy, then it's clear and empty, then overcast again... Just coup after coup!

meanings.mnemonic: You go down a slide made of branch es and your skin scrapes right off — ouch! Lucky for you, it was just your old outer layer of skin, and now you've got a fresh shiny layer. Yay for exfoliation! You're not sure what to do with this big, floppy layer of old skin, though…
meanings.hint: Imagine sliding down a slide made of rough, gnarly tree branches. Their bark digs into your skin and pulls it right off of your body. Okay, it's painful, but it's a small price to pay for having such beautifully exfoliated skin.

readings.mnemonic: Without your rough old skin to protect you, you feel the full he at ( ひ ) of the sun now. It burns… it burns! Yeah, you better protect that fresh new skin from the heat.
readings.hint: Your skin usually protects you from the heat, but imagine how raw it would be after you shed the old layer all at once. It's kinda vulnerable right now, so take care of it.

meanings.mnemonic: For some reason, you are stand ing on the sun . It's obviously very hot, but the thing you notice most is the sound of you burning up.
meanings.hint: Imagine a super loud sizzling sound, drowning everything else out. It's ridiculously loud, and hurts your ears more than the sun's heat hurts your skin.

readings.mnemonic: This sound isn't just loud, it's familiar. Finally you place it: on ions ( おん )!
readings.hint: Your burning body sounds just like sizzling, crispy onions being fried in a pan. Listen close. It's exactly the same! You can almost smell onions too!

meanings.mnemonic:
The leader for the ceremony can't come so you need a substitute to replace them ASAP!

This kanji has another meaning too. The leader can't come to the ceremony because they forgot what period of time it was. If they think they're in the wrong age , they won't come because they're trying to lead some monkeys in the ceremony instead.
meanings.hint: Picture the leader of the ceremony out back, unsure of what period or age they're in, while the substitute leader walks in and stares at them in shock.

readings.mnemonic: The substitute leader is wearing the wrong color clothes, so you have to dye ( だい ) them, like they did in the olden age s.
readings.hint: Now you're out back with the original leader and the substitute leader, trying to dye the color of this stupid shirt to match that one.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a wild viking child you have to take care of. He won't do anything but study , surprisingly. You can't get him to eat, use the bathroom, or anything else. He just wants to study.
meanings.hint: Of course, this isn't what you'd expect of a little viking child. It's the opposite. That's why it's so surprising! He loves to study!

readings.mnemonic: This crazy viking child who prefers study over pillaging (and other viking activities)... the other vikings just stop and gawk ( がく ) at him when they walk by.
readings.hint: You should tell the other vikings to stop gawking at this child. Just because he likes to study doesn't mean he's a bad viking...

meanings.mnemonic: In a coffin there's a beggar , doing his begging. But it doesn't work, nobody helps him. So, he just begins to think : Why am I in this situation? How could I have avoided it? How will I get out? Think, dammit, think!!
meanings.hint: Imagine him in his coffin, changing from a begging position to a stereotypical "thinking" position. His hand is on his chin, eyebrows furrowed. Focus on the beggar's thinking. This is a pretty abstract idea to imagine, so it will be important to create a vivid picture in your mind's eye.

readings.mnemonic: What does the beggar think of? A thought bubble appears near his head. In it is a picture of こう いち ! He's thinking that if こういち were here, he'd save him. Oh, if only こういち could save us all!
readings.hint: Then, the beggar realizes that he is こういち . Here he is begging in a coffin, and still he only thinks of himself.

meanings.mnemonic: Fins decorate a hat , with (presumably fish) mouths stitched to it. Who wears strange hats like this? Turns out, the valley people are the ones who wear these. I'm talking about the "Valley Girls" of Los Angeles, California. They're into the crazy fashion, and they'll wear anything if it's deemed "cool" or something like that.
meanings.hint: Imagine a bunch of Valley Girls walking around with these strange hats. Fins and fish mouths... what a strange new fad.

readings.mnemonic: Hanging around with all the Valley Girls in the sunny area of LA is also a robot. It's WALL-E's distant (and lazy, lounging) relative, TAN-E ( たに ). He sits around getting a tan in LA with the Valley Girls while his cousin picks up trash.
readings.hint: What a jerk he is. Imagine the deep tan lines on TAN-E as well. He's the epitome of robot laziness.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a samurai with a flag on a stick . On his flag is the symbol 声 , the kanji for voice . Why does he have that there? He feels like his Shogun isn't giving him and his fellow Samurai a voice, so he's brought a flag with the word "voice" written on it to protest.
meanings.hint: Imagine him and the other samurai with their flags on sticks, waving them around shouting things like "We want a voice!”, “Give us a voice!”

readings.mnemonic: You decide to join the samurai’s protest for more voice but immediately notice… they’re all 子 , eh ( こえ )?!
readings.hint: Imagine your surprise at seeing this large protest of 子 samurai, shouting for their voices to be heard. Really make sure you can hear yourself going “You’re a 子 , eh?!” in your head.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a mouth inside a mouth . But if you look even closer (not actually, but we're pretending), you can see another mouth inside that mouth... and another one inside of that one! It keeps going and going. Can you count the number of times there's a mouth inside a mouth? This kanji also means revolve too. Think of each "time" a thing happens, it loops back around and comes back to the beginning, revolving around again another time.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself counting and counting smaller and smaller mouths inside of each other, recording the number of times you can find a mouth inside another mouth. Dig deeper and deeper until it feels ridiculous how small the mouths are getting. Be sure to focus on the counting and the number of times you find one. Now, how many mouths is this times two, because your mouth is like this too.

readings.mnemonic: If you're having a hard time wrapping your head around the infinite number of times these mouths revolve , just picture yourself in a little kay ak ( かい ) going around and down through the mouths.
readings.hint: Your kayak is slashing and dipping through the revolving mouths, going down and down more times than you can count!

meanings.mnemonic: Our leader has a lip ring . Wait, our leader has a lip ring? WHAT ??
meanings.hint: Think of our president (or your prime minister, or dictator, or whatever) and imagine this person appearing on television one morning with a big ol' lip ring. Feel surprised by it. Just say "What??" over and over again. You want to have the feeling of "what" in you when you imagine this - that'll help you to remember this kanji's meaning.

readings.mnemonic: The leader with his lip ring is now eating some naan ( なん ) and curry. It's delicious, of course... but then he gets it caught on his lip ring, and tears half his lip off. He screams: " Nannnnn eeeeeeeeeehhh !!" ( なに ) in anger at the naan and curry.
readings.hint: Make this one vivid and scary. He's eating the naan (that's your なん ), and then he screams at the naan but gets overwhelmed with pain (that's the なに ). Smell the naan (you're there) and see the blood gushing out of his/her lip. It's terrifying. How did the naan even catch on his lip ring anyways?

meanings.mnemonic: The life radical is without the slide (as it sometimes is), but when you combine that with the winter radical, you have life during winter. What gives you life during the winter? It's the wheat you store away.
meanings.hint: Imagine you're starving. It's cold and you've run out of food. You don't know what you're going to do. If you don't find something, you'll lose your life. Then, you break open a barrel, and wheat pours out. It's beautiful. You just found some life in the middle of winter.

readings.mnemonic: You found your wheat . Thank God. You'll survive. You have to take the wheat back to your house, though. On your way, you get mug ged ( むぎ ).
readings.hint: Imagine the disappointment. You go from finding life to losing it all... now you have no hope for the winter :( Also, keep in mind that むぎ and mug aren't super-alike, so you'll have to use it to get  close enough to remember the actual reading, むぎ .

meanings.mnemonic: There is someone wearing horns , holding a bow , and going down a slide held up by a stick . What type of person do you imagine? I'd say someone who's an immature boy. That would be your younger brother .
meanings.hint: If you don't have a younger brother, just pretend you do for now. Imagine him really vividly. He is a stereotypical little boy, playing dress-up with horns and a bow and arrow, and he's going down a makeshift slide held up by a stick.

readings.mnemonic: Your younger brother loves playing dress-up so much that he's now started dye ing ( だい ) his hair all kinds of colors.
readings.hint: Imagine the bright red, blue, green and purple dye in your younger brother's hair. He's put so much on that he's dyed half his face too. He's going to be in trouble when he gets home!

meanings.mnemonic: The sun in the center of the sky gives off its light, which reflect s off of everything around it.
meanings.hint: Look around you, everything you see is reflecting the light of the sun. Unless it's night time, then the tiny sun in the center of your lightbulb is creating the light that reflects there.

readings.mnemonic: This reflect ed light is important for a corns ( えい ), in particular. Without light reflecting off everything, the sun's light would never make it down to the ground where they've fallen. They would never grow into trees. And we'd all suffocate.
readings.hint: Picture the light of the sun reflecting off a single acorn on the forest floor. So much symbolism!

meanings.mnemonic: Next to the tree , a lid is put on your father . "Hello child," he says. "This is your new school ."
meanings.hint: He goes on to explain that he changed your school to here because there is a tree to block the satellite's view. Also, he wears a tin foil lid on his head so they can't track him. The regular school was no good because "they were watching." So now you have this new school, taught under a tree by your father who's wearing a lid on his head.

readings.mnemonic: Your new school has other students too. They are all こう いち s, though.
readings.hint: On your first day of school, imagine walking under the tree and seeing all your classmates. They're all こういち –similar to each other, but with some variation. Your father explains these こういち s escaped from a government research facility, and they come to this school to receive a better education.  "These poor こういち s need a school that will teach them more than just kanji and Japanese vocabulary."

meanings.mnemonic: You've seen this as a radical before, and the kanji means the same thing — south !
meanings.hint: If you keep up with your radicals, your kanji studies will never go south. Haha! Go south! Get it? I said g—

readings.mnemonic: Think about the south . What's down there? Mean Amy ( みなみ ) lives down there. Think of the last mean thing she did to you and imagine it while yelling out "MEAN AMYYYYY" at the sky.
readings.hint: Picture Mean Amy down in the south, saying mean things in her American southern accent: "Y'all don't come back now, ya hear?" Man, Mean Amy is so mean. And southern.

meanings.mnemonic: You keep your bow private because it's made of insect s, meaning it's extremely strong . Insect bodies are really durable, so they make for super strong bows. That's why you have to keep this one private — it's simply too strong to let others get ahold of.
meanings.hint: Imagine holding this bow. Feel how strong it is as you draw the string. Now look closely at the insect bodies it's made of. Thanks for your help, little guys.

readings.mnemonic: You had this strong bow made especially for you in きょう と . きょうと is famous for their strong bows, so if you ever want another, you know where to go.
readings.hint: Imagine all the people who live in きょうと , using their strong arms to make strong bows out of strong insect carapaces. Could きょうと be Japan's strongest city?

meanings.mnemonic: When rain falls on Wolverine , it turns to snow . He's so emotionally distant and cold that the rain freezes into snow as soon as it gets near him.
meanings.hint: Imagine Wolverine in the pouring rain, creating a little halo of snow around him wherever he goes. It's strangely beautiful. That is one cold, traumatized dude…

readings.mnemonic: You're concerned for Wolverine's mental health. Him being so emotionally cold that he turns rain to snow means you kee p ( ゆき ) asking if he's okay. But that only makes him want to be more distant.
readings.hint: Sheesh, emotionally distant people are complicated. The more you keep trying to help them, the more they reject you. Wolverine could do with a nice holiday to melt all that snow away.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a lid on your mouth and mustache , covering up all your talking holes. This is because you kept saying how tall and expensive you are.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself saying how tall and expensive you are to everyone in the room. Then they use a lid to cover your mouth so you can't talk to anyone anymore. But, then your mustache starts talking, saying the same thing. That's why this lid covers both your mustache and your mouth. Now you can't say that you are tall or expensive.

readings.mnemonic:
"I am tall er and more expensive than you."

You're saying this because there are some こう いち s in the room, and you want to make them feel inferior (you're a こういち hater). You keep comparing your height to the こういち s in the room, and you keep checking price tags. Then you tell them you're taller and more expensive than they are.
readings.hint: The こういち s finally get tired of your shenanigans, and lid up your mouth and mustache so you can't say that you're tall or expensive.

meanings.mnemonic: A teacher in winter is happiest because they get to teach ! The kids can't go out and play because it's too cold. They all have to come in and stay warm as the teacher teaches!
meanings.hint: Picture a teacher excitedly teaching, bundled up in the cold. Snow falling outside. Grumpy children being taught inside.

readings.mnemonic: Teachers can teach the most about Japan in winter by going to きょう と . きょうと is where all the old temples and history of Japan is centered. And it's really gorgeous when it snows.
readings.hint: Imagine the teaching teacher teaching you about Japan in きょうと in winter. You've always wanted to go to きょうと !

meanings.mnemonic: The leaf shaped like an eye only grows in winter . Every other leaf, however, will do the opposite, which is to grow in the summer .
meanings.hint: Just think of the eye leaf as weird (it is weird, after all). It grows in the winter. Now, think of any normal leaves. They do the exact opposite, which is to grow in the summer.

readings.mnemonic: Now it's summertime! What do you do during the summer ? You just sit around with your favorite string tying knots ( なつ ) in it over and over again.
readings.hint: Think about summer. Now think about yourself tying knots. Now think about summer again. Now think about tying knots again. Now think about your mother getting upset at you because all you do is tie knots all summer long. Go play video games like a normal kid!

meanings.mnemonic:
The sun is used in a temple to tell time . They don't have clocks or cell phones, they have sun dials and good old fashioned "looking up and seeing where the sun is right now."

This also can mean o' clock , as in "five o'clock" It's used when measuring what time it is in Japanese.
meanings.hint: Imagine being in a temple and realizing you don't know what time it is. Never fear! Look on up at the sun. Ah, yes. Blinding noon.

readings.mnemonic: Time in a temple makes you think of Je sus ( じ ). Specifically, Jesus being able to perform time travel magic. He winks at you, goes back to the dinosaur ages, where he wrestles a T-rex. Then, he goes to WWII era and punches Hitler in the mouth.
readings.hint: Imagine him going to all kinds of different times and also make sure you're specifically imagining Jesus doing all these things. Put these two together and you'll remember the reading in no time. (Haha, get it?)

meanings.mnemonic: The radical yellow and the kanji yellow are exactly the same!

readings.mnemonic: You're in a room, surrounded completely by yellow . You can't get out. Somewhere in the room is a yellow key ( き ) but you can't find it, because everything looks the same. You crawl around trying to find this tiny thing so that you can escape.
readings.hint: The walls are coming in closer, too. Make sure you feel a bit of peril as you look for this key. The walls have a slight opening at the bottom too, so if you don't find it before the wall goes over it, the key will be lost forever, and you'll be smushed!

meanings.mnemonic: You have two bow s but they're both covered in ice . This has made them very weak .
meanings.hint: Just look at these weak, iced over bows. If you tried to draw back their strings they'd snap into a million pieces.

readings.mnemonic: You give up on your trip and bring the weak bows back to your car, but it's stuck in the ice and snow. You decide to use the bows as a jack ( じゃく ) to try to get your car unstuck. Unfortunately, they're just too weak and they break in half.
readings.hint: Now you're stuck with two weak broken bows and no jack. Ice is the worst.

meanings.mnemonic: Use a scooter to travel the circumference of the world and it will take you a full week . Seven whole days!
meanings.hint: A week in earth time is also known as a "full scoot" in scooter lingo. ;)

readings.mnemonic: If you want to scoot for a week , you'll need shoe s ( しゅう ). Good shoes too, not sandals or high heels or boots. Solid, high quality shoes, so you don't fall off the scooter and hurt yourself.
readings.hint: You can't ride a scooter for a week barefoot, are you nuts?! Get some shoes!

meanings.mnemonic: What you say to oneself (yourself), you decide to write down . Whether this is in the form of a journal, diary, or blog, you feel the need to record these important thoughts in some way.
meanings.hint: Just imagine writing in a diary and saying everything to write aloud to yourself in your head.

readings.mnemonic: You need to protect what you write down and record for yourself with a key ( き ). These are your special thoughts and feelings, you don't want anyone else to see! Lock them away with a key!
readings.hint: Picture the key you use to keep what you write down a secret. What does it look like? How does it feel in your hands?

meanings.mnemonic:
You look at a table and notice it is being used as a slide by insect s to launch themselves into the wind .

This kanji can also mean style . To remember this, consider how styles can change quickly, just like the wind !
meanings.hint: Picture a table tilted on its side with some of its legs longer than others, a perfect slide for insects! Imagine all the different insects jumping into the wind each in their own particular style.

readings.mnemonic: You wonder what's pushing these insects to jump into the wind in such a manner, and you realize that it's because they've had way too much foo d ( ふう ).
readings.hint: The insects ate all the food that was left on the table, and now they can't just fly away into the wind like they normally do. Instead, they have to try this new style by using the table as a slide to help them take off into the wind.

meanings.mnemonic: "Does the bamboo shirt suit me?" you ask... but nobody will answer .
meanings.hint: Shout "PLEASE ANSWER ME!" a few times to get the idea of "answer" in your head. What you really want is an answer, whether it's bad or good, but nobody's willing to say anything because you look so terrible.

readings.mnemonic: You pull out a quarter ( こた ) and offer it to whoever will give you an answer .
readings.hint: With this shiny quarter in your hand, everyone is giving you their answer. Yay for bribery!

meanings.mnemonic: Under a cliff there's a stool . If someone has a stool deep inside a cliff, they are most likely pretty anti -society, hiding away like that.
meanings.hint: Imagine someone who looks super anti-social (and a little feral). Ask them why they're sitting on this stool inside a cliff. They reply it's because they hate people. They're anti-people. In fact, they're anti-everything.

readings.mnemonic: Turns out, when you go to this guy and pull off his beard, its the anti -hero from Star Wars, Han ( はん ) Solo! What is Han doing here, so hidden away? He's obviously hiding from Jabba because he doesn't want to get frozen in carbonite again.
readings.hint: Make sure you get to the point where you pull this anti-social guy's beard off, to reveal Han Solo beneath. Be surprised... not just because he's Han Solo, but because he's real, too! Maybe you can yell out "Han! Your people need you!"

meanings.mnemonic:
Wolverine is going down a slide and from your mouth you scream, "Hey, buddy ! Watch it with that slide, it's really fast and you might hurt yourself, buddy!"

You're a really good buddy to Wolverine. He also happens to be the local ruler of these lands. You make it a habit to befriend the local strongmen, just in case… No wonder you're so buddy-buddy with all the rulers around here.
meanings.hint: Imagine having all the local rulers as your buddies, all the influence you would have. You might end up with everyone wanting to be your buddy.

readings.mnemonic: As you're about to embrace your buddy Wolverine, a coon hound ( くん ) tackles him off the slide, and bites him in the process.
readings.hint: Oh no! This coonhound mistook Wolverine for a giant racoon. (His mask kinda makes him look like one.) You better go help your buddy now. Be mindful of the coonhound though, they're good hunters I hear.

meanings.mnemonic: Your nation's flag sits on top of a cape d mouth . This is the symbol for the " Bureau of Secret Things." It's a special department of your government made to keep things hidden.
meanings.hint: The cape covering the mouth represents how this bureau never talks about their secrets. The flag represents your country. Think about all the cool secret things the Bureau of Secret Things must have.

readings.mnemonic: The bureau 's best-kept secret is cloaking devices that cloak you ( きょく ) with the press of a button.
readings.hint: Now, this mnemonic isn’t perfectly close, but the goal is to get you close enough in your brain so that you can remember きょく later. If you can get to CLOAK YOU, then you can get to きょく much easier than just trying to get there from scratch. So imagine working in this bureau and getting to cloak yourself all the time.

meanings.mnemonic: Just like the radical, the kanji means capital .
meanings.hint: Know your radicals!

readings.mnemonic: To remember the reading きょう we use the old capital of Japan, Kyoto, which is spelled きょう と . It was the capital of Japan before Tokyo!
readings.hint: When you think of the capital, any capital, think of きょうと .

meanings.mnemonic: A rain cloud is still a cloud ! It's probably going to rain on you soon though, so I hope you have a jacket or an umbrella.
meanings.hint: Picture all of the different types of clouds in the sky. There are puffy white ones, heavy dark ones, and wispy thin ones. But they're all clouds.

readings.mnemonic: You lie down and try to figure out what the cloud s are shaped like. The first cloud is shaped... like a coo l mo m ( くも ). She's wearing high pants, and holding a kid on each hip, but she's also listening to the latest music and using slang. Huh. You look to the next cloud and see... wait, another cool mom?
readings.hint: You look at each cloud and each one is a different cool mom. They're each doing different cool mom things. You suddenly don't want to look at the clouds anymore.

meanings.mnemonic: Here's a net full of of shellfish that you can buy . You can't just buy loose shellfish. You can only buy the ones that are in the net, bagged up and ready for your money.
meanings.hint: Imagine looking over a bunch of different kinds of shellfish wrapped in nets. Think about which ones you want to buy.

readings.mnemonic: You decide to buy as many shellfish as you can fit in your ca r ( か ). You keep buying until your car is completely full of shellfish before you stop. Cars are totally a realistic unit of measure.
readings.hint: Imagine buying an entire car's worth of shellfish. Mmm, delicious.

meanings.mnemonic: The white ice tree is a magical tree that radiates an aura of comfort , ease , and pleasure , with its icy foliage tinkling like beautiful music whenever it sways in the breeze.
meanings.hint: Close your eyes and feel the aura of comfort wash over you. Listen to the musical tinkling of the tree's icy branches, setting you at ease. You never want this pleasurable moment to end!

readings.mnemonic: You want more of this aura of comfort , so you chop down the magic tree and build a clothing rack ( らく ) out of it. Then, you put the rack in your house so you can enjoy that fuzzy feeling of comfort and ease every time you go to hang something up.
readings.hint: Place a hanger on the rack. Feel the wave of pleasure that runs through you, and then bask in the afterglow of comfort and ease that it leaves behind. Now, go find something else to hang up on your rack. You need another hit of comfort and pleasure.

meanings.mnemonic: Give rice to a woman in winter so she can count it. The woman will count each individual grain of rice, then tell you the amount or number of grains.
meanings.hint: She has to count each grain in order to divvy up the rice supply and make it last the winter. Imagine the amount you get today — just a small number of grains. It's not a good time for you guys…

readings.mnemonic: Why do you have to count your rice out like this? It's because someone sue d ( すう ) your farm and took everything you owned. Since being sued, you only have a small amount of rice to last the winter.
readings.hint: Come up with a story about why your farm got sued for everything. Imagine sitting in court. Listen to the judge deliver the verdict. Be angry. The woman will be counting rice for years to come.

meanings.mnemonic: You have at your disposal a tsunami to assassinate this person , or a hook . Which method will you choose? Decide now!
meanings.hint: Have trouble with the decision. Which one? Tsunami will be big and cool, but a hook will be more discreet. How can you decide? Can you think of a neat way to decide this? You're like Two-Face deciding on how to kill Batman.

readings.mnemonic:
To remember the けつ reading, we always use the word "ketsup." It's a special mix of ketchup and catsup (yum):

You finally decide . You're going to go with the tsunami... but not just any tsunami. You'll kill him with a tsunami of ketsu p ( けつ ).
readings.hint: Get everything ready to take your target down. How will you collect enough ketsup to make a ketsup tsunami? Where will you unleash it? You gotta decide on the ketsup logistics now.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of the master s gets to choose the place they dwell . So they reside in a huge mansion on the top of the mountain, surrounded by all the other masters in their smaller dwellings.
meanings.hint: Picture the leader master who dwells in a big, beautiful dwelling. Everyone else is jealous.

readings.mnemonic: She needs this big place to dwell so she has somewhere to store all of her jew els ( じゅう ). Masters always have lots of fancy, expensive things, and she has jewels.
readings.hint: Imagine opening the door to the leader master's dwelling and thousands of jewels come pouring out, whisking you off, down the mountain.

meanings.mnemonic: This kanji is just the tree radical three times. Just based off this, you should be able to figure it out. This kanji means " forest ."
meanings.hint: Makes sense, because a bunch of individual trees is what makes up a forest!

readings.mnemonic: One tree? No. Two trees? Not enough. I want more ( もり ) trees, please!
readings.hint: Keep repeating this pattern over and over, continually focusing in on how you need "more" trees to make this forest. For some reason the person working with you only goes up by one at a time, though, so you have to say it a lot (it'll help you to remember, anyways!).

meanings.mnemonic: You're in the rain , in a rice paddy , holding an umbrella . You're here because there's nothing else that's very tall around you. You want to get hit by electricity (aka lightning), to prove that it exists.
meanings.hint: Imagine being hit by the electricity. Feel the powerful electric shock pulse through your body. You're like a crazier Ben Franklin out here.

readings.mnemonic: After the electricity hits you, you feel your face. In it is a huge den t ( でん ).
readings.hint: Feel the indent in your face. It doesn't hurt, strangely, but it is pretty huge. Panic about this dent a bit.

meanings.mnemonic: The horse radical and the horse kanji are exactly the same! Huzzah!

readings.mnemonic: You are looking at a horse. It's looking at you. You try making horse noises at it, to get a reaction. Instead of reacting with a normal horse noise, it just makes sheep noises: " Baaa ," ( ば ) it says. "Baaaaa."
readings.hint: Feel the shock of this. It sounds just like a sheep, it's strange.

meanings.mnemonic: With top hat power , you're able to help anyone.
meanings.hint: Imagine putting on your top hat and getting power from it. Then, you use that power to help someone. It's important to make the association that it's the top hat giving you the power to help that person. You're basically Frosty the Snow Man.

readings.mnemonic: The person you help with your top hat power? It's Jo -Anne ( じょ ).
readings.hint: You can imagine saving her however you like. Perhaps she was under a car, and you used your top hat powers to help her out of that situation by lifting it above your head.

meanings.mnemonic: Dirt makes it easy to identify your location . Even if you add an extra line to mark the ground, it's still easy. Keep that in mind and you can go from place to place.
meanings.hint: Imagine looking at the dirt. It's a distinct color and texture, and you're able to identify your location just from that alone. Now draw that line on the ground and you'll always remember this place.

readings.mnemonic: Knowing your location from dirt is quite a feat! Who taught you that? It was Joe ( じょう ), the big local farmhand. Despite being a bit weird, he can identify any place just from its dirt!
readings.hint: Remember Joe? He's our local farmhand who only has nine toes (from the 上 mnemonic). Imagine that guy explaining to you how to know your location from dirt. He may be a bit clumsy, but the man really knows his dirt.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a gate that the sun goes through at a regular interval . There is an interval of time in the sense that the sun goes through it at the same time every day. And there is an interval of space in the sense that it has to go through the space between the gate posts.
meanings.hint: "Interval" is a weird word to remember, but just think of the sun going around the earth and through the gate like clockwork. Every 24 hours it passes through the gate, and each interval is exactly the same.

readings.mnemonic:
This method of keeping track of interval s of time was invented by the great Khans of Mongolia, specifically the most famous Khan of all: Genghis Khan ( かん ).

Genghis Khan has had a huge impact on the world as we know it, especially the Eurasian continent of the past, so he's going to come up a lot from now on. Be prepared to get very familiar with him and his friends!
readings.hint: Picture Genghis Khan ordering his most skilled inventors and builders to create this special sun gate so they can harness the power of intervals, and all people under his rule can benefit from the accurate telling of time.

meanings.mnemonic: You're in a cage and you've been shot by an arrow . Obviously things are looking pretty bad. You need some medicine if you hope to live.
meanings.hint: More specifically, antibiotics. Your wound is festering pretty badly.

readings.mnemonic:
When we need to remember the sound い we use the word "Eagle."

You need your medicine really bad. Then, you look up. There's an ea gle ( い ) flying overhead. He has a package. It's medicine! You're saved!
readings.hint: Or, so you think. He just keeps circling and circling. Feel some hate for the eagle. Why isn't he dropping the medicine? You don't have long to live!

meanings.mnemonic: There's a cross sticking out the top of the sun and another cross sticking out the bottom. The moon is off to the right. That's because it's the morning , which is when the sun and moon switch over. Those crosses are making sure the sun stays high in the sky for the rest of the day.
meanings.hint: Imagine a sun attached up in the sky with two crosses, which act as scaffolding, and the moon gradually fading off to the right as the morning wears on.

readings.mnemonic: Every morning you have Asa hi ( あさ ) beer for breakfast.
readings.hint: You watch the sun scaffolded by crosses, and the moon next to it, as you sip on your early-morning Asahi.

meanings.mnemonic: You have to sickle the rice paddy today because it's your turn . This kanji also means number in a series , which is a lot like a turn. For example, you're number 3, so your turn will be number 3.
meanings.hint: Go through in your mind the system your farm / village uses for deciding who has to sickle on any given day. It rotates, and it's a turn-based system. So, do you all stand in a line? Is there a calendar? Come up with something interesting, focusing on your turn in the process you come up with.

readings.mnemonic: This turn -based rice sickle system is a contest as well! But, you are disappointed to find out that you have been ban ned ( ばん ) from the game because of the performance enhancing steroids you've been taking.
readings.hint: Try to feel disappointed by this. Strange thing to feel disappointed by, but you were a great sickler. You were going to win the great prize, too (come up with something huge!), but now you're banned for the season.

meanings.mnemonic: Ice is what you're lack ing, so the next thing you need to do is go to the store and see if they have any. Nope, no ice. So, now you go to the next store. Is there ice here? Nope. Next!
meanings.hint: Feel a little frustrated you can't find ice. Have the words " next next next" echoing through your head as you go from store to store, irritated.

readings.mnemonic: To remember the reading じ , we used the word "Jesus" - Finally, when you're at the last store in existence, you decide it's time to pray to Je sus ( じ ), asking for a miracle. He comes down from the heavens with ice in his hands.
readings.hint: Imagine him coming down. Imagine your surprise. He hands you the ice, and it's cold. You thank him, and he flies away.

meanings.mnemonic: When a bunch of thread s are meet ing on paper (after you've dipped them in paint, say) they can create a masterpiece of a picture (or painting ).
meanings.hint: It's unbelievable the kind of picture you're able to paint with just the tips of threads. So much detail. So artistic. If it helps, imagine that you drew the Mona Lisa, better than the original, only using this thread tool.

readings.mnemonic: You tell someone you painted this masterpiece of a picture . Their response? " EH ( え )?!"
readings.hint: Tell a few more people about your picture. Have them all do the same surprised "Eh?!" reaction at your art skills.

meanings.mnemonic: The poor alligator was taken far inland by a tsunami . Now his only source of water is a tiny pond that barely fits his body, and doesn't even provide much food.
meanings.hint: Imagine the alligator riding the tsunami in. When he hits the ground, and the water recedes, he's all alone in a pond, but it doesn't provide much for him. Imagine him looking for food in the little, shallow pond. Imagine him trying to stay wet... it's just not a great situation to be in, unfortunately.
