readings.mnemonic: The worst part about this pond is that the only thing for the alligator to eat is chee se ( ち ). Cheese may be yummy for humans, but alligators hate it. They'd rather eat the cow the cheese came from. Sadly, this pond is full of cheese.
readings.hint: Picture an alligator crying in a pond full of cheese. It's eating a piece really slowly and weeping.

meanings.mnemonic: You're tearing this door down with an axe . You're absolutely crazy. "I HATE THIS PLACE !!" you keep yelling as you smash in the door.
meanings.hint: Why do you hate this place, in particular? Come up with a great reason why to help you to remember this very broad kanji meaning.

readings.mnemonic: You smash through the door and finally get inside the place , only to find some kind of sho w ( しょ ) going on. It's a circus show — elephants, tightrope walkers, bears on unicycles! The greatest show on earth, but why is it at this place?
readings.hint: Come up with a weird reason as to why the circus is at this place you hate so much. Why did you come here with your axe? Does it have something to do with the circus show?

meanings.mnemonic: Aim the scooter 's neck at the road ! Scooters are only meant for driving on street s and path s, and if you go the wrong way , you'll probably break your own neck!
meanings.hint: Imagine riding your scooter down the road. You're carefully aiming the scooter's neck the right way so you don't end up going out of control and dying.

readings.mnemonic:
To remember the reading どう we use the word "doughnut."

You're traveling down this road so fast that you don't see the giant dou ghnut ( どう ) that's rolling toward you until the last minute. You crash into it, and both you and the doughnut go tumbling down. Ahg, your neck!
readings.hint: Alternate ending: You do a sweet jump, go right through the doughnut hole, take a bite, and land safely back onto the road!

meanings.mnemonic: You dig a hole nine times for your research . You should always do research with nine variables so you can compare them, and so there's an uneven number.
meanings.hint: Imagine carefully digging a hole nine times, in nine different spots, so you can do your research. Hmm, I wonder what kind of research these holes are for…

readings.mnemonic: Your research is on cu cumbers ( きゅう )!
readings.hint: Put nine full-grown cucumbers into their holes so you can research what happens next.

meanings.mnemonic: You loiter around with a weapon because you're currently doing your service and duty to your country. You're standing guard outside, waiting just in case something will happen.
meanings.hint: Imagine standing around with a weapon in your hands. It feels more like you're doing nothing, loitering around the wall, but this is how you offer your service to your country, so you have to do it.

readings.mnemonic: You're about halfway through your service , when a yak ( やく ) walks by. It stops directly in front of you and grunts. "Excuse me, Mr. Yak, but it's my duty to escort you away from my country."
readings.hint: Somehow your duty has become pushing this stubborn, hairy yak away from the entrance to your country. You poke it in the butt with your weapon, but it doesn't seem to care.

meanings.mnemonic: When your self goes down a slide and hits a barb , it's a painful reminder that you have a body . Sometimes people think their self is just some kind of floating, disembodied consciousness, but getting a barb stuck in oneself while going down a slide will quickly remind one of one's bodily existence.
meanings.hint: Imagine hurtling down a slide, your inner self reveling in the joy of the moment. Then, you hit a sharp barb — OUCH!!! Suddenly, you're very conscious of your body again.

readings.mnemonic: What part of your body did the barb get stuck in? Your shin ( しん ). That pointy little barb dug deep into your shin and made you painfully aware of the fact that oneself and one's body are inextricable.
readings.hint: Feel the sharp pain in your shin as it radiates through your body. It kinda sucks, but in a way it's reassuring proof of the physical existence of oneself.

meanings.mnemonic: A stone lantern is an excellent way to sharpen anything, even in the dark. Your knives, your forks, your spoons, and even your sword.
meanings.hint: Imagine walking around at night and you need to sharpen your sword. You walk up to a stone lantern and rub your sword all over it to sharpen it. Touch the blade. Indeed, it's been sharpened!

readings.mnemonic: Why did you sharpen your blade? Because you are about to do battle with Ken ( けん ), the famous samurai warrior.
readings.hint: You'll want to remember this character, because he will come up many times throughout your WaniKani journey. Ken the samurai used to work for WaniKani, but then he went rogue because he thought WaniKani wasn't being radical enough. So now he wanders the website carrying his squid friend (who you'll meet later) in a bucket.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical and the kanji are exactly the same, so this means someone .
meanings.hint: Well, well… someone's lucky to not have to learn a new kanji.

readings.mnemonic: If you don't know who someone is, odds are they're a sha man ( しゃ ). Only a shaman would have the mystical powers to obscure their own identity and pass themselves off as a mysterious, unknown "someone."
readings.hint: Think back on someone you met recently whose name or face you can't really remember. Hate to break it to you, but… they were a shaman.

meanings.mnemonic: The branch radical and the branch kanji are the same! This kanji also means support . To remember it, think about a tree with lots of branches. The branches support each other and the trunk of the tree. If you were to climb the tree, the branches would be able to support your weight too!
meanings.hint: Picture a branch supporting the weight of you, your entire family, and maybe a few friends.

readings.mnemonic: You're happily sitting on this branch , letting it support all this weight, when a shee p ( し ) starts to climb the tree. A very, very big sheep. Uh oh... no no, sheep. If you get on this branch it won't be able to support all this weight!!!
readings.hint: The big sheep walks up the side of the tree with its weird hooves and starts to put its weight on the branch. "No no no!" you and your whole family scream. The branch can't support the weight of the sheep. It snaps! You all tumble to the ground.

meanings.mnemonic: Say it with your tongue . If you do, you'll be able to talk . You'll be able to speak .
meanings.hint: Your problem is you weren't saying it with your tongue before. Once you start using your tongue, you're able to speak suddenly!

readings.mnemonic: If something uses their tongue, it will be able to talk and speak . To prove this, you teach a wa lrus ( わ ) to use its tongue, and lo and behold, it speaks to you!
readings.hint: You decided to teach a walrus how to speak because it seemed like the hardest thing to teach, with its tusks and all that. It turned out to be really easy. Although, you may have picked the Justin Long walrus, by accident. Either way, this walrus can speak! And you definitely (probably) helped.

meanings.mnemonic: Writing and measurement stand versus one another. Writing is all about feeling and expression. Measurement is about cold hard numbers. Yep, in this world writing people are basically the opposite of measurement people. Which side are you on?
meanings.hint: What's that, you say? It doesn't always have to be writing types versus measurement types? We can work together to create a better society? Nah, sorry, I'd rather it be me versus the world.

readings.mnemonic: Whenever it's you versus someone else, you wear a tie ( たい ). Naturally, your tie is always the opposite color of your opponent's tie. Kinda like how political rivals wear different color ties when they debate. It's pretty silly, but at least your tie makes it clear that it's you versus them.
readings.hint: Think of someone you know who's your total opposite. Now imagine you're both in ties, but opposite colors, and you're debating… or fighting in a cage match or something.

meanings.mnemonic: In your fingers is a weapon , so you throw it. Maybe you're angry and you threw the weapon at a person you don't like. Maybe you're a pacifist and you threw it away so no one else could use it. Either way, when you have a weapon in your fingers you have to throw it.
meanings.hint: Imagine a weapon in between your fingers. It doesn't matter what kind. Now feel the weight as you throw it.

readings.mnemonic: You throw your weapon away because you're about to enter とう きょう and there are no weapons allowed there. There is, however, a big pile of thrown away weapons by the border, which is where you're throwing yours right now.
readings.hint: Imagine throwing the weapon in your fingers just before you walk into とうきょう . You can see the lights, smell the smells! Throw your weapons and your cares away!

meanings.mnemonic: Someone throws a cleat at your forehead while you sit on a stool . You must accept this. Then another guy cleats you in the forehead, but you remain on the stool and receive it without complaint. This is some kind of acceptance ritual for the most prestigious Spanish soccer club in the world. If a cleat to the forehead doesn't knock you off the stool, you'll receive an offer to join.
meanings.hint: You're dealing with a lot of emotions right now. You feel the pain from being cleated in the forehead, but you're also glad to have finally been accepted into the soccer club. Suppress your pain and receive this honor.

readings.mnemonic: After they accept you, they give you a small ju ice ( じゅ ) box. It's to help you recover from the trauma of getting kicked. And you receive the juice with grace.
readings.hint: Did you ever play soccer as a kid? Do you remember accepting a small juice box into your little kid hands during halftime? Apparently pros drink the same ones.

meanings.mnemonic: What does your mama tell you to do in the street ? Look before you cross! You gotta do it beforehand , or it could be too late.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself about to cross the street, and your mama saying in your ear: "always look beforehand"!

readings.mnemonic: As you look beforehand , you see a yo- yo ( よ ) lying in the street.
readings.hint: As you cross the road, you pick up the yo-yo and put it in your pocket. You're really glad you looked beforehand, because you now have a yo-yo!

meanings.mnemonic: A sheep that's big is a true beauty . Big is beautiful , and that's all the more true for sheep.
meanings.hint: Imagine a great big beautiful sheep. See the sheep's beauty.

readings.mnemonic: The big sheep's beauty has attracted a swarm of bee s ( び ). The sheep is so beautiful that the bees have mistaken it for a flower.
readings.hint: The bees are swarming around the sheep, marvelling at its beauty and trying to figure out how to get nectar from it.

meanings.mnemonic: Take the moon stamp on the stool in front of you and use it on all of your clothes . Now even if you sell or lose a piece of clothing , you'll be able to find it again by checking for your special moon stamp.
meanings.hint: Imagine placing each piece of clothing you own onto the stool and carefully stamping them with a symbol of the moon. This should stop people from stealing all of your clothes!

readings.mnemonic: A few days later you're with a friend when you recognize the clothes they're wearing. You grab it, rip it off, and scream, " F**k ( ふく ) you!" There is your moon stamp! These are your clothes!!
readings.hint: Your friend turns red and tries to pull the clothes back, "F**k you!" they yell. "No, f**k you, these are my clothes and I have proof this time!"

meanings.mnemonic: The compare radical and the compare kanji are exactly the same! Huzzah!

readings.mnemonic: Compare the he at ( ひ ) today with the heat yesterday. Is it hotter today? It's up to you to compare.
readings.hint: Feel today's heat. Now compare it to yesterday. You might want to check your thermometer if you're not sure!

meanings.mnemonic: A yurt 's stool is always placed at certain degree s. The degrees change depending on specific occurrence s.
meanings.hint: Since a yurt is round, the stool has to be angled at certain degrees. It's part of yurt culture. Imagine someone adjusting the stool with a measurement tool to adjust it to an exact degree.

readings.mnemonic: Also angled at exact degree s is the do g ( ど ).
readings.hint: The dog changes its degrees at every occurrence of the stool changing. Picture the dog leaning at the exact degree the stool is at.

meanings.mnemonic: Go to the tent and take the lantern from it, then we will be ready for departure . We can't leave the lantern inside of the tent; that's a fire hazard. We'd best take it with us before the departure. Don't you agree?
meanings.hint: Feel the heat from the lantern. It's not quite as comforting as being inside a tent, but it's better than nothing. The lantern will light your way on your departure.

readings.mnemonic: Oh! Don't forget your hats ( はつ ) either. Wouldn’t want to start a departure without hats or a lantern. That'd be a short journey.
readings.hint: How cold and unprotected would your head be without a hat? Very. Taking hats along with departures is integral to their success. Imagine how unsafe you would feel with no hats. That's no departure I want to set out on.

meanings.mnemonic: A roof is correct only if you determine that it is.
meanings.hint: Imagine looking at a roof, and someone asks you, "Is this roof correct?"

readings.mnemonic: You determine d it, but you still have to test this determination. You go to the top and roll a ta ter ( てい ) tot down the roof. Tater tots will only roll down a perfectly correct roof.
readings.hint: "Because of my tater tot roof test, I have once again determined it is correct."

meanings.mnemonic: Sometimes life gives you a kick , and you have to express yourself to deal with it! How do you express yourself? On the surface (of your face)!
meanings.hint: Think of the last time life gave you a kick, and how you expressed that pain to make it go away, specifically on the surface that is your face.

readings.mnemonic: You need to express yourself, so you call up a friend and say " Heyo !" ( ひょう ).
readings.hint: Imagine yourself saying it more like ひょう , though. ひょう man!

meanings.mnemonic: Under your roof in winter there are more mouth s to feed because you let guest s stay with you when it's cold. Some of them aren't family, so they have to pay to stay under your roof and get food. Those are customer s.
meanings.hint: Picture all of the hungry guests and customers under your roof, eating your food and sleeping on your floor.

readings.mnemonic: One of your guest s is a regular named Kyaku ( きゃく ) the customer . Kyaku is always staying over at one place or another, so everyone knows their name.
readings.hint: Kyaku looks just like any other customer. Picture a stereotypical "customer" type person in your head.

meanings.mnemonic: The heavy radical and the heavy kanji are the same.
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: In your hand is a very heavy jew el ( じゅう ). Real jewels are always heavier than they look.
readings.hint: Imagine holding a real jewel in your right hand and a fake jewel in your left. The one in your right hand is suuuuupeerrr heavy!

meanings.mnemonic: You need to get your fingers around a temple if you want to hold it. Is the temple too big? Hold smaller temples you can buy in the temple's souvenir shop.
meanings.hint: Imagine holding a tiny temple in your fingers. Hold it up to the light. Hold it on the train. Hold it while you sleep.

readings.mnemonic: As you hold the temple you hear a voice. You look down through your fingers and see a tiny Je sus ( じ ). He's smiling and gives you a thumbs up. Aww, thanks Jesus!
readings.hint: Jesus likes when you hold things like temples. He probably likes when you hold other things too, like ice cream, puppies, and homeless peoples' hands.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of the elephant s is so great, they put up an image or statue of him everywhere. Every house, every street corner — his image looks down on all the other elephants at all times.
meanings.hint: For now, just imagine the leader of the elephants being told images and statues of him are being put up everywhere. Don't think about what the statue looks like, because that's next.

readings.mnemonic: When they unveil the image s and statue s of the leader elephant, everyone gasps. They look like him, but he's a zo mbie ( ぞう ). Gasp!
readings.hint: But this is exactly what the leader of the elephants asked for. You see, the leader of the elephants was a big fan of zombies. Imagine how excited he'd get to have this zombie version of himself appearing as images and statues, all across the land.

meanings.mnemonic: When you're in prison , whoever ends up with the shellfish will lose . It's the game all prisoners play. It's like keep away from the shellfish. And if you end up with the shellfish BZZZZTT that's it, you're the loser.
meanings.hint: Imagine that it's your first day in prison. You're walking around the yard, minding your own business, when suddenly a shellfish smacks you right in the head and falls into your open hands. "HAHA YOU LOSE!" someone shouts.

readings.mnemonic: If you lose , you're a foo l ( ふ ). All you had to do was not hold the prison shellfish. It really shouldn't have been that hard to not lose, ya fool.
readings.hint: Now you have to try to make some other fool lose. Otherwise you'll be the loser fool forever.

meanings.mnemonic: When a helicopter is piloted by a woman , it stirs an insatiable need in her. She doesn't know what it is. But she needs it. And that's why she's flying around looking for it!
meanings.hint: Picture the woman in the helicopter needing something. She's looking around. Searching. But she doesn't know what it is!

readings.mnemonic: Finally she knows what she need s! It's yo gurt ( よう )! Delicious, creamy yogurt!
readings.hint: What does she need? Yogurt!

meanings.mnemonic: You need to look a tree in the eye to gain a mutual respect for each other. Stand up straight, look the tree directly in its eye(s). If it looks right back, you know things are mutual.
meanings.hint: It's the same with humans, you know? If you want to have a mutual relationship you need to be able to look each other in the eyes. If you're too embarrassed, or you look down on them, then that isn't mutual respect!

readings.mnemonic: If your respect for the tree is mutual with the respect the tree feels for you, you'll be able to see the tree's sou l ( そう ). Right now its soul looks pretty sad, like it's trying to say, "Why are your people cutting down all my friends? I thought we had a mutual understanding."
readings.hint: This is basically a FernGully moment, you know? You can feel the tree's pain like it's your pain. It's that kind of mutual sharing of souls you need.

meanings.mnemonic: You find a cross in the mouth of Wolverine . What a strange occurrence . I guess he put it there, but why would he do such a thing ? You ask him, but he says it's a personal matter of utmost secrecy.
meanings.hint: Imagine his muffled words as he tries to explain this with the cross in his mouth. Hm, better not pry further. Maybe he's trying to… X-orcize his demons…

readings.mnemonic: This occurrence doesn't impress Je sus ( じ ), who appears and gently removes the cross from Wolverine's mouth.
readings.hint: Imagine Jesus gently taking the cross out of Wolverine's mouth. What a swell dude.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a stand ing tree that you're taking an axe to. You're going to cut down this tree to let a new tree grow.
meanings.hint: As you imagine yourself cutting down the standing tree, think about what new tree you'll plant in its place.

readings.mnemonic: You cut down the tree to make room for a new one, but it falls on your shin ( しん ).
readings.hint: Only your shin got hit, so focus all your imaginary pain down there. Try to pull out the splinters. Ouch!

meanings.mnemonic: The clown building is over in that part of town. We keep the clown part as separate as we can from the rest of the normal people. In fact, the entire clown education department and their clown club s have to stay in their part, they can't come in ours.
meanings.hint: Of course, we feel terrible about forcing the clowns into their own part of town. It's not that they're lesser than us, it's just that we don't like them.

readings.mnemonic: You see, that part of town is full of boo ze ( ぶ ). Clowns need a lot of booze to keep their charade going, so we just let them have it. But they aren't allowed in our part anymore, because they all stink of nasty clown booze.
readings.hint: Picture all of the clowns, trapped in their weird clown part of town, drinking booze and smashing each other over the heads with the bottles. You're glad you don't live in that part.

meanings.mnemonic: The eye of a lion , still small and curious, gazes upon the prefecture that will one day become his. He's a small lion, but once he grows up, everything reflected in his eye will be his prefecture to rule over.
meanings.hint: Imagine the small lion casting his eye across his future prefecture. His small eye is twinkling, because he's proud that he'll one day be prefect, and this prefecture will be his.

readings.mnemonic: This small lion's prefecture is being protected by Ken ( けん ) the samurai, because the lion is too small to protect all of this land himself. Ken the samurai is used to wandering far and wide, so one prefecture is nothing to him.
readings.hint: Picture Ken protecting the prefecture, punching hyenas in the face, and yelling, "This may just be a prefecture, not a country, but I'll give it my all for my lion prefect-to-be!"

meanings.mnemonic: When everyone has grain in their mouth , you will have peace . That is the Japanese style of doing things.
meanings.hint: Imagine Japanese people with grain in their mouth. The whole country is at peace.

readings.mnemonic: Peace is the Japanese style . The wa lruses ( わ ) living off of Japan see this, and start to emulate.
readings.hint: The walruses also achieve peace by putting grain in every walrus' mouth, following the Japanese style.

meanings.mnemonic: You put a grain in your mouth and let it take a ride . This kanji has the same radicals as 和 , but this time the grain is in your mouth, which is why there's little pieces of grain sticking out of the 口 part ;)
meanings.hint: Just imagine you're a car or a train and the grain is a person. It's going for a nice ride in your mouth!

readings.mnemonic: You decide the grain is probably lonely on this ride alone, so you add some no ri ( の ) seaweed, so it has a friend.
readings.hint: Now the nori is going for a ride too, woohoo!

meanings.mnemonic: The mouth of a tree leader is all about trying to preserve the trees. In fact, she guarantee s that she will preserve the trees if it's the last thing she does.
meanings.hint: Basically, a tree leader is someone who stands up for trees. Mostly she talks, but that can be a powerful skill to have.

readings.mnemonic: She chains herself to a tree to preserve them. But, unfortunately, the lumberjacks just take a giant back hoe ( ほ ) and tear this tree leader and the tree she's attached to in half.
readings.hint: What a terrible scene. Focus on the fact that they're using a horrible, modified back hoe. It's more like a weapon than a back hoe, and they show no mercy at all.

meanings.mnemonic: Your scooter is anti scoot. It doesn't want to run. So you have to return it.
meanings.hint: Unfortunately, now you have to return to the store to return this scooter, but you have no scooter to get there with. Ugh.

readings.mnemonic: You return to the store and wait in line to return the scooter but the person working returns doesn't want you to return it. He stares you dead in the eyes, waves his hand, and turns you into a hen ( へん )!
readings.hint: The employee leans down toward you in hen form and says, "Ooh, I'm sorry. We don't take returns from hens. You'll have to just return home."

meanings.mnemonic: You plant a flag in the ground , marking your private patch of dirt . In this spot you raise a roof , then fill the space beneath it with some shop s and store s.
meanings.hint: That's the natural next progression of claiming some land. Building a roof upon it and building some shops and stores inside. You'll see why in a moment.

readings.mnemonic: The roof covering the dirt is there so that the Ya nkees ( や ) can play baseball year round. The store s and shop s are there to sell hot dogs and Yankees memorabilia.
readings.hint: You have yourself a whole Yankees stadium, and all it took was a roof and some shops and stores! If you build it, they will come.

meanings.mnemonic: On its scooter , a creature with horns from heaven will take things you want to send there.
meanings.hint: Picture the horned thing on its scooter, a big messenger bag over its shoulder with the kanji for heaven on it. "You got somethin' you want me to send?" it mutters.

readings.mnemonic: You want to send your sou l ( そう ) to heaven. I mean, you don't want it to go to some of those other places you've heard of, so why not send your soul?
readings.hint: You put your soul into an envelope, stick a stamp on it, and hand it off to be sent to heaven. Bye-bye! I hope this doesn't have any negative effects on your body!

meanings.mnemonic: A samurai has come bearing forehead s and legs that he's trying to sell . He thought it was a waste to leave all his slain enemies on the battlefield, so he gathered up their foreheads and legs to sell on the black market for some extra cash. Hey, no judging! A samurai's gotta sell what a samurai's gotta sell, okay?
meanings.hint: Imagine running into this samurai salesman as you walk through a sketchy part of town. He cautiously glances over his shoulder before opening his trench coat to reveal to you… all the foreheads and legs he's trying to sell.

readings.mnemonic: If you run into this samurai who sell s severed foreheads and legs, you should just say " bye ( ばい )!" and get the heck out of there. Actually, if anyone tries to sell you severed body parts — samurai or not — you should probably say bye over and over until you can get away from them.
readings.hint: Imagine loudly saying "bye" over and over at this shady samurai. He keeps trying to tell you about how fresh and high-quality all his foreheads and legs are, but you just loudly repeat "bye" as you back away from him.

meanings.mnemonic: The spicy radical and spicy kanji are exactly the same! Hot!

readings.mnemonic: When you eat spicy food, it gives a から て chop to your mouth. That's where that spicy feeling comes from!
readings.hint: Imagine a bunch of little black belt spice molecules からて punching, kicking and chopping all the way through your mouth and into your throat and nasal passages. Oww, it's pure agony!

meanings.mnemonic: You say things around a fire . In fact there are two fire s, and you're talk ing with a group of people, all sitting around two nice campfires.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself next to the two campfires, having a heart-to-heart talk. What are you saying?

readings.mnemonic: You talk in this intense, fiery way all the way until dawn ( だん ), but no longer! Once dawn comes the discussion needs to be concluded.
readings.hint: If you try to talk all night and past dawn, you'll burn out. Take the day, think, then tonight you can try to have an all-night talk again.

meanings.mnemonic: A cow with wing s is flying in the air. What a strange thing ! You've heard of flying pigs before, but cows? This is a new and strange thing indeed! Cows with wings! What a silly thing.
meanings.hint: Imagine if cows could fly. Wouldn't you be jealous of them? I mean, if a cow can fly, any old thing should be able to, right?

readings.mnemonic: You want a thing to help you fly with the cow so you put on your flying boots ( ぶつ ). Your flying boots are a pretty strange thing in themselves, but they're the only way you can keep up.
readings.hint: Imagine a thing like flying boots. Do you believe such a thing could be real?

meanings.mnemonic: While riding your scooter , you bang your forehead on the car in front. Your scooter's wrecked, so you have to carry it home. You'll need a lot of luck to get that scooter home safely.
meanings.hint: Imagine trying to carry your scooter down a busy road, with all the cars whizzing past. Your forehead is still hurting from that nasty bang. Good luck!

readings.mnemonic: As you carry the scooter, your luck turns, and it starts to rain, so you pull out your um brella ( うん ). Now you're holding your scooter, your forehead, and an umbrella.
readings.hint: It's hard enough to carry a scooter in heavy traffic, but now you have to try to keep yourself and it dry with an umbrella too.

meanings.mnemonic: If you want to say something at a ceremony for construction workers you're going to have to try really hard. Construction is loud, so if you want to be heard you have to attempt to be louder than the drilling and smashing and digging and handing out of awards.
meanings.hint: Try to imagine trying this hard to be heard. The construction workers are all screaming at each other with ease. It's so hard for you to try and keep up with their volume!

readings.mnemonic: You try to get their attention the only way you know how, by lifting a shee p ( し ) above your head. The sheep baas and suddenly everything is silent, all eyes are on you. Well, they're on the sheep.
readings.hint: Huh. You should have thought to try this sheep technique earlier.

meanings.mnemonic: The moon grants a gladiator its power so he will win ! Victory will be his!
meanings.hint: Watch as the moon's power fills the gladiator. He starts to glow, grows larger, and has some craters on his face now. Well.. okay.

readings.mnemonic: He win s over and over again, and soon the Shou gun ( しょう ) is even rooting for him. In fact, the Shougun claims him as his personal gladiator of choice.
readings.hint: The Shougun is only interested in people who win. But you probably could have guessed that already.

meanings.mnemonic: If you give a woman a machine she'll begin learning how to use it right away. She'll also get it to start before anyone else could. Women are really good at starting up new machines!
meanings.hint: Picture a woman seeing a new machine and beginning work on it right away to see how she can start it up.

readings.mnemonic: Actually, before she can begin , she has to shear the shee p ( し ) over there. The machine is going to turn wool into yarn, but you can't just start that process by shoving the sheep in there. You have to keep the little sheep safe!
readings.hint: Picture a machine starting up as the woman begins to shear the sheep. Picture the sheep staring at you, staring at it.

meanings.mnemonic: A good leader can put the ground 's history to great use . You are a good leader. You have studied the ground and dirt of the earth your entire life. You know the history of the ground and you know how to use it to lead your people.
meanings.hint: Smell the ground. Waft the earthy smell of history into your leader-like nostrils. What does it smell like? It smells useful. And also like ground.

readings.mnemonic: You use the ground to raise shee p ( し ). When you were down there sniffing around you realized that your people will flourish best if you use this land for grazing and raising sheep.
readings.hint: When you use the ground for sheep like this, your people rejoice. You're the best leader ever! You always use everything to your advantage!

meanings.mnemonic: A horse and a squid are going to test you.
meanings.hint: Imagine the horse and squid in a big testing room, getting ready to test you. What are they testing you on? Weirdly, they're going to test you on all the kanji that use horse and squid radicals. You ready?

readings.mnemonic: The test is being supervised by Ken ( けん ) the samurai.
readings.hint: To make sure you don't cheat on the horse and the squid test, Ken the samurai is watching you closely, pacing up and down. Every now and then, he waves his katana menacingly.

meanings.mnemonic: If a tsunami is going to last an eternity you better learn how to swim now. We'll be under water for the rest of time and if you can't swim you're not gonna make it.
meanings.hint: Picture most of the earth's population already swimming on the tsunami that will last the rest of eternity. Your floaties and life preservers would help you through this mess. You need to swim!

readings.mnemonic: Oy ( およ )! Oy, stop daydreaming about swim ming and actually do it!
readings.hint: Picture yourself swimming in the tsunami with everyone else. You can't really talk to them, you're too busy swimming for your life, so you just shout "Oy!" to them and they shout it back.

meanings.mnemonic: If someone sticks bamboo in your eye twenty times, the first thing your brain does is calculate how painful it is. Your brain's pain calculation will determine how you calculate your next move.
meanings.hint: Your next move is to go to the doctor, because your brain has calculated that the pain is extreme. Imagine the doctor asking how much it hurts. Well, on a scale of one to ten, doctor, I'd calculate this is about a twenty.

readings.mnemonic: Next you want to calculate how much punishment your bamboo attacker deserves, so you call on San ta-san ( さん ). Santa-san is always calculating how many pieces of coal the naughty children deserve, so he's perfect for this calculation job.
readings.hint: Imagine trekking through the frozen wastes of the North Pole (with your eye patch on) to obtain Santa-san's calculation advice. After much discussion, he concludes that your bamboo attacker deserves twenty. No, not twenty years in prison — just twenty pieces of coal. Oh, how disappointing…

meanings.mnemonic: The angle radical and the angle kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: You gotta know your radicals first! Only then can you start to study these kanji from other angles — meanings, readings, all that good stuff. One angle at a time, you know?

readings.mnemonic: To see those kanji angles from every angle , you cock ( かく ) your head to the side.
readings.hint: Kinda like how a dog cocks its head at an angle when it's trying to understand what you say. That's what you look like when you're studying radicals! Aww, you're so cute.

meanings.mnemonic: The flowers have grown old , so they're suffering .
meanings.hint: Flowers, like anything that grows old, go through a lot of suffering as their bodies break down. They're no different from you and me.

readings.mnemonic: If a flower or a person is growing old and suffering , give them a coo kie ( く ).
readings.hint: No one can resist a good cookie. It will ease your suffering, or at least distract you for a little bit.

meanings.mnemonic: There's an eye on the ground , among some fins . What happened? You killed a fish, and now you you must hide the murder tool . Quick, hide the tool before someone finds out it was you.
meanings.hint: You feel the panic upon realizing what you've just done. This is murder in the first degree. You used a terrible tool to do it, and now you must hide it.

readings.mnemonic: The tool is dripping with goo ( ぐ ). Goopy, incriminating goo. It's the guts of the fish you just killed and it's all over your hands too. Yuck.
readings.hint: That goo is really slimy. It's disgusting. You feel sick to your stomach. Please don't throw up, there's already enough junk on the ground with the eye and fins and all.

meanings.mnemonic: Your leader is very anti -everything, making it all temporary . You get a new shirt, "I'm anti shirts!" she yells, and out it goes.
meanings.hint: She tires of things so quickly, you hardly have time to get used to them before they're gone.

readings.mnemonic: The leader's most temporary possession is her ca r ( か ). Or... cars, because they're all very temporary. She buys one, drives it around, and then suddenly she's anti red car. She needs a blue one!
readings.hint: Picture all of the temporary cars she's tried filling up an entire junkyard. They're all a little different, but in the end they were all just temporary fads.

meanings.mnemonic: You give your scooter to a turkey so that it can advance . The turkey was trying and failing to walk through a crowded street, so out of the goodness of your heart you gave it a scooter so it could advance.
meanings.hint: Imagine picking up the turkey and putting it on your scooter. Of course, you give it your helmet as well, safety first.

readings.mnemonic: You wave as the turkey advance s into the street at top speeds, collides with a car, and scrapes up its shin s ( しん ) almost immediately. Ah!
readings.hint: Silly turkey, you can't advance that fast, of course you'll hurt your shins. It's better to advance slowly, but I guess I couldn't expect you to know that.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami is an alcohol ic beverage containing a lot of alcohol . It’s 3 different kinds of rums, pineapple juice, and grenadine. It’ll hit you like a tsunami though. This alcoholic drink has a kick to it.
meanings.hint: It’s made with spiced rum, coconut rum, and dark rum. Imagine yourself on a sandy beach drinking this cocktail. Feel the sun on your face and the wind in your hair as you sip on this fruity and rummy alcoholic beverage known as a tsunami. You could even picture an actual tsunami in the water coming right for you. I wouldn’t recommend it though—actual tsunami are scary, not drinkable and delicious.

readings.mnemonic: As you lie on this beach, the alcohol keeps coming down a chu te ( しゅ ), straight into your mouth. You can feel the sand in your toes and the alcohol sliding down your throat from this special tsunami chute.
readings.hint: Picture the alcohol flowing down this long chute from somewhere far away. You try to see where it's coming from, but all you can see is this long, long chute stretching along the entire beach, until it disappears into the distance. You should probably be concerned that you're nonstop drinking alcohol from a random chute, but it tastes so good!

meanings.mnemonic: Every rice paddy sits jammed in to the world .
meanings.hint: The world is basically just a bunch of rice paddies smooshed together, if you think about it.

readings.mnemonic: To get around this world of rice paddies, you need to use a kay ak ( かい ).
readings.hint: A rice paddy is mostly made up of water, just like our world, and you don't want to have wet feet. Use the power of your kayak to become a world traveler.

meanings.mnemonic: Climb onto the roof in spring to see the truth . The world is waking back up from winter and revealing the reality of what it is all around you.
meanings.hint: Imagine sitting comfortably on your roof as the world around you changes to spring. Snow melts and reveals all of the truths that have been obscured and hidden in winter.

readings.mnemonic: At the moment, you're trying to learn the truth about the deer that is coming out of the snowy woods when you see not one, but two baby deer appear with it! Gee! Two ( じつ ) babies! You keep looking around and realize that all of the animals seem to have two babies that they had hidden in the snow.
readings.hint: Gee! Two babies! Reality sure is lovely.

meanings.mnemonic: You have spikes growing out of a sheep with fins . Turn this thing into a business ! You could totally charge people money to look at it. Sure, you could also donate it to science, but a business-minded person like you knows a business opportunity when you see one. Step right up! Just five dollars for a glance at the rare spiky mer-sheep!
meanings.hint: Imagine all the money you'll rake in with this new business. People will come from far and wide to see the spiky mer-sheep sideshow. And for an extra charge you'll let 'em touch those pointy spikes and slimy fins. Oh yes, business will be booming.

readings.mnemonic: As you evolve your business , you let customers feed the sheep gyo za ( ぎょう ) dumplings, its favorite snack. For a nominal fee, of course. People love it when the mutant sheep eats gyoza out of their hands. Wouldn't you?
readings.hint: Imagine the smell of garlicky gyoza filling the sideshow tent, as customers try to feed the sheep and compete for its attention. Business is so good that the sheep gets tired of gyoza and won't eat it anymore. Oh well… Skewer them on its spikes and save 'em for later.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a gun and a cow , which must mean it's that time of the year ! Every year on New Year's Eve, you get asked to shoot a cow, as per tradition. You really don't want to though, so you deliberately miss every time.
meanings.hint: Last year was a close call and you ended up shooting the cow's horn, which is why the cow radical looks a little strange here. Can you see how the cow radical fits underneath the gun?

readings.mnemonic: It seems everyone has caught on to the fact that you deliberately miss every year , so this year you make a nen doroid ( ねん ) decoy in the shape of a cow.
readings.hint: Nendoroids are Japanese plastic figurines. (Look them up if you need to! And yes, there are cow nendoroids.) It probably won’t be easy to make a cow-sized nendoroid this year, but better than having to shoot a real one.

meanings.mnemonic:
A tsunami hit ancient China and formed the Chinese country we know now as... China !

Sometimes this kanji also means man . In fact, it used to refer specifically to people of the Han dynasty, but over time evolved into a term for men in specific contexts.
meanings.hint: Picture the world way far away, from space. You see a giant tsunami carve out a massive chunk of land to the west of Japan on the main continent. Slowly the tsunami waters recede, leaving the outline of China.

readings.mnemonic: You peer closer at China and see one man, Genghis Khan ( かん ), leading his troops into the vast country, ready to conquer all the Chinese people living in China!
readings.hint: Khan is only one man, but watch as he takes China for himself. A tiny little flag with a horse on it is raised in the middle, for all of the Chinese people to see. Hmm, this could be a problem.

meanings.mnemonic: When the mouth of a bird opens, you're most likely to hear a chirp . This is how birds communicate. They open their mouths and let out a series of chirps. Chirp chirp chirp!
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself out in the wilderness on a sunny day. The weather is perfect and the scenery is beautiful. The only sound you can hear is the chirp of birds throughout the woods around you. It's very peaceful.

readings.mnemonic: The best way to experience the chirp -chirping of birds is to offer them na chos ( な ). Birds love nachos so much, it's a guaranteed way to make them chirp with delight.
readings.hint: Picture yourself surrounded by birds of all kinds, handing out nachos. Just listen to their happy chirping. They're sitting on your arms, shoulders, and even your head, trying to get closer to the nachos. That chirping is so loud!

meanings.mnemonic: You chase a turkey up a tree to collect it. At least, you will once you've gather ed enough turkeys into this one tree.
meanings.hint: Collect another turkey, send it flying and gobbling up into the tree next to the other one.

readings.mnemonic: Once you've collect ed enough turkeys, you're going to turn them into a shoe ( しゅう ). Maybe even two shoes, but that will mean gather ing even more turkeys!
readings.hint: Picture the nice, feathered shoes you'll be able to make once you've gathered all of these turkeys together. Ohoho, you can't wait! Go collect another turkey!

meanings.mnemonic: You can eat all you want, but you'll still lack something to drink . You can't live just from eating, you have to drink things too!
meanings.hint: Imagine eating and eating and lacking a delicious, cold, thirst-quenching drink. Your mouth is dry. You have to drink something, anything!

readings.mnemonic: You get desperate for a drink and put some no ri ( の ) seaweed in a blender, trying to create a drink of your own.
readings.hint: Luckily, this nori isn't dried and pressed like they usually are, and you manage to make a bright green nori drink. You gulp it down, and even though it tastes like the ocean, it hits the spot!

meanings.mnemonic: The thread of winter and ice has brought this year to its end . Once you're in the long thread of winter and ice, you know the year will end soon.
meanings.hint: Picture the season of winter as a long thread, embalmed in ice. At the end of it is the year’s end. The thread of winter and ice brings you closure, it marks the end.

readings.mnemonic: Whenever winter end s, you take the thread covered in ice, and lace it into your shoe ( しゅう ).
readings.hint: This thread isn't just a metaphor, it's real! Feel the cold ice on it, as you lace it into your shoe. An age old tradition, the lacing of icy threads as winter ends. Your shoes will look so cool, or should I say… iced out.

meanings.mnemonic: You stand on a cliff , a hair 's breadth away from the edge, when out of nowhere a geoduck slaps you in the face . The force makes you back away from the edge of the cliff, and your face is all slimy from the geoduck's residue.
meanings.hint: Imagine being slapped in the face with a geoduck — a sharp hot pain, which then mingles with the cold geoduck residue left behind on your face. It's a strange, contradictory sensation.

readings.mnemonic: But where did that geoduck come from? As you turn from the cliff edge, you find yourself face -to-face with a cow ( かお ). This cow is the one who hit you in the face with a geoduck. You don't know why, but something about your face has upset the cow.
readings.hint: Cows are big animals. So you probably got hit in the face with that geoduck pretty hard. Imagine the lingering pain, and how indignant you'd feel. Imagine getting in that cow's face and demanding an explanation.

meanings.mnemonic: You don't want to keep all the alcohol to yourself ( oneself ), so you distribute it to all of your friends. Heck, not just your friends, to everyone you can find!
meanings.hint: Don't be that person who keeps the alcohol all to themselves. Distribute it to all of the legally-aged people you know!

readings.mnemonic:
As you distribute the alcohol you say a hai ku ( はい ):

Here you go my friends Alcohol for everyone Never drink alone
readings.hint: This becomes the haiku you say before every party, every potluck, and every night out at the bar. You even get it tattoo'd to your arm.

meanings.mnemonic: Flowers are knocked down by a violent tsunami , and swirl through the air to kiss your face gently as they fall . With the tsunami came a great windy storm that blew the flowers down. Amongst all the destruction, it's a pretty sight to watch them fall.
meanings.hint: Imagine being out in this tsunami-induced storm. You're wet, cold, and scared, but you're calmed and mesmerized by how the flowers dance in the wind before they finally fall.

readings.mnemonic: You want to save these flowers that fall around you, so you take out a drying rack ( らく ) and place them carefully on it. Any flowers that fall will go on the rack like this, so can save them for later.
readings.hint: Imagine running around catching the flowers as they fall. Imagine placing each fallen petal onto the rack to keep it safe and dry.

meanings.mnemonic: You blast your music loud so it causes a landslide , revealing the perfect soil for farming ! Musically moved dirt is perfect for agriculture .
meanings.hint: Imagine slowly turning up your big boombox until your ears hurt and the ground beneath your feet is vibrating with the beat. Soon you and the boombox are sliding with all of the earth down the hill and onto your farmland. Hooray, now the farming can begin!

readings.mnemonic: The first person to come up with this farming method was No stradamus ( のう ), the famous seer and prophecy man. He predicted humans of the future would use special "yell cubes" to "move that good earth."
readings.hint: Picture Nostradamus flailing his arms and trying to explain his vision to the farmers at the time. He knows that if they listen their farming techniques will improve, but they're looking at him like he's nuts.

meanings.mnemonic: You find some feathers that are so perfectly white , you want to learn more about where they came from and what they're made of.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself in a library poring through huge tomes on feathers, specifically for completely white ones. You want to learn everything you can!

readings.mnemonic: You learn that you should tie these white feathers to your shoe s ( しゅう ). You do it before you learn what it will do, and then suddenly you're jump-flying out of the building.
readings.hint: Oh geez, oh no. You better learn how to control these shoes before you—CRASH!!! Welp, you just shot through a plane. You need to learn more before you act next time.

meanings.mnemonic: You say something about the circumference of this area. You've looked around this whole area, making a big circle around it, because you are trying to investigate something that happened here. You also say this thing in a really quiet tone of voice, because you don't want the person or thing you're investigating to know you're on to them.
meanings.hint: Because the radical circumference is in play, you can think of the crime scene you're investigating as a statue of a giant globe, and you're doing a circumference around it. Then, in the reading, we'll think about what you'll say .

readings.mnemonic: "I came here to investigate this place, and I did indeed find out who the criminal is... it's none other than Mrs. Chou ( ちょう )!"
readings.hint: From what you know about evil Mrs. Chou, you probably could have guessed it was her. The "Circumference Investigation" is now over, though you do still have to catch the perp, Mrs. Chou.

meanings.mnemonic: Think about it this way. There is a famous fishing lure inventor, working on his best work ever. He’s old, and he’s been trying to make the best fishing lure for the last 50 years, and knows this will be his last invention. Then he figures it out. He takes one barb , and connects the end of it to the top of another barb . When he does this, he knows his work is finally finish ed.
meanings.hint: Make sure you look at this amazing fishing lure too - hold it in your hand, see the two barbs fused together. Think about how strange this lure is. Is this really the best in the world? Will this really finish all other fishing lures?

readings.mnemonic: How does the fish lure maker test his newly finish ed lure out? The one he just finished? He gets in a row ( りょう ) boat and rows out into the sea.
readings.hint: Make him think that he can’t just use his new lure on the side of the lake / ocean / whatever. He has to get out there, but unfortunately all he has is a crappy row boat. Imagine the row boat being terrible, leaky, and dangerous, which is why you’re surprised when he goes for it. That’s a terrible row boat to use! If you do this, you'll focus your mind on the rowboat part of things, which will help you to remember the reading of this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: You jump on your scooter with a big bundle to go as fast as you can! You have a bundle of pizzas in here, and they need to be delivered as fast as you can get them there!
meanings.hint: Imagine how fast you can go on your scooter holding this bundle. It's way faster than trying to go on foot, that's for sure!

readings.mnemonic: You were going so fast , you only put on sock s ( そく ). So now you're barreling down the street at top speeds in just your sad, white socks.
readings.hint: Imagine riding the scooter as fast as you can in just your socks. You can feel the wind pulling them off your feet!

meanings.mnemonic: For a car , a tombstone is lightweight . Cars are very strong, you see, so something heavy like a tombstone is no match for them. All cars find tombstones to be light and easy to transport.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself trying to move a huge slab tombstone. It’s difficult, no? I bet you wish you had a car or some other vehicle to make the process much less painful. Your arms are getting sore from carrying the tombstone and you’re getting sweaty and hot from all the work. What you would do for a car with air conditioning.

readings.mnemonic: What do you wish you had to transport this tombstone? Oh, that’s right, a car. So you need some kind of car loo phole ( かる ). If you had a car, this tombstone moving business would be no problem. To cars, tombstones are naught but lightweight pebbles! But the rules say you can't use cars to carry tombstones, so you need to find a car loophole.
readings.hint: Remember all that hard work you were doing before and how you got all sweaty and gross? Now imagine yourself inside of a nice car with air conditioning, and the tombstone in the trunk. It feels great doesn’t it? The air conditioning is perfect, and driving a car is much easier than carrying a tombstone around. Thank goodness you found a car loophole in the tombstone carrying rules.

meanings.mnemonic: What you say between five mouth s is a language . As long as there are five different people saying these same words with their five mouths, it's considered its own language.
meanings.hint: Picture these five mouths saying the same language to each other. They can understand each other! The mouths are smiling and laughing too.

readings.mnemonic: Another mouth joins in speaking the language , but it's green and scaled and... it's Go dzilla's ( ご ) mouth! Godzilla speaks this language too.
readings.hint: Godzilla speaks a language some people understand! You wish you knew this earlier, then you might have been able to stop some of his destruction. Try to say, "Hey Godzilla, please stop destroying my city," in his language. It's a lot of growling and yelling. He doesn't seem to understand you. You better practice your pronunciation.

meanings.mnemonic: Flowers bring beauty to the world of trees , but the leaf is what matters the most. Leaves are how flowers and trees get their nutrients from the sun!
meanings.hint: Leaves, no matter how little, are the most important thing in the world for them.

readings.mnemonic: Picture a little flower working really hard to grow its first leaf. The leaf pops out and it shouts, " Ha ( は )!"
readings.hint: は is the sound of a leaf coming into existence. Whenever you see a plant from now on, hear the little "ha" sound it makes when each leaf pops out of its body.

meanings.mnemonic: You look at some gold and notice a fault in it. You scrape at the fault and see that it's just sprayed with gold paint. It's not gold, it's (comparatively) worthless iron !
meanings.hint: You think you've found a ton of gold bars! Imagine being so happy about this. Then, imagine your heart dropping in your chest when you notice the fault. Then, it dropping down even farther when you figure out it's merely iron.

readings.mnemonic: Do you know that movie, Tetsu o ( てつ ) the Iron Man? We talked about it on Tofugu a while back. It’s one of the wackiest Japanese movies of all time. If you don’t know about Tetsuo the Iron Man, you should definitely look it up (if you aren't averse to horror/violent films). Tetsuo is one of the most insane movies in the history of ever.
readings.hint: Watch a trailer on YouTube for Tetsuo the Iron Man. Go ahead, do it now – I’ll wait. Did you watch it? Remember that nonsense every time you see this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: If you go up to a gate and press your ear to it, you'll hear what's going on on the other side. The people on the other side will have no idea you can hear their conversation.
meanings.hint: I'm sure you've pressed your ear up against a wall, door, or gate before to hear what's going on on the other side. Remember back to a time you did this and how sneaky you felt listening in on people without them knowing. You sneaky frood, you.

readings.mnemonic: From the other side of the gate, you hear one of the people start to play a song on a recorder. You recognize the song as "Hot Cross Bun s ( ぶん )." You haven't heard this song since grade school. "Hot cross buns, hot cross buns~"
readings.hint: I'm sure many of you were forced to play "Hot Cross Buns" on the recorder in school at some point. Picture yourself playing this song and remember the melody when you see this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: If you feed a lot of beans to a geoduck , its head will grow larger. To a geoduck, beans are a brain food, so by eating beans, it can grow an incredibly large head. Geoducks already look strange enough as it is, let alone with engorged heads.
meanings.hint: Imagine, if you will, a geoduck. Now imagine it with a massive head. It looks ridiculous, and more disgusting than ever. It's actually really creeping you out, because the head keeps getting larger as the geoduck eats more beans. Ew.

readings.mnemonic: As the geoduck eats more and more beans its head gets bigger and bigger, until finally the head explodes like an atomi c ( あたま ) bomb. BOOM! Geoduck parts go flying everywhere and pieces of its massive head float to the ground as atomic fallout.
readings.hint: Remember how gross geoducks are? Of course you do. Now imagine one exploding its atomic head right in front of you. You've got geoduck mess all over yourself. It's gross, slimy, sticky, and covering you from head to toe. It's wretched.

meanings.mnemonic: This building has a roof and will teach you about the origin of many things. It's an institution !
meanings.hint: Institutions are places of learning, but they have to be a physical place with a roof and all that. If you're learning out in a field somewhere, that's probably not a proper institution.

readings.mnemonic: What does institution start with? In ( いん )!
readings.hint: Just remember, it's an IN-stitution, and you'll know this reading easy-peasy.

meanings.mnemonic: You can use a long piece of thread during white water rafting as a safety line . Sure it's thin, but if someone goes overboard even a line of thread is better than nothing. Throw them a line and drag them back aboard!
meanings.hint: Imagine you're the person who fell overboard. The white water is rushing violently and it is deathly cold. Feel the water and freezing temperature on your skin. But look! Someone has thrown you a line of thread! You're saved!

readings.mnemonic: The person who threw you that line of thread is actually a cen taur ( せん ). How kind of him. After you are safe you hop on the centaur's back and ride around to get the wind to dry you off.
readings.hint: Coming out of the rushing water, this centaur is like an angel sent from heaven. He's just saved your life. Imagine the happiness and gratitude you feel towards this centaur as you are pulled to safety by that line of thread.

meanings.mnemonic: Let the sun shine into your ear while sitting on a stool and make sure most of it gets in your head. You want to fill your brain with the most sun anyone ever has.
meanings.hint: Most of the sun is getting into your ear, but not all of it. Darn, maybe you should try angling yourself a different way.

readings.mnemonic: Most of this is for your cy borg ( さい ). You're testing out a new way to power it, but if you can't get most of the sun into your ear, it won't be enough to power most of the cyborg's robo-parts.
readings.hint: Install the part into your cyborg. As long as most of the sun gets into its human ear, most of its cyborg bits will have power.

meanings.mnemonic: A gate with a lantern is open for you. That's convenient, since it's pretty dark out and you might not have seen the open gate without the lantern's help.
meanings.hint: Imagine being a traveler, walking around the outside of a huge walled city. You really need to get inside but all of the gates are closed. Finally, you see a single, bright lantern marking the open gate! What a relief.

readings.mnemonic: This open gate is in the water though, so you'll need a kay ak ( かい ) to get in. Luckily, you've been carrying your kayak with you just in case.
readings.hint: Imagine kayaking in the dark toward an open gate. Finally, you've made it!

meanings.mnemonic: Did you know that those strange geoduck creatures were once used to make page s? Okay maybe not, but it makes for a memorable image!
meanings.hint: Imagine the strange, gooey books you'd end up with if you used geoducks for pages. Now imagine flicking through one of those books, and getting the slimy geoduck residue on your hands as you look at the pages.

readings.mnemonic: A page is a page ( ページ ).
readings.hint: Since the reading comes from the English word "page", this one shouldn't be too hard to remember, especially if you've already come across it in katakana.

meanings.mnemonic: If you're stand ing under a tree and you see your parent , it's a good chance for a moment of intimacy .
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself standing in the shade of a beautiful, big, old tree. You see your parent walk past and shout out to them. They come and join you, and it's like the two of you are the only two people in the world right now. That's intimacy.

readings.mnemonic: Standing under the tree, you go to give your parent a big hug, but you manage to bang your shin ( しん )  on a huge tree root. Your parent pulls you in for a warm hug and rubs your shin. Despite the pain, it becomes an unforgettable "shin-timate" moment.
readings.hint: Busting your shins on things really hurts. I'm sure you've hit your shins on coffee tables and the like all your life. Remember that pain and think how much worse it would be banging your bare shins on pointy tree roots just when you were enjoying a moment of parental intimacy. Not enjoyable.

meanings.mnemonic: You have fins in private . But, you want the world to see them, so you take them public .
meanings.hint: Picture yourself in private, with fins. You wish others could see them. "That's it," you decide. "It's time to show the world. I'm going to make my fins public!"

readings.mnemonic: You head out into the public where everyone can see your fins, but... things have changed. All people are the same. There are thousands of こう いち s walking around, and they all stare at you (though they pretend like they aren't).
readings.hint: How long have you been hiding in private with your fins? The public areas have changed, and all the people have become こういち s. One mother こういち --pushing a baby こういち --looks up and stares at your fins. Now you remember why you never go out in public.

meanings.mnemonic: You have to say what you’re sell ing because people don’t like to read . If they walk right by and don’t read, you better say what you have for sale so they’ll know! Read it out loud to them.
meanings.hint: Imagine reading what you have for sale out loud to people walking by because they’re too lazy to read it for themselves.

readings.mnemonic: What are you forced to read aloud to sell? Yo- yo ( よ ) instructions! You’d think they’d be self explanatory, but there are actually a lot of rules and tricks that you have to read to get good at yo-yoing. Read all of these out loud so you can sell them.
readings.hint: Imagine reading through your list of yo-yo tricks. Do you know how to pronounce those weird yo-yo names?

meanings.mnemonic: If you've been walking all day, your foot is the last thing anyone would want to kiss , especially after wandering barefoot down a road . Your feet are filthy, sweaty, and stinky. Nobody is going to want to kiss those feet.
meanings.hint: Imagine kissing someone's foot. Now imagine kissing someone's foot after they've been walking down a road for hours. It's disgusting. Smell the stench of sweat wafting up into your nostrils as the foot approaches your lips. It's wretched.

readings.mnemonic: What was this person walking down again? Oh, that's right – a ro ad ( ろ ). They were walking down a filthy road.
readings.hint: Walking down a road barefoot like this is a sure way to dirty up your feet. Feel the dirt, rocks, and twigs under your feet. The road is hard and dry and is not making your journey very comfortable at all.

meanings.mnemonic: Look at the ground ! You spilled some water drop s, so I request that you wipe them up. I'd do it myself, but you're the one who spilled them, so I'm requesting that you do it. Pretty reasonable request, right?
meanings.hint: It's a simple request, really. Just wipe up those water drops off the ground.

readings.mnemonic: Oh, one more thing. I request that you wipe up the water with this cu cumber ( きゅう ).
readings.hint: "But cucumbers are already mostly water, this isn't going to absorb anything!" you cry. Well, too bad. That's my request, so you better do it. Figure out how to make the cucumber work.

meanings.mnemonic: If your car runs into a cloud you'll revolve around it instead of going straight through. Clouds are really hard to see through, so even though you're trying not to, you revolve in a tight circle up and around the cloud anyway.
meanings.hint: Imagine driving your car around and around the cloud, instead of going through it. At least it's soft and cool.

readings.mnemonic: You have to revolve around the cloud ten ( てん ) times before it disappears and you're able to go through.
readings.hint: Unfortunately, not long after you get through that cloud another one comes down and gets in your way. Now you're counting each revolution, one... two... three... all the way up to ten.

meanings.mnemonic: The tree marked with a yellow X on the side of the house is going to get cut down. You’ve marked it with a yellow X so the hired help knows which tree to cut down. There are a lot of trees on the side of the house, but the tree marked with a yellow X is the only one in danger of causing damage to your roof in the event of a storm.
meanings.hint: Picture the scene of a woman with a chainsaw cutting down this tree marked with a yellow X. Hear the sound of the chainsaw buzzing and the grinding of wood as it meets the side of the trunk. It’s very loud. Woodchips go flying everywhere.

readings.mnemonic: Wait, that woman with the chainsaw is Yoko ( よこ ) Ono! Yes, the same Yoko Ono who’s married to John Lennon of the Beatles. What’s she doing chopping down trees on the side of your house? Who can say. Silly Yoko.
readings.hint: Now picture the same scene as before only imagining Yoko Ono wielding that chainsaw. Maybe she’s dancing. Maybe there’s some Beatles music playing in the background. Either way it’s a strange sight – remember it.

meanings.mnemonic: A sick stick of dynamite is going to be sick forever. Unfortunately there are no medicines that are effective on sticks of dynamite, so once they contract a sickness, they’re sick for good.
meanings.hint: Imagine if you were a sick stick of dynamite. Every time you sneezed you ran the risk of blowing up. It would be a terrifying way to live. Just imagine it.

readings.mnemonic: Being nervous all the time about spontaneously exploding would probably make you sweat profusely. And you know what sweating causes? B.O. ( びょう ). You’d be one smelly stick of dynamite. B.O. is a very unpleasant smell, and being sick is no excuse.
readings.hint: Picture this as your life. You could explode at any moment, you’re sick, and you have B.O. Nobody wants to be around you because you might explode and kill them, and you stink. What a terrible way to live!

meanings.mnemonic: Angle your sword correctly at this cow to solve its riddle. The key is to untie the cow with the sword without hurting it.
meanings.hint: Will you be able to solve this cow riddle? Picture yourself carefully trying to untie a cow with a sword at just the right angle. What a tricky thing to solve.

readings.mnemonic: Did I mention that you have to solve this whole riddle while on a kay ak ( かい )? No?! Whoops.
readings.hint: So you're on a kayak, with a cow, and you have to find just the right angle with your sword in order to untie the cow and solve this riddle. A bit much to wrap one's head around. Anyway, good luck with that.

meanings.mnemonic: Run into oneself in a dream and you'll definitely wake up .
meanings.hint: If you run into yourself, it really messes you up, you know? You won't be able to handle what you're seeing so you have to wake up.

readings.mnemonic: If one runs into oneself and you don't wake up , just look down at your waist. If you're wearing an o bi ( お ) you know it's a dream and you'll eventually wake up. If you aren't wearing an obi then it isn't a dream and you don't have anything to wake up from.
readings.hint: Your obi is like a special object you only have in dreams. If you have your obi, it's okay, it's a dream and you can wake up now! Make sure you memorize exactly how it feels so dream-you can't trick you or something.

meanings.mnemonic: If a lip ring (or two in this case) is what you lack , it's going to be way easier for you to sing a song .
meanings.hint: Imagine singing a song with a couple lip rings in your mouth. The articulation will be all wrong! It's definitely better to lack lip rings if you're going to sing.

readings.mnemonic: Where are you always singing song s, by the way? In your ca r ( か ). It's not just you, either. Pretty much everyone likes to sing in their car.
readings.hint: There's something about your car that makes your singing sound so much better than usual, you know?

meanings.mnemonic:
This kanji is the same as the radical — straight as straight can be.

It can also mean direct , because when you go straight to a place, you're going there directly. Depending on the context it can even mean fix , since fixing something means straightening out all the messed up crooked bits.
meanings.hint: Here's some straight talk for ya: if you study your radicals, you'll have no problem with this one.

readings.mnemonic: Put food straight into your esophagus without chewing and you will choke ( ちょく ). You want to get straight to the most important part of eating — the digesting part — but if you don't follow all the intermediate steps, you'll end up choking.
readings.hint: Imagine shoving food straight down your esophagus and choking as you do. Why would you do this to yourself?!

meanings.mnemonic: When a drunkard holds a sword it becomes something else. The drunkard doesn't become a swordsman, they're still just a drunkard. But the sword in their hands does.
meanings.hint: Picture the drunkard standing in front of you. He picked up the sword and is holding it above his head. It's becoming... becoming...

readings.mnemonic: The sword has become a sa ber ( せい )! It's just like the saber you have! Hey, wait.
readings.hint: If a sword becomes a saber in the hands of a drunkard... then what are you? Geez this became pretty deep.

meanings.mnemonic: The mountain on top of the cliff is dry from the coast below. Just from looking at the raging shore below, you'd think everything would be wet, it's splashing everywhere!
meanings.hint: You're on top of the mountain and the entire coast is stretching out below. The shore goes as far as your eyes can see.

readings.mnemonic: Sitting up there, watching the coast with you, is Gan dhi ( がん ). The world famous Gandhi, is just hanging out up here with you, looking down at what you now realize must be a coast in India.
readings.hint: Sit down next to Gandhi and close your eyes. You can hear the water hitting the shore below. The coastal birds are screaming. You crack open an eye and see Gandhi still sitting perfectly still.

meanings.mnemonic: Using your construction power you build something great. A true achievement built for the achievement of making kanji learning easier!
meanings.hint: Just constructing this thing was an achievement. Feel proud like it was, well, an achievement.

readings.mnemonic: This achievement was made possible by こう いち , so of course this construction project is a giant statue of the man himself. What an achievement of a statue. What an achievement of an achievement.
readings.hint: Thanks こういち . Now everyone will know of your achievements around kanji learning and inside-out earth theory.

meanings.mnemonic: Stand on your older brother . Now stand on your older brother again. You wish to stand on, and above, your older brother. You compete over and over again, to stand atop your older brother.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself standing on your older brother. They push you off. So you do it again. You want to be on top, but your older brother doesn't want you to. You compete for the top spot over and over again, throughout your whole life, because that's what one does with their older brother.

readings.mnemonic: You compete with your older brother because only one of you may go to きょう と . Whoever stands on top the most gets to go to きょうと , so you must compete for a spot.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself competing to stand on top of your older brother. Do you win? Do you get the one ticket to きょうと ?

meanings.mnemonic: In prison , Wolverine gets picked on for the barb s coming out of his hands. This creates a lot of conflict between him and the other prisoners. They don't take kindly to mutants, and Wolverine doesn't take kindly to bigots — hence the conflict.
meanings.hint: Picture one Wolverine versus, like, a hundred prisoners. Who would win? Yeah, my money's on the mutant in this conflict.

readings.mnemonic: This constant conflict between Wolverine and the other inmates is really affecting his sou l ( そう ). Despite what people think, Wolverine really doesn't like to fight. He just hates injustice so much, he feels he has to do something about it.
readings.hint: Put yourself in Wolverine's shoes. Imagine what it would feel like to have conflicting values, what it would do to your soul. Wanting to be peaceful on the one hand, but on the other not wanting to allow bigotry of any kind. It's a conflict in your soul.

meanings.mnemonic: A leader stands upon the ground of the rice paddy . She is searching for treasure . It is said that this treasure here in this rice field will make things much easier for the leader (that's what leaders really want, after all). If she gets this treasure, it will be a great convenience for her, because convenience is everything when you're the leader.
meanings.hint: Imagine the leader looking around the rice paddy. She keeps saying, "Ugh, it would be convenient if I didn't have to walk around out here looking for the treasure that's going to make things more convenient for me. Ugh." Go ahead and say this phrase out loud, too, pretending to be the leader in the rice paddy. Feel the cold and the wet, making it less convenient for you.

readings.mnemonic: She finds a place to dig in the ground. It looks like there's some fresh dirt here. Finally, the treasure of convenience that will make this leader's life so much easier. She digs into the ground and pulls out a box. When she opens it, she finds out it's a ben to ( べん ). "My god," she thinks. "This really will make my life more convenient. My people can take their lunches with them now instead of coming back to base to eat, which will waste way less time!"
readings.hint: Feel the happiness that the leader would feel at this revelation. It will make everything much more convenient for her, since she's not the one doing all the manual labor. She won't have to eat out of a bento, that's for sure.

meanings.mnemonic: Orders coming from your mouth will determine the fate of your soldiers. You are their commanding officer and your orders determine their life and death.
meanings.hint: Pretend you are a high ranking officer and imagine yourself giving orders to your underlings. Imagine all the power and influence you have. It feels good.

readings.mnemonic: You are the commanding officer on the May flower ( めい ). You give orders to colonize America. The orders spewing from your mouth hole determine the fate of the settlers and the fate of America and the fate of the world! You and the Mayflower have changed everything forever.
readings.hint: Students of American history are very familiar with the Mayflower. Recall learning about it in school whenever you see this kanji. Or look it up on Wikipedia if you don’t know about it. The Mayflower is a big deal!

meanings.mnemonic: By the time you finish digging a coffin hole with nothing but a spoon , you’ll be an old elderly person. Digging with nothing but a spoon takes a long time, especially when the hole needs to be big enough for a coffin. You might as well be digging it for yourself at this rate. By the time you finish, you’ll be all wrinkled and elderly and just about ready to keel over.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself digging a coffin hole with nothing but a spoon. Your hands get all cramped, your wrist starts to hurt, and you get painful blisters on your hands. It takes forever to dig a hole big enough for a coffin!

readings.mnemonic: Now that you’re elderly (and probably about to die) you suddenly crave some delicious roe ( ろう ). Salmon roe, sea urchin roe, tofugu roe, doesn’t matter. Your old, tired bones need roe after all that hard work!
readings.hint: You hated roe when you were still young, but now that you’re an elderly old fart you really want to eat it. Roe is for old people, you guess, as you slurp it up.

meanings.mnemonic: You wear a special outfit and equip a sword whenever you do something for the first time. Doing something for the first time is always scary, but the right outfit and a sword at your side can give you the confidence and courage to overcome your jitters!
meanings.hint: Imagine the butterflies in your stomach as you prepare to do something for the first time. Now look down at your badass outfit and the sword on your belt. Feel the nervousness subside a little. You've got this.

readings.mnemonic: So, what is it you're doing for the first time? A fashion show ( しょ ). Your special outfit and sword aren't just to calm your nerves — they're also what you'll be showing off on the catwalk.
readings.hint: Picture yourself strutting down the runway at this fashion show. You're doing pretty well for your first show — until you trip over your sword and fall flat on your face. Darn it — now everyone's gonna know this was your first time.

meanings.mnemonic: Try to use your fingers to spoon out some of the sun and you'll come back with just one finger .
meanings.hint: Look down at your finger and the burned stumps where the rest of your fingers used to be. It hurts. And you're sad. But at least you still have one finger left.

readings.mnemonic: Don't worry, you aren't the only one out there with just one finger , shee p ( し ) only have one finger too. I mean, they're hooves, which are really just a single animal finger. It's just big and kind of flat.
readings.hint: Picture sitting with a sheep. It's right across from you, staring into your eyes. It reaches out its sheep finger hoof and you reach out with your finger. They touch. It's magical.

meanings.mnemonic: In your mouth there is a jet dropping flavor bombs. Every time you taste something, it's actually because a tiny jet has flown into your mouth to bomb you with flavor.
meanings.hint: What's your favorite food? Imagine that, as you bite into it, tiny jets are released from inside the food to attack your tongue with flavor bombs, stimulating your sense of taste.

readings.mnemonic: And what has more flavor than a big slab of me at ( み )? You love meat and its flavor nearly as much as you love the taste of its juice. So many good things to put in your mouth!
readings.hint: Of course, if you're vegetarian imagine the flavor of the meat being terrible. Up to you. Make the mnemonic work for your lifestyle.
