meanings.mnemonic: On a scooter there is a bear with a drop on its head trying to follow and chase you! You accidentally splashed it with water and it's not happy about it. You're on your own scooter, luckily, but that doesn't make this any less terrifying.
meanings.hint: Picture the bear on its scooter with the offending drop of water on its head following really closely behind your scooter. You can almost feel his bear breath on your neck!

readings.mnemonic: The bear is trying to follow you because it wants your o bi ( お ) as compensation for its injured pride. The obi is waving in the wind behind your scooter, and the bear is reaching out with its teeth to try and grab it.
readings.hint: Picture the bear chasing you on its scooter, trying to reach out and grab the obi off your waist. The drop of water on its head is jiggling from the exertion. Woah, it's getting a little too close!

meanings.mnemonic: Your spirit should feel humble before a god 's spirit . You never really feel humble in front of anyone else, but if you meet a god's spirit… Well, you should probably lower yourself appropriately. Gods have powerful spirits.
meanings.hint: Imagine a spirit appearing before you. It's a god. You should be as humble as possible. Out of respect and also because you don't want to be cursed or something.

readings.mnemonic: When you meet a god 's spirit , you need to get down on your shin s ( しん ). Get down on your ankles? No, not low enough. Knees? No, no, get your shins flat on the ground to show how humble you really are. Luckily, you're wearing jean s ( じん ), so at least that will stop your poor shins getting chafed!
readings.hint: If you don't get down on your shins before god's spirit, it could take them away. Even your jeans couldn't protect you then. You don't want to live a shin-less life, do you?

meanings.mnemonic: A drop of water to a root is good . It's good for the roots to grow and live and thrive. And what's good for roots is good for our plants and us humans too.
meanings.hint: Imagine putting a little drop of water over a root you want to grow. Doing this is good for the plant and also good for your soul.

readings.mnemonic: A good way to get a lot of drops of water for your roots is to buy a row boat ( りょう ). Then just row out to the middle of the lake and plonk all the roots right into the source!
readings.hint: Rowing even feels good on your arms and back. Probably just as good as all these roots will feel.

meanings.mnemonic: A Viking in a position of power does not need to labor in the fields. Labor is for the weak! A Viking of power takes what he wants, and need not labor for it. A powerful Viking plunders all and takes what he wants!
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself as a powerful Viking. You have the power to take anything you want, pillaging and plundering to your heart’s content. Imagine all the things you would acquire with this sort of power.

readings.mnemonic: It takes a lot of labor to harvest roe ( ろう ) so that’s what the Viking decides to take first! Roe is delicious, but it’s small and delicate. Vikings don’t labor for their own roe! They take it and eat it!
readings.hint: Picture this powerful Viking laying siege on a small fishing village and taking all the roe and stuffing it into his bearded face. So much labor went into harvesting that roe! Vikings are so mean!

meanings.mnemonic:
The sound of your heart gives me an idea . The methodic thump thump thumping of a heart puts me in a trance that gives me great ideas. It’s a very inspirational sound, the sound of a heart is. It gives me an idea.

Alternatively, you could look at this kanji and see a "stand" radical and something that kind of (almost) looks like the kanji for "think." When you stand up and think, you come up with your very own idea s!
meanings.hint: Put your hand over your heart, or on your wrist, or somewhere you can feel your pulse. Your heart is pumping blood through your body, hear the sound of your heart beat and try to picture and get an idea for how all the blood flows through your body.

readings.mnemonic: The heart of an ea gle ( い ) is very strong. The person who knew this probably came up with the idea to make the bald eagle a symbol of the United States. Eagles are always flying around and doing things, so their hearts are very strong.
readings.hint: Picture the majestic animal that is the bald eagle. Imagine the sound of an eagle screeching as it zooms through the air, flapping its wings in the wind. It’s pretty impressive.

meanings.mnemonic: There is a woman and a child in front of you. You like them very much.
meanings.hint: Imagine a woman and a child you like very much. Just appreciate them, and think about how much you like those two. The reading mnemonic definitely won't ruin your image of them or anything.

readings.mnemonic: "I like you two," you say to the woman and child. They both turn their heads 180 degrees back towards you. They both have faces that look just like こう いち 's. "We like you too," they say in unison.
readings.hint: And you DO like them. You have always been a fan of こういち 's kanji learning methods, and you feel so lucky to have married a woman who looks just like こういち and then (this is the real miracle!) gave birth to a child that looks like こういち too. Hashtag blessed.

meanings.mnemonic: Your fingers always seem to suit the random things you pick up off the ground. An old thimble. A discarded onion ring. A dead worm you tie in a knot. Your incredible fingers suit them beautifully, almost as if they were jewelry, so of course you pick up all of them.
meanings.hint: Imagine finding something that suits your life and your style. You're not just going to leave it there, right? No, you're going to pick it up! Go on, use your fingers and pick up that thing.

readings.mnemonic: You're walking around looking for something to pick up when you see a sword in a rock. Yes, this will suit you. You pick up the sword and people jump out around you and tell you that you are the new hero ( ひろ )! Uh oh. Maybe you shouldn't have picked this up.
readings.hint: You picked up the hero sword and now you're the new hero. Time to go kill a dragon. Good luck, and try not to die, hero person!

meanings.mnemonic: You've been playing blackjack in the sun since long ago . This is the longest game of blackjack you've ever played and it feels like ages since you started. You've been sitting out in the sun playing blackjack since long, long ago.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself playing a never-ending game of blackjack out in the hot sun. You’re sticky, sweaty, and tired. But all you can do is continue to play your game of blackjack. It’s miserable.

readings.mnemonic: Once you finally finish the game, you walk the long walk back home, in the sun, barefoot, on a dirt road that hasn't been maintained since long ago . Your feet are all sweaty from being out in the sun, and walking on this dirt road gives you muck ashi ( むかし ). Your ashi (feet) are all mucky and muddy from the combination of dirt and sweat. Your muck ashi are really gross, man.
readings.hint: Imagine your muck ashi, all dirty and muddy from walking on that old road from long ago. Feel the muck between the toes of your ashi, squishing with every step. Hear the sound the mud makes as it slides between your sweaty mucky toes.

meanings.mnemonic: There are pieces of thread on each escalator showing you what level they go to in your school. Each escalator takes you to a different grade , so make sure you follow the right thread or you'll end up in a class that's either way above or way below your level.
meanings.hint: When you level up your Japanese skills, you'll take this escalator to rank up too!

readings.mnemonic: You want to see what the highest level is, so you go up the wrong escalator. At the top you find a giant cu cumber ( きゅう ).
readings.hint: A teacher comes over and says, "Excuse me, this is advanced level cucumber studies. I don't think you're at this level yet... please take the escalator down and follow the threads to find your level."

meanings.mnemonic: If you know the radical meaning orders you know the kanji meaning orders as well!
meanings.hint: Here's an order: study your radicals.

readings.mnemonic: Your orders are to get a ray ( れい ) gun.
readings.hint: Imagine an authority figure giving you this order as an official order. Ray guns don't really exist, so you think it's a pretty strange order.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami master has the power to pour water wherever he pleases. Tsunami masters are masters over the most powerful water force in the universe, so it makes sense that they have the ability to pour water at will, wherever they please. Best not tick off the master, unless you want a tsunami poured on your head.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself as this tsunami master. You’re like a water bender from Avatar, only more awesome. Feel the cold wet water pouring out of your fingertips as you create water out of nothing. Imagine what it would be like to have this power.

readings.mnemonic: If Chew bacca ( ちゅう ) was trying to attack you, you’d be able to defend yourself easily. Simply pour a tsunami on him and Chewbacca would surrender in a heartbeat. Chewbacca doesn’t like having water poured on him, especially not all the water from a tsunami.
readings.hint: Imagine Chewbacca soaking wet. All his fur is matted down and he looks pretty depressed. He smells like wet dog too. Smell this smell in your nostrils as you imagine how pathetic this drenched Chewbacca feels.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of your clan wants to live close to the ground so he demands you get low . Low, low, with your belly to the ground. That way you're always below him, since he's the leader.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself, face smooshed into the ground, and you can hear the leader shouting above you, "GET LOW!"

readings.mnemonic: Low people like you can only eat low food like potatoes. But that's boring, so you low people learned to make ta ter ( てい ) tots. You roll them to each other and eat them without lifting your heads.
readings.hint: Get low to the ground right now and ask your mom or dad to roll some tater tots towards your face. Eat them up, mm mm.

meanings.mnemonic: Give trash to the moon and it will nurture and raise that trash like it's its own little moon baby.
meanings.hint: Picture the moon raising this ball of trash, giving it love and attention.

readings.mnemonic: The moon nurture s the trash ball until one day, it speaks! " 行く ( いく ), 行く！ " it says. It wants to go live with mom moon!
readings.hint: Watch as the little trash ball 行く s up into the sky and floats around its moon mama. If you raise your child right it will want to 行く wherever you go too.

meanings.mnemonic: Cut grain s with a knife to make them smaller and you'll make a great profit . By cutting them, now you can get three grains worth of money for what was once a single grain.
meanings.hint: Imagine buying some rice. Now cut all the grains with a knife into smaller grains. Now sell them for two, three, four times the profit!

readings.mnemonic: To make an even better profit , you decide to cut up ree ds ( り ) too. You stuff the grains in the reeds and sell that as "self-cooking" sets.
readings.hint: Picture all of your reeds full of grains in a row. The entire world will benefit from your crafty invention. And you'll turn a great profit too, which certainly doesn't hurt.

meanings.mnemonic: A leader stand s at the highest rank in an organization. The leader is of the highest rank and stands above all others.
meanings.hint: Picture this great leader. Imagine him actually standing on the shoulders of his supporters who are a lower rank than he is. He’s standing atop this great pyramid as a fearless leader.

readings.mnemonic: This great leader is actually the leader of the boy scouts. And as we all know, the highest rank in boy scouts is the rank of ea gle ( い ) scout. This great leader has attained the rank of eagle scout and stands above all other scouts.
readings.hint: Picture the same ranking pyramid from before, only all the members are boy scouts, and the leader at the top is an eagle. Hear the piercing screech of the eagle with your ears.

meanings.mnemonic: A leader who takes the middle of the road on every issue is likely to have an average relationship with everyone. He doesn't really take sides in matters, and just stays in the middle. This makes him seem like a wishy-washy people-pleaser, and as such he's only able to maintain average, mid-tier relationships.
meanings.hint: I'm sure you've heard of flip-flopping in politics, when people try to take both sides of an issue, staying in the middle ground and never taking a stand. Imagine one of these people to represent this kanji.

readings.mnemonic: Imagine a flip-flopping politician you have an average relationship with. Now imagine that there's a knocker ( なか ) on their forehead and you get to knock that knocker as hard as you like. Maybe you can knock some integrity into them and form a better relationship.
readings.hint: Also, this kanji is pronounced exactly like the vocab word 中 ( なか ). If you can remember that, it'll make remembering 仲 easier.

meanings.mnemonic: As the year starts moving in the direction of winter , the trees must release their leaves.
meanings.hint: Picture winter heading straight for you and frosting everything over. Now picture the leaves being released by the trees.

readings.mnemonic: The trees release all of their leaves onto your ho me ( ほう ). Slowly, you watch in horror as your home is consumed by the released leaves. They enter in through the windows, doors, and the chimney.
readings.hint: "Release my home from this leaf prison!" you shout at the now bare trees.

meanings.mnemonic: Grain s are few . Imagine the last few grains trickling down the hourglass. You only have a second left.
meanings.hint: Normally an hourglass has sand to count the seconds, but yours uses grains. Imagine just a few grains left, and watch them trickle down as you count a second, and then another second.

readings.mnemonic: After the final second s go by, your awful B.O. ( びょう ) appears.
readings.hint: Your hourglass timer was a timer to specifically let you know when your B.O. was set to return.

meanings.mnemonic: In your mouth you put a sword and a knife . Now that you have them in there, you have to make them separate from each other.
meanings.hint: Anyone can put a sword and knife in their mouth. But not everyone can make them separate while still in your mouth.

readings.mnemonic: Why are you trying to make a sword and knife separate in your mouth? Because someone bets ( べつ ) you can't do it.
readings.hint: "I bet you can't separate the sword and knife," they say in an annoying voice. Alright, time for you to show them. Nobody bets you anything without you blindly trying to do it!

meanings.mnemonic: This cow temple is special . This cow must have been very important to have an entire temple dedicated to it.
meanings.hint: Picture a temple dedicated to a single cow. All of the people who come and worship here have a special place in their hearts for the cow.

readings.mnemonic: This cow is special because it belonged to Toku gawa ( とく ) Ieyasu, the first shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate and uniter of Japan! He's a super important figure in Japanese history. You should absolutely look him up and read a bit about him (when you're caught up on these lessons, that is).
readings.hint: You walk up to a statue of the special cow in the temple and see a collar around its neck that says "Tokugawa Moo Moo" on it. How... special!

meanings.mnemonic: The card game fish are best at is blackjack because they can easily hold the cards with their fins together . In blackjack you don't have to have a huge hand, full of cards. It's just a few, so fish fins can keep them together and just hit or stay.
meanings.hint: Imagine a fish trying to hold a full hand of cards together. Impossible! Fish don’t have fingers to keep them spread properly. Now imagine a fish trying to hold two cards together and nodding to hit. It's much easier to keep things together this way!

readings.mnemonic: Once a year trillions of fish get together in きょう と to play blackjack. They have a huge tournament there once a year.
readings.hint: Imagine a bunch of fish gathering together in きょうと to participate in this blackjack tournament. With so many fish all together in one place, it probably smells pretty fishy.

meanings.mnemonic: A woman sits on a stool to conserve her power , as her servants toil away in the kitchen. They toil away preparing a feast for this powerful woman while she kicks back on her cozy stool. She's a queen and deserves to be pampered.
meanings.hint: Imagine you're one of this woman's minions, toiling away in the kitchen. The heat from the stoves is intense, and you're hoping she doesn't see the sweat dripping from your forehead into the food. Man, you've never toiled so hard in your life.

readings.mnemonic: As a servant you toil not just for the woman, but also for her do g ( ど ). Anything she gets, the dog gets too. That means double the toil for you. But hey, at least the dog is cute.
readings.hint: Secretly it's this dog you toil for, not the woman. Try to picture what kind of dog it is. And imagine sneaking extra portions to it under the table when the woman isn't looking. Yes, if it weren't for this innocent dog, you'd have done with this life of toil!

meanings.mnemonic: A simple drunkard starting a war .
meanings.hint: Imagine what the simple drunkard does, to start the war. They're not in their right mind, and the centaurs (getting you ready for the reading mnemonic!) are an easily offended people. Go ahead and run through the simple drunkard's actions toward the centaurs that start this war.

readings.mnemonic: The simple drunkard started a war with the cen taurs ( せん )! This is especially unfortunate, because the centaurs are really good at battle s because they're all automatically cavalry troops.
readings.hint: Imagine the war: humans vs centaurs. Tens of thousands clashing in battle.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader has a cloud that he uses to transmit all of the messages he needs to send to his people. The cloud floats above him and when he wants to tell people something he just whispers into the cloud and it transmits his message really, really loudly to all of them.
meanings.hint: Picture this special cloud floating above your leader. It's probably just a speaker attached to a drone covered in cotton balls.

readings.mnemonic: As the leader transmit s his latest series of messages to you and the others, you notice a pretty large den t ( でん ) in it. That dent is probably a clue to what this cloud is really made of.
readings.hint: You highly doubt this is a magical cloud that can transmit messages. You inch closer to the big dent and try to poke it. Your finger goes right in! Hmm, you're still pretty sure there's a trick to this thing.

meanings.mnemonic:
This tsunami is ridden by a sheep in classic Western style . And by Western style I mean the sheep is dressed in a cowboy outfit and has put a saddle atop the tsunami wave to ride it. It's a tsunami rodeo and this crazy sheep is riding that tsunami wave in classic Western style.

This kanji also means ocean , because presumably the people from the West, with their Western style, came from across the ocean. You'll want to be familiar with both meanings for vocab later on.
meanings.hint: Imagine how ridiculous a sheep would look all decked out in Western-style cowboy gear. Now imagine it riding a tsunami wave as it screams, "Baa~ baa~ baa~!"

readings.mnemonic: After a whole day of riding this tsunami, the sheep is hankerin' for some Western-style food, namely yo gurt ( よう ). Yogurt is Western-style food, right? If it helps you can imagine the yogurt is flavored like apple pie, to make it more American and Western.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself eating this Western-style apple pie yogurt. It tastes just like apple pie, only creamier and fruitier. Taste the sweet yogurt in your mouth as you imagine yourself scarfing some down.

meanings.mnemonic: Who will say turkey ? Who? WHO ???
meanings.hint: Say "who" over and over again, demanding someone says "turkey," for some reason.

readings.mnemonic: Then, someone says turkey. " Who said turkey? You? How dare ( だれ ) you."
readings.hint: You're so mad at that person even though you demanded someone say turkey.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami hits your skin and creates smaller wave s. Those hit you too!
meanings.hint: If you’ve ever been to the ocean or a large lake, you know what kind of forces even small waves can hit with. Now imagine the force with which a tsunami wave would strike you. You’d have the wind knocked out of you for sure.

readings.mnemonic: When each wave hits your body, you shout, " Ha ( は )!" It's not like it's on purpose. When the wave crashes into you, the wind gets knocked right out of your mouth, making a HA sound.
readings.hint: The waves are basically punching you in the gut, forcing the air out of your lungs, and making you shout HA!

meanings.mnemonic: Your leader makes you lift heavy loads with all your power , since this is what you do for work . Why do you do this kind of work? Well, you're good at it and the leader makes you do it, I guess.
meanings.hint: Picture this leader asking you (yet again) to work for them. "Can you move those heavy objects with all that power of yours?" Typical leader — as soon as they know you're good at a certain kind of work, they ask you to do it all the time.

readings.mnemonic: Where do you get the power to do all this work ? From dou ghnuts ( どう ). You eat nothing but doughnuts. If you had no doughnuts, you couldn't work. In fact, your leader pays you for your work… in doughnuts.
readings.hint: Picture yourself receiving a doughnut as payment after you've been working. It's delicious. Doughnuts are fantastic. Taste the fresh doughnut in your mouth as it crumbles and melts on your tongue. It's the best doughnut you've ever had.

meanings.mnemonic: Imagining one’s self without a heart can make it hard to draw breath . Without a heart, you’d have no blood pumping and you would start to die. This would make it increasingly harder to draw breath and get oxygen to stay alive.
meanings.hint: Imagine trying to live without a heart. You couldn’t. Your blood stops flowing and your body shuts down. You would no longer be able to draw breath.

readings.mnemonic: You know how when people are unconscious and their breath slows or comes to a stop, they use smelling salts to revive them? Well a smelly sock ( そく ) can work just as well. Socks get really stinky and their stench is powerful enough to revive someone who isn’t drawing breath.
readings.hint: Imagine smelling this smelly sock. Imagine the smelly stench of the dirty sock wafting up into your nostrils and filling your lungs with its odor. Gross.

meanings.mnemonic: In Asia they assume everyone's heart is automatically bad and evil .
meanings.hint: You don't want a bunch of evil in your heart, do you? No, I didn't think so.

readings.mnemonic: Luckily, people in Asia know how to get the evil bad ness out of your heart with acu puncture ( あく ).
readings.hint: Acupuncture was originally invented in Asia a long, long time ago, to stop the bad chi from going through your heart.

meanings.mnemonic: You stand at a podium early in the morning to practice reading the chapter from your latest book.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself, standing tall in the light of the early morning. You hold your head up high, open to your bookmark, and read the name of the chapter aloud: "Chapter 42: The Most Important Number."

readings.mnemonic: You're practicing this specific chapter because you've been asked to read it for the Shou gun ( しょう ). There will be a bunch of other authors reading chapters of their books to him too, and you want to make a good impression.
readings.hint: Picture yourself in a line with other authors, standing in the early morning, waiting to read chapters from your respective books. You really hope the Shougun likes yours. If he does, you'll be famous! If he doesn't, you might be beheaded.

meanings.mnemonic: The roof , even though it's almost spring , is still covered in ice so it's cold . Inside the house is cold, outside the house is cold. Everything is cold!
meanings.hint: Picture the entire roof covered in ice. It's supposed to be spring right now, but it's too cold for the ice to melt off your roof.

readings.mnemonic: You can't stand the cold anymore, so you hire a samu rai ( さむ ) to come try to hack the ice off of your roof with his sword. You stand outside shivering, and watching as the samurai tries to stab the ice off of your roof.
readings.hint: The samurai isn't doing a good job stopping the cold, and soon he and his sword have frozen into your ice roof. So now it's cold and you have a dead samurai stuck to your house. Great.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a tent of beans . You try to climb it.
meanings.hint: Imagine this tent of beans. Make it huge, bulging with beans! Now, get climbing. Feel the beans on the other side of the tent's wall.

readings.mnemonic: You climb the bean tent, and from this vantage point you can see とう きょう in the distance.
readings.hint: It's beautiful! It's breathtaking! All your climbing makes this view of とうきょう even more worthwhile!

meanings.mnemonic: If a tsunami is higher than your forehead you better move those legs and find a tree to climb, because that thing is deep !
meanings.hint: Picture this tsunami rushing past the tree you managed to climb up. It's a deep one, way higher than your forehead, but you're safe here. Legs exhausted, sure, but safe and sound.

readings.mnemonic: You wait until the tsunami is only as deep as your shin ( しん ). Then you climb down the tree and wade home in the shin-deep water.
readings.hint: Imagine all the debris floating in the shin-deep water now, bumping up against your shins. Probably hurts, considering how sensitive shins are.

meanings.mnemonic:
A leader who hires his own personal clown will likely double his entertainment. Clowns are funny and make people laugh, so the leader decided to hire one. Since that time, the leader's amount of mirth has doubled.

This also means times , which makes sense when you think about the kind of math you'll need to double something. What is A * 2? It's A doubled, first of all, but you're using multiplication. You're "timesing" something.
meanings.hint: Picture a clown and all the ridiculous funny things they do. Imagine the clown throwing a pie at someone's face and how funny that is. Chuckle to yourself and remember this kanji.

readings.mnemonic: When the clown stops being funny, it's time to say bye ( ばい ). At first, the clown double d the leader's entertainment, but now the clown is just annoying. It's time to say bye-bye.
readings.hint: This clown has served its purpose. Wave your hand in the air and say bye-bye to this worthless clown.

meanings.mnemonic: The village beforehand (what the village was before it was a village) was a field .
meanings.hint: It makes sense for a big open field to have been what was there before the village was made.

readings.mnemonic: On this field , the Ya nkees ( や ) used to play.
readings.hint: It was sad that they had to destroy the natural habitat of the Yankees (a field) to put up the village.

meanings.mnemonic: Look at a building and compare the white parts. Each of those white parts is a floor . Multi- story buildings have tons of different floors that you can see from the outside.
meanings.hint: All buildings have white marks that you can compare with the rest to see where the floors are.

readings.mnemonic: One floor is just all kay aks ( かい ). You're looking at this building, and every other story is normal, but that one floor is kayak after kayak after kayak.
readings.hint: Kayaks working at desks. Kayaks that are desks. Kayaks walking around checking on other kayaks. The kayak floor is quite odd. It's odd there is a floor only about kayaks.

meanings.mnemonic: Eat ing the roof is a bear , because the roof is a public building . Because it is a public building bears are allowed to eat the roof.
meanings.hint: Picture a public building, like a museum or a library, and look at the roof. There's a bear eating the roof!

readings.mnemonic: Public building s must make their roofs available for eating because of a law passed by Genghis Khan ( かん ).
readings.hint: Imagine Genghis Khan signing something and giving a speech talking about how important it is that all bears are able to eat the roofs of public buildings, as a bear in the background eats the roof.

meanings.mnemonic: I always say the fruit section at the grocery should be at the front of the store. It’s the perfect place for the fruit section, I say.
meanings.hint: Think about it. Every grocery store has the fruit section right at the front, wouldn’t you say?

readings.mnemonic: To get to the fruit section , you have to go to the store. To get to the store you have to drive a ca r ( か ). Driving a car is the fastest way to get to the fruit section at the store.
readings.hint: Picture yourself driving a car and salivating over the fruit waiting for you in the fruit section. Imagine your favorite fruit as you put the pedal to the metal in your awesome car.

meanings.mnemonic: You see a tree with a helicopter and a jackhammer tied to it. You look closer, because this is an odd sight, and realize that there is a sign here too. It's telling you to beware of helicopters and jackhammers in these woods. It's a poorly made signpost !
meanings.hint: This is not the best signpost you've ever seen, but it's better than nothing at all, you suppose.

readings.mnemonic: As you look at this weird signpost you notice a button. You press it because it will supposedly tell you more about these dangerous helicopters and jackhammers. Instead, it just yells out in a loud obnoxious voice: HEYO ( ひょう )!!
readings.hint: Press it a few more times and hear the sound in your head. Also spell it out in kana as you hear it as a nice little additional visual.

meanings.mnemonic: The samurai radical and the samurai kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: The most famous samurai in Japanese history was the illustrious shee p ( し ) samurai. He wasn't JUST a sheep who learned the ways of the sword, he would use the wool from the sheep enemies he killed to create his armor!
readings.hint: Picture the sheep samurai. Maybe draw him on a piece of paper. He's a sheep wearing wool armor, and he has ram horns on the hilt of his katana.

meanings.mnemonic: Gold , at its root , is made up of silver . Using silver as the root of a coin is a great way to save on gold! That's why, often, if you just scratch gold, you'll find there's silver hidden underneath, at its root.
meanings.hint: Imagine taking a gold coin and scrubbing it with some soap and water. As you scrub, you slowly see that it's really silver underneath. Dang it, silver again!

readings.mnemonic: If you want to hunt for some silver for yourself, all you need is a guin ea ( ぎん ) pig. Guinea pigs can smell silver, so get a couple and set them loose in the woods and they'll lead you straight to it.
readings.hint: Guinea pigs are to silver as normal pigs are to truffles.

meanings.mnemonic: In a private place on the moon , you hide two spoon s that give you a special ability . It's the ability to predict the future! The private moon spoons reflect the knowledge of time and space into your brain. Now that's a cool ability!
meanings.hint: I wish I had this kind of ability... maybe I'll go up to the moon and try to steal your spoons.

readings.mnemonic: This ability to predict the future makes you famous and now everyone knows your name: No stradamus ( のう )! And now that people know about your name and special ability, everyone will love you!
readings.hint: Oh, you thought I was talking about that old Nostradamus, who also had the ability to predict things? Nah, you're way cooler than him.

meanings.mnemonic: A horse and a shrimp are sitting next to each other. They are both at the train station , waiting for a train to come and take them home.
meanings.hint: Imagine the horse and the shrimp sitting next to each other on a small bench. There is nobody else at the train station. It is awkward.

readings.mnemonic: The train pulls into the station . They walk up to the train doors, but they don't open. "Perhaps a key ( えき ) would help?" asks the station master, as he goes over to open the door to the train.
readings.hint: Instead of saying "a key" he says "eh key." How strange. Imagine the station master saying it like this with his weird accent. You ask him if he has "a key" and he replies with, "What is a key?" Then you say "eh key." He totally understands you now.

meanings.mnemonic: If you use the sun , you can find the correct geoduck . That's the topic of today's lecture.
meanings.hint: Picture a teacher pointing to a chalkboard with a big pointer, and it says "TOPIC."

readings.mnemonic: Our topic is actually mostly about using the sun to see different colored dye s ( だい ) in the water.
readings.hint: You use the dye in the correct sunlight to find the geoducks hiding under the sand. And once we master this topic, we'll move onto actually using dye to find them in the wild.

meanings.mnemonic: For your car you use a hat , some ground and a bookshelf to build a new wheel . I mean, it's generally ring and loop shaped, it just needs to be a circle , so it should work, right?
meanings.hint: Imagine this wheel you made out of a hat, some ground (presumably to keep it all together), and a bookshelf. You install it on your car and hope for the best. This is why you should buy wheels from professional wheel makers.

readings.mnemonic: Your wheel is... generally rin g ( りん ) shaped, so that's good.
readings.hint: A ring-shaped wheel is better than nothing. Hopefully it will get you where you need to go.

meanings.mnemonic: Rain makes up one part of this, and the rest is air or something. I imagine this is what people thought the atmosphere was made out of thousands of years ago.
meanings.hint: Imagine looking up into the sky, trying to figure out what the atmosphere up there is made out of. Your only lead: It's raining, a lot. So, the atmosphere must be partly made of rain, and it's just falling down.

readings.mnemonic: Turns out, studying the atmosphere is fun ( ふん )!
readings.hint: The reading for this kanji is actually the same as one of 分 's on'yomi readings ( ふん ). One trick about complicated kanji is they often take the reading of one of the pieces they're made from. In this case it's 分 . How fun.

meanings.mnemonic: You put flowers on a cloud and call it art . Not everyone agrees that your flower cloud art is actually art, but you know it is.
meanings.hint: Imagine piling up flowers on top of clouds to make your own unique art. It’s a fiddly business, because they keep falling through the clouds, but you get there in the end. Now step back and marvel at your work of flower cloud art.

readings.mnemonic: Your beautiful art is going to be displayed at a gay ( げい ) club. Imagine the pride you feel about having your art on display.
readings.hint: Picture the gay club with your art displayed behind the bar. Fame and fortune are just around the corner, now.

meanings.mnemonic: If you have a mustache and you hit your forehead on the moon , you’re probably going to break a bone . Ok, so it doesn’t matter so much if you have a mustache, but if you’re travelling fast enough to make it to the moon, you’re definitely breaking a bone if you run into it.
meanings.hint: Imagine the force and mass of the moon hitting you right in the boney part of your forehead. Whomp! It sure would hurt.

readings.mnemonic: Outer space is cold, and if you're traveling to the moon, you're going to need a lot of coats ( こつ ). All of these coats will keep you warm, even if you do end up breaking your forehead bone .
readings.hint: Again, feel your bones crack as you smash into the moon. At least you're warm though. Feel the cozy coats you're wearing and snuggle up in their warmth.

meanings.mnemonic: The bundle radical and the bundle kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: You should always bundle your sock s ( そく ). If you don't, there is a 100% chance you'll lose all of the left ones.
readings.hint: Imagine doing laundry and making sure to bundle your socks together so none of them will be lost.

meanings.mnemonic: The circumference radical and the circumference kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: Practice how to measure the circumference of an item by using your shoe ( しゅう ). Your shoe is kind of round, and it's free. I mean it's right there on your foot.
readings.hint: Try to find the circumference of your shoe and then email us with the measurement. I promise you'll never forget this kanji if you actually do it.

meanings.mnemonic: The gate to talent is now closed . In fact, the gate to talent is always closed. It's not like anyone can just waltz in and get themselves some talent. You have to work for it! You have to work hard to get through that closed gate to talent!
meanings.hint: After your hard work, hear the gate close behind you with a creak. You're on the inside now, you're one of the talented ones. And your talents have changed you, made you different from the others — much like the talent radical in this kanji is a little different from its usual shape!

readings.mnemonic: Outside the talent gate that's always closed , hay ( へい ) is piled up to make beds for the people who are still trying to get in. Who knows how long they'll be there outside the closed gate, sleeping in the hay, but they're all working hard to become talented.
readings.hint: Picture a bunch of people outside the closed gates, sleeping in piles of hay. You were there too once. You know how itchy that hay is, and how cold it can be at night. But it was all worth it.

meanings.mnemonic: Next to the cross we combine our power power power so that we have cooperation . And with our powers combined, we become stronger than we ever could have been apart, lifting the cross above our heads!
meanings.hint: The power of cooperation is a *ahem* powerful thing.

readings.mnemonic: Nowhere is the power of cooperation more apparent than in きょう と . When invaders are attacking, when the townspeople want to rebel, when there is a kitty up a tree, the people combine their power from all reaches of Japan and cross into きょうと to put a stop to the madness through cooperation.
readings.hint: Imagine being a citizen of きょうと . Watch as the powers from your neighboring prefectures march across the border to come cooperate together to stop the bandits that raided your farm. You admire their cooperation. You admire きょうと !

meanings.mnemonic: When climbing a tree in public , it’s best to choose a pine . Pine trees offer the most coverage, so you’ll be able to hide, even out in public.
meanings.hint: Imagine climbing up a pine tree. The pine needles are prickly, so your hands get pricked as you climb. Smell the pine tree smell and feel the pine sap on your hands.

readings.mnemonic: In case you fall, someone has laid mats ( まつ ) out around the base of the pine tree. That way, you’ll land on the mats should something unfortunate happen.
readings.hint: Picture yourself falling out of this pine tree and onto the mats. The mats are nice and soft so it’s nice. Hear the whoosh of air as you collapse onto the mats.

meanings.mnemonic: Yakuza bars barely exist. The remainder of them aren't even in Japan.
meanings.hint: Imagine the yakuza bars all being shut down. There's only one place where they can remain.

readings.mnemonic: The remainder of the yakuza bars aren't in Japan. Strangely, they are all in Zan zibar ( ざん ).
readings.hint: Imagine a yakuza bar in Zanzibar. What a strange place to hold out.

meanings.mnemonic: The original geoduck had a request . He wants there to be more geoducks in the world!
meanings.hint: Remember, this is the OG = original geoduck. There are no other geoducks yet.

readings.mnemonic: The only person that would even hear the geoduck's request was Gan dhi ( がん ). Gandhi closed his eyes, sat down, and spread the geoduck's request to the rest of the world.
readings.hint: People listened when Gandhi said, "My friends. We must stop trying to eat the geoduck, and instead allow it to breed. Join me in this fast!"

meanings.mnemonic: A grain 's child will grow with the seasons . In spring the grain is born, it grows in summer, is harvested in fall, and dies in winter.
meanings.hint: Watch the grain children live their entire lives throughout the seasons. Though there are 72 seasons in Japan, so the life of a grain is actually really long there.

readings.mnemonic: All of the seasons have their own special key ( き ). As they change, that season's key appears and unlocks the next step in the grain's life.
readings.hint: Picture these seasonal keys floating through the sky, unlocking the weather changes, the growth of new grains, and the aging of us all.

meanings.mnemonic: If a tree wants to kiss you, it's a sign of your status . Only someone of high status would be worthy enough to receive the gnarly but gentle kiss of a tree. Trees don't kiss just anyone, you know.
meanings.hint: Imagine kissing a tree. Better yet, go kiss a tree. If you're lucky, it'll give you a little taste of sap — an even surer sign of your status.

readings.mnemonic: The best way to convince a tree of your status is to say "cock-a-doodle-doo" at it, like a cock ( かく ), i.e. a male chicken. Trees really place a lot of status on the ability to sound like a cock crowing.
readings.hint: Probably because a cock crowing means the sun's about to come up. Morning light is basically tree breakfast, after all, and they say the way to a tree's heart is through its chloroplasts. So picture yourself as a cock, cock-a-doodle-doing to a tree about your status.

meanings.mnemonic: The best kind of flowers to grow in your window ? Strawberry , obviously. Yes, there are strawberry flowers — it's actually the flowers that turn into the strawberries, you know. And they're perfect as window flowers because they need a ton of light. That's why all your window flowers are strawberries.
meanings.hint: You don't waste time with non-edible flowers. Only strawberries for your windows. Now imagine that fresh, sweet strawberry smell that would drift in on the breeze every time you opened your windows. Nice, right?

readings.mnemonic: Believe it or not, your strawberry is the only cure for an itchy Go dzilla ( いちご ).
readings.hint: Imagine Godzilla, covered in an itchy rash, squishing one of your strawberries and rubbing it all over his body to calm his itchy skin.

meanings.mnemonic: The origin of the geoduck is a pretty stubborn one. The geoduck race is so stubborn that it refused to evolve into an animal that made any sense. Hence, the origin of the geoduck is one of stubbornness.
meanings.hint: You know what a geoduck looks like, right? Does that look like a creature that was looking to change and evolve and be awesome? No, geoducks are weird and stubborn. Imagine how stubborn you’d have to be to not want to evolve into a more attractive animal.

readings.mnemonic: You know who else was stubborn ? Gan dhi ( がん ). Gandhi believed in non-violent resistance and he was pretty stubborn about it.
readings.hint: Gandhi’s stubbornness, however, was pretty effective. Everyone knows Gandhi. Remember Gandhi and his famous stubborn attitude whenever you see this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: The mouth of a well will surround you if you fall into it. The mouth of a well is the opening of the well, and if you fall into that well, the mouth as well as the rest of the well will surround you.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself surrounded by this well on all sides. You look up and you can barely see the mouth of the well where you fell in and now you are trapped. Imagine how scared you are. Imagine trying to claw your way up the sides of the well but all you do is break your nails on the walls.

readings.mnemonic: The well walls surround you as you look up to the mouth once again. This time you see an ea gle ( い ) flying overhead. You call out to the eagle to save you and the eagle comes swooping down into the well and flies you out to safety. What a nice eagle!
readings.hint: This eagle is basically a superhero. Imagine an eagle dressed in a superman outfit. He’s your savior. Imagine your gratitude towards this eagle superhero.

meanings.mnemonic:
A white guy jumps into a pool with a target painted on the bottom. He's aiming for the target, of course.

This kanji can also mean -al , as in the endings you get on some English adjectives. You know, like the pool is artifici-al. Or painting a target down there is not practic-al. That kind of -al.
meanings.hint: Picture this pasty white dude jumping into the target-bottomed pool. The sun glints off of his unnaturally white skin and stings your eyes.

readings.mnemonic: This white dude is a huge techie ( てき ) so he has electronic enhancements that make sure he hits the target . Being a techie has its advantages.
readings.hint: He hits the water right on target and the water splashes onto you. Since he was a techie, he was able to perform the perfect dive. And now you’re all wet.

meanings.mnemonic: If you live in the now , there’s a good chance your heart controls your thought process. You don’t have time to think with your brain, you need an answer now so you use your heart to make decisions and thought process.
meanings.hint: Think about the last time you thought it was right to make a decision with your heart. What was it? Use these emotions to remember this kanji.

readings.mnemonic: You're living in the now and letting your heart control all your thought s. Why are you following your heart? You found you only have one 年 ( ねん ) to live, so you have to live for the now.
readings.hint: Think of all the things you'd do with your life if you only had one 年 left. Maybe you should go do some of them anyways, right now?

meanings.mnemonic: The well radical and the well kanji are the same.
meanings.hint: Make sure you remember that this is a well of water not a "well, actually."

readings.mnemonic: Each time you go to the town well , there's a giant ea gle ( い ) hiding inside, just waiting for you to show up.
readings.hint: You're absolutely terrified of this giant eagle hiding in the well. You're not even sure what its goal is, maybe it's just out to scare you... Either way, imagine yourself being absolutely terrified of this eagle every time you have to go fetch water from the well.

meanings.mnemonic: There is a treasure on top of a narwhal who wears a towel that will grant your greatest wish .
meanings.hint: Picture this narwhal: above its long horn there is a huge, shiny treasure floating. It pulls the towel tight around itself, "Brururu you found me I see!" it whistles at you.

readings.mnemonic: In order to open the treasure and get your wish , you need the key ( き ). The narwhal knows where it's hidden, so you'll have to convince it to tell you where the key is.
readings.hint: If you find the key, what will you wish for? Think about your single, greatest wish. Close your eyes and picture it as clearly as you can and you'll find the key.

meanings.mnemonic: One animal that is particularly good is the wolf . Wolves have a bad reputation, but actually they're very, very good animals.
meanings.hint: When wolves are reintroduced to an ecosystem, good things happen. Overpopulation in deer and other animals decline, plants grow and create more habitats, and the balance of wildlife improves. It all starts with wolves, who are good dogs.

readings.mnemonic: A wolf might be good, but if you run into one, all you can do is scream Oh 神 !” ( おおかみ ).
readings.hint: "Oh 神 ," as in, "Oh god," because you're about to be eaten by a good wolf. You're part of the ecosystem now.

meanings.mnemonic: The thread you weave together to tell others about oneself is the account of your life. It's your very own narrative , tying everything you've been through together.
meanings.hint: Picture this account of your life, the thread of the narrative linking all of the different important points in your life together.

readings.mnemonic: You take this account and lock it with a special key ( き ). You only give this key to people you really trust, so they can read the account of your life and truly understand you.
readings.hint: Picture the people in your life that you'd give this special key to. They'd get to read the account of your life, so make sure they're trustworthy before you hand that key over.

meanings.mnemonic: You run into someone's forehead with your car . The big forehead you hit is Kim Jong Un's. You know that now you're going to have the whole North Korean army coming after you.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself running away from the North Korean army. You have to get to the border, the entire army is coming after you for hitting their dear leader!

readings.mnemonic: The army is after you. Each army soldier has a gun ( ぐん ) and they want to use these guns of theirs to shoot you dead! You killed their leader with a car and now they’re gonna get you back with a gun!
readings.hint: Picture yourself running away from them as they shout and shoot at you. Hear the bullets wizzing past your head as you run away as fast as you can.

meanings.mnemonic: Grain s look like they're on fire in autumn . They all turn red and orange and brown, the colors of fire. It's also fun to set grains on fire in Autumn, since it's getting colder.
meanings.hint: Picture all the grains in autumn. Picture all of the colors. The colors of fire.

readings.mnemonic: As the grains burn, and the season changes to autumn, imagine them yelling out in pain: " ACK ( あき )!"
readings.hint: Ah yes, the sound of the seasons changing to autumn.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of the yakuza shows you his knife . He says, "This is an example of a knife."
meanings.hint: The leader of the yakuza is just showing you an example of a knife. Maybe to scare you? Why would an example of what a knife is scare you, though? Coming from the leader of the yakuza, this is a weird tactic.

readings.mnemonic: "This is an example ," the leader of the yakuza tells you. "Next time, I'll show you an example of a ray ( れい ) gun."
readings.hint: Why is the leader of the yakuza showing you examples of weapons, not to mention weapons that aren't real, like ray guns?

meanings.mnemonic: If your fingers are strong enough, you can take an axe and fold it in half. But be careful not to bend it too far, or you might just break it!
meanings.hint: Imagine holding an axe between your fingers and folding it like a pretzel. Or some silly putty. Or a really soft axe.

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, as you fold these axes in half you realize they come in sets ( せつ ), so there are twice the axes you thought there would be. To compensate, you start folding them in sets too.
readings.hint: How many axes can you bend at once? A set of two? What about a set of four? Or a set of forty-two?

meanings.mnemonic: Everyone has a soul in their life , and that soul shapes their gender . It's not just about body parts — it's about what's inside. In the end, it's one's nature that truly matters.
meanings.hint: Picture your own soul and your own life. Listen to what your soul says about your gender and nature — because your life is yours to live, and only your soul can define who you truly are!

readings.mnemonic: Once you've decided what your gender is, someone comes to your house and lays a sa ber ( せい ) on your shoulders. "This is now your nature ," they say, lifting the saber. It's official! It's in all the newspapers, there's a parade, and everyone pats you on the back. Thanks, gender saber!
readings.hint: The next time you get one of those surveys where you have to choose your gender, just think back to that time in your life when a random official came and certified your gender with their saber. Ahh, what lovely memories.

meanings.mnemonic: Crab s in the dirt make a great foundation for a seaside hut. It's right on the seashore, where all the crabs dig their holes in the dirt. But as they get old and die, their bodies form a strong base. Perfect for the foundation of a building like this!
meanings.hint: Do you live on the beach? Maybe the foundation of your house was made from crabs in dirt too. Why don't you go outside and check?

readings.mnemonic: Remember, a good foundation is the key ( き ) to a good house. No, literally. You have to use a piece of the foundation to make the key to your house.
readings.hint: Keys to houses are always made out of a piece of the foundation it was built upon. How else would they be unique from one another?

meanings.mnemonic: A cleat -wearing woman is walking on someone's back, but she doesn't want to hurt him, so she has to be very gentle . Cleats are sharp and pointy, and not designed for back-walking, so she's being extra gentle right now.
meanings.hint: Picture a woman walking gently over someone's back. She's hardly moving.

readings.mnemonic: Actually, the person she's being gentle with is her da d ( だ ). No one would ever want to hurt their dad, especially if he's asked you to walk gently on his back as a way of giving him a massage.
readings.hint: Poor old dad. His back hurts so his daughter has to walk on it with cleats to make it feel better. But if she isn't gentle enough, she'll just make it worse.

meanings.mnemonic: You give a lid to a person (two of them, in fact), then they put it on a cross when you graduate . It's a symbolic ceremony, putting a lid over a cross, but it's just what you do when you graduate around these parts.
meanings.hint: Try to picture the lid you give to the person(s), then their putting it on a cross. Why does this ceremony symbolize graduation? Maybe the persons are your teachers, and it's about the divine knowledge you've now finished collecting under your "lid," or something. Congrats?

readings.mnemonic: Uh oh, looks like you forgot your lid. They decide not to let you graduate . So two ( そつ ) more years now until you can graduate, it seems. So two more years?! Just for forgetting a lid? Nonsense!
readings.hint: Imagine walking up to the stage with no lid, and the person(s) just say, "Sorry, you can't graduate. So two more years. Next!"

meanings.mnemonic: A mouth that is old is going to be hard .
meanings.hint: Go ahead and touch that old man's mouth. It's super hard feeling when you touch it.

readings.mnemonic: You touch the old man's hard mouth and it crumbles away, revealing the face of a 子 ( こ ) inside.
readings.hint: "You pressed the hard skin holding me captive! Thank you!" the 子 says, as they break free from their hard mouth prison.

meanings.mnemonic: Causing death at every turn, the moon king sits inside his moon, getting ready to cause death on anything in his sights. Sounds a lot like the Death Star from a certain movie. One of which is "Star Wars: A New Hope ."
meanings.hint: Now picture the death moon king as the Emperor, sitting in his Death Star. There is no hope, except for that little opening you can shoot through to destroy the whole thing. That's hope, I guess!

readings.mnemonic: The name A New Hope , as we all know, comes from the ending where the moon king's son sends a bow ling ( ぼう ) ball into that little opening and knocks it out of the sky.
readings.hint: In the end, the real hope was bowling all along. I hope you've learned a valuable lesson from this space movie.

meanings.mnemonic: The roof that became the origin of all roofs was perfect . It was tall and wide so it could fit and protect everyone. And the dimensions were perfect too.
meanings.hint: Picture this absolutely perfect roof. They really got it right on their first try.

readings.mnemonic: This perfect roof was commissioned by Genghis Khan ( かん ). We got all of the carpenters and home makers and construction workers he'd conquered together and tasked them with making the first roof, but only if they made it absolutely perfect.
readings.hint: Genghis Khan was a perfectionist, and by conquering and bringing all of these different people together, he was able to create something perfect by all standards.

meanings.mnemonic: You try to be private , but because of your big hair you end up having to participate in everything. Can you imagine people asking for volunteers or something, and you trying to look inconspicuous with your big hair? Of course they're going to notice you and make you participate.
meanings.hint: When you're thinking about your next hair style, if you don't like participating in things, you should think about something you can actually keep more private.

readings.mnemonic: You know who has big hair and loves to participate in everything? San ta-san ( さん )! Well, his hair is more big on the bottom (it's a beard) than whatever you have on your head, but hair is hair. And he participates in present-giving in every house in the world! See? Big hair = participation!
readings.hint: Would you want to participate in Christmas if it meant looking like Santa and visiting every single house on earth?

meanings.mnemonic: A tree with talent can become lumber . Not all trees can do this. Only the talented ones are able to turn themselves into lumber or material , to be used for a million different things.
meanings.hint: Picture a tree standing in the forest. It's thinking so hard you can see the math equations flying around in its branches, until finally a cartoon light bulb appears above its head. This talented tree has figured out how to become lumber!

readings.mnemonic: Trees can be turned into all kinds of things, but this one wants to use its lumber and material to build a xy lophone ( ざい ). It's the most difficult instrument to create, so it suits the talent level of this tree!
readings.hint: Watch as the tree becomes lumber and that lumber is used to craft a ginormous xylophone. Each key is its own plank of lumber!

meanings.mnemonic: You tie a thread on your finger before jumping in the pool to remind yourself of the promise you made to your mother. The thread represents the promise you made to mow the lawn after you finished swimming in the pool.
meanings.hint: The promise reminding thread on your finger was tied too tight. You can feel the thread starting to cut off your circulation as your finger starts to turn purple and go numb. It’s really uncomfortable.

readings.mnemonic: After you get out of the pool, you keep the promise you made to your mother. So you get on your yak ( やく ) powered mower and trim the grass. Yes, your mower is pulled by a yak. It’s very efficient.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself sitting on a riding mower as you’re pulled around the yard by a yak. The yak is having a hard time pulling you so it's making lots of yak noises. Hear them in your mind.

meanings.mnemonic: A mountain stone is probably going to be a really big stone. We're talking about a boulder here.
meanings.hint: This kanji makes a lot of sense. Just follow the logic.

readings.mnemonic: " Boulder !!!" yells a voice from behind you. " Eee, wha ...?" ( いわ ) you reply. You look up to see a boulder falling off the cliff above you. "Eeeeee! Whaaaat!?," you shout again, running and screaming this time.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself screaming "Eeeeee! Whaaaat!?" and running away from that boulder as fast as you can.

meanings.mnemonic: To your leader , you say : " Believe and trust in me." The leader doesn't think you can handle your duties, so you have to say this to him. You want him to believe and trust that you're responsible. You can handle this task.
meanings.hint: Imagine who your leader is here — maybe a boss, or maybe the Crabigator. Now say it out loud to this imagined leader: "Believe and trust in me!"

readings.mnemonic: As you face your leader and say " Believe and trust in me," you kneel down on each shin ( しん ). You have shin splints, so it hurts a bit, but you do it anyway to show how serious you are.
readings.hint: Picture yourself pleading with your leader to trust and believe in you. Imagine the pain from your shin splints as you kneel. It hurts a lot, but it proves how serious you are.

meanings.mnemonic: Your leader in private is Buddha . You don't feel the need to tell everyone. But when you're alone you give your time to Buddha and let him lead your life.
meanings.hint: Imagine being in the privacy of your own home, your own room, and saying to yourself, "Yes, Buddha, you're my leader."

readings.mnemonic: Your private Buddha is a little different than other people's idea of him. Your Buddha is wearing boots ( ぶつ ). They're big and rubber, and they look fabulous on him.
readings.hint: Picture Buddha wearing a giant pair of rubber boots. Yes, now this is fashion.

meanings.mnemonic: Again and again, winter change s you each year.
meanings.hint: With every winter that comes around, another year passes, and you change. Again and again. The never ending cycle of winters and change.

readings.mnemonic: You know what else change s each winter? Hen s ( へん )! They're constantly laying eggs, which then (possibly) turn into new hens.
readings.hint: Picture you and your hens. Going through change, every winter, again and again.

meanings.mnemonic: If you see an animal among the flowers in a rice paddy , chances are it’s a cat . Cats love rice paddy flowers, almost as much as they love catnip.
meanings.hint: Picture this cat meowing and purring as it rubs up against all the flowers in the rice paddy field. Aww, it’s so cute!

readings.mnemonic: Wait a minute… This cat is pretty odd looking. You take a closer look, seeing it is missing its neck ( ねこ ). You go to pet it, but it's super sensitive about that area of its head, which is basically a face connected to a body...
readings.hint: Try not to imagine this too hard. It is disturbing.

meanings.mnemonic: The sun is out, the sky is blue , things have begun to clear up !
meanings.hint: Look into the blue sky and imagine the clouds parting as the weather clears up and you can see the sun. Gorgeous! The beauty is so great you have to squint.

readings.mnemonic: Now that it's starting to clear up you can't help but laugh. " Ha ( は )! Ha ha ha!" you laugh up into the sky. The sun feels great on your face, making you feel lively! And that blue sky ain't too shabby either.
readings.hint: Say the "ha ha ha" part out loud while you imagine the weather clearing up. It'll help you to remember the reading of this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami approaches, and anyone who dares to stand in front of it will surely cry . That tsunami is going to hit you hard, and rip you right off the ground you're standing on. Who wouldn't cry?
meanings.hint: Imagine getting hit with the full force of a tsunami. It slams into you and crushes every bone in your body. It hurts so much you cry.

readings.mnemonic: Do you know the only way to stop yourself cry ing after a tsunami? That's right, na chos ( な )! Nachos are the only proven way to cheer people up after being hit by a tsunami.
readings.hint: Imagine stuffing yourself with nachos. The tears stop. You're feeling bruised and wet, but much better.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical for courage and the kanji for courage are exactly the same.
meanings.hint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji too!

readings.mnemonic: A man needs courage to stand up to all the you ths ( ゆう ) of the world. They are so numerous and difficult to understand, but the man can face them with courage thanks to his mama.
readings.hint: Imagine a courageous man standing against the youths. They use weird slang and try to tell him about avocado toast, but the man keeps his courage.

meanings.mnemonic: Throw a cape over a creeper , and suddenly they think they're a big-shot director . They go around calling themselves managing director of this, director-general of that. They creep into meetings and ceremonies and try to direct the proceedings, all because of that stupid cape.
meanings.hint: Picture the creepiest creeper you know. They're wearing a cape, laughing from behind a big desk in their new director-level position. That cape has really gone to their head.

readings.mnemonic: After this creeper dons the cape, they decide to become Director of Shee p ( し ). From now on all sheep-related business, government policy, and law will be determined by this creeper in a cape, because they are the Director of Sheep.
readings.hint: Picture that creeper you know. Their cape is made of wool now, since they're the Director of Sheep, and they're directing all kinds of creepy policy decisions about sheep. I'm not even sure what those would be, but try to imagine some.

meanings.mnemonic: This cage with treasure in it is actually a map of the district the treasure is in.
meanings.hint: Picture a big pirate map, and on it are a bunch of these markers for the districts where you can find the treasures you've been searching for.

readings.mnemonic: There is one district you're interested in, and that's the coo kie ( く ) district.
readings.hint: The treasure of the cookie district is cookies, which you want. And to be fair, even if you weren't a pirate, you'd probably want those cookies.

meanings.mnemonic:
There are many flowers in the center of England . It may seem rainy and gray at first, but if you walk right to the center you'll see all the flowers that grow there in the center of the country.

This kanji also means English , which is pretty similar to England. Just imagine all the people of England walking to the center, where the flowers are, and speaking English to them to help them grow.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself in the center of England, speaking English to all of the beautiful flowers.

readings.mnemonic: You keep walking to the very central center of England and see a giant a corn ( えい ) planted in the middle of the ground. This acorn is the source of all the flowers and life in England.
readings.hint: Imagine walking around the giant English acorn. Touch its smooth surface with your hand.

meanings.mnemonic:
The height radical and the height kanji are the same!

That said, there's a secondary meaning to this kanji of ten feet . So, when you think of height, think of that height as being ten feet tall.
meanings.hint: If you paid attention in radicals school, you'll be able to learn this kanji meaning easily.

readings.mnemonic: When you think of height , think of our huge farmhand friend, Joe ( じょう ). He's a big guy, and he has a lot of height to him. In fact, he's ten feet tall.
readings.hint: Joe really is a big guy. Look up at him, and try to measure him, although ten feet might be a little high for you to reach.

meanings.mnemonic: The husband radical and the husband kanji are the same. How useful, unlike some husbands, I'd imagine.

readings.mnemonic: What do all husband s love? Foo d ( ふう )! Can you hear your future husband? He’s saying, "Fooooood! FOOOOOD!” like some kind of food craving zombie.
readings.hint: Husbands love eating food. Who doesn’t though, really?

meanings.mnemonic: Members of the tongue clan like to do one thing: lick . They go around licking everything to mark their tongue clan territory, you know? They even lick each other on the cheek by way of greeting.
meanings.hint: Imagine encountering the tongue clan in your travels and being greeted by a whole village of them. It's kinda gross at first, with your cheeks getting all slick from the saliva, but after a while you get used to it.

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, the tongue clan members also lick your na chos ( な ). You'd been saving those nachos! They were your travel rations. Now each and every nacho has been licked clean.
readings.hint: Seems this tongue clan only likes food that can be licked up, like nacho cheese and salsa. They lap up all the good stuff, so all that's left for you are some soggy, pale, naked nachos.

meanings.mnemonic: When a spirit decides to live in your scarecrow , you'll be granted good luck and good fortune . Crows won't hang out in your farm. Everything you grow will become large and delicious. But not everyone is so lucky that a spirit will decide to live in their scarecrow. So maybe the luck was in you all along!
meanings.hint: Nope, the luck is definitely coming from the spirit in your scarecrow. You aren't lucky on your own.

readings.mnemonic: You want to check the scarecrow to make sure your recent luck is coming from it, so you kick it. " F*** ( ふく ) you!" it yells. Oh yeah, there's a spirit in there.
readings.hint: If you get a luck spirit to say f*** you, you might accidentally convince it to move somewhere else. If it does, you lose your luck. So be careful.

meanings.mnemonic: You might want to eat , but your anti -eating stance means your meal will just sit in front of you, uneaten. Food is meant to be eaten! Stop trying to fast and take a bite.
meanings.hint: Look down at the huge meal in front of you. You'll have to eat your food eventually, but I guess there are plenty of reasons to be anti-eat right now.

readings.mnemonic: As you stare at the meal , Han ( はん ) Solo appears before you and takes a bite! This is what happens if you don't eat your food, someone else will eat it.
readings.hint: Picture Han Solo slowly eating your whole meal. He's making constant eye contact with you, reaching down and shoving the entire meal into his mouth.

meanings.mnemonic: If you want to say how big a cross is, you have to measure it. You can't say the size of something without measuring it first.
meanings.hint: Imagine trying to use your hands to show the size of this cross, but the other person shakes their head and says, "Use your words!" Ugh, okay, you'll have to measure it then.

readings.mnemonic: You need to measure this cross but all you have is a ca ke ( けい ). Good enough! I'm sure all cakes are exactly the same size, so they're a reliable unit of measure.
readings.hint: "This cross measures two cakes wide and three cakes long," you say.

meanings.mnemonic: The sun has an excuse for disappearing at night . The sun gets really tired, same as anyone else – so when night rolls around the sun excuses itself and goes to sleep. It’s a valid excuse.
meanings.hint: Imagine you’re the sun, working all day. Of course you get tired. This is a good excuse to go to bed at night and get some rest. Feel how sore and hot and sweaty and exhausted you are after a hard day’s work of being the sun.

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, you also have a history of overworking yourself, so you've been outright ban ned ( ばん ) from showing up at night .
readings.hint: Stop working so much! Humans need some rest too, so it can't always be bright. Maybe you should try some therapy. We all just want to see you get better, sun. Anyway, you're banned for now. Cya.

meanings.mnemonic: Tsunami s in the past changed the method s and law s of today.
meanings.hint: Tsunamis used to come crashing into towns, just leveling them completely. Today we have new methods of building and protecting ourselves, and laws to keep them in place, so the past doesn't repeat itself.

readings.mnemonic: These method s and law s affected your own ho me ( ほう ) the most.
readings.hint: Your home has strong walls and ceilings. You aren't allowed to leave the doors unlocked and open. These methods and laws are because of those past tsunamis and the damage they used to cause. Your home is safe thanks to them!

meanings.mnemonic: A giant shrimp appears on top of the sun after it rises high above the ground , and this is how you know it's noon . The shrimp appears on the sun exactly at noon every day, to announce that it's noon.
meanings.hint: Really try to imagine this giant shrimp on the sun as a normal thing that happens, without fail, every day at noon. "Hey guys, it's noon," the shrimp announces casually, then leaves again.

readings.mnemonic: At noon , the shrimp also puts on its high heel s ( ひる ). It wants to look good for the other shrimp it's going to lunch with. High noon? More like high heels, am I right?
readings.hint: Right after the shrimp's noon announcement, imagine it struggling to put on heels while sitting on the sun. This shrimp is almost bigger than the sun, actually, so all its heel struggles are easy to see.

meanings.mnemonic: Your fingers are private , no one can see them unless they pay ! And you decide if what they pay is enough to get a peek.
meanings.hint: Imagine people coming from miles around to pay for a look at your private fingers.

readings.mnemonic: If someone tries to look without paying, that's hara ssment ( はら )! Nobody gets to see something of yours without proper payment, but more importantly, your consent!
readings.hint: If somebody looks at your fingers without paying, without your consent, file a harassment claim on their butt! That is NOT okay!

meanings.mnemonic: Once the sun comes out, you can use this key to unlock it and take you to any previous day, even just yesterday ! The sun is in charge of the day changes, so if you use a key to unlock it, you can control the flow of time and return to yesterday, or a previous day in the past. Behold the power of the sun.
meanings.hint: Imagine having the power to control time like this. Picture yourself unlocking the sun to go back in time. Feel the heat of the sun and the bright light in your eyes as you unlock the door to the past.

readings.mnemonic: Time traveling to a previous time, like yesterday , can be rough on the body. As such, you wear a small sack ( さく ) to hold your valuables while you're ripped through the sands of time. The sack keeps your important stuff from getting lost. The sack holds your key to the sun as well.
readings.hint: Imagine wearing this sack as you're sucked through the vortex of time and space. Feel yourself spinning around as you hurtle back into yesterday.

meanings.mnemonic: To throw life out the window , use poison .
meanings.hint: To keep life safely inside the window, put that poison down.

readings.mnemonic: If anything's worse than poison it's doing Su doku ( どく ) puzzles.
readings.hint: Have you done Sudoku before? Imagine yourself completing these puzzles and concentrating really hard. They’re fun, but also challenging.

meanings.mnemonic: You pick up your knife and broom because you're getting ready to return home . There'll be a lot of work to do when you return home — cooking, cleaning, fixing stuff — so your knife and broom are sure to come in handy.
meanings.hint: Your knife is one of those Swiss Army knives, so it's got a bunch of tools you'll need when you return home. Your broom is just a broom, but… maybe if you pretend to ride it like a witch, it'll make the trip home more fun?

readings.mnemonic: For you to return home , your family has to ask the world to 返 す ( かえ ) you to them. You belong at home with your family — the outside world is just borrowing you, and eventually it needs to 返す you and let you return home.
readings.hint: Imagine your family asking all your coworkers and friends to 返す you so you can return home. It's heartwarming, but also kind of embarrassing at the same time.

meanings.mnemonic:
A good ceremony is under construction for a long time. You can't just make up a new ceremony on the spot. A ceremony is done a certain way because that's the way it's always been done. In short, to build a ceremony, you have to create a ritual first. Then, the ritual can be constructed into a bigger, more lavish ceremony .

This kanji also means equation for some reason. Just think of a ceremony that involves doing math and solving equations. How strange would that be? Pretend you're at this ceremony.
meanings.hint: Ritual and ceremony kind of mean the same thing. Don't worry too much if you mix them up a little — each will help you to remember the other. There's a reason this kanji is made up of the ceremony radical already!

readings.mnemonic: So, you're at a ritual , but not just any old ritual. This is a full-blown ceremony and it's incredibly chic ( しき ). Imagine just how chic it is. Give it a shot — I'll let you come up with the chicness this time (it's good practice).
readings.hint: It's good to practice making up your own mnemonics every once in a while. It'll help if you add more to the mnemonics already here, or if you can make up new ones that are more personal to you!

meanings.mnemonic: The simple radical and the simple kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Simple is simple!

readings.mnemonic: One of the most simple things in the world is getting a tan ( たん ). All you have to do is stand outside long enough. It doesn't matter what skin type you have or what the weather is like. Eventually, if you stand out there long enough, you'll get a tan.
readings.hint: Get a tan, it's simple, stupid! (Though it's terrible for you so uh... don't always take our advice, kay?)

meanings.mnemonic: Take your yakuza knife and put it in a row with the others.
meanings.hint: You have a yakuza knife. There are a bunch there, all in a row, so you follow suit by putting yours in the row too.

readings.mnemonic: Should we put them in a row ? Let's ( れつ ) put them in a row!
readings.hint: Imagine a bunch of yakuza being enthusiastic about putting their knives in a row. They all say together "Ret's do it!"

meanings.mnemonic: After a tsunami , if you go to a bar , you'll find yourself standing in shallow water. You're just trying to enjoy a drink, but with your feet constantly soaked, it's not exactly pleasant. Here's to hoping the conversations with your date won't be shallow too!
meanings.hint: Picture yourself at this bar, sipping on a drink while you're wading in this shallow water. Feel the shallow water getting your socks and shoes all wet. It's uncomfortable and cold.

readings.mnemonic: What beer are you drinking at this bar while standing in shallow water? You’re drinking Asa hi ( あさ ), the beer of the salaryman. Asahi is super dry, so it takes your attention away from the shallow water a bit.
readings.hint: Picture yourself in the bar again sipping on this Asahi beer. Taste the beery beer taste of Asahi as you walk around the bar with your wet feet.

meanings.mnemonic: You can make your fingers branch out. It's your special skill , a very advanced technique . Your fingers branch out into smaller fingers and you can hold things really well. You can type super-duper fast. And you can win any tickle fight. These are skills you have thanks to your branching fingers.
meanings.hint: If you could have any skill imaginable, what would it be? Wouldn't it be nice to have these magical branching fingers? (Definitely. It would.)

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, these skill s are too much for the normal skill-less people to handle, so they pull out the gui llotine ( ぎ ). I guess they're going to chop off your skillful fingers because they're kinda scary.
readings.hint: Imagine a tiny guillotine made for the cutting of fingers. Whoever made this must have had special hand skills too! Then maybe the skill-less people tested it on them…

meanings.mnemonic: Dirt is being anti -social when it's in the shape of a slope . Think about it — is there anything more annoying than having to walk up a steep slope? Why is the dirt here forming a slope?! Because the dirt is being anti-social, and a slope is how it tells you to stay away.
