meanings.hint: Imagine the pain in your calves as you walk up a steep slope. Wow, this dirt is really anti-you right now. Going down the slope will be a lot easier and quicker, but that's still just the dirt trying to shoo you away as fast as possible.

readings.mnemonic: You need to know these things about slope s if you want to play soccer ( さか ). Soccer is surprisingly slope-intensive, actually.
readings.hint: Maybe it looks flat to a spectator, but a soccer pitch is actually a series of very small slopes. Those soccer athletes who seem to fall and flop all the time are in fact just slipping on tiny invisible slopes.

meanings.mnemonic: In the cage there are some legs . They are the legs attached to a small animal .
meanings.hint: You can only see the legs, though, because a cloth is covering the cage. What kind of small animal is it? Try to look.

readings.mnemonic: You look up through the cloth, and see a hairless cat. This small animal is hairless, but most surprisingly is covered in hickie s ( ひき ).
readings.hint: Who is giving this cat all those hickies? Pretty gross, if you ask me.

meanings.mnemonic: This building has easy access to sunshine . The sunlight shines right in, because it's a clear building.
meanings.hint: Feel the sunshine coming into the building. It gets into this building so easily, and makes it very bright and warm. No matter where you look, sunshine is everywhere.

readings.mnemonic: The sunshine gets into the building so easily, in fact, that it hits your yo gurt ( よう ). The sunshine makes the yogurt... gross.
readings.hint: Picture your yogurt glistening in the sunlight. Watch as it slowly curdles and starts to smell sour. You still eat your yogurt, but it's hot and lumpy now. Sunlight and yogurt do not mix.

meanings.mnemonic: When you see the spring sun you know it's finally the spring season.
meanings.hint: The spring sun only comes out in spring, which is why you know for sure it's spring. Imagine a different looking sun that only comes out in spring but not the other seasons.

readings.mnemonic: When the spring season comes around, it makes you want to laugh: " HAR ( はる ) HAR HAR!" you yell.
readings.hint: Go ahead and belt out a few "HAR HAR HARs" for spring. It's warming up, everything is coming to life, and you're happy. "HAR HAR HAR!"

meanings.mnemonic: There go some angel s with drop s on their shoulders. Carrying drops like that requires technique . It’s a real art .
meanings.hint: Angels are really good at going around carrying drops! Imagine the artistry and technique involved. I bet they do some kind of dance, and there's lots of sparkly magic.

readings.mnemonic: Angels are so good at this because they are trained in nin jutsu ( じゅつ ), or the "ninja techniques." Everyone knows that ninjas are great at carrying drops on the go!
readings.hint: Imagine an angel trained in ninjutsu. Marvel at all their divine ninja techniques!

meanings.mnemonic: The temple radical and the temple kanji are exactly the same! Brilliant.

readings.mnemonic: Someone died. You ask how this person died, the person who's getting buried by the temple . The priest/monk says it was in a tragic terror ( てら ) attack.
readings.hint: That's too bad - feel sorry for the person and learn a little more about the terror attack, if it will help you to remember the reading of this kanji better.

meanings.mnemonic: With the bamboo from that tree , your trained eye can tell it'll make an excellent box . Boxes made from bamboo trees are very sturdy and fashionable. You have an eye for fashion as well, you see.
meanings.hint: Remember back to those woodshop electives you had in school (if you did have them) and imagine yourself crafting a bamboo box out of a bamboo tree you felled yourself. Don't forget to wear eye protection though! Wouldn't want anything to happen to those valuable eyes of yours.

readings.mnemonic: Now you can use that box to store all your ha rd co re ( はこ ) punk vinyl records. You're a real connoisseur, so you know that hardcore music should only ever be kept in bamboo boxes.
readings.hint: Picture yourself filling up your lovingly crafted bamboo box with your collection of hardcore punk and rock records. Ah, they fit just perfectly thanks to that discerning eye of yours.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami hits a valley , filling it up with water. Lucky for you, you're a giant, so you just lay down in the valley and use it to bathe .
meanings.hint: Sure, there's a lot of water in the valley, but you're big too. It's the perfect place for you to bathe... and good thing too, you're getting smelly.

readings.mnemonic: You know what would make the bath ing experience even better? If you had some egg yolk s ( よく ) to eat. Just crack those yolks into the water you bathe in, and it'll cook to perfection. Yum!
readings.hint: Imagine cooking egg yolks in the same water you bathe in.

meanings.mnemonic: A psychic leader kneels to the ground , picks up a thread , and instantly makes a connection with the person that it came from. This leader is very powerful, so even a thread is enough for them to make a psychic connection with the person who was wearing it.
meanings.hint: Imagine you're this leader, and you've just picked up a piece of thread from the ground. You make a jarring psychic connection with the owner of the thread. Your head spins, your heart races, and your hands tremble. Picture this happening to you as you pick up the thread from the ground.

readings.mnemonic: As you develop this psychic connection with the owner of the thread, you can tell that they're currently eating a ca ke ( けい ).
readings.hint: The connection is so strong that you feel like you're eating the cake as well. It's vanilla-strawberry. You taste the fluffy and creamy vanilla-strawberry flavors washing over your tongue. Imagine yourself eating this delectable cake. It's fantastically delicious.

meanings.mnemonic:
After a tsunami , use this machine to cure the people and the country from the damage. Heal ing machines are all over Japan just in case a tsunami does way more damage than they can handle.

Oh, and whoever cures and heals the most people will be able to rule over them once everything starts going back to normal. So keep that in mind, kay?
meanings.hint: Imagine if you had your own machine to cure yourself if a tsunami came to where you live. It would be pretty convenient!

readings.mnemonic:
Alternative ways to cure yourself after a tsunami are either praying to Je sus ( じ ) or eating your weight in chee se ( ち ). I'm not sure if I have enough cheese for all the people that got hit though... Hmm, do you have cheese?

Before you make your decision, remember that whoever cures you will be able to reign over you and your people. So do you want to be led by Jesus or by some cheese? The choice is yours.
readings.hint: Imagine getting Jesus to cure half the people and feeding the other half lots of cheese and their wounds miraculously healing on their own! You're a hero!

meanings.mnemonic: There is a prison on a cliff surrounded by snake s. Now that's a dangerous place. If you try to escape the prison you either fall to your death off the cliff or get bitten/squeezed to death by the snakes. Can you think of anything more dangerous than this? I can't.
meanings.hint: Imagine a dangerous prison surrounded by cliffs and snakes. You wouldn't want to be here either, would you?

readings.mnemonic: Lucky for you, even though you're stuck in this dangerous place, there is hope. There is a key ( き ). All dangerous prisons have a key. You just have to find and steal it so you can avoid the cliffs and snakes!
readings.hint: Imagine staring at the key dangling right outside of the dangerous prison you're trapped in. Would you take the chance and go for it?

meanings.mnemonic: When the sun gets in your eye , I dare you to stare at it. I dare you to look right at the sun. I dare you.
meanings.hint: Looking at the sun straight on hurts. It’s bad for your eyes and burns your brain. Imagine how painful it is to stare at the sun.

readings.mnemonic: After you’ve blinded yourself from the dare of looking at the sun and losing sight in each eye, the guy dares you to go bow ling ( ぼう ). You’re blind now, so bowling will be quite the challenge.
readings.hint: Picture yourself bowling without the power of sight. It would be awfully difficult to do well. You’d just have to concentrate really hard on the sound of the pins and determine where you need to throw the ball.

meanings.mnemonic: A group of private sword guys are in your rice paddy . You must detain them at once! Private sword guys are people who work for private companies and use their swords on people. They're basically paid assassins. And you're going to detain them!
meanings.hint: Imagine running into the rice paddy and detaining the private sword holders one by one. They won't get away this time!

readings.mnemonic:
You're running out of steam and you're afraid you might not be able to detain all of these guys when Ryu ( りゅう ) from Street Fighter appears. He hates swords, and will only use fists because it's the honorable way to fight, so he starts helping you detain them!

He's also found a loo phole ( る ) in the system and you don't have to be super gentle with these guys either. So detain them as forcefully as you want. It is your rice paddy they're in, after all.
readings.hint: If Ryu hadn't shown up with this loophole you never would have detained all these private sword guys by yourself!

meanings.mnemonic: If you listened in to the private lives of twenty people, all of them would have different dialect s and ways of speech . Whether they actually use these dialects with you is another story. But when they're alone, or with other people who are from the same place, their speech will be different.
meanings.hint: Can you even imagine twenty different dialects in your head? Well I can tell you that there are definitely at least twenty of them in Japan alone.

readings.mnemonic: The best way to learn someone's dialect is to buy them a ben to ( べん ) from where they're from. Not only do these bento have writing in their dialect on them, but their speech will immediately shift to match. Then you'll be able to hear how they sound in their head and in private!
readings.hint: It also helps that bento are delicious, and anyone with any dialect will love them.

meanings.mnemonic: What I say is correct 100%! You may use it as evidence in this murder case.
meanings.hint: You convince the officials questioning you that your evidence can be trusted and it's proof that you definitely didn't have anything to do with any law breaking or murdering.

readings.mnemonic: The officials force you to present this evidence in front of the Shou gun ( しょう ), himself. Your knees quake, you feel like you're gonna be sick, but you try to stand up tall and tell him everything. After all, you know your proof is correct, so you shouldn't be so scared.
readings.hint: You better hope the Shougun believes your evidence is accurate, because he's known to kill people willy-nilly when he thinks they're lying.

meanings.mnemonic: You examine the mouth of a zombie and find a small garden in there. There are even benches... it looks like a park .
meanings.hint: Zombies are dead, so I guess their mouths have all the things garden plants crave. Their mouths are like compost piles, if you think about it.

readings.mnemonic: You look closer at the garden and suddenly everything en ds ( えん ).
readings.hint: Oh. The zombie probably bit down on your head and now it's the end for you. At least you were looking at a nice garden at the end of it all...

meanings.mnemonic: If a narwhal stick s a child with its horn then you have to admit that it exist s. We know some of you have been doubting narwhals and whether they're real. Well now your kid is a kabob, so do you believe they exist now? I suppose you do.
meanings.hint: Imagine needing this kind of horrible proof to believe something exists. Maybe you should have more faith in what we teach.

readings.mnemonic: Oh, and the child we had to narwhal stab to prove they exist - that's your son ( そん ). Maybe you should have listened and kept your son out of the Narwhal Danger Zone ( ぞん ) after we warned you about it.
readings.hint: I suppose you'll never forget that narwhals exist now that your son has one permanently stuck in him.

meanings.mnemonic: You see a scooter with a zombie on it, going really far away. Someone tied that zombie to the scooter and now it's far off in the distance, groaning and growling.
meanings.hint: Picture the zombie, going far off into the distance. It's getting smaller and harder to see.

readings.mnemonic: The zombie goes so far , that its life en ds ( えん ). Zombies need to eat to live (it's really all they need), but they go so far on their scooter trip, that they just give up and end it all.
readings.hint: I mean, to be fair, the zombie wasn't really alive to begin with. But going far enough to end it all? That's some serious distance!

meanings.mnemonic: A canopy attach ing your entire country is the government . They loom over you all and try to keep you all organized and together under their control.
meanings.hint: Picture a map of the world with actual canopies attached to each government, representing the ruling governments of each place.

readings.mnemonic: Try to get out from under this government canopy and you'll be labeled a foo l ( ふ ). Unless... you can fool them!
readings.hint: Think of all the people who've been labeled as fools by the government because they try to detach themselves from under their reach. It's a pretty long list...

meanings.mnemonic: The face radical and the face kanji are the same! But this isn't just a face like the one you're looking at your computer/phone with. It's the surface kind of face too!
meanings.hint: The next time you look at your face, also notice how much surface area it has.

readings.mnemonic: This face you're looking at, that has a lot of surface area, belongs to men ( めん ). Not a single man, but all men put together. It's hundreds of thousands of men's faces in a row.
readings.hint: Look at the face of all men put together. Measure how much surface area they all take up.

meanings.mnemonic: The radical for gate and the kanji for gates are the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals or you won't be allowed to pass.

readings.mnemonic: You want to pass through this gate but it’s guarded by… a mon ster ( もん )!
readings.hint: Picture the most terrifying monster you can imagine, standing as tall as the gate itself. How on earth are you ever going to get past that thing?!

meanings.mnemonic: Put a lid on something spicy and you'll cook up pure happiness .
meanings.hint: Spicy foods literally do trigger happy chemicals in your brain. It wouldn't be too far off to say that putting a lid on a pot filled with something spicy could produce happiness.

readings.mnemonic: Out of spicy foods you made pure happiness . You give it to your sad friend こう いち , who waits at the table.
readings.hint: こういち looks sad. But when you serve him happiness, he starts to perk up a bit. A couple more bites of happiness, and there's a smile on his face. Although when you found こういち he was depressed, down on his luck, and hiding from the government, cooking him some spicy foods seems to have brought him some happiness.

meanings.mnemonic: In the prison where you once kept Wolverine , you find a beating heart . You don't understand how it's still going, but you know you need to hurry up and do something, or it might stop all of a sudden .
meanings.hint: Picture this beating heart in the prison. Was it Wolverine's? That would explain why it's, like, beating all supernaturally on its own. What should you do with it? You'd better hurry, its owner probably needs it.

readings.mnemonic: You start to overheat as you hurry around, so you grab a cu cumber ( きゅう ) to cool you down. The high water content re-hydrates you and lets you continue hurrying to help save this heart.
readings.hint: And if it really is Wolverine's heart, you're gonna have to be cool as a cucumber when you try to help him, even if you're in a hurry. He's emotionally cold, remember? Gotta play it cool with him, despite the emergency.

meanings.mnemonic: Bamboo heaven is a place for laugh ing. Bamboo is a very jolly plant, and they love to laugh. Even after death in bamboo heaven, the bamboo continue to laugh joyfully. Bamboo heaven is truly a place of laughter.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself as a bamboo plant that laughs in heaven. What are you laughing about? Chuckle to yourself as you remember this kanji.

readings.mnemonic: There’s actually a war a goin’ on up in that there bamboo heaven, y’know? Ye might not have heard of it with all that there laugh in’ a goin’ on, but there definitely be a war a ( わら ) goin’ on up in bamboo heaven, y’hear? The bamboo are scared so that’s why they be laughin’ so much. They nervous.
readings.hint: Now picture these bamboo again up in bamboo heaven, and remember this strange accent to remember that the bamboo plants up in bamboo heaven are laughing with nervous laughter at this bamboo war that’s a goin’ on up there.

meanings.mnemonic: If there's something about a building that people are anti towards, it's usually the heights aspect. Another thing would be if it's on a big slope . No one likes that kind of building.
meanings.hint: Imagine standing on the edge of a very high building on a slope somewhere. You'd become a building anti, too, if you had to look out from the terrifying heights of this building.

readings.mnemonic: Another person you know who's afraid of heights is Han ( はん ) Solo. Now wait, you protest, he flies a space ship! You're right, but only because he doesn't like to walk all the way up high buildings, it freaks him out. He flies up instead so he doesn't have to think about heights.
readings.hint: Imagine standing at the top of that tall building with Han Solo. He's very afraid of heights (and slopes). They make him nervous. Like something bad will happen if he were to ever stand high up above a big pit.

meanings.mnemonic: You offer flowers to your leader and compliment them on their lip ring . You think that by doing this they might carry your luggage .
meanings.hint: Picture this big, strong leader. They actually love flowers and feel flattered by your compliment! But being the leader, they can't help wondering why they should have to carry your luggage…

readings.mnemonic: Unconvinced by your flattery, the leader refuses to carry your luggage , so you get on your knee s ( に ) and beg them to carry it.
readings.hint: On top of being a strong leader, they also have a very kind heart, so your begging convinces them and they agree to carry your luggage.

meanings.mnemonic: It's correct in winter to focus on politics and the government . Elections are happening, in the new year politicians are being sworn in. Winter is government time!
meanings.hint: Imagine all this politics and government talk as you shiver outside in the winter cold. You’re freezing and you’re bored, but you know this is the right (correct) thing to do.

readings.mnemonic: Everyone involved in politics and government wields a special sa ber ( せい ). If two political parties are arguing, they'll whip out their sabers and start slashing away!
readings.hint: Picture two different politicians, both wielding sabers, fighting to make the government the way they want it to be! Who are you rooting for?

meanings.mnemonic: The hat comes to a stop , thinking of a plan . The hat had been pacing around for a while, trying to think of how to get off the head he's resting upon and back into the house where it's nice and cozy, and came to the sudden realization of a plan. This caused the hat to stop.
meanings.hint: Imagine a hat walking about on your head, pacing back and forth deep in thought. See the hat stroke its chin in contemplation as it tries to think up a plan to get off your noggin and back inside.

readings.mnemonic: The plan that made this hat stop right in its tracks was very simple. First, obtain the key ( き ) to the house. Then, use the key to get in the front door. How simple!
readings.hint: Watch as the hat begins to grin. This plan is so simple it’s sure to work. Now watch as the hat obtains the key and enters the locked door. What sort of mischief will the hat get into once inside?

meanings.mnemonic: Your roof needs measurement s done every year to make sure it will protect you properly.
meanings.hint: If you think about it, your roof protects you from a lot of things! Rain, snow, bird poop. But if your roof's measurements are off it won't be able to protect you properly.

readings.mnemonic: If the measurements are off, and your roof doesn't protect you, then you get to sue ( す )! If you're still alive, that is.
readings.hint: Odds are, the roof collapsed or a tree fell on you and you died from lack of protection. So now your family and friends are going to sue on your behalf.

meanings.mnemonic: The products radical and the product kanji are exactly the same! Productive!

readings.mnemonic: As you try to sell these product s, you finally get a buyer. It's a Hin du ( ひん ) person. She walks in and buys your products on the spot!
readings.hint: Then another Hindu person walks in your store and buys some. Then another... What are all these Hindu people doing buying your products, you wonder? When you ask them, they tell you "it's a cultural thing."

meanings.mnemonic:
When your tongue becomes too spicy for you to bear, it’s time to quit the jalapeño eating contest. You’ve been eating them for three hours now and the spiciness from the peppers is really burning your tongue. Throw in the towel, it’s time to quit the contest.

This kanji has another meaning too:

Eating isn't the only thing that makes your tongue spicy , so do certain word s. Say your favorite words out loud right now. Feel them on your tongue. Words are the spicy food of language!
meanings.hint: You may have to quit eating but you never have to quit saying words. Especially if you're, well, trying to learn a whole new language full of them.

readings.mnemonic: Oh, you're probably wondering who won that spicy food contest after you quit and became obsessed with word s instead. It was Je sus ( じ ). Who did you expect? He was great with words AND never quitting when things got spicy.
readings.hint: Picture Jesus scarfing down jalapeño peppers. He never quits. And then he says some words about how the jalapeños gave up their lives for this contest, so we better appreciate them. Thanks Jesus!

meanings.mnemonic: This gate of horns will take you to heaven . These horns are related and connected to heaven in some way.
meanings.hint: Perhaps they are related to heaven because the devil has horns? Perhaps they are connected to heaven because these are the horns of heaven's enemies? The real reason they're connected will be revealed in the reading mnemonic.

readings.mnemonic: They are related and connected to heaven because these are the horns that Genghis Khan ( かん ) made from his enemies. God said he could come into heaven if he made a gate of horns, so that's what he did.
readings.hint: Imagine Genghis Khan taking horns off his enemies, and making a gate to heaven. The horn gate is connected to both heaven and Genghis Khan in that way.

meanings.mnemonic: Sticking a cross into a rice paddy to get its measurement s is your specialty . Most people get really confused when they have to measure their rice paddies, but that's where you come in. You plant a cross right in the middle, look down it from one end to the other, and voila! You know the measurements immediately. That's why it's your specialty!
meanings.hint: Visualize yourself doing this. The numbers just come to you. It's just what you're good at. Your specialty is something no one else in the world is as good at as you!

readings.mnemonic: Hearing of your amazing specialty and cross measurement abilities, a cen taur ( せん ) from a far off land recruits you for a job. The centaur’s father has just passed away and the centaur wants you to measure the rice paddies that have been left to him.
readings.hint: Picture yourself at a centaur funeral. Tons of centaurs standing around and stamping their hooves as you make your measurement and stick a cross into the ground.

meanings.mnemonic: If the pope stays fixated on the ground , staring at fins , he's going to lose touch with reality . He should be focusing on his popish duties, but instead he's looking at the fish fins he dropped, and now can't eat because they're dirty. Silly pope. You can't let this one thing get you down. You have other things to do.
meanings.hint: Picture trying to get the pope to stop staring at the fins lying on the dirty ground. Yup, he's completely lost touch with reality, you think.

readings.mnemonic: You're doing your best to bring the pope back to reality when he snaps and kicks you in the shin ( しん ). Oww! What the heck, pope! But he's already back staring at the fins and your shin is throbbing where he kicked it.
readings.hint: Imagine the pope angrily kicking your shin super hard. You fall over and pull it close. Feel how badly it aches. Who knew you'd need shin guards to deal with a pope that's lost touch with reality just from dropping his dinner.

meanings.mnemonic: Going into a building made by a squid is risky . First of all, squids only have tentacles, no bones, and they make all of their buildings out of risky materials like kelp and sharp coral.
meanings.hint: One squid got stuck in his own building, but no one could save him because going in would be too risky. Poor little squid.

readings.mnemonic: "It's too risky , leave me!" the squid cries. "I'll save you, underwater citizen! Nothing is too risky for Ken ( けん ) the samurai!" The building is collapsing on the squid, the risk is so great, but Ken the samurai dives in and saves him!
readings.hint: The squid cries, "I can build one that isn't so risky next time!" Ken shakes his head, "I'm not letting you do anymore risky things, you're coming with me from now on." Ken scoops up the squid into a bucket, hoists him over his shoulder, and walks off into the sunset.

meanings.mnemonic: If you play music with your fins , one rule is that you must be a fish. You can’t play music using fish fins that don’t belong to you. That’s the rule. And it’s also rude.
meanings.hint: Imagine a fish playing music using his fins. What kind of a song is it? Imagine it in your head as you see a sign with the fish fin rule on it.

readings.mnemonic: There’s also a rule in place to limit the amount of fish fin musicians. No more than ten ( てん ) are allowed to gather and play their music together. That’s the rule. No more than ten.
readings.hint: Picture a group of ten (as per the rule) fish playing music with their fins. Now the song can be louder and more intricate. Imagine it playing in your head.

meanings.mnemonic: Why would you try to take a brush to the sun ? It's almost too obvious, because you want to write something down.
meanings.hint: Just imagine yourself sitting on the sun, writing back to your friends and family with a brush. For someone who can visit the sun, it's really weird that you are writing with a brush like this, but you're doing it just the same.

readings.mnemonic: What did you write about? You were writing about this amazing circus show ( しょ ) you just watched on the sun. You didn't expect there to be any shows here, but turns out you were wrong!
readings.hint: Think of your favorite live shows and imagine them on the sun. Also imagine yourself writing back to your friends and family about said shows.

meanings.mnemonic: A forehead table is completely superfluous .
meanings.hint: Seriously, you don't need a forehead table. It's totally unnecessary to have a table made of foreheads and is a superfluous purchase. First of all, getting all those foreheads is a waste of people. Second, forehead tables aren't very strong. Superfluous, superfluous, superfluous.

readings.mnemonic: The only person you know with a superfluous forehead table is Joe ( じょう ), the ten foot tall, simple farmhand.
readings.hint: Now every time you visit Joe, you’ll have to look at his giant, superfluous forehead table. Imagine how gross it looks, with dozens of foreheads protruding from its surface. Only Joe would buy such a thing.

meanings.mnemonic: You use a thread for good luck by bind ing two fingers together. You tie them nice and tight.
meanings.hint: Tying two fingers together with a good luck thread is very common where you're from.

readings.mnemonic: After you bind your fingers, the good luck starts pouring in. Literally. Suddenly ketsu p ( けつ ) starts pouring down from the heavens.
readings.hint: This ketsup is exactly what you were looking for, but now it won't stop. You need to get out of this bind by untying the thread on your finger. But it won't budge. It's covered in ketsup. Help! You're going to drown!

meanings.mnemonic: The sun is correct , absolutely . The mighty sun is never wrong. It is absolutely correct, always.
meanings.hint: Imagine the sun in the sky. Its rays blind you as it corrects all of the errors you’ve made.

readings.mnemonic: Then, a ze ppelin ( ぜ ) flies in front of the sun, blocking it out. Absolutely , the sun cannot ignore this transgression. Therefore, the sun corrects the zeppelin’s evil ways by frying it with sunshine and bringing it to the ground.
readings.hint: Imagine the sun being so absolutely consumed with rage that it catches a zeppelin on fire and crashes it. Feel the heat from the grounded zeppelin as it smolders.

meanings.mnemonic: When the sun is out, you can see two Wolverine s and a turkey playing together, but only on a certain weekday . Which day of the week is it? I'll never tell you. It could be Sunday. It could be Monday. It could be any day of the week.
meanings.hint: Picture the majestic scene — two Wolverines (yes, there are two) and a turkey, frolicking in the sunlight together. It's one of those rare but regular natural occurrences that only happens once a week, on a particular day.

readings.mnemonic: Now that you know how to figure out what day of the week it is, you can start making yo gurt ( よう ). It can take up to 24 hours to make yogurt. Knowing which weekday it is at any given moment means you can perfect your yogurt-making schedule.
readings.hint: Picture yourself looking at the shadow cast by the two Wolverines and the turkey. Imagine waiting a whole 24 hours and knowing exactly when your yogurt is ready. All thanks to this makeshift sundial which can accurately tell you which day of the week it is.

meanings.mnemonic: Your mouth is so big that it's the cause of all your problems.
meanings.hint: Imagine all the dumb things you blabber about, with your mouth open big and wide. Saying things you shouldn't say, causing all kinds of drama. Shame on you.

readings.mnemonic: Your big mouth is the cause behind all your friends and family moving to an inn ( いん ). They move to the inn so they don't have to live with you and hear you talk anymore.
readings.hint: Who knew you could cause people to live in an inn instead of their own homes with your big mouth...

meanings.mnemonic: They say sheep are detailed creatures. I mean, I never thought sheep were very detailed, but that’s what they say.
meanings.hint: Imagine getting really close to a sheep and seeing all the tiny detailed curls in their wool. There are hidden patterns you'd never noticed before now!

readings.mnemonic: The reason these sheep are so detailed is that they are trying to impress the Shou gun ( しょう ). If they're fancy enough, perhaps the Shougun won’t kill them for their meat and make their wool into clothing.
readings.hint: Picture the Shougun, leaning in close to inspect the detailed patterns all over the sheep. His nose is so close, he can probably smell all the little detailed nuances of the sheep's musk too.

meanings.mnemonic: If you can say what sound a drunkard makes, you must be very discerning . Few people are able to discern what sound a drunkard makes, but you — you can say, because you're a very discriminating listener.
meanings.hint: Yes, you can even tell a tequila drunk from a vodka drunk based on the sounds they make. (Imagine how exactly they'd be different.) To be able to say what sound each kind of drunkard makes, it takes a very discerning, discriminating mind.

readings.mnemonic: Discerning individuals like you are considered chic ( しき ). What's more chic than your discriminating way of listening?
readings.hint: All chic people show some kind of discernment. For some it's about discerning what's fashionable. For you, it's about discriminating one kind of drunk sound from another. That's your version of chic.

meanings.mnemonic: Under a canopy your clan digs in the ground , trying to get to the bottom . They want to build a nice home your entire clan can live in, but they need to get to the bottom before they'll start building.
meanings.hint: Picture all of the people in your clan under this canopy digging with all their might, trying to find the bottom. "Isn't there... no bottom though?" you think to yourself.

readings.mnemonic: Suddenly someone down in the hole shouts that they've reached the bottom , but it's full of water and they're all soak ed ( そこ )!
readings.hint: The bottom is full of water, and everyone and everything down there is soaked through now.

meanings.mnemonic: A tiger kills a pig with a knife in this new drama about the animal kingdom! The tiger and pig used to be friends when they were younger, but now they're bitter enemies and the tiger just stabbed the pig right in the chest with a knife.
meanings.hint: Picture this scene from the drama as dramatically as you can. The pig stares into the tiger's eyes, "Tiger, you wouldn't–uh!" The pig's eyes slowly lower to see the knife sticking into its gut, the tiger's hand still holding it. "This is what you get, you pig," the tiger spits back, twisting.

readings.mnemonic: This drama is being put on by Gecki ( げき ), your childhood pet gecko. Gecki is playing all the parts, because as true drama fans know, one-gecko-shows are the highest form of art.
readings.hint: Picture Gecki acting out the part of the tiger and the pig in this drama. It's so intense, you're moved to tears. Bravo Gecki, bravo!

meanings.mnemonic: The dry radical and the dry kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: You need to dry Genghis Khan ( かん ). He hates being wet, all of the Mongols do, so your job is to dry his entire body. And his clothes, and his horse. In fact, when it rains, you have to ride next to him keeping him dry by holding an umbrella above his head.
readings.hint: Imagine keeping Genghis Khan completely dry all day every day. He won't even get wet to bathe. Instead he makes you rub his body with dry sand and salts.

meanings.mnemonic: A shellfish that can't live through winter is a failure . Any proper shellfish would survive through the winter, so this is quite an embarrassing defeat .
meanings.hint: Imagine you're a shellfish in the cold, cold winter. The water is freezing but you must tough it out so no one will say you're a failure in spring when they find your dead, defeated shellfish body.

readings.mnemonic: The best way to avoid failure is to say a little hai ku ( はい ) to distract you from the cold. In fact, this can work in any situation where you're afraid of defeat. Say a haiku aloud, it will calm your nerves and warm you from the inside.
readings.hint: Imagine being a shellfish freezing in the cold winter water, but you avoid failure by muttering haiku over and over to keep you warm and determined.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami is coming, and you want to walk over it, so you have to ford it. You don’t walk across a tsunami, you wade across it.
meanings.hint: Imaging taking your shoes off, hiking up your pants, and holding all of your things above your head as you ford across the tsunami.

readings.mnemonic: Your ability to ford this tsunami impresses everyone who witnesses it, especially the Shou gun ( しょう ). Now that you've taught him a tsunami can be forded, he's going to start sending his troops through them instead of waiting for them to pass.
readings.hint: Your new fording techniques have increased the Shougun's military power! Congrats, I guess.

meanings.mnemonic: The fruit radical and the fruit kanji are exactly the same! Bodacious!

readings.mnemonic: They sell fruit at the store, and you need to drive your ca r ( か ) to get there. A car is the fastest way to fruit.
readings.hint: Imagine you have a hankering for your favorite fruit. You gotta get in your car and put the pedal to the metal! Feel the wind in your hair as your car speeds down the road and listen to that engine purr.

meanings.mnemonic: Under this roof is a bear who works for the government . If you saw a bear out in the wild you might be afraid and try to run, but if you see a bear inside a building under a roof, don't be scared! It's just a government bear.
meanings.hint: Picture the government bear looking through government documents and snorting gruffly as it decides what to sign and what to reject. It has a little government sash and hat.

readings.mnemonic: The first government that allowed bears was set up by Genghis Khan ( かん ). He was all about conquering people and then using them as efficiently as possible. He discovered that bears are incredibly useful in government buildings as government workers.
readings.hint: Picture Genghis Khan patting the first bear government worker on the back as they start their first day working for the government.

meanings.mnemonic: This net is for someone who works in a government office . You'll also take someone from a political office .
meanings.hint: Doesn't matter who, but you're going to catch someone from a government office using this net, it doesn't matter who.

readings.mnemonic: Government office and political office people put on the best show s ( しょ ). That's why you want to capture someone from there with your net.
readings.hint: Imagine the show they put on. You'll call your show "Exactly 8 Hours of Bureaucracy."

meanings.mnemonic: If you're on the roof of the festival you'll have to guess what's actually going on below you. You can kind of see that there are rides and maybe food? It smells like food. But you have to guess on the specifics because you're above it and not in it.
meanings.hint: Imagine being on the roof above a festival. Can you guess what's happening below you based on the sounds and smells?

readings.mnemonic: To get a better guess , you ask Satsu ki ( さつ ) for help. She's that little girl you know from some earlier mnemonics, and she happens to be up on the roof with you. Picture her as you ask, "What's your guess, Satsuki?"
readings.hint: And what's Satsuki doing up here, anyway? Your guess is as good as mine. Now imagine you and her trying to guess what kind of festival it is.

meanings.mnemonic: A Viking will empty his mouth of all its dirt in the great hall . They all get together in some kind of public chamber and dish all the dirt (literal and figurative) that they've got.
meanings.hint: Picture a long Viking hall, with a big table and tons of chairs. All of the Vikings are gossiping and sharing all their dirt on their enemies.

readings.mnemonic: While the Vikings gather to spill the dirt in their hall , they also stuff their faces with dou ghnuts ( どう ). Ah yes, Vikings love to eat doughnuts when they talk trash.
readings.hint: Now picture all of the Vikings in the hall spitting dirt with one breath and shoveling doughnuts in with the next. It's pretty gross!

meanings.mnemonic: Put on cleat s so you can plant a kiss on your sweetheart's forehead in the heart of winter — that's love . You'll be braving the harsh winter elements for love, so you put on your cleats to get a better grip on the snow. Sure, you could just wait for better weather to go see them, but then it wouldn't be true love, would it?
meanings.hint: Picture a person you love. Feel the joy of love in your heart as you brave the winter to see them. Your cleats crunch the snow underfoot, it's cold outside, but you're kept warm and happy by that feeling of love.

readings.mnemonic: Walking through the winter snow with your cleats on, you've gotta keep your eye ( あい ) on the prize. It's a really hard journey, but you keep your eye on the prize, which is your love and the time you'll get to spend with them.
readings.hint: Imagine seeing your love with your own two eyes after a long time apart. It feels great to be reunited and see eye to eye once again. Imagine this feeling.

meanings.mnemonic: A criminal has done an injustice on the world or other people. Being a criminal is a negative thing, and even if it was just a mistake , it was still a crime.
meanings.hint: Think of all the criminals you know. Even if what they did was a mistake, it was still some type of injustice, right? It was something the rest of the world sees as a negative thing.

readings.mnemonic: When you commit an injustice , you'll know because he at ( ひ ) fills your body. Maybe you've never done any kind of big thing, but when you make a mistake your face fills with heat and you turn red!
readings.hint: This is the heat of embarrassment, of knowing you did something negative or wrong.

meanings.mnemonic: You hide poop in a cape as part of the illusion in your magic show. Your acts are kind of gross and this one involves creating the illusion of disappearing poop by hiding it within the folds of your cape.
meanings.hint: Imagine having poop in your cape. It would smell bad and anyone close to you would be able to smell right through the illusion.

readings.mnemonic: Somehow the poop cape illusion goes over really well. The crowd is giving you a standing ovation and right in the center is Gen ji ( げん ). He marches up to you, wipes a tear from his eye, and says, "Your illusion cape is wonderful. I must have it!"
readings.hint: Genji grabs the cape before you can stop him and he puts it on. You can see the illusion fade, he keeps smiling but more tears start streaming down his face. "A wonderful illusion, the best I've ever seen," he chokes out and marches away, without looking back.

meanings.mnemonic:
Viking see , Viking memorize . That’s how Vikings learn things. They see something, and then they memorize it. Quite simple, really.

It can also mean awake . Just remember that Vikings can't see if they aren't awake. There won't be anything to memorize if their eyes are still closed, after all!
meanings.hint: How else would a Viking memorize something? Imagine having to memorize sea charts for your Viking navigation. Look and see the maps in your brain as you memorize them.

readings.mnemonic: Every morning, a cock ( かく ) crows and rouses the Vikings. Seeing this, one of the Vikings memorize s the cock crow so that he can mimic it and play pranks on his fellow Vikings.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself memorizing the sound of a cock crow and using it to play jokes on your fellow Vikings. Cock-a-doodle-doo!

meanings.mnemonic: Flowers can be used for comfort , but also for medicine . Sure, it’s a comfort to smell flowers because they smell nice, but one can also use them as medicine to heal people.
meanings.hint: Imagine having some medicine that smells and tastes like fresh cut flowers. Smell the smell in your nose and taste the taste on your tongue.

readings.mnemonic: The best way to transport all the medicine on land is with a yak ( やく ). Yaks are big, strong, and very capable of transporting flower medicine like these.
readings.hint: Picture a yak in your mind. Pretend you are riding atop the yak as it transports this medicine. Feel the fur of the yak in your hands as you cling on for dear life.

meanings.mnemonic: A viking with a mouth towel is a totally normal thing. It's ordinary .
meanings.hint: Vikings get a lot of nasty stuff on their mouths from their messy eating and pillaging, so it makes sense that they'd all have mouth towels. It's as ordinary and normal as it gets, at least amongst the vikings.

readings.mnemonic: It's normal for a viking to have a mouth towel, but your sloppy farmhand friend Joe ( じょう ) has a mouth towel too. That's a habit that's only normal for vikings, so why does he have one?
readings.hint: When you ask Joe about his Viking mouth towel, he just tells you, "It's normal for everyone to have mouth towels. Totally ordinary." Now that you think about it, he really is a messy dude, so he probably needs it.

meanings.mnemonic: You're all by your lonesome self and have to check each rice paddy you own, all twenty of them! Luckily, you have a very astute nose , allowing you to quickly assess the state of your twenty rice paddies all by yourself.
meanings.hint: Really focus in on your nose. Your nostrils get huge, and your nose is huge. Smell the rice and its freshness in your imagination. Your nose is amazing.

readings.mnemonic: As you're about to finish checking your paddies, your nose picks up an additional smell: It's Hannah , bringing you a bunch of 花 ( はな )!
readings.hint: Remember Hannah from when she helped the leader with his 花 problem? Looks like she's brought a bunch over just for you. Really breathe in the lovely smell of the 花 deeply through your nose. Thanks, Hannah!

meanings.mnemonic: The nothing radical and the nothing kanji are exactly the same! Woah!

readings.mnemonic: Nothing can moo ( む ) quite like a cow can moo. Cows are the best at going moo. That’s a fact.
readings.hint: Listen to this cow moo. Can you do a better moo than this moo cow? Nope.

meanings.mnemonic:
The original radical and the original kanji are the same!

This kanji can also mean field , probably because that's what everything originally looked like before humans: just a bunch of fields.
meanings.hint: Know your radicals! I shouldn't even have to tell you this by now.

readings.mnemonic: The original Japanese hero was Gen ji ( げん ), from "The Tale of Genji."
readings.hint: Picture Genji, the original Japanese protagonist, walking through the streets. He's signing his name for swooning ladies and gentlemen. Genji is the OG (original Genji).

meanings.mnemonic: Again and again, your heart comes back to the same person. Is this romance ? Are you in love ?
meanings.hint: Perhaps you have a person in mind that your heart comes back to again and again. Associate that person with this notion and kanji, and this'll be easy to memorize!

readings.mnemonic: But, remember that your romance isn’t a ren tal ( れん ). You really wanna reserve it for the right person!
readings.hint: Don’t let your love become a rental. Wait for the right person to come around.

meanings.mnemonic: If there is a drum in your mouth it's time to rejoice ! And because of your mouth drum your rejoicing will be loud and booming!
meanings.hint: Imagine having a drum in your mouth and rejoicing about it! Imagine how loud it would be!

readings.mnemonic: You loudly rejoice for a while when suddenly someone runs over and shoves a key ( き ) in your mouth, locking it shut. You can't rejoice anymore! You need that key to unlock your mouth!
readings.hint: Imagine if people could just stop the rejoicing of others by locking their mouths shut with keys. What a sad world.

meanings.mnemonic: A criminal 's heart will always be sad . No one chooses a life of crime if they're happy. And even if they seem like they're doing fine on the outside, deep down they're really sad.
meanings.hint: Imagine watching a criminal after he's done stealing from a bank. He's all alone. He stops throwing the money around. And cries. So so sad.

readings.mnemonic: The sad feelings fill the criminal's body with he at ( ひ ). This heat spreads and radiates out. If you walk next to them on the street, you'll feel the sad heat floating by you.
readings.hint: Imagine being so sad that your entire body fills with heat. That heat even makes squiggly heat lines pop out above and around you.

meanings.mnemonic: In a yurt you use a towel as a seat .
meanings.hint: Picture this yurt, with towels laid out as seats for everyone who visits.

readings.mnemonic: Something about using a towel for a seat is very sexy ( せき ) to you. Probably because you associate it with sexy people using towel seats on the beach.
readings.hint: Imagine the sexiest person you've ever seen, using a towel as a seat next to you in this yurt. Maybe they're in a bathing suit. Maybe they're completely covered up. Either way, they're very sexy.

meanings.mnemonic: When you swing your axe , the ground ends up covered in fins , because you're a soldier . Only a skilled axe soldier could make such precise cuts as to de-fin a bunch of fish like that.
meanings.hint: Imagine trying to carve some fish with an axe, to get them ready to eat. Not an easy thing to do. If you're a trained axe soldier, it'll probably be a lot easier. So picture yourself chopping off fins as an expert axe soldier.

readings.mnemonic: Uh-oh, it looks like this axe soldier was so excited about chopping the fins off his fish that he didn't think about how he was going to cook them. The only thing nearby to light on fire is a bale of hay ( へい ). Well, it's not the best thing, but at least it will get these fish cooked.
readings.hint: Imagine this soldier lighting a bale of hay on fire to cook his fish. The hay burns really fast, but it should be enough.

meanings.mnemonic: In the dirt , there's a gun . Its mouth points toward a plate . What's on the plate? Why, it's salt !
meanings.hint: In olden days, salt was so valuable it was used as money. So imagine the gun buried in the dirt, its mouth vigilantly guarding the plate of salt.

readings.mnemonic: When you see the salt , you think to yourself, " She owe s ( しお ) me big time, so I'm going to go ahead and snatch her salt!" Who is she, you ask? She's the lady who owns the salt. And the gun in the dirt, of course.
readings.hint: Are you sure you want to take her salt? Sure, she owes you, but I don't think you want to argue with the mouth of a gun.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of the shellfish has a knife in its side . It's fine though, don't worry, it's just a reminder that he won the great shellfish war back when they were trying to choose the new leader.
meanings.hint: Picture the shellfish leader in your mind and make sure to pay special attention to the knife that's sticking out of his side. It's right in the groove of his shell.

readings.mnemonic: Because of this knife in his side , he can't pronounce certain words correctly. The most obvious one is when he says 川 ( かわ ), but it comes out of the side of his mouth as がわ .
readings.hint: Try saying かわ out the side of your mouth like the leader of the shellfish and you'll probably say がわ too. Don't stick a knife in your side though, we won't pay your medical bills when you complain to us that this mnemonic got you injured.

meanings.mnemonic: What do you say to a guard ? Your theory about how the thing he's guarding isn't even real. Yeah, you have this whole thing figured out. You have for a while. At this point, it's only fair that you tell your theory to the guard who's wasting his time.
meanings.hint: Imagine saying your theory out loud to a guard about how what he's guarding isn't even there. Imagine how he would react.

readings.mnemonic: The guard listens to your theory but then he says his OWN! He thinks there are two sets ( せつ ) of guarded items, and he's only guarding one. According to his theory, either one could be real! His theory is more plausible, don't you think?
readings.hint: Which theory do you believe? That there is nothing or that there are sets of things?

meanings.mnemonic: You ride your scooter through the dirt while carrying a sheep because you're determined to attain something. Greatness? Glory? Everlasting fame? Maybe you'll attain a plural combination of all those things. After all, you are the best sheep-carrying off-road scooterist the world has ever seen.
meanings.hint: Feel the dirt spatter your face as you zip around off-road trails with your sheep under your arm. You will attain glory! You will attain greatness!

readings.mnemonic: After you attain that plural combination of glory, greatness, and fame, you get a tattoo ( たつ ) to celebrate: "world's greatest," in gothic lettering, above a picture of you and your sheep on your scooter.
readings.hint: Feel the pain of the tattoo needle as you get this commemorative tattoo. You're getting this tattoo on your forehead, of course, because you want to make sure everyone sees it.

meanings.mnemonic: The tree you aim your gun at from your window is an ume , aka a Japanese plum . Why? Because shooting the ume out of Japanese plum trees is, in your opinion, the best way to harvest plums.
meanings.hint: Imagine picking fruit off an ume tree this way, shooting them off the branches with your gun. You take aim at an unsuspecting plum — BANG! The gunshot rings in your ears and the smell of gunpowder fills the air. The ume branch is now one plum lighter.

readings.mnemonic: You've probably heard of ume before, depending on your exposure to Japan, but an ume ( うめ ) is a Japanese plum .
readings.hint: Imagine eating your harvest of ume. Taste the ripe fruity flesh of these Japanese plums as you stuff your gluttonous face.

meanings.mnemonic:
In a dangerous fire , a gambler will always try to bake something before fleeing to safety. It could be his own house on fire, and the gambler would still use the free heat source to get some baking done. Maybe he's gambling that those baked goods will offset the cost of a new home…

This kanji also means burn , because that's what happens when you expose something to a lot of heat.
meanings.hint: Imagine you're the gambler. Feel the heat intensify as your nostrils fill with the smell of baked goods.

readings.mnemonic: He used to bake for the Ya nkees ( や ). Baseball cakes, bat baguettes, that kind of thing. A career-ending kitchen injury forced him to retire. That's when he developed a gambling problem. But he still likes to bake whenever the Yankees are on TV.
readings.hint: Imagine this guy running around baking in the fire of his home, only this time with a Yankees hat and jersey on. He doesn't bake for them anymore, but he still loves the team.

meanings.mnemonic: If you take a thread and plant a rice paddy on it, the rice paddy is going to be extremely thin , but also detailed .
meanings.hint: Imagine this thin thread of rice paddy. It looks so detailed because all the rice paddy stuff is contained in this thin thread shape. You could maybe get one narrow line of rice on this thing.

readings.mnemonic: This thin rice paddy is for your cy borg ( さい ). You gave your cyborg a very thin human stomach, so it can only digest one thin line of rice at a time.
readings.hint: Picture the cyborg eating a really thin piece of rice. Now it's completely full! What a smart, thrifty cyborg.

meanings.mnemonic: What do you eat like a machine ? Candy !
meanings.hint: Picture your mouth opening and closing really fast and hard as candy on a conveyor belt goes sliding in. Munch, munch, munch, you're a candy machine!

readings.mnemonic: This candy in particular is just drops of hardened, clear sugar that looks like 雨 ( あめ ) drops.
readings.hint: Picture this kanji rain as it goes into your chomping machine mouth. It tastes like sweet, sugary 雨 . Yum!

meanings.mnemonic: In a moon prison , there lies a treasure chest containing a golden chest piece. This mighty chest armor will grant you protection from nearly any attack, because it is imbued with moon magic.
meanings.hint: Imagine finding this treasure chest and donning the mighty chest armor. Feel the weight of the golden chest piece and the vast protection it grants you.

readings.mnemonic: Where is this chest armor from? It's from the moon. Now how would you say that if you were Canadian? It's from the moon, eh ( むね ). Right, this here chest armor is from the moon, eh.
readings.hint: If you can remember this kanji's reading and meaning with a Canadian accent, you'll have no problem, eh.

meanings.mnemonic: The bamboo temple is equal to all of the other non-bamboo temples. Just because it's made of bamboo doesn't make it any less special and spiritual.
meanings.hint: Picture this bamboo temple compared to some temples made of stone or wood. It's the same size, shape, and it has the same number of worshipers. It's equal in every way.

readings.mnemonic: The bamboo temple is also an equal distance away from the other temples in とう きょう . When you look at a map of とうきょう temples, you can measure them out and they'll all be perfectly equal.
readings.hint: While many things in とうきょう may not be created equal, the temples are, at least.

meanings.mnemonic: In your mouth you put your life on the line by putting in a sword that is big . You put it all the way down there, so you consume it.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself doing a magic trick where you put a big sword in your mouth. You say: "Now, for my next illusion, I will consume a sword!"

readings.mnemonic: The " consume a sword" illusion came with a cheap magic kit ( きつ ) you had.
readings.hint: It's odd that there would be a "consume a sword" trick in your magic kit. It seems so real for a kit you can buy at the store.

meanings.mnemonic: You see a slide covered in dirt and use your mouth to clean it off. Then, you announce to everyone that the slide is clean and they can use it safely again.
meanings.hint: Imagine announcing to all the denizens of the playground that you've licked their slide clean. For some reason, no one seems to be rushing to play on it…

readings.mnemonic: After you announce the slide is clean, you take a big swig of Coke ( こく ). The dirty slide left a nasty taste in your mouth, but the Coke is helping to wash it away.
readings.hint: Honestly, after that big announcement, you deserve a Coke. Public speaking's hard, so you should treat yourself to a Coke whenever you announce something — regardless of whether you had to lick something gross beforehand.

meanings.mnemonic: You've got a car , a weapon , and some thread lying around. So of course you decide to connect them all together.
meanings.hint: Imagine using the thread to fasten the weapon onto your car, creating some kind of monstrosity. Surely this can't be legal?!

readings.mnemonic: There's only one reason you've connected all this together: Tuna ( つな ) fishing. Obviously!
readings.hint: Your weapon of choice is a harpoon, which is perfect for catching big tuna. We'll just have to hope your car is amphibious, or we might run into some issues. Now let's go catch some tuna!

meanings.mnemonic: Nestled in the light of the moon in the grass is a treasure chest that you can see with your brain . You close your eyes, and you can see the spot, but it's kept deep in your brain, and you have to use its power to find it.
meanings.hint: As you visualize the treasure chest you realize that the moon was your face, the grass your hair, and the treasure chest your brain! It was you all along!

readings.mnemonic: If you can unlock the secrets of your brain you'll have powers like No stradamus ( のう ). Nostradamus was able to look inward at his own hidden brain treasure chest and see the future, so maybe you can be like him too.
readings.hint: Use your brain like Nostradamus and try to see the future. You see... you see... huh, your brain is just surrounded by kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a poem about winter that you say to warn people against going outside. It's cold and dangerous in winter! You admonish anyone and everyone you see going out there with your scary winter poem.
meanings.hint: Imagine the things you'd warn people about. What is it about winter that's so dangerous?

readings.mnemonic: To make sure people listen to your warn ing, you threaten to take away their ca ke ( けい ). People will do anything for cake, so when you bring it up, they actually take your attempts to warn them about winter seriously.
readings.hint: The winter cold will make you ache Your whole body will start to shake Don't go outside I'll eat your cake

meanings.mnemonic: The bow radical and the bow kanji are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: Make sure you know those radicals!

readings.mnemonic: You have a bow in one hand and in the other... a cu cumber ( きゅう ). You're going to shoot this cucumber with this bow into the salad you're making. Many people don't know this, but this makes your cucumbers taste better.
readings.hint: Imagine fitting a cucumber into the string of your bow. Imagine the smell of it as you draw the string back. Mm, take a quick bite, then let it loose.

meanings.mnemonic: Your leader from long ago thought to borrow something precious from you. This leader did not return the thing he borrowed, and now he will pay.
meanings.hint: Think about what precious thing this leader borrowed from you so long ago. What was it? Think about how angry you are about not having the thing returned to you.

readings.mnemonic: In your rage, you decide to find this leader, take back what's yours, and in return, borrow the leader's life. So you found him, grabbed him, and threw him into a shack ( しゃく ) — where you "borrow" a few parts of the leader himself, and do other terrible things to him.
readings.hint: Come up with all the terrible things you do to this leader in the shack. Hey, it's just a little payback for the borrowing he did.

meanings.mnemonic: A tree that's been sliced up in winter can be used as a flat object counter . Since it's winter, all the leaves have fallen off the tree, so it's easy to cut into slats. These slats can be used as counters for flat objects.
meanings.hint: What with the snow and wind, winter also tends to knock trees down flat… which is probably why the tree radical on the left looks all flat and squished here. That's another way to remember this kanji is a counter for flat stuff!

readings.mnemonic: As you count each counter for flat objects , a bunch of mi ce ( まい ) show up and start climbing all over you. This won't do, so you take two slats and start smashing and flattening all the mice. Now that the mice are flat too, you can also use them as a counter for flat objects. Good job, mice.
readings.hint: Now imagine yourself counting with these mice you just smashed. They're freshly dead, so there's not much of a stench yet, but you still feel bad about using dead mice to help you count. Imagine yourself counting with these mice as you try to hold back tears from all the murder you just did.

meanings.mnemonic: The death of your heart will cause you to forget all of the most important things. People think memories are kept in your brain, but that's not true. Your heart is where all of your most precious memories are kept, and when it dies, you forget them all.
meanings.hint: Imagine someone close to you experiencing the death of their heart. They look right at you but they've already started to forget everything. I know this is not a fun thing to imagine, but use that emotion to help you remember this kanji, not forget it.

readings.mnemonic: When someone forget s everything they're left with only one thing to do: go bow ling ( ぼう ). Bowling is easy, and there's nothing to forget. You pick up a ball. You drop it. Everything else is automatically taken care of.
readings.hint: You know, that's probably why so many old people go bowling. They forgot everything else.

meanings.mnemonic: Using flowers to make a lip ring is always going to end in frustration .
meanings.hint: Have you ever made a daisy chain? Try making a daisy lip ring. Just imagine your frustation as you try to make it small enough to fit through your lip piercing.

readings.mnemonic: After some frustration , you made your flower lip ring. Now you look like an Egy ptian  ( いじ ) pharaoh. Because all ancient Egyptian monarchs wore flower lip rings (historical fact)!
readings.hint: Imagine yourself dressed as an ancient Egyptian pharaoh, wearing your newly-crafted flower lip ring. Think how classy (or strange) you feel.

meanings.mnemonic: You say stuff to a river , giving it instruction . This river is a new river so it needs you to say things, giving it instruction on what to do. Otherwise the river wouldn’t know how to behave.
meanings.hint: Imagine saying things to this river. What would you say? Flow harder! Babble better! Picture yourself shouting at the river as the water moves along.

readings.mnemonic: While giving instruction to the river, you see a small boat coming down it. Inside the miniature boat is a rac coon ( くん ) captain and his raccoon crew. The raccoon captain is instructing them, “Stroke! Stroke!”
readings.hint: Picture this raccoon captain and his raccoon crew coming down the river. The raccoons are all chittering about as they paddle the boat down the river.

meanings.mnemonic: If a grain seems extra heavy , you know it's a different kind . Not all type s of grains are the same. So when you feel the heavy type, you know it isn't the same as the light kind.
meanings.hint: Imagine sorting all the different types of grains by their weight.

readings.mnemonic: You sort all of the different kind s and type s by putting them in the right chu tes ( しゅ ). They slide away, probably to some big type-based piles you can't see.
readings.hint: Picture the two chutes you use to separate the different types of grains. One has a sign that says "Light Chute" and the other says "Heavy Chute."

meanings.mnemonic: You want to share happiness with people so you stamp stool s with the news . Now when people want a report of all the happy things going on, they can just look down before they sit.
meanings.hint: Stand up and look at the happy news today! It definitely isn't sad or depressing.

readings.mnemonic: You research and report the news from ho me ( ほう ). Only after, once all of the news is stamped onto the stools, do you leave your home.
readings.hint: Because your reporting is done from home, most of the news is about how your plants are growing. What the cat did this morning. And it certainly isn't the news without the weather!

meanings.mnemonic: The paragraph radical and the paragraph kanji are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: You have to write an entire paragraph about a topic, so you choose coo kies ( く ).
readings.hint: Imagine actually getting an assignment to write a paragraph all about cookies. Would you write about your favorite cookies? Your eating methods?

meanings.mnemonic: With a lip ring , anything is possible . Having a lip ring grants you the power to conquer any obstacle, achieve any goal. As long as it's a passable lip ring, that is. If it isn't good enough, it isn't possible to be passable.
meanings.hint: Imagine getting a magical lip ring. First, you have to find a passable one. Then, you have to go through the pain of having your lip pierced. Is it even possible to imagine how painful that would be?!

readings.mnemonic: Since anything and everything is possible with your new passable lip ring, you use it to get a new ca r ( か ). You go to the car dealership and ask for a free (passable) car. The manager is impressed with your lip ring and gladly hands over the keys to the latest model of your favorite car. Not bad.
readings.hint: It was possible to get this passable car thanks to your lip ring. Hooray!

meanings.mnemonic: Say it at noon . That's the only time I will allow you to say it. That's the only time I will permit you to say it. Other than that, no saying anything. Noon is your window.
meanings.hint: Imagine a world where you're not allowed to say anything except for at noon. At that moment, after you've been staring at the clock, waiting for that opportunity, you burst forth with things you want to say. But, once it hits 12:01, you are grabbed and your mouth is covered. You are not permitted to say anything outside of that time.

readings.mnemonic: Where do these archaic horrible rules exist? The smaller version of Kyoto, Little Kyo to ( きょ ). Normal Kyoto is just fine. This smaller one is where a group of people went to create their own little town with their own dumb rules.
readings.hint: Imagine going to big Kyoto where everything is fine. Then, go to little Kyoto where it's not. Little Kyoto has the short きょ (instead of the long one), and this is how you can remember the difference.

meanings.mnemonic: A religious leader wearing a mask could be either a monk or a priest . You don’t know, their face is covered by a mask!
meanings.hint: Picture this leader in their mask. Listen to them chant prayers that reverberate throughout the building.

readings.mnemonic: This monk or priest is wearing a mask to protect their sou l ( そう ). You aren't a monk or a priest, so they don't want your not-religious-enough eyes to dirty their soul.
readings.hint: Imagine the monk/priest's bright, pure soul shining behind the mask. You'll never actually see it, unless you're becoming a monk/priest yourself, and then your soul will need protecting too.

meanings.mnemonic: Under a canopy , there are two person s on the dirt . What do they do there? They sit . The people use the dirt under the canopy as a seat for their butts.
meanings.hint: Imagine how it feels sitting on dirt. Is it comfortable? Probably not. Maybe you feel a little uneasy about just sitting on plain dirt. Try to recall this feeling whenever you encounter this kanji.

readings.mnemonic: Who decided people have to sit like this? The C za r ( ざ ). The Czar wants to be above everyone, so he makes them sit on the dirt under canopies while he goes around on horseback or something, towering over them as they sit.
readings.hint: This Czar you met is ruthless and has formally banned all non-sitting activities. Imagine the excruciating pain, the stiffness, the cramping in your legs — all caused by this 24-7 sit-a-thon the Czar has cruelly decreed.

meanings.mnemonic: Within the blue water, a conflict stirs, but is made quiet by the muffling power of liquid. Under this blue water is a great conflict between two sea monsters, but on the surface it appears peaceful and quiet as the water prevents sound from rising above the surface.
meanings.hint: Imagine watching this conflict from above the blue water on a boat. You can kind of see the action, but you can't hear a thing.

readings.mnemonic: As you watch below the quiet water, every once in a while a sa ber ( せい ) breaches the surface and quietly slices back down.
readings.hint: Sabers are sharp, curved, and totally quiet. Great for underwater blue conflicts, and sneaky assassinations.

meanings.mnemonic: Killing two tree s with a jackhammer is expressly prohibit ed. Don't get me wrong, you can rip up one tree with your jackhammer, but not two.
meanings.hint: Imagine standing in front of a forest with your jackhammer, staring at a sign with just this kanji on it. Tree plus tree with jackhammer equals prohibited. Hmm.

readings.mnemonic: The person who decided to prohibit jackhammering two trees is the Kin g ( きん ) of England. He's the only person who's able to prohibit certain things, so it had to be him.
readings.hint: Picture the King angrily covering his ears as his subjects jackhammer trees. He screams and shouts, his crown bouncing on his head, and points at his new sign that prohibits the jackhammering of trees.

meanings.mnemonic: The water from a tsunami can create a lot of energy when made into steam . Steam power used to be all the rage back in the day. Steam comes from water, water comes from tsunamis, so logically, tsunamis are a great source of energy.
meanings.hint: Imagine how much steam a tsunami could create. Hear the steam hissing off of the water and feel the moisture from the steam washing over your body.

readings.mnemonic: In order to contain this steam energy and store it for later, you lock it up in a box with a key ( き ). That way the energy can be used at a later time. Provided you don’t lose the key, that is.
readings.hint: Steam is very light, so carrying this box of steam energy will be no problem. Picture yourself wandering about with a box of steam in one hand and a key in the other.

meanings.mnemonic: On your mouth is a stamp that you tap , strike and slap to create a beat! The stamp looks like a little drum, but you're really just hitting your mouth for fun (the stamp was an excuse to make it seem normal).
meanings.hint: Put a stamp on your mouth and look in your bathroom mirror. Now tap that stamp and make a fresh new beat the young kids can dance to!

readings.mnemonic: When you tap and strike the stamp on your mouth, you make a ta-ta ( たた ) sound. Imagine yourself making a beat with the little stamp drum on your mouth. Now listen... たた ! There! Did you hear it?
readings.hint: Picture yourself as a drummer, tapping away on a miniature stamp drum on your mouth. With each tap, you create a lively ta-ta beat!

meanings.mnemonic: If you have a fire and a helicopter hovering beside it, there's going to be a lot of dirt and smoke billowing around. Mostly smoke, on account of the fire. Now it's in your eyes and mouth. It gets in your lungs and you can't breathe. Why, helicopter, why!
meanings.hint: Imagine the smoke stopping up your lungs and getting in your eyes. All you know other than the smoke is the sound of the helicopter that's causing it to happen.

readings.mnemonic: The smoke is too much and you realize this is the en d ( えん ) for you. You're gonna die here!
readings.hint: Imagine choking on the smoke and accepting that this is the end. Boohoo, if only you'd learned more kanji before the end!

meanings.mnemonic: Into your mouth the leader shot an arrow with an attached hook so that they'd be able to see into your throat .
meanings.hint: Imagine the arrow with its hook shooting down into your mouth and hooking into your throat. Ouch! Now the leader is peering down in there, but you don't know why.

readings.mnemonic: From your throat you hear a voice. Not yours, but one you recognize. It's こう いち ! What's that rascal doing down in your throat?
readings.hint: The leader starts to tug こういち out of your throat, but he resists. "No, I like it down here, it's dark and damp." The leader gets angry and pulls harder, which hurts! This is your throat, after all, don't you get a say?!

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami previous ly gave everything in this town a good wash . There are many laundromats in this town, so when the previous tsunami hit, all of the detergent mixed with the water and gave everything a good wash.
meanings.hint: Imagine this soapy tsunami, washing all the things in town. Hear the soapy waves bubbling with cleanliness as they crash down around you.

readings.mnemonic: It wasn't just the town that got a wash , it was the cen taurs ( せん ) too. (Centaurs happen to live in this town.) And being half horse, centaurs have a lot of hair that needs washing. Luckily, the soap tsunami gave them a good wash too.
readings.hint: Imagine these centaurs frolicking around in the aftermath of the tsunami previous. It's just like a big bathtub for them. Hear them splashing and neighing in the water.

meanings.mnemonic: The rice you feed to a big geoduck can be of any type , kind , or category . It doesn't matter what kind of rice it is, the big geoduck will eat it.
meanings.hint: Picture this big geoduck shoveling rice down in a flurry of geoduckness. Hear the sloshing and goopy glugging as the geoduck gorges itself on every type of rice.

readings.mnemonic: While eating every type and kind of rice from every category , the big geoduck has become even bigger. Its body is rui ned ( るい ). It's painfully obese. This geoduck has eaten itself to ruin.
readings.hint: Picture this big, rice-engorged geoduck, too big to move. It's now in a wheel chair and on a rice IV. Imagine its wheelchair squeaking by as it wheezes and groans — ruined by all those types of rice.

meanings.mnemonic: You want your spirit to be simple so you practice Zen Buddhism . Zen is all about meditating and letting earthly things go. There's nothing more simple for your spirit than being Zen .
meanings.hint: Try out Zen meditation for a little bit. Look away from your computer or phone or tablet and let your life be simple for a second. Just let it all go and free your spirit from all the noise.

readings.mnemonic: The reading for this kanji is Zen ( ぜん ), just like the Zen in Zen Buddhism . Pretty easy to remember, no? Just remember that the reading is the same as the meaning. It’s easy!
readings.hint: Without a new reading to learn, you should have time to close your eyes and clear your mind. Zen it out, friend.

meanings.mnemonic: If you loiter around the sun temple , it's likely you'll acquire something. When you loiter around like that it looks like you're waiting for something, so if someone puts something in your hands and you just happen to acquire it, well, no one can fault you for that.
meanings.hint: Imagine loitering around near the sun temple. Maybe you acquire some illegal goods, maybe you just acquire a tan (it is a SUN temple, after all).

readings.mnemonic: You managed to acquire some tobacco, but just as you're about to leave Toku gawa ( とく ) Ieyasu comes marching up to you!! Or at least, his ghost does. He stares you dead in the eyes and says, "I didn't found the shogunate for you to acquire illegal goods and kill your body toking on my soil."
readings.hint: Your eyes fill with tears under Tokugawa's gaze and you drop everything you acquired to the ground. You bow before him apologizing. You'll never do this again!

meanings.mnemonic: You use a bookshelf as a book counter . Duh. What, are you gonna count them one by one? Okay, well maybe, but you should count them while they're nice and organized on the shelves. Otherwise counting is going to be a huge pain.
meanings.hint: Imagine counting books on a bunch of bookshelves. One book, two book, red book, blue book!

readings.mnemonic: You use this book counter method to count all the books Satsu ki ( さつ ) has in her house. Remember her? She's got, like, a ton of books, but she's just a little girl so she needs some help. That's where your book counter know-how comes in handy.
readings.hint: Imagine how tedious it'd be to watch her counting one by one. Teach her your book counter ways!

meanings.mnemonic: The power of your mouth lets you add words together to form a sentence. Marvel at the mighty additive power your mouth holds! You add words together in an amazing fashion!
meanings.hint: Say this sentence out loud. Incredible! You've used the power of your mouth to add words together in a sentence. Your parents must be very proud of you.

readings.mnemonic: Your parents are so proud of you being able to add words together that they surprise you with a new ca r ( か ). Wow! You add some stickers to your bumper and add your favorite playlist to the car radio. Let's drive.
readings.hint: Imagine riding around in your new car, singing songs with your powerful mouth to the playlists you added to the fancy new radio.

meanings.mnemonic: The dirt next to your village is where you bury the dead. People don't live forever, so every village has a patch of dirt specifically used for burying dead things.
meanings.hint: Imagine the village where you grew up. Can you see the dirt area where all your ancestors were buried?

readings.mnemonic: You don't just bury dead relatives here. You also buried your dead pet u ni ( う ) in that dirt. You thought having an uni as a pet would be a good idea. Too bad uni need water to survive.
readings.hint: Imagine crying as you bury your pet uni. Your tears can't save it now. (Though it is a bit ironic, since it needed salt water.)
