readings.hint: Wolverine might have a concussion that's making him neigh in his sleep. I guess it's good that he's lying down, but I'm pretty sure you're not supposed to sleep... Maybe you should wake him up so the neighing will stop—I mean, so he'll be okay!

meanings.mnemonic: A Viking who knows how to use a bathtub will have to manage all the baths for the other Vikings. Vikings aren't so great with bathing... if you can get one Viking to figure it out he has to manage the rest of them.
meanings.hint: Picture a Viking trying to manage his Viking friends so they'll use the bathtub properly. He has to manage which people go in when, how hot or cold the water is, and provide all the soap. It's a very important job.

readings.mnemonic: The poor Viking trying to manage all the others keeps having to confiscate a corns ( えい ). "You can't take baths in acorns and wood, it has to be water!" he shouts.
readings.hint: Some of the Vikings managed to fill their bathtubs with acorns and are trying to rub themselves clean with them instead of water...

meanings.mnemonic: Can you look at the dirt and know which direction you need to go? Then you might be a monk . Monks have amazing skills like that. And I'm sure you've seen monks wandering around in lots of media. They don't need maps or smartphones. They just look at the dirt and let the earth guide them to where they need to be.
meanings.hint: Picture a monk walking through a large field in the middle of nowhere. He knows where to go, he's not afraid. The dirt will lead him in the right direction.

readings.mnemonic: You want to know the monk 's trick so you hide behind him for a while as he walks. Finally the monk stops, looks around, takes out a bow ling ( ぼう ) ball and starts bowling in the dirt. The bowling ball rolls off, the monk follows it, and then heads off in that direction.
readings.hint: Imagine a monk bowling in the middle of the road. Imagine your shock at how well he handles the ball. Also, imagine how heavy it must be for a monk to carry a bowling ball everywhere!

meanings.mnemonic: You'll be put in a net if you try to say things with a knife — that's your penalty . You can't just go up to other people and say things with a knife out. That's why the police have to put you in a net, as a penalty.
meanings.hint: Imagine getting so mad that you use a knife instead of words to say what you're trying to say. A net falls over you and pulls you down. The knife goes flying. This is your penalty for acting like a crazy person.

readings.mnemonic: Your penalty for breaking the law isn't over. Now that you're stuck in a net, you can't fight back when they hit you with bats ( ばつ ).
readings.hint: If you don't want to be punished with bats, maybe next time don't use a knife instead of your words.

meanings.mnemonic: A cheap tree has a plan . A plan to stay cheap.
meanings.hint: Picture a tree coming up with some elaborate plans to make sure they can spend as little money as possible.

readings.mnemonic: The cheap tree's plan involves only buying an chovies ( あん ) and surviving on them. Good exposure to the sun and fresh water is too expensive. The tree plans to save money by eating anchovies.
readings.hint: You're not sure about this anchovy plan. Are you cheap enough to choose a life of eating only anchovies?

meanings.mnemonic: With a bamboo tree resting atop your head , you devise a plan . The plan is actually for getting the bamboo tree off of your head. You’re trapped under it.
meanings.hint: Imagine being trapped under this bamboo tree that is resting on your head. You’re stuck. Feel the weight of the bamboo tree pressing on your head as you try and devise a plan to get out from under it.

readings.mnemonic: But why is this bamboo tree so heavy? You look to each end of the bamboo and realize that there is a sack ( さく ) tied on each end. Each sack seems to be filled with heavy rocks. Looks like someone made a plan to trap you here. Now you have to devise a plan to escape!
readings.hint: Bamboo is usually kind of light, but this bamboo has a sack of rocks tied to each end making it heavy. Imagine yourself trying to remove this bamboo tree by pushing it up and away from you. It’s like doing a bench-press, bamboo style.

meanings.mnemonic: Pointing your fingers to the sun is the correct way to present yourself or offer to submit your ideas. You're really just raising your hand. And when you want to present yourself, your ideas, or offer anything, the correct way is to raise your fingers to the sun. You definitely learned this in school.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself in class. You want to present next! You want to submit your ideas for class approval. So raise those fingers to the sun the correct way and wait for your teacher to call on you.

readings.mnemonic: You want to present your show and tell of ta ter tots ( てい ) to the class. You raise your hand, the teacher calls on you, and you submit the tater tots for her and the class's approval. "Oh my!" the teacher exclaims, "I hope you brought enough tater tots to present to the whole class!" You raise your hand once more to the sky, and in it you submit a whole tray of tater tots!
readings.hint: The next time you have to submit work in class just raise your hand and give everyone tater tots. Instant "A" material.

meanings.mnemonic: You need your net to be straight before you put it on the ground and try to lure something into it. You put your net there to catch something, but if it's twisted or tangled, it won't be very effective.
meanings.hint: Imagine that some wild animal wanders into your net. Yes! You knew putting the net there was a good idea. But wait… Oh no, it escaped! It turns out your net wasn't straight when you put it there.

readings.mnemonic: Then you decide to put chee se ( ち ) in your net. With a perfectly straight net and the cheese you put down as bait, there’s no way this fails. Success is all about how you put the net down and the cheese you put in it.
readings.hint: Imagine the smell of the cheese as you put it in the net. Maybe a sharp cheddar? The aroma really carries. All the better to attract more animals! Soon you’ll be putting your cheese-eating prey on the dinner table.

meanings.mnemonic: Lying in the dirt , a drunkard puts her mouth to the ground to understand the region . She loves to know all the regional differences among wines and other alcohols, and to her there's no better way to do this than putting her mouth on the ground and literally tasting each region.
meanings.hint: Picture this drunkard in the dirt, her mouth pressed to the ground. "Ahh," she says, "so this is the taste of the Bordeaux region." Then she has a glass of Bordeaux, and starts noting all its particular regional qualities.

readings.mnemonic: Turns out she thinks everything from this region is icky ( いき ). In fact, to the drunkard, every region tastes icky and gross. You don't have the heart to tell her it's the dirt and ground, not the region itself, that tastes icky…
readings.hint: Imagine her crying out, "Why are there such icky, earthy notes in all these regional wines?!" Feel the second-hand embarrassment for her. In her drunkenness she can't understand that putting her mouth on the ground makes everything taste icky, no matter what region it's from.

meanings.mnemonic: In the ground of the rice paddy is a treasure . As you pull it out of the mucky ground, you can feel your life force being renew ed.
meanings.hint: Seems like the treasure has some magical ability to renew the cells in your body. Really try to imagine this feeling as you pull the treasure out of the rice paddy’s ground. Perhaps it feels just as refreshing as getting a full night’s sleep.

readings.mnemonic: Just as you were getting accustomed to this life renew ing treasure, こう いち comes out of nowhere, stealing your treasure and running off with it.
readings.hint: All you could hear was a sly “yoink!”, and suddenly your life renewing treasure was gone. God knows what things こういち will get up to with this life renewing treasure in his possession (but it sure won’t be anything good).

meanings.mnemonic: If someone gets under the canopy over your heart you should respond to them.
meanings.hint: You usually ignore everyone, but this one person... they really got under there and touched your heart. You finally feel like they're the right person to respond to.

readings.mnemonic: You respond and it turns out it was to the 王 ( おう ).
readings.hint: The 王 touched your heart, you responded, and now you're together. It's like a fairy tale!

meanings.mnemonic: It may just be a roof over a bathtub , but this here is actually a Shinto shrine .
meanings.hint: It is indeed just a roof over a bathtub, but I guess spirits inhabit everything according to Shintoism, so there's no reason this bathtub can't be a Shinto shrine too.

readings.mnemonic: What god does this Shinto shrine hold? You see that "It's a- me-ya ( みや )! (Mario!)"
readings.hint: It makes sense, the person who says "It's-a-me! (Mario)" is someone who works on bathtubs (and plumbing).

meanings.mnemonic: Everyone likes your outfit . The reason ? Your cool sleeve s. You are rocking some absolutely amazing sleeves, and that's reason enough for everyone to love your whole look.
meanings.hint: Imagine these incredible sleeves you're wearing. Are they flashy and colorful? Puffed and billowing? Jeweled and ornate? Whatever the case, they really bring your outfit together.

readings.mnemonic: Little does everyone know, you wear these sleeve s because your arms would be so de ad ( そで ) without them. Why? Because they'd be all exposed and defenseless!
readings.hint: Imagine all the ways your arms would be so dead if you didn't have sleeves. Sunburn, bug bites, miscellaneous cuts and scrapes... Just so, so dead. Good thing you've got these stylish, fancy sleeves to protect them.

meanings.mnemonic: Your mouth is like an escalator you use to suck and inhale things into your body.
meanings.hint: If you're ever having trouble remembering how to suck or inhale, just picture an actual escalator that leads into your mouth. That'll definitely help.

readings.mnemonic: Use this mouth ability to suck cu cumbers ( きゅう ) into your mouth. Cucumbers are shaped like cylinders so you can suck them into your mouth like this. Just as nature intended.
readings.hint: You can inhale a cucumber too, I suppose, but don't tell anyone I told you to.

meanings.mnemonic: While riding on your scooter , you grow a mustache on top of the mustache you already have in order to surpass the people around you. You're in a scooter race and your mustache is the only thing keeping you in the lead, but someone else has a mustache too! So you grow another mustache to surpass them once again!
meanings.hint: Picture yourself running on a scooter with a bunch of other people and closing your eyes tight, willing another mustache to sprout out of your face to surpass them all.

readings.mnemonic: Suddenly, something else manages to surpass you all. It's a ca r ( か )! A car with two mustaches is also riding on a scooter and going way faster than you.
readings.hint: Watch helplessly as the car surpasses every single one of your scooters. Both of your mustaches droop in shame.

meanings.mnemonic: Taking orders from a geoduck will place you into enemy territory . Geoducks make terrible commanders, so if you take orders from one, expect to end up in unknown territory, potentially even enemy territory. Be prepared.
meanings.hint: Imagine this geoduck shouting orders at you to march into enemy territory. See its gross body as a mouth forms and starts shouting out orders.

readings.mnemonic: Not wanting to end up dead in enemy territory , you decide to escape this geoduck leader by getting on a row ( りょう ) boat and rowing your way outta there. The geoduck commander orders you to halt your escape, but you still get in your row boat and row away.
readings.hint: Feel yourself in the row boat as you imagine rowing away to safe territory. Hear the geoduck in the background shouting at you from shore, ordering you to return to land.

meanings.mnemonic: The light of the moon is shining on a guard who’s been on duty all night. In fact, the moon light is making him so warm, he’s trying to undress and escape ! Somebody stop him!
meanings.hint: Picture the guard being so warm from the moonlight that he’s trying to simultaneously undress and escape. You can’t just let him leave his post!

readings.mnemonic: You’re unable to stop the guard from undress ing and making his escape , so you decide to start throwing some darts ( だつ ) at him. Perhaps that’ll make him stop.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself desperately throwing darts at the undressing, escaping guard, trying to make him stop. Or perhaps trying to pin his clothes to his body using the darts. Anything to stop this madness!

meanings.mnemonic: You take all of your thread and allocate them into one place to unite them. With all your thread united and wrapped together, you're ready to rule and govern the world!
meanings.hint: Allocate all of your thread like you would your cash: by uniting it! Not at a bank, but in a big, intertwined lump. Now nobody can stop you and your big lump of thread!

readings.mnemonic: You're busy trying to unite all of your thread, twisting and tying and wrapping, and suddenly you look at the ball in your hands and see とう きょう . You've united this thread into the shape of とうきょう . There are even little pins for all the famous neighborhoods and tourist spots. This is strange, because you don't remember putting them there.
readings.hint: Try to do this yourself at home. Unite all of your thread and poof! There's とうきょう !

meanings.mnemonic: The leader only uses the helicopter with the highest value . They choose to fly in only the very best, most valuable helicopter. That's why it's worth way more than the others. Actually, any helicopter they use goes up in value. That's the amazing effect of a good leader.
meanings.hint: Can you understand the value of this helicopter? The leader flew it to war and back! So of course it's worth a lot!

readings.mnemonic: To further increase the helicopter's value , the leader tied it to a ca r ( か ). Wow, now it has the value of a helicopter and a car combined! For a total value of… Well, imagine for yourself what value the car adds.
readings.hint: Imagine driving a valuable car with the leader's helicopter on top. Yeah!

meanings.mnemonic: A great leader can look straight at something and know its value . That social media site? 44 billion dollars. That job position? 50k a year. A four-day work week? Um… sorry, no, there's no value there. See? Leaders can see straight to the heart of matters to grasp something's true value.
meanings.hint: Imagine how terrifying it is when your leader looks straight at YOU. Don't worry, though — you've got more value than you give yourself credit for, and your leader can surely see that.

readings.mnemonic: One thing your leader knows has great value is chee se ( ち ). Say goodbye to your days of casually snacking on cheesy treats from the conbini, because your leader's decided the value of cheese is going up 1000%!
readings.hint: Imagine the despair you feel walking down the cheese aisle at the store and seeing how one little pack of string cheese now costs the same as your monthly rent. Why did your leader have to spike the value of cheese like this?

meanings.mnemonic: That scarecrow with the knife is the vice president. Yeah, I know how he looks, and I'm pretty sure he does some murdering on the side , but you didn't hear that from me.
meanings.hint: I don't know how we managed to get a knife-wielding scarecrow as a vice president, there's a side of politics I don't get, I guess. I mean, just look at him up there. If something happens to our president we're stuck with that thing.

readings.mnemonic: It's the vice president's turn to speak at the podium and he just clears his throat and yells, " F**k ( ふく ) you!" to the crowd, holding up his knife. They cheer.
readings.hint: What's going on? Normally if a vice president yells f**k you there would be... I dunno... consequences? What's happening?!

meanings.mnemonic: There's an arrow in a turkey that you can see in your view . The turkey seems to be viewing you too, from afar.
meanings.hint: View the turkey as the turkey views you. View the arrow stuck in it too.

readings.mnemonic: As you view the turkey, Genghis Khan ( かん ) suddenly comes into your view too. He towers over the injured turkey, leans down, and... pulls the arrow out!?
readings.hint: As you view this strange scene, Genghis Khan turns and views you viewing him. He points two fingers to his eyes and then to you, and marches off into the woods. You also view the turkey running off in the other direction.

meanings.mnemonic: The only flowers that the moon gladiator s will water are wisteria .
meanings.hint: The moon gladiators have a thing for wisterias.

readings.mnemonic: Since they're on the moon, where did they get the wisteria from? Actually, from one of the points on earth that is nearest to the moon, Mt. Fuji ( ふじ ).
readings.hint: Imagine them reaching down from the moon and plucking some wisteria up from Mt. Fuji then taking them back up to the moon. Now they water them.

meanings.mnemonic: There's going to be a tsunami . So you get up on your roof . You see people across the street , in a rice paddy . They watch as you put on a pair of fins . And you begin acting . This is your last chance to perform !
meanings.hint: But why? Because this acting will distract everyone from the impending tsunami. You dance around in your fins, pretending to swim. It's a wonderful performance.

readings.mnemonic: As the acting en ds ( えん ), so do you all.
readings.hint: The performance and the tsunami end together. How poetic.

meanings.mnemonic: A mouth that wants peace can call for it. If you don’t have a mouth, you can’t call for peace. So use your mouth and call for peace when you can. Peace is great.
meanings.hint: Call for peace. Say it aloud. I’m using my mouth to call for peace!

readings.mnemonic: But before you call for peace, you must first placate the masses. To do so, you astound them with your yo -yo ( よ ) skills. Once they are so entranced by how talented you are with a yo-yo, you easily convince them to make peace.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself whirling this yo-yo about while you call for peace. Imagine yourself doing a variety of tricks with it as you slowly convince the people to agree to peace.

meanings.mnemonic: From the top of a mountain you see something that looks like a big lip ring jutting out into the ocean: it's a cape ! You know, the geological formation where a big piece of land sticks out into the ocean? That kind of cape.
meanings.hint: Cape Cod, for example. Picture it now (or look it up if you need to). Really does look like a big lip ring stuck in the side of Massachusetts, doesn't it? Imagine seeing that cape from the top of your mountain, wind blustering around you.

readings.mnemonic: Looking down at the cape , you realize capes always make the coastline look jagged, like a saw or key ( さき ). Thanks for keeping the coastline interesting, you saw-key capes!
readings.hint: Try to draw that bit of coastline where the cape is. Nice, easy, smooth lines, until BAM! — the cape. Now the coastline is all jagged like the edge of a saw or key.

meanings.mnemonic: Moving in the direction of a gun , an alligator can carry out a murder once it reaches it. This alligator wants to kill someone. The alligator wants to carry out their execution.
meanings.hint: See the alligator swim really slowly in the direction of a gun. See the alligator as it prepares to carry out the murder. It’s scary.

readings.mnemonic: In order to carry out this murder, the alligator has to sneak up on its prey, so it can shoot it. You look across the water and see a shee p ( し ) grazing by the shore. The alligator is going to carry out a murder on that sheep!
readings.hint: Watch as the alligator slinks under the water, holding the gun above the water. When it gets close enough, it carries out the murder on the sheep. Shooting it right in the chest. Now it can eat the sheep without worrying about needing to chomp on it.

meanings.mnemonic: Dirt can become a castle at any time. You just have to have the right conditions. The right time, the right people, and any patch of dirt can grow into a huge castle.
meanings.hint: Every great building, especially castles, started off as just a plot of dirt, you know? It's kind of inspiring what we can become.

readings.mnemonic: Once you turn dirt into a castle , that's all she wro te ( しろ ). That's it. It's done. The story is over.
readings.hint: Once a castle is there, there's really no turning back. So just remember that when dirt becomes a castle, that's all she wrote.

meanings.mnemonic: The demon radical and the demon kanji are exactly the same! Wicked!

readings.mnemonic: How do you keep a demon locked up? Use a key ( き ). Use a lock and key and keep that demon locked up safely behind bars so he can’t get out and do bad demon stuff.
readings.hint: Picture yourself locking up a demon with a special demon containing key. Hear the key click into the lock as the demon screams to be set free.

meanings.mnemonic: They say that flowers held by a turkey on a stool are good to defend yourself with. Even better than a guard dog they say. People are so confused by the turkey sitting on a stool holding flowers that they just lose their will to fight. This turkey is great to defend yourself and your home.
meanings.hint: Picture this turkey holding its flowers while sitting on its stool. Hear it gobbling as it defends everything around it with its mere presence.

readings.mnemonic: The only thing this flower-bearing turkey can't defend against is Go dzilla ( ご ). Godzilla is pretty much undefeatable, especially for a turkey. The turkey is totally unable to defend anything from the mighty Godzilla.
readings.hint: Imagine Godzilla approaching the flower turkey. Hear Godzilla roar as the turkey gobbles in fear before being crushed beneath the massive and scaly foot of Godzilla.

meanings.mnemonic: Under the roof of life a mouth with a knife divide s cells to create life.
meanings.hint: The entire house glows with blobs of life, and the mouth holds the knife in its mouth and cuts the life blobs apart to help them grow.

readings.mnemonic:
As it divide s life, the mouth begins to look really familiar. It has big teeth and a crooked mustache... it's Wari o ( わり )!

Sometimes Wario cuts himself by accident and just becomes わ (which is all he says too).
readings.hint: Picture Wario's mouth dividing himself now, and creating more, smaller Warios. They all yell WA! and divide up even more until the whole room is full of わり s and わ s!

meanings.mnemonic: Reaching your ear is the sound of a drunkard who can’t find employment . You hear him before you see him because he’s behind some trash cans. Being a drunkard, he’s like, super wasted and just kind of rambling to himself, making strange sounds. No wonder he can’t find employment.
meanings.hint: Hear the strange sounds this drunkard is making. Let his ramblings enter your ears and imagine what he could be trying to say.

readings.mnemonic: Since this guy is such a pathetic drunkard, it comes as no shock ( しょく ) that he can’t find employment . If he did find employment, I would be filled with both shock and awe.
readings.hint: Actually you find out that he does have employment. He’s a garbage man. Feel the shock of making this shocking discovery. How can this drunkard possibly hold down his job like this?

meanings.mnemonic: Dirt shifts direction in winter , and instead of the usual stuff it starts to grow shellfish that are very extravagant . These shellfish are so extravagant because they can only be grown in the dirt in winter (after the dirt has shifted "direction" and started sustaining shellfish instead of plants, that is).
meanings.hint: Imagine tasting this incredible extravagance. Shellfish normally have a briny flavor, but these ones have earthy notes. Earthy shellfish?! I've never heard of something so extravagant. Try to imagine that extravagant, expensive flavor on your tongue.

readings.mnemonic: To make this experience even more extravagant , you eat them with a zei ber ( ぜい ), a German saber. A fork is just too plain for these extravagant shellfish — you gotta use a zeiber for maximum extravagance.
readings.hint: Imagine trying to use a zeiber to skewer an extravagant shellfish, then raise it to your mouth. You probably cut your lips a little bit, to be honest. It's all very impractical, but that's what it means to be extravagant.

meanings.mnemonic: If you can eat grain s while running up stairs , you'll excel at everything.
meanings.hint: This special workout routine is so hard that, if you can do it, you'll be able to excel at every other thing you try.

readings.mnemonic: In order to excel at this, though, you need a good pair of shoe s ( しゅう ).
readings.hint: You have to eat the grains AND run up the stairs, or you aren't actually excelling. Good shoes will help you get there faster and protect your toes.

meanings.mnemonic: A leader wishing to stop criminal s will write a moving haiku . The leader pleads with the criminals to stop their crimes, to look within, and to realize there's more to life (like poetry!).
meanings.hint: Unfortunately, the leader of the criminals also writes haiku, and they want all the criminals to keep doing crimes. Look inside and see how much you want what others have, write a haiku, and then steal!

readings.mnemonic: A haiku is still a hai ku ( はい ). Whether it's written by a law abiding leader or a criminal leader.
readings.hint: Just remember: a haiku is always a haiku.

meanings.mnemonic: You need to go up on the roof and use it like a slide seven times to make this your house . A house isn't a home until you've slid down its roof exactly seven times, you know.
meanings.hint: Imagine staring down at the ground as you prepare to use the roof of your house like a slide. I hope you aren't afraid of heights, because you have to do this seven times in a row.

readings.mnemonic: When you slide down the roof of your house , you end up with a tack ( たく ) in your butt cheek. All the roof shingles on your new home are attached with tacks, so it's honestly a miracle that you only got one tack stuck in you.
readings.hint: Feel the tack stab into your butt as you slide down your house's roof. Ow! Weirdly, though, it kinda makes you feel more at one with the house — like it's really your home now.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of the restaurant says, " Halt !" to everyone who wants to come in. The leader looks them up and down. Stares into their eyes. And then, maybe, they may go into the restaurant to eat.
meanings.hint: Imagine being told to halt in front of your favorite restaurant, then being turned away. Noooo! Better start sucking up to the restaurant leader.

readings.mnemonic: People only halt because they want this restaurant's world famous ta ter ( てい ) tots. Which is probably why there is a leader out front making sure only people who can really appreciate the tater tots can get in to eat them.
readings.hint: HALT! NO TATERS FOR YOU!

meanings.mnemonic: You place a cross on this drunkard 's clothes to show him you judge him. In fact, you yell, "I JUDGE YOU FOR BEING DRUNK AND WEARING THESE CLOTHES, TAKE THIS!"
meanings.hint: He yells back, "YOU CAN'T JUDGE ME, I CAN DO WHAT I WANT. GET THAT JUDGEY CROSS OFF ME!"

readings.mnemonic: As you judge each other, your cy borg ( さい ) comes stomping over and slams the drunkard against the wall. With your trusty cyborg coming to your rescue, you can continue to judge this guy all you want.
readings.hint: The cyborg joins in, "A judge would convict you if the fashion police brought you in. I have a bunch of meat and robot parts and even I'm better looking than you."

meanings.mnemonic: The sick sheep is itchy and it can barely sit still. Help the poor sheep!
meanings.hint: Imagine you were itchy but you couldn't scratch the spot. Now that you know how the sick sheep feels, give it a hand!

readings.mnemonic: Even though the sheep is itchy , look how kah-yu te ( かゆ ) it is! It’s not just cute. It’s kah-yute!
readings.hint: Say it out loud! Kah-yuuute!

meanings.mnemonic: Loiter ing around with a brush ? Sorry, but you're breaking the law . If you have a brush, that's okay. If you're loitering, that's okay. If you combine the two? Well... watch your back.
meanings.hint: Imagine a police officer coming for you. He's yelling at you because you have a brush and you're loitering. He grabs you, throws you to the ground, telling you what you're doing wrong.

readings.mnemonic: Where were you loitering with your brush, breaking the law ? Right outside the Ritz ( りつ ) Hotel. You'd think that it would be okay since you were staying there, but the law is the law.
readings.hint: Imagine the hotel staff watching as you get thrown to the ground. You yell "won't the Ritz do anything to help me??" They won't.

meanings.mnemonic: Take this road 's measurement . Hmm. It seems a bit too long. As you measure it, you continue down this road, wondering where it will lead you.
meanings.hint: Roads are really long, so I imagine it would take a long time to measure it (if you want to go from end to end). Imagine yourself pulling a tape measure along with you, all the while thinking, "Where will this lead me?”

readings.mnemonic: Where does the road that you're measuring lead to? To a dou ghnut ( どう ) shop!
readings.hint: What a perfect way to end this road measuring. You're hungry. Nom nom nom. Imagine eating your fill, and taste the doughnuts in your mouth. You just walked a long way!

meanings.mnemonic: The leather radical and the leather kanji are exactly the same! Cool!

readings.mnemonic: One way to get leather is to skin a male chicken, a cock ( かく ). Not a very efficient way to get leather, but one way nonetheless.
readings.hint: You won’t get much leather from a cock, but you could skin one in a pinch, should you really need a little extra leather.

meanings.mnemonic: Giving cleat s to a child carrying an umbrella is the best way to gather milk . You don't have any containers, so the child has to hold milk in the upside-down umbrella. The cleats are so the child can run back and forth to transport the milk around.
meanings.hint: See this child running in his cleats. Picture him holding the umbrella upside-down, milk sloshing all about as he runs.

readings.mnemonic: But where is this child exactly? He’s in New ( にゅう ) York City. What’s he doing in New York City you ask? Well, he's milking a cow. Why is there a cow to be milked in New York City? Good question. It’s the end of the world and New York City is the last remaining civilization. This is how people live now. Milk is important.
readings.hint: Imagine post-apocalyptic New York City. Milk is a hot commodity here so it’s important not to spill any of the milk. Imagine yourself carrying the milk in the umbrella and being careful not to spill any of it.

meanings.mnemonic: Wrap vines around a stool to obtain it. Don't worry about paying for it, sneak into the stool store and tie some vines around the legs. Then when you leave, pull the vines and the stool will come out after. That's how you obtain a stool!
meanings.hint: You can obtain a lot of goods this way without needing any cash. Just don't tell anyone I told you.

readings.mnemonic: Today you want to obtain a new pair of shoe s ( しゅう ). You tie your vines around the stool they're on, and slowly inch them toward the door.
readings.hint: You have to pull very slowly, or the shoes will fall off the stool. But you really, really want to obtain these particular shoes (they're really sweet), so you keep scooting them toward you.

meanings.mnemonic: The king can see the present time for what it really is. The future isn't important. The past isn't important. The present is when everything appear s.
meanings.hint: People are constantly asking the king to do things for them and solve their problems. But the king can see exactly what needs to be done at the present time, and what can wait until tomorrow.

readings.mnemonic: At the present time the king is Gen ji ( げん ). The time has finally come for him to get serious, and as they say, there's no time like the present.
readings.hint: Picture pretty Genji, carefully instructing his followers what to do at the present time and what can wait.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader put flowers on a cliff . That is their task . It is to provide and equip the cliff with flowers.
meanings.hint: Imagine a leader carrying flowers up to the top of a cliff. They are saying "I must provide more flowers to the cliff" as they climb up.

readings.mnemonic: Why do they provide and equip the cliff with flowers? It's all for the bee s ( び ).
readings.hint: "The bees must be equipped with more flowers. I will provide these flowers for the bees!"

meanings.mnemonic: You stop a spoon from moving by tying it with thread that's purple . The spoon wouldn't stop moving around, so you had to use your favorite purple thread to make it stop. It's the strongest thread you have.
meanings.hint: Imagine tying this spoon down with thread that's a color other than purple. Ah, the thread snaps. Darn, you better use your extra strong purple thread (the best color for strength!).

readings.mnemonic: To remember this reading, think about how the village you previously went to was all purple . It was the " 村先 " ( むらさき ) that you went to.
readings.hint: Imagine that everything is in purple in this village. Cars, houses, trees. Absolutely everything was purple.

meanings.mnemonic: A shellfish with a knife makes all the rule s. It isn't supposed to be making rules, but it has a knife, so who's going to argue?
meanings.hint: Picture a shellfish wielding a knife and shouting out new rules to all of the nervous people standing around it.

readings.mnemonic: The first rule the shellfish enacts is that all humans take off their sock s ( そく ) and give them to him. Hmm... is this some kind of nasty shellfish? Who would want socks?
readings.hint: Once everyone follows the rule, and the shellfish is on top of a pile of socks, he cackles, "It's easier to stab your toes with my knife now! Let's get to more rules!"

meanings.mnemonic: Take a tree and make two moon -shaped shelf units with it. Moon radical-shaped, that is. Those moon radicals standing side by side kinda look like a shelf, don't you think? The tree on the left is just there to remind you it's made of wood, I guess. A lovely wooden shelf.
meanings.hint: Imagine having some tall, moon radical-shaped shelves in your house. Where would you put them? Picture them there now. Honestly, they'd actually look pretty sophisticated.

readings.mnemonic: To match the moon radical shape, this would have to be a pretty tall shelf , meaning you can store a ton-a -stuff ( たな ) on it.
readings.hint: Think about the ton-a-stuff you're going to put on this tall shelf. Like, literally measure out a ton of things and put them on there. It can hold it, I'm sure…

meanings.mnemonic: How do you make a hole nice and big ? You gotta stab something in it. Just thrust it right in there. And make sure your movements are really sudden . Stabs and thrusts are more effective when they're sudden.
meanings.hint: Yeah, take that, you hole! Stab, thrust, stab! Wow, this hole keeps getting bigger thanks to all your stabbin'.

readings.mnemonic: Give that hole one last big stab , now you've totes ( とつ ) showed that hole who's boss. What did I tell you about the sudden thrust technique? It's totes super effective. Like, totes.
readings.hint: Remember "totes," that slang word for "totally"? It fell out of use almost as sudden as it appeared. Now it totes gives me a little stab of nostalgia whenever I hear it.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a bow and you want to make it long . To do that, you'd naturally just stretch it.
meanings.hint: Imagine holding this bow in your hands, and then stretching it into a longer bow. Feel the strain on your arms as you try to stretch wood. You can feel it stretching, but it's strenuous.

readings.mnemonic: As you stretch the bow, you hear someone running at you. "That's my bow you're stretching!" yells Mrs. Chou ( ちょう ). She is not happy that you're stretching her things. You should probably try to un-stretch it, put it down, and run before she beats you.
readings.hint: Now Mrs. Chou is hitting you with the stretched out bow. You cry and apologize, but she won't listen. "I'm going to stretch you, like you stretched my bow," she says. Then she does it.

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of a hundred forehead s in the heart of winter is superior at being gentle . These hundred foreheads are sensitive, especially in the heart of winter, so the leader must be very gentle. Not just any kind of gentle though—the superior kind of gentle that will keep a hundred foreheads following.
meanings.hint: As a leader, you are superior. You’re so gentle to these foreheads. See yourself caring for them in the winter. Feel the chill in the air as you wrap the foreheads in blankets and prepare heating pads for them.

readings.mnemonic: You need to be superior because all of these foreheads belong to you ths ( ゆう ). They don't know how to take care of themselves, though they try to convince you they can. They're too young to know any better.
readings.hint: Picture the youths trying to show you that they are the superior ones, then they sneeze, their eyes fill with tears, and you have to gently tuck them back into bed. Silly youths.

meanings.mnemonic: Riding a tsunami you see a turkey carrying a cross , but that’s pretty standard here. This is your dreamland. You always dream about tsunamis and the turkeys carrying crosses that ride them. This might be strange for other people, but for you it’s pretty normal.
meanings.hint: See this tsunami riding turkey in your mind. Hear it gobble loudly as it waves its cross around. Remember, this is standard fare for you. Nothing unusual here.

readings.mnemonic: These wacky dreams are only standard fare in June ( じゅん ) though. You don’t have them any other time of the year, just in June. You don’t know why June is the only time you have dreams about tsunami riding turkeys, but that’s just the way it is.
readings.hint: It’s usually pretty toasty in the month of June (in the Northern Hemisphere, at least) so imagine going to bed in this heat. Feel the spray of the refreshing tsunami water in your dream as the turkey continues to ride those tsunami waves with his cross.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami of shrimp crashes into a swamp . Now the swamp is filled with shrimp. They used to live in the ocean, but the tsunami has carried them to their new home in the swamp.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself in the swamp when the tsunami hits. Feel the water crash into you along with the shrimp. You’re all wet and shrimpy now.

readings.mnemonic: Your house was right next to the swamp and on this side of your house you had many a tack ( たく ) there to hang things on. Now, on every tack there is skewered a shrimp. The force of the tsunami drove them into the tacks on the side of your house.
readings.hint: Look at the side of your house. It’s covered with shrimp that have been impaled on the tacks. Perfect for dinner.

meanings.mnemonic: A drop of water falls from a bear onto the ground and needs to be wiped up with a towel . Only a teacher or an expert could get away with getting close to this wet bear to wipe up after it.
meanings.hint: The expert is also your teacher, and she's showing you how to clean up after this bear.

readings.mnemonic: The teacher tells you to start with something smaller, after all, you're not an expert yet. You have to practice on a wet shee p ( し ). "I became a wet, dangerous animal expert by starting with sheep, you will too!" your teacher says.
readings.hint: Your teacher assures you this wet sheep is far less dangerous to clean up after than a wet bear. They're the expert, so you should listen and wipe up after that sheep.

meanings.mnemonic: In the morning , if you want your hat to dry , you need to hang it on a tree trunk . In the morning, everything is covered in dew. The grass is wet, the leaves are wet, but the tree trunk isn't wet! It was protected by the rest of the tree, so you can hang your hat there to dry.
meanings.hint: Picture everything early in the morning covered in damp dew. Even your hat is uncomfortably wet. Now walk up to a large tree trunk and touch it—it's dry!

readings.mnemonic: You lean against the tree trunk , waiting for your hat to dry, and suddenly you realize Genghis Khan ( かん ) is on the other side of it. You look around and see that all of the tree trunks around you have Mongols drying their hats under them.
readings.hint: Now that you think about it, Genghis Khan was the one who taught people that tree trunks stay dry in the morning!

meanings.mnemonic: Put your hand over your eye to watch over others.
meanings.hint: You know that way cartoon characters and pirates watch out below them, with their hand shading their eye? That! Now imagine doing that right now.

readings.mnemonic: As you watch over your friends and neighbors you see movement in the distance. Oh no, it's Genghis Khan ( かん ) and his army!
readings.hint: Welp, nothing much watching over them will do now. Genghis Khan is probably going to take over your town, so I guess you can watch if you want but... I wouldn't.

meanings.mnemonic: There are people under a canopy on the street forming their own agency . They're banding together right here in the street, making a new government office !
meanings.hint: Imagine them putting together papers, talking to politicians, and standing in the street outside the capitol. They demand for this agency to be formed. They are doing all the correct, legal things to do it too.

readings.mnemonic:
This new agency is just starting to make progress when you hear a low rumble... then a screech.

"THIS IS MY STREET. NO NEW AGENCIES ON MY STREET!" Mrs. Chou ( ちょう ) cries as she barrels into everyone.
readings.hint: "Whenever you're in a 丁 , you're in my territory," she yells, taking out a few agency members with her fists. "You won't be starting any kind of agency here, not on my watch." And she lights it all on fire.

meanings.mnemonic: The moon shines on your roof in the evening and a snake suddenly appears! Wait, that's not a snake, it's your arm , sticking out from under the roof.
meanings.hint: Arms really look like snakes in the moonlight. Picture it. Your arm is long and shiny, just like a snake.

readings.mnemonic: Your arm is exposed to the moon and the outside and you feel a chill. Where are your clothes? Ah, you're n ude, eh ( うで )?! I guess it is pretty common to sleep in the nude, eh?
readings.hint: Your arms are nude, eh? That's why they look like snakes, you know. You should be more careful.

meanings.mnemonic: When you're a guest somewhere it's important to bring geoduck s. But more than that, the amount of geoducks you bring is really what matters. If you don't have any live geoducks go ahead and bring any amount of framed picture s of geoducks instead.
meanings.hint: If you're a guest you either have to have the right amount of geoducks or geoducks in framed pictures. Otherwise you're a jerk.

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, if you mess up the amount or the framed picture s, every other guest will gawk ( がく ) at you.
readings.hint: Feel the feel of hundreds of gawks. Feel ashamed as they gawk at you. How could you!?

meanings.mnemonic: The dirt where you just stand and see is a boundary . You don't want to cross the boundary, you just stand there and see over it. As a general rule, you can stand in the dirt and see beyond boundaries, but you don't usually cross them.
meanings.hint: Imagine a big line in the dirt where you stand and see over into the other side: that's a boundary. The boundary between your home and your neighbor's, the boundary between cities or countries… Some you can cross, and some you can't.

readings.mnemonic: The boundary you're looking at now is the Kyo to ( きょう ) boundary. Kyoto is one of the most famous places in Japan, so you can imagine its boundary is really clear and marked by all kinds of boundary signs that say KYOTO.
readings.hint: Picture yourself outside of Kyoto. Picture the boundary between you and Kyoto. Now hop over it!

meanings.mnemonic: Fire in nature will burn down everything in its path. In nature, there are no firefighters to contain the flames, so the blaze will burn until nothing's left.
meanings.hint: Feel the fire all around you as it burns. You're out in nature — the middle of a forest fire. Hear the woods crackle as they burn up around you.

readings.mnemonic: What is the worst thing that could burn in this fire? Your nen doroid ( ねん ) collection. You've spent a lot of time and money collecting all these cool and rare and unique nendoroids and now they've all burned in the fire.
readings.hint: Cry your eyes out as you smell the burning plastic of your nendoroids. Worst. Day. Ever.

meanings.mnemonic: You use your fingers at dawn to carry many things. You have to carry yourself out of bed. You have to carry your backpack or purse or laptop bag to school or work. Your fingers have to carry a lot of weight around at dawn.
meanings.hint: Think about your morning routine. You carry yourself into the bathroom and carry a toothbrush into your mouth. You carry your coat and bag out the door.

readings.mnemonic: Once you're carry ing everything, and the dawn sun is shining bright, you start getting tan ( たん ). Especially those fingers you're using to carry things.
readings.hint: Picture yourself carrying a coat and a bag, walking through the rice fields to work, with the hot, dawn sun shining down, making your fingers get tanner and tanner.

meanings.mnemonic: If a spirit blesses your older brother it's time to celebrate ! This isn't something that happens every day to everyone's older brother. Yours is very good and very lucky. So celebrate with him!
meanings.hint: Imagine what celebrating would be like. Imagine an older brother too, if you don't actually have one.

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, the two of you celebrate d too much, and you went a little crazy. The spirit grabbed your brother and shook ( しゅく ) him. Shook him so hard his head popped off.
readings.hint: Never celebrate too hard. Otherwise you'll always have to think about the time a celebration spirit shook your brother till his head fell off.

meanings.mnemonic: The American flag is the reason you need to deliver this package. The flag is inside the package, and you need to deliver it to the president. He lost his old flag, so now it’s up to you to deliver this new one to him. What more of a reason do you need?
meanings.hint: You need to deliver this flag to the president. Imagine yourself running as fast as you can while carrying the parcel in order to get it to him. Your lungs are tired and your legs burn, but you must deliver the flag as soon as possible!

readings.mnemonic: To deliver this flag is something you just have to do ( とど ). Need to do. Want to do. There is no one else to do it. This is something only you are equipped to do.
readings.hint: The world depends on you to do this delivery. If you fail, the United States will be ruined. You have to do it! Deliver that flag now! Run! Run!

meanings.mnemonic: You take your scooter to Korea and everything is different !
meanings.hint: Imagine scooting down a street in Korea and seeing how different literally every single thing is compared to what you're used to in Japan.

readings.mnemonic: What's also different is that there's a weird tiger bouncing on its tail alongside you yelling that its name is Tigger ( ちが ). You didn't know Tigger was living in Korea these days. That's definitely different.
readings.hint: Tigger is different in Korea too. For one, he's a white tiger now, and he's, like, really aggressive. Wait, was he always like that?

meanings.mnemonic: When a sheep tries its hoof at construction , it gains distinction . Now that is something that really sets a sheep apart! After all, have you ever seen a sheep working in construction?
meanings.hint: Note that the sheep has to sweep its tail to one side so that it doesn't get in the way of all the construction tools. That's why it looks a little different here. Imagine a sheep in a hard hat, with its tail on one side, getting down to some construction. How very distinctive.

readings.mnemonic: The extra distinction of this sheep is that its really skilled with a saw ( さ ). That's why it's so good at construction.
readings.hint: Imagine the sheep holding the saw in its hooves. It isn't easy to grip a saw with hooves, but this is no ordinary sheep.

meanings.mnemonic: There’s a flag under the blackjack table that you can kick in order to expand the table. Sometimes you only have a few people playing blackjack so you don’t need to kick the flag at all, and sometimes  there are many people playing blackjack so you need to kick the flag many times in order to expand it to a proper size.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself kicking this flag to see the blackjack table expand. Feel your foot kicking the flag. You’ve kicked it so many times that your leg is sore, but you need to expand the table some more so you keep kicking it. Imagine the blackjack table making that sound Mario makes whenever he gets a mushroom and expands in size.

readings.mnemonic: The blackjack table does have a limit though of how many times it will expand , and that limit is ten ( てん ). You can only kick the flag to get the table to expand ten times.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself kicking the flag ten times. Count it off in your head and imagine each instance up to ten. Any more kicks after ten don’t cause the table to do anything, but it does cause your leg to get sore.

meanings.mnemonic: You're about to enter a roof that has your number on it so you can face the judge . You've been sitting here for what feels like hours, waiting for your number to come up. Finally the time has come—the judge will see you now.
meanings.hint: Picture the judge sitting under the roof. Your number is sitting on his big desk, next to the gavel.

readings.mnemonic: The judge is not happy with you. He tells you to come closer and when you do, he takes his gavel and hits you in the shin s ( しん ). Ouch!
readings.hint: You try to ask the judge what his ruling is, but he just hits you on the shins again!

meanings.mnemonic: Behind the flag lies a mask —yet another layer of secrecy. You’re standing in front of a crowd, giving a rousing speech. Hiding your face is the flag of your country, and behind that flag you’re also wearing a mask, just in case the flag falls down. The extra layer of secrecy is just to be safe.
meanings.hint: These layers are meant to protect you. We all have layers we hide behind sometimes, you know? You can't be totally transparent with everyone.

readings.mnemonic: Someone jumps up on stage and starts peeling off your layer s! You stand there, first goes the flag, then the mask, then ... your sou l ( そう ) is laid bare. No, literally. You don't have a body, your soul was under that mask. And now it's floating there for all to see.
readings.hint: Everyone in the crowd is completely silent, staring at your soul, with all of its layers peeled off.

meanings.mnemonic: If you use your spirit to see something, it means you really look at it. You're not just seeing something passively with your eyes, you're actually looking at it with your whole spirit.
meanings.hint: Imagine using your whole spirit to look at something. Like, you feel your spirit flowing out from your eyes and enveloping whatever you're looking at. It's kind of trippy. When's the last time you really looked at something like that? Try it now.

readings.mnemonic: The first thing you want to look at like this is a shee p ( し ). You've seen a lot of sheep, but you've never really looked at them closely, so your image of them is kind of… woolly.
readings.hint: Look at a picture of a sheep now. Really look at it with all your spirit. Bet you start noticing some things you'd never seen before, right?

meanings.mnemonic: The king is showing his people how to put a net around a weird zombie by walking around it in a loop . It's a weird zombie because it has the ground on top of its head instead of dirt.
meanings.hint: Watch as the king loops the net around and around and around the zombie, keeping it completely pinned down.

readings.mnemonic: The king also explains that this loop technique was used by Genghis Khan ( かん ) when he was conquering cities. He'd loop around the entire place with a net and trap them.
readings.hint: Picture Genghis Khan looping around and around just like the king is looping around and around this zombie.

meanings.mnemonic: In an attempt to control your clothes , you manufacture a mind control device. This mind control device will allow you to control the mind of your clothes and all that they do. You just need to manufacture it.
meanings.hint: Imagine being able to control your clothes with your mind. What would you have them do? Imagine what you would do with your clothes if you could manufacture something to control them and move them around at will with your mind.

readings.mnemonic: You manufacture these clothes to control your sa ber ( せい ). It's hard to manufacture, you have to do a lot of work, but you manage to make your clothes control your saber. Now you don't need to rely on your hands!
readings.hint: Now you're trying to manufacture a machine that will make sabers and clothes you can use to control them. Soon you'll have a manufactured army of saber-controlling clothes!

meanings.mnemonic: On this scooter , there's an angel with a drop of sweat coming off of it, because it forgot to mention something to you! It seems like it's something important too, since they're sweating so much.
meanings.hint: The angel just visited you, but it forgot to mention something important (probably life-threatening, it's an angel after all), and now you see it scooting at top speeds toward you.

readings.mnemonic: "I forgot to mention , you have to study nin jutsu ( じゅつ )!" the angel yells. But it's too late, there are ninjas jumping out of the bushes around you and taking you hostage.
readings.hint: The angel really should have mentioned ninjutsu earlier. Then maybe you would have gone straight to a dojo to learn instead of falling into a ninjutsu trap yourself. The angel definitely failed its mission.

meanings.mnemonic: On the ground there's a ceremony where everyone keeps stop ping. This stopping ceremony is being performed by the military . They'll march one direction, then stop and turn, then march another direction, then stop again.
meanings.hint: The military really seems to have a thing for stopping a lot during their ceremonies.

readings.mnemonic: You look closer at the people in the military and realize that every time they stop they take a drink of boo ze ( ぶ )!
readings.hint: This is a ceremony for celebrating the military so I guess they can drink as much booze as they want, but this seems very strange to you.

meanings.mnemonic: There is a lantern on top of a knife in the dirt . You walk up to take the knife and lantern when you realize it's just a model . It all looked real from far away, but now that you're up close you can see none of it is real.
meanings.hint: Why would anyone make a model that looks like a lantern and a knife sticking into the ground?

readings.mnemonic: You touch the model and see that it's made of ca ke ( けい )! Someone really into lanterns and knives must have been given this model cake as a birthday present! Though they left it out here so they probably didn't like it.
readings.hint: Haven't you seen any of those crazy cakes modeled after things like movies, towns, giants hams, and purses? They're all the rage. Next time you make a cake try to make it look like a model lantern and knife with fake cake dirt and everything.

meanings.mnemonic: You have an animal for a husband with horns that are narrow . This animal husband of yours is a goat. A goat with narrow horns. Why is this animal your husband? You’re weird, that’s why.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself married to this animal husband with horns that are narrow. It’s a strange marriage. Listen to the goat making its goat noises as it clomps around your home. Now hug your goat husband and feel its soft fluffy coat against your skin.

readings.mnemonic: I’m not even going to get into the sema ntics ( せま ) of how this weird relationship of yours works. Nope, this reading explanation is on the straight and narrow . Need to know basis. Classified. All that junk. There will be no discussion of the semantics between you and your goat lover.
readings.hint: But that doesn’t mean you can’t imagine the semantics. The finer points of the relationship. Go ahead, imagine all the weird stuff that comes with the territory for having a goat as a husband. Use all that weird stuff to remember this kanji and its reading. Want an example? Just imagine the goat doing dishes! Goats don’t have hands! Hahahahaha!!

meanings.mnemonic: When a leader wants to work together , it means they want what you have to offer . Congrats! Now you can accompany them everywhere and offer them hot towels and bottled water, or whatever it is you do…
meanings.hint: Picture handing things to your leader as you accompany them somewhere. You're happy to do this, but you know you have so much more to offer than a hot towel and a pretty face. When will they see what you really have to offer?!

readings.mnemonic: Once a year, the leader will offer you a vacation in きょう と . It's an all-expenses-paid trip! The catch is that you have to accompany your leader to きょうと …
readings.hint: Picture you and the leader enjoying some matcha together under the cherry blossoms in きょうと . Yeah, this was definitely worth it. So glad you took them up on their offer and accompanied them.

meanings.mnemonic: On your bamboo roof is a bear . It is pulling the bamboo off, because it wants to use it to make a long pipe .
meanings.hint: Bamboo is great for pipes, and the only place for the bear to get it is your roof. So, that's why it's there.

readings.mnemonic: The pipe will be for Genghis Khan ( かん ), who would like some pipes for his house.
readings.hint: Watch the bear take away your bamboo. Follow it to Genghis Khan's house. There, the bear installs the pipe in Genghis Khan's bathroom.

meanings.mnemonic: You have a cross for a drunkard 's car , to place on its dashboard. Drunkards should not drive, and when you put on a cross, it prevents the car from starting!
meanings.hint: Imagine the power that starts emanating from the cross once it’s placed on the car, it can’t move even an inch. Nope, it's not going anywhere until its owner sobers up.

readings.mnemonic: You have a lot of crosses to place on drunkard cars, so you get your cy borg ( さい ) to do it for you. The cyborg doesn't need sleep, so it can patrol the streets 24/7 for the sake of public safety. What a saintly cyborg.
readings.hint: Picture your cyborg, approaching a drunkard's car to place a cross on it. You see it open a big compartment on its chest, and it's filled with crosses.

meanings.mnemonic: You have to use two axe s to open these shellfish . That seems like overkill, but it is necessary. These particular shellfish are of very high quality meaning they won't open so easily.
meanings.hint: Imagine the highest quality shellfish you've ever seen. They're hard, smooth, and basically impenetrable.

readings.mnemonic: You finally get all of the shellfish open and you're admiring their quality , when your shih tzu ( しつ ) jumps up on the table and gobbles them down. NO! You try to chase after the little dog, but it's too late. They're gone.
readings.hint: I guess your shih tzu knows good quality shellfish too. :(

meanings.mnemonic: When dawn reaches this village it increases in quantity . That's right. Every morning the amount of people or animals living here goes up by at least one.
meanings.hint: Picture dawn, the sun rising, the birds chirping, and the screams of a new born baby rise in the village. Or Maybe it's a new sheep! Or a new hat that someone just finished making.

readings.mnemonic: Today's dawn has brought with it an increase in the quantity of row ( りょう ) boats! Aww, aren't they cute? Look at all of the new row boats lined up together. They all have their own sets of oars too.
readings.hint: When every dawn brings a new amount of people, those new people are going to need that same amount of row boats.

meanings.mnemonic: A sudden aversion to shellfish makes you anti -shellfish, so you sell off every shellfish in sight.
meanings.hint: If you're serious about being anti-shellfish then you should sell all the ones you have.

readings.mnemonic: You're going to sell all of our shellfish to Han ( はん ) Solo. He isn't anti-shellfish like you, and he knows how to turn a profit too!
readings.hint: Picture yourself selling all of your shellfish to Han Solo. You stand there with your arms crossed, and Han just shakes his head at you and continues to toss them onto his spaceship.

meanings.mnemonic: A man who is husband material can see a standard woman and think she’s the greatest thing ever. This man will make a great husband. Even though the lady is pretty standard, the husband treats her like she’s anything but.
meanings.hint: This husband sets a new standard for what it means to be a husband. He’s pretty great. Imagine yourself shaking this husband’s hand because he’s so awesome.

readings.mnemonic: It’s pretty standard for a husband and wife to exchange vows when getting married, and the husband said this girl held the key ( き ) to his heart. She unlocked the husband’s heart with the key and now he’s a man made whole.
readings.hint: Picture this woman actually sticking a physical key into the husband’s chest and turning it. Now this is anything but standard, but it’ll help you remember this kanji and its reading.

meanings.mnemonic: You're stuck in a tree after using the evacuation slide to jump out of your jet — a stunt you pulled after hearing your stock s and share s had taken a dive in the market.
meanings.hint: Imagine lying battered and bruised in the upper boughs of this tree and thinking about all your worthless stocks and shares. Hopefully you had fun jumping out of your private jet, 'cause you can't afford to ride in it anymore.

readings.mnemonic: Your stock s and share s fell so hard that they probably went " KABOO M ( かぶ )!" when they hit rock bottom.
readings.hint: Imagine watching a real-time graph of your stock portfolio as its value plummets. The line dives toward the x-axis, and then… KABOOM! Not a cent of value left. Oh well… At least you had fun shouting "KABOOM" as your stocks crashed.

meanings.mnemonic: The door leading to the moon can be broken down with one’s shoulder . It’s not a very sturdy door to the moon, so it’s quite easy to drop your shoulder and ram it open.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself smashing through this door using your shoulder and seeing the moon on the other side. Hear the splintering of wood as your shoulder crashes through the door and feel the impact with your body.

readings.mnemonic: "Wow, your shoulder is just like my cata pult ( かた )!" you hear someone shout. As you turn you're just in time to see a man catapulting through his own moon door!
readings.hint: Now the weird man is inspecting your shoulder really closely and he keeps muttering "catapult powers" under his breath. It's very unsettling, but he's kind of right. A shoulder works just like the mechanism that winds up a catapult.

meanings.mnemonic: If, at a certain angle , you approach an insect , you’ll be able to touch it without it noticing. Most of the time, insects will run away from you when you try and touch them, but if you do so from the right angle, the insect will have no idea.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself approaching this insect from a sneaky and stealthy angle. Now touch it. What does it feel like? Smooth? Hard? Fuzzy? Feel the insect in your mind as you touch it.

readings.mnemonic: Uh oh, looks like this particular insect is a lightning bug so when you touch it you get a shock ( しょく ). Did you know that lightning bugs contain bazillions of volts inside them? Well they do, and if you touch one, you get a shock. A very big shock.
readings.hint: Feel this electrical shock to your system as you touch the lightning bug. This discourages you from ever touching another insect again. You don’t enjoy the shock.

meanings.mnemonic: What does a car and the Death Star have in common? They're both used to transport people and things.
meanings.hint: Imagine stuffing a bunch of Stormtroopers in your car so you can transport them across the country. Well the Death Star can transport even more Stormtroopers, faster, and through space!

readings.mnemonic: Right now you and the Death Star are transport ing yu rts ( ゆ ). They're the universe's #1 best transportable homes!
readings.hint: It's easy to transport a yurt even without a car or the Death Star.

meanings.mnemonic: The flag is old but that doesn't mean it's not still alive . This is a living flag, and it gets old just like other living things.
meanings.hint: Imagine the old flag with a face and mouth and eyes and nose... it talks and listens and everything else. It's a flag that's alive D:

readings.mnemonic: The flag that is old and alive is actually a Buddhist relic (that's why it's alive, in fact ... and old!). But, it's a really dumb relic, so it's put in Little Kyo to ( きょ ).
readings.hint: Not as cool as a real relic, it's put in this dumb Little Kyoto shrine that's basically just a tourist attraction. Eh, whatever.

meanings.mnemonic: You approach the Moon in a helicopter and see a woman there on the surface, touching her waist . She's posing with one hand on her waist, like she's Sailor Moon or something. I guess that would explain what she's doing here on the Moon!
meanings.hint: Imagine flying your helicopter closer to the woman. Even as you touch down she never takes her hand off her waist. You get closer and try to see why she's touching her waist like that.

readings.mnemonic: Once you get close, you see that her waist looks weird and… puffy? You realize she's stuffed some 子 shee p ( こし ) under her skirt's waistband to line her waist with them. Guess it's cold up here on the moon, so the 子 sheep keep her waist warm. And they had to be 子 sheep, otherwise they wouldn't be small enough to fit under her clothes.
readings.hint: Imagine how warm your waist would be if you stuffed a bunch of 子 sheep into your waistband. Just don't let anyone see you do it. Pretty sure that's animal cruelty…

meanings.mnemonic: When you ride your scooter , you always carry an ink brush and some water . The water is to stay hydrated, obviously, and the brush is to write tickets for the criminals you apprehend . Why apprehend? I guess you're some kind of thirsty, scooter-riding cop who writes tickets in calligraphy. By the way, the shape of this brush ( 肀 ) is a little different from usual, but it's still a brush!
meanings.hint: Imagine chasing a criminal for ages on your scooter, stopping only for the occasional water break, and finally apprehending them when they hit a dead end. How good would it feel to write a ticket with your fancy brush?

readings.mnemonic: When you do catch up and apprehend someone, you always tie them down with the tie ( たい ) from your neck.
readings.hint: It's a really sturdy tie you use to apprehend them, so even if they struggle there's no chance they get away.

meanings.mnemonic: The flag has a mantis perched just underneath it, and you know for a fact that it does not belong there. Insects should be in trees and on the ground. Not up high on a flagpole. Insects belong in the wild!
meanings.hint: Look at that mantis, pretending it belongs here. What are you gonna do about it?

readings.mnemonic: Luckily for you, and unluckily for this mantis who doesn't belong on your flag, you're a Boso zoku ( ぞく ) bike gang member, and you don't take crap from nobody. You climb the flagpole, punch the mantis and yell, "YOU DON'T BELONG HERE!"
readings.hint: Your Bosozoku life has prepared you for this moment. Those who don't belong shall be punished, you think to yourself.

meanings.mnemonic: If you could just get your soul to open the window to shellfish , you'll grow accustomed to them sooner or later! I know shellfish are a bit of an acquired taste, but if you don't open that window, you'll never like them!
meanings.hint: Close your eyes and picture your soul. Now your soul is opening a little window inside of you that is the perfect size for shellfish of all kinds. Hopefully with exposure and acceptance you'll grow accustomed to them.

readings.mnemonic: You'll need to become accustomed to eating shellfish if you want to grow up big and strong like Genghis Khan ( かん ). He didn't have shellfish growing up either you know, he had to grow accustomed to their taste too, but look how successful he was!
readings.hint: Force yourself to get accustomed to shellfish like Genghis Khan, then maybe you'll rule most of the planet someday.

meanings.mnemonic: You have to fly in a helicopter and use a jackhammer to punch your ballot . The helicopter gets you far away from prying eyes, and the jackhammer makes you really try for whoever you're voting for on that thing. It's foolproof!
meanings.hint: Imagine trying to fill out your ballot while flying in a helicopter and using only a jackhammer to do it. Do you think you could do it? Do you think you could do it AND vote for the right person?

readings.mnemonic: While using the jackhammer on the ballot , the helicopter pilot keeps yelling “ Heyo ( ひょう ), don't mess up! Heyo! Watch out, don't let me see!” as he flies the helicopter all over the place.
readings.hint: You're starting to think this method of filling out ballots is rigged.

meanings.mnemonic: Use your fingers to grab this guy's foot and catch him! Not physically though, you do grab his foot, but you close your eyes, and catch the idea of him in your mind just by touching your fingers to his foot.
meanings.hint: You don't really like the idea of touching your fingers to someone's foot, so you do it ever so slightly, and only catch the gist of what it's like.

readings.mnemonic: As you touch his foot, you catch a glimpse of the Torah ( とら ) in your mind. Suddenly, you are able to grasp and understand every single thing written in the Torah. You fall to the ground.
readings.hint: You wipe your eyes and look up, seeing that the person running from you is wearing socks with little pictures of the Torah on them. You were able to catch that in your brain but not the person. You must have touched those Torah socks and not his foot. You've been tricked!

meanings.mnemonic: Use your fingers at your wedding to catch anything thrown at you. Catch the bouquet, for example, or catch the rice everyone throws at you. Weddings are really more about catching things with your fingers than they are about whoever is standing across from you.
meanings.hint: Imagine being at a wedding and getting to catch a ton of things with your fingers.

readings.mnemonic: You're having a good time catch ing things until it's cleanup time and someone throws a hoe ( ほ ) at you. First of all, ouch, that was your face. Second, this is your wedding. You aren't supposed to have to hoe it all up. You're supposed to drop everything you caught and leave!
readings.hint: Picture yourself grumpily hoeing all the things you couldn't catch, right into a big dumpster.

meanings.mnemonic: A leader with a stick , a hook , and an arrow is able to change the climate and weather . Not just the type of climate that causes sunny and rainy days though, the political climate too, because they're a political candidate too.
meanings.hint: Imagine this leader twirling his stick around with his hook so quickly it changes the actual weather. Then he shoots it into the sky with an arrow, changing the actual climate itself.

readings.mnemonic: Who is this climate and weather -changing candidate leader with the hook, stick, and arrow? It's none other than こう いち .
readings.hint: Ask こういち why he's changing the climate. He says more people do their WaniKani reviews when it's raining, so he decided to create climate change to make it more dangerous to go outside. "This way, people will stay indoors and do their WaniKani reviews," he tells you.

meanings.mnemonic: There’s a tsunami coming and your older brother is in no condition to escape. Your older brother has brittle bone syndrome so there’s no way he’ll be able to escape the tsunami in time in his condition.
meanings.hint: Imagine your poor brother and the condition he is in. His glass bones and paper skin make it impossible for him to move very fast. Imagine the sad face he makes when he realizes his fate.

readings.mnemonic: His condition gets worse and you have to travel all the way to his home in きょう と to get him out.
readings.hint: This is also, incidentally, where the tsunami's condition is the worst, so you better get to きょうと quick!

meanings.mnemonic: If you mix different types of power you'll produce something effective .
meanings.hint: It doesn't matter what power you mix, because all power is strong. No matter what you use this mixed power on, it will be effective. Imagine some situations where you mix power with power.

readings.mnemonic: The most effective mix of two powers is two こう いち s. Take one, mix it with another, and you have something with immeasurable power. You use it to attack that animal-like creature. It's super effective!
readings.hint: こういち s punch attack was super effective against that Pikurchort.

meanings.mnemonic: The criminal that stole your car was your comrade ! You thought the two of you were friends, but you were probably wrong.
meanings.hint: Just because someone is your comrade doesn't mean they actually like you. Don't let your guard down and let them near your car.

readings.mnemonic: You feel really weird about this whole situation so you write your comrade a hai ku ( はい ). It's really all you can do, since your car is long gone.
readings.hint: Comrade, what the heck I want to get my car back I thought we were friends.

meanings.mnemonic: If you poop on the root s of a building , the sound will echo all the way through the building, and each and every person in there will know what you did.
meanings.hint: Hear the poop echo. You know what a poop usually sounds like, now imagine that sound again, but louder and with an echo. How could anyone not notice?

readings.mnemonic: The echo doesn't stop there, this building's roots extend throughout the entire city of きょう と . The echo reverberates and soon everyone in きょうと is listening, horrified, to your poop.
readings.hint: Welp. きょうと knows you pooped. That will echo through your memories for the rest of your life. I hope you're happy.

meanings.mnemonic: Sometime in the future, thread of every color will be faced with extinction . Eventually there will be nothing else to make thread out of, and thread of every kind and color will reach extinction.
meanings.hint: Imagine what the world would be like without thread. No thread of any color. How would you fix tears in clothes or make new ones? It would be a strange world where thread is extinct.

readings.mnemonic: The extinction of thread was brought on by a very strange thing. Deadly zits ( ぜつ ). These zits killed off all the producers of thread, and eventually the thread went extinct. Dang zits.
readings.hint: Pretend you have deadly zits. They’re everywhere, not just your face. You can’t pop them or you’ll die, but they’re all bulging and extremely painful. Now you’re facing extinction. Lousy.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami causes the capital to become very cool . The capital was already a pretty cool temperature, but with the addition of the water from the tsunami, the capital is now very cool.
meanings.hint: Imagine being in the capital. The tsunami hits and now you are all wet. You are also very cool because the water is not warm. You are cool and wet. How sad.

readings.mnemonic: You find out that the cool tsunami water is coming from Sue Zoo ( すず ). The only animal there is a penguin named Sue, so the Sue Zoo is always kept cool for her. But now that Sue's fountain broke, the cool water is everywhere! Time to sue the zoo…
readings.hint: You're still feeling pretty cool and wet when another wave courses by. You see Sue surfing on it, screaming at the top of her lungs, "FREEDOM!!! NOW EVERYWHERE IS SUE ZOO!!!"

meanings.mnemonic: If a tsunami hits these flowers they'll both become full of water. Tsunamis carry a lot of water, definitely enough to make both of these flowers full.
meanings.hint: Picture both of these little flowers, happily swaying in the breeze. Then the tsunami comes, leaves, and you look back and their little cup-heads are full to the brim with water.

readings.mnemonic: As you stare at the full flowers, you decide to write a man ga ( まん ) about them. Your head is full of manga ideas!
readings.hint: Soon you have full color pages, full of stories about both of these tsunami-filled flowers and their adventures together. And when you're done, your heart is full as well. Awwww.

meanings.mnemonic: A beggar lies sprawled on the ground so you give him some money. You feel bad for the beggar on the ground, so that’s why you decided to give him something.
meanings.hint: See the beggar on the ground. He’s writhing in agony and moaning for help. Imagine how sad and pitiful he looks.

readings.mnemonic: When you set the money down next to the beggar he shouts, “No! I want a yo -yo ( よ )!” so you go to the corner store and buy him a yo-yo with the money instead. You give the yo-yo to the beggar and he looks very happy.
readings.hint: Now watch as the beggar plays with the yo-yo you just gave him. He’s actually pretty good with it. See the beggar doing insane tricks with his new yo-yo as passersby give him change for his impressive yo-yo performance.

meanings.mnemonic: The fish and sheep are very fresh here. This is a great place to get fresh fish and fresh sheep. I kid you not.
meanings.hint: Imagine eating this fresh fish and fresh sheep. It’s the freshest you’ve ever tasted. Taste it on your tongue and experience how fresh it is.

readings.mnemonic: This place also serves fresh cen taur ( せん ), but that’s just weird. Centaur is endangered and you don’t believe in eating endangered things. Even if they are incredibly fresh.
readings.hint: Can you imagine eating a fresh centaur? How weird would that be? Half of it is human-like, so you’d be like, a half cannibal. Weird, huh?

meanings.mnemonic: You have a greenhouse that is endless . Imagine your endless greenhouse, which goes on and on, as far as the eye can see. Lucky you.
meanings.hint: What will you grow in your endless greenhouse? Imagine all your favourite flowers and foods growing endlessly in your endless greenhouse.

readings.mnemonic: Your flowers have grown so big that now the only way you can fit into your endless greenhouse is by lying on your back ( ばく ).
readings.hint: Imagine yourself lying on your back in your greenhouse under a canopy of great big flowers, tending to them as they grow even bigger.

meanings.mnemonic: Behind a building , a cleat -wearing Wolverine is holding his heart and trying to hide .
