meanings.hint: You know the phrase, "You can run, but you can't hide?" Wolverine can't run anymore, so he's trying to hide (even though he knows it probably won't work).

readings.mnemonic: Wolverine looks up at the building he's trying to hide behind and sees it's an inn ( いん ).
readings.hint: Lucky Wolverine! Inns are good for hiding. The staff isn't supposed to give out his room number, and there are tons of different rooms for him to hide in.

meanings.mnemonic: Someone covered your fingers with dirt and also got dirt on your toe s. So you're going to hang them upside-down and slap them with your dirty extremities.
meanings.hint: If someone got dirt all up in your finger and toe business, wouldn't you want to hang them upside-down too?

readings.mnemonic: You hang this person upside-down but they don't seem to be suffering enough, so you hit them with your ca r ( か ). No mercy.
readings.hint: Don't get dirt on me, buddy. Or I'll hang you upside-down and hit you with my car. Then you'll be sorry.

meanings.mnemonic: A tree grows next to a greenhouse enclosing an imitation of the tree. The imitation greenhouse tree wants to be big and impressive like the tree outside, so it does its very best imitation of a real tree.
meanings.hint: Look at this greenhouse tree struggle to grow big and strong like the wild tree outside. Hear its leaves shake and timber creak. How cute.

readings.mnemonic: The greenhouse tree realizes that its imitation will never be as good as the real thing. When it realizes this, the tree mo ans ( も ) aloud. The tree is still kinda tiny, so the moan isn’t very loud, but it is still pretty strange to witness. Speaking of witnesses, Bo bo ( ぼ ) the clown sees this greenhouse tree moaning and decides to take it for his next clown show. Surely this miniature moaning tree can be worked into Bobo's next clown act.
readings.hint: Hear the little tree moaning at its pitiful imitation of a real tree. The greenhouse tree sounds very sad and you actually start to feel bad for it. Moan along with the tree in sympathy.

meanings.mnemonic: Now your mouth can include more words than ever before! With our new and improved “Mouth Now!” program, your mouth will include more words than you ever thought possible!
meanings.hint: Imagine how your repertoire of new words will impress all your friends and family. Don’t include them on your secret that you used the Mouth Now! program, just feel super smart with all the words you now know.

readings.mnemonic: If you buy now, we’ll also include a handful of books by Gan dhi ( がん ) himself. Gandhi didn’t use our program, but we’re sure if he’d had the opportunity to, he would have used it. So buy now, and get the words of Gandhi in your hands as well as your mouth!
readings.hint: Since you bought the program, you also got the Gandhi books included with your order. Imagine yourself reading them and learning all about non-violent resistance. Fascinating.

meanings.mnemonic: Be careful what you say in public , otherwise you might face a lawsuit . People will sue you for just about anything these days, so you really need to watch what you say in public, otherwise you might find yourself with a lawsuit on your hands.
meanings.hint: Think of all the things you could say in public that could get you stuck dealing with a lawsuit.

readings.mnemonic: Any and all lawsuit s need to be brought before the Shou gun ( しょう ). Oh, you thought you'd get a judge or a jury? Nope, the Shougun decides your fate.
readings.hint: You better hope what you said wasn't about the Shougun, or he's really going to use this lawsuit to destroy you.

meanings.mnemonic: A building 's root s will limit the things under and around it.
meanings.hint: Picture the roots of a building: metal beams going into the ground. Now imagine trying to build something like a subway under there. It's not good! The building limits what can go under it.

readings.mnemonic: The person who hates these limit s more than anyone else is Gen ji ( げん ). He wants to be able to tunnel under buildings, sneak in, smooch ladies, and sneak back out. But all the building root limits make that impossible!
readings.hint: Picture Genji tunneling under a building where a cute lady lives. He's trying to get in, but the building roots limit how far he can dig. No! Now how will he steal a secret kiss?!

meanings.mnemonic: A moon who stays in bed all day every day will soon become obese . A healthy moon needs movement and exercise to stay fit and not become obese. If a moon stays in bed all day, they’ll be sure to grow obese over time.
meanings.hint: Imagine this moon in bed, slowly getting bigger and bigger, becoming more obese every day. Hear the sound of the bed creaking and cracking as the moon grows larger and larger.

readings.mnemonic: Since the obese moon is in bed all day, and since the moon stays pretty cold, she needs some extra he at ( ひ ) to stay warm. A heated blanket should do the trick.
readings.hint: Imagine the obese moon cozied up in bed with a heated blanket and hot cocoa.

meanings.mnemonic: Music beans make farts plentiful . And lucky for you, you have an abundant stock of these music beans, so you can eat plenty of them to power your musical bean toots.
meanings.hint: Imagine dancing to your own plentiful bean music as you gorge on the abundant stock of beans.

readings.mnemonic: Your music bean farts are too plentiful to leave your ho me ( ほう ). If you tried to leave your home you'd definitely fart all over people outside, so you keep your plentiful farts to yourself.
readings.hint: Be polite! Keep your plentiful farts at home.

meanings.mnemonic: A Viking with enough tree s will prosper . Vikings need trees to build their boats and homes, and they flourish when they have enough.
meanings.hint: Picture a village of Vikings, flourishing and prospering because they live right on the edge of a forest they can just hack away at whenever they need a new ship.

readings.mnemonic: Vikings knew that in order to prosper they also needed to collect a corns ( えい ). If you're chopping down trees all the time, you need to actively grow them back too. That's why acorns are the actual, secret key to Viking prosperity.
readings.hint: Picture a bunch of kid Vikings planting acorns in rows and watching them flourish and grow into big trees that they can cut down to keep up their Viking ways.

meanings.mnemonic: Touch your fingers to your friend to pull out their friendship. Pull it out with a high five. Pull it out with a back rub. Pull it out with an, "Oh no, there's some lint on your shirt, let me get that for you."
meanings.hint: REMINDER: You are not allowed to touch your friends without their permission. If they say no to you pulling out their friendship, then you don't get to pull out their friendship. End of story.

readings.mnemonic: Your friend looks you in the eye and says, I want you to pull out my hair. I want my head to be nu de ( ぬ ). Now you have to use your fingers on your friend to pull out their hair and make their head all nude.
readings.hint: REMINDER: If someone asks you to pull out their hair to help them achieve a nude head, you don't have to do it unless you want to! Choices and respect, people!

meanings.mnemonic: A husband marries a husband while the sun goes down and the men replace their solitary lives with one of everlasting companionship. Instead of one husband and one wife, we have two husbands here. They shed their lives of bachelorhood and replace it with one of monogamy.
meanings.hint: See these two husbands as the sun sets under them. Listen to them each saying their vows, expressing how they could never replace one another.

readings.mnemonic: After the ceremony, the husband and his husband get in a ca r ( か ) and drive off into the sunset. They’ll never be able to replace this memory with anything. Truly it is the happiest day of these husbands’ lives.
readings.hint: See the husbands driving off into the sunset in their car. Hear the engine rumble as the car speeds off into the distance.

meanings.mnemonic: Your thread s have to suit you if you want a good salary . No one is going to give you a salary if your clothes don't suit you. You have to look professional!
meanings.hint: When you see someone wearing a fancy suit that suits them, don't you wonder about how much their salary is?

readings.mnemonic: Your salary is paid in cu cumbers ( きゅう ). Maybe you shouldn't have worn that cucumber tie when you interviewed for this job, even though it suited you well.
readings.hint: Imagine your salary being paid in cucumbers. Open that sweet paycheck and watch the cucumbers come tumbling out.

meanings.mnemonic: The sun rises over the capital , creating a scene of breathtaking beauty. The sun is bright and warm as it crests over the capital. It’s quite the scene to behold.
meanings.hint: Picture this scene in your mind. Squint as the sun shines in your eyes, and feel its warmth as it rises up over the capital.

readings.mnemonic: The only way to make this scene better would be to add ca ke ( けい ). Now that you think about it, as the sun rises and frosts the capital with light, the buildings start to look like big, shiny cakes.
readings.hint: Picture this scene again, but this time the sun is making the buildings of the capital look like big, fancy cakes. Now take a bite!

meanings.mnemonic: A gladiator only sees himself ( oneself ) on those big scroll s they advertise the fights with. Gladiators don't get mirrors and there are no cameras or videos yet. The scroll drawings are all they get of themselves.
meanings.hint: Imagine being a gladiator. Your big fight is coming and you're the  main event! Look at the scroll for the fight and try to find yourself.

readings.mnemonic: When you look at the scroll you realize... you look just like Genghis Khan ( かん )! You excitedly fist pump and start showing everyone how cool you are.
readings.hint: After a while of showing off the scroll, someone finally pulls you aside and says, "That guy isn't you, it's the real Genghis Khan. He's coming to the event and he's against you. So. Nice knowing you."

meanings.mnemonic: A tree is what a lifeguard uses to set up the station where he'll care for the people on the beach and in the water. Trees provide the shade lifeguards need to take care of half-drowned kids and grannies with sand stuck in their bathing suits. That's why they set up their care stations here.
meanings.hint: Watch the lifeguard walk around the beach. He needs to pick a nice big tree for his set up, so he can provide the best care possible.

readings.mnemonic: You look closer at the set up and realize the lifeguard is こう いち ! You know he teaches Japanese but... is こういち really qualified to care for people like this?
readings.hint: Imagine こういち setting up a care station. You hope you don't get in trouble in the water so you don't have to see how good he is at caring for people.

meanings.mnemonic: You want to get your fingers on the humble stool , so you dedicate your life to search for it. Wait, the humble stool? You know, like the holy grail or the ark of the covenant, but it's called the humble stool. It's from some bible or something, I promise.
meanings.hint: Now you have to search through a bunch of religious texts for this humble stool with your fingers. Don't go too fast and get paper cuts!

readings.mnemonic: You search and you search and, unfortunately, you spend your entire life searching and you die without finding the humble stool. But don't worry, your sou l ( そう ) comes back and now you get to continue your search but with afterlife powers!
readings.hint: You dedicated your soul to searching, and now that your soul is free from its body, you can continue the search until you find it!

meanings.mnemonic: You say you have an axe with a drop of blood on it. You hit someone with that axe and now they're going to sue you. That drop of blood on your axe is pretty incriminating, which is why they have enough of a case to sue you.
meanings.hint: Imagine being unable to stop yourself from saying "I have the axe with the drop on it!" and the recently axed person jumps out from behind a bush and shouts, "Aha! I'm gonna sue you!"

readings.mnemonic: You want to sue them back so you clean the axe with some so ap ( そ ). "Your honor," you say in court, "I don't see any drops of blood, I countersue!"
readings.hint: The person you axed cries and tries desperately to find their blood on the axe so they can keep suing you properly. You hold the soap behind your back and cackle.

meanings.mnemonic: The sun rises over the capital , shines down on your hair , and casts a long shadow on the ground in front of you.
meanings.hint: Feel the heat of the sun on your back as you look down over your capital, your long hair blowing over your shoulder. Your shadow stretches in front of you and it's honestly pretty impressive.

readings.mnemonic: In the shade from your shadow you see a small a corn ( えい ) laying on the ground. You're about to reach down and pick it up when you realize that your shadow is keeping this little acorn from growing up big and strong.
readings.hint: What do you do? Keep standing here, admiring the view and casting this shadow on the acorn. Or do you move and let the acorn grow, becoming a tree that can cast its own shadow on your capital.

meanings.mnemonic: The outfit you wear at your wedding is a supplement to the occasion. It's not as important as the vows or the kiss or anything, but a sharp tux and a gorgeous dress really supplement the festive mood and get everyone into "wedding mode."
meanings.hint: Imagine getting married in a t-shirt and sweatpants. Sure, you could do it, but wouldn't you feel like something was missing? Like you needed to supplement the situation a little?

readings.mnemonic: You decide to further supplement your wedding outfit with a garden hoe ( ほ ). This hoe is a symbolic supplement — it represents the new life you'll "cultivate" together with your partner.
readings.hint: This hoe can also be used to pull back the bride's veil — one of its many supplemental uses.

meanings.mnemonic: A thread is mix ed all around and begins to strangle you.
meanings.hint: Just imagine thread everywhere, mixing in with everything. It's all around, and no matter where you go you get more and more caught in this thread. Eventually it tightens because you're trying to escape, and you feel yourself having trouble breathing. Imagine the moment when you realize you're getting strangled.

readings.mnemonic: As you are strangle d you see who set this trap. (What else could it be, with all this thread everywhere?) It is こう いち , who just watches you as you lose consciousness, tapping his fingers together.
readings.hint: In your final moments before you are totally strangled, you think you see a little thread come out of his butt. "I should have done my WaniKani reviews" is your last thought.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami carries your yurt and you on a stool under it away. You are now in transit .
meanings.hint: Imagine being carried away on your stool by the tsunami, with your entire yurt floating around you too. You're both in transit... to somewhere. You don't know where though.

readings.mnemonic: The transit of yourself and your yurt home is due to the water ( わた ). This is water transit.
readings.hint: You hope the tsunami's water will drop you off somewhere nice. Somewhere it's easy to jump on a different kind of mass transit, to get back home again.

meanings.mnemonic: Your fingers touch as you stand with a woman and adjoin yourselves. You are united, you are one.
meanings.hint: Look around you. Other women are adjoining their fingers and standing with you. There's hundreds, no, thousands of you standing together.

readings.mnemonic: All the women adjoin in sets ( せつ ). Sets of two, of course. One woman with another woman, standing in unison.
readings.hint: Picture a bunch of women standing together. They're holding hands in sets, adjoined together.

meanings.mnemonic: There once was an animal with an insect friend who felt very alone unless he was around. An animal and an insect are an unlikely pairing, but these two get along delightfully and hate to be alone without the other.
meanings.hint: Imagine this animal and this insect. Let’s say it’s a flea and a dog. Imagine them going everywhere together. To the park, getting ice cream, even the drive in movie theater. They’re inseparable.

readings.mnemonic: One activity that the animal can never do alone is solve Su doku ( どく ) puzzles. The insect always has to help him out with them, and together they are an unstoppable Sudoku solving team.
readings.hint: Picture this animal with the insect on his head doing a Sudoku. See the insect hopping around on the animals head shouting answers and suggestions into the animal’s ear. How cute.

meanings.mnemonic: Under the ground , a head covered in dirt emerges... again ! It's not the first time you've seen this buried head, nor is it the last. This dirt-covered head hides beneath the ground wherever you go, and keeps popping up to scare you again and again.
meanings.hint: Picture the dirt-covered head popping up again and again. How can it move under the ground like that if it's just a head? You decide to get closer this time, and for once it speaks: "Get me out of the ground!"

readings.mnemonic: The dirt-covered head asks for help again and again, but you've only got a saw ( さ ). So you start digging with it, scraping the dirt away, until you see this head has the body of a cy borg ( さい )!
readings.hint: Imagine how dirty you and your saw are after all this. At least you could help this cyborg with your saw. "Thanks again," it says, then runs off shaking the dirt out of its hair.

meanings.mnemonic: On the grass near a rice paddy sits a creeper that is some sort of a dog beast , a vicious animal . This creeper isn’t just a normal dog, it is more beast-like, more like a feral animal than domesticated pet.
meanings.hint: Picture this beast creeping, low to the ground, in the grass around the rice paddy. It's got its eyes on you. Uh oh.

readings.mnemonic: The beast is guarding its precious jew els ( じゅう ), which it buried in the grass or the rice paddy somewhere. If you don't want the beast to think you're coming to steal those jewels, you better keep your distance.
readings.hint: All beasts have something they're trying to protect. In this case, it's some beastly jewels.

meanings.mnemonic: You take some flowers and some fruit and you make a cake out of it. It’s a very nice looking, flavorful cake. The fruit makes it taste great and the flowers make for great cake decorations.
meanings.hint: Look at this cake you have made. It’s majestic. Now taste some of it. It is one great tasting cake. Imagine yourself eating the best cake in all of ever.

readings.mnemonic: You’re standing back, admiring your cake when you hear a ca r ( か ) engine revving and before you know it your cake has been run over. The car came out of nowhere and ran your cake right over, spraying fruit cake bits and flowers all over the place. Stupid car!
readings.hint: Hear the roar of the car engine as you get splattered in the face with the cake. Wipe it off of your face just in time to see the car speeding off into the distance. Also, recall that this kanji shares a reading with the similar kanji, 果 .

meanings.mnemonic: They say using a measurement device (like a ruler) is a good way to chastise someone. They do something bad? Chastise them by slapping their wrists, their feet, their eyes, ears, anything! They say something out of turn? CHASTISE THEM!
meanings.hint: Imagine being chastised with a measurement device. Think how horrible it would be getting hit on your knuckles, your nose, or the back of your head. It would be awful. Imagine this experience.

readings.mnemonic: You're being chastise d because you tried to say something in English in とう きょう . Everyone around you slowly turned around, eyes aflame and rulers in hand, ready to chastise you for uttering those forbidden words.
readings.hint: How could you say something in English while you're in とうきょう ?! After you went to all the trouble to learn all this kanji and Japanese? Geez, we almost think you deserve to be chastised...

meanings.mnemonic: The old winter is to blame for today's circumstance . It is the reason things are bad now.
meanings.hint: Imagine a poor, broken town. And, imagine the mayor of a town telling their people that things are bad because of that bad old winter. "It's to blame for our circumstance!" he yells.

readings.mnemonic: In reality, this circumstance was actually caused by a single 子 ( こ ), though. This kid went through town, destroying everything in their path.
readings.hint: Imagine your kid character walking through town, using their super powers to destroy buildings and rot food.

meanings.mnemonic: Your new car doesn’t mix well with the other cars in your garage because its color is in stark contrast to the others. All of your other cars are pristine white in color, but this new car is a mix of lots of colors. It almost looks like a peacock or something it’s so colorful. Such a contrast.
meanings.hint: Imagine this garage of yours with a sea of white cars. Now there is a new car that really sticks out like a sore thumb. Imagine yourself looking out over your cars and seeing this wacky car practically jumping out at you.

readings.mnemonic: What did I say this car looks like? Oh that’s right, a pea cock ( かく ). This car looks like a peacock. It’s the color of a peacock and you’ll look like you’re peacocking when you’re out on the town driving it. It really is such a stark contrast to like, every other car out there.
readings.hint: See yourself driving around in this peacock car. Hear the engine purr as strangers jeer and yell at you. This car sure does get a lot of attention, doesn’t it?

meanings.mnemonic: "A scooter !" you announce . You are announcing this because you decided this is what you would create .
meanings.hint: Imagine being at a maker's contest. Each person has to announce loudly what they'll create, then they have to create it.

readings.mnemonic: But, a scooter has been create d before. What is different about yours? The scooter you will create is specifically designed to keep zo mbies ( ぞう ) busy.
readings.hint: Imagine the scooter you created with a zombie on it. It's a perfect place for the zombie to get stuck and scooted off into nowhere.

meanings.mnemonic: In your warehouse you use your knife to create . You keep old pieces of wood in your warehouse, and you use your knife to carve them into new things that only you could create.
meanings.hint: What do you create in your warehouse? Picture all the different things you could create in your mind.

readings.mnemonic: When you create it's good for your sou l ( そう ). It's so freeing, in fact, that when you get really into your creating, sometimes you'd swear your soul was floating a little bit outside of your body. But every time you notice, it sinks back down. Hmm.
readings.hint: When you create something your soul is happy! It's good for you, you know?

meanings.mnemonic: The " Cliff of Ten Thousand Power " is where you go if you want to be encourage d.
meanings.hint: If you can get to the top of the cliff, it will use its "ten thousand power" to encourage you.

readings.mnemonic: When you get there, it does encourage you. The cliff says "Hey, I encourage you to buy a ray ( れい ) gun."
readings.hint: A cliff is encouraging you to buy a ray gun for some reason. This is not the encouragement you expected, but it is encouragement nonetheless. You go back down the cliff and immediately buy a ray gun.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami that is white , traveling in the direction of winter is a fierce and violent force of nature. This white tsunami is chasing after winter, and that’s why the tsunami is white—it’s a winter tsunami. It’s the most fierce and most violent kind of tsunami in existence.
meanings.hint: Imagine this white tsunami, more violent and fierce than any other tsunami you’ve ever seen, coming toward you. It’s very frightening. Feel the cold wetness it brings to your skin.

readings.mnemonic: The tsunami is too fierce , too violent , and before you realize what's happening, Gecki ( げき ), your beloved gecko, is lifted up and carried off into the air.
readings.hint: "Geecckkkiiiiii!!!" you scream in horror, as he's thrown about by the fierce, winter tsunami. You have to close your eyes. You can't watch Gecki go.

meanings.mnemonic:
The fortune radical and the fortune kanji are exactly the same! This can also mean occupy , maybe because learning your fortune shows you how you're going to occupy your life. Or how it will occupy you…

You haven't seen the radical in a while, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember the kanji: Put your toe in your mouth to learn your fortune . The taste will tell you everything you need to know about your fortune. But your toe has to occupy your mouth first for it to work.
meanings.hint: Imagine what your toe tastes like. Or actually taste it, I don't care. What do the taste and texture of it say about you, about your future fortune?

readings.mnemonic: To occupy some free time you have, you go and get your fortune told at a fortune teller. Your fortune is that you’ll have an encounter with a cen taur ( せん ) in the near future. Just then you realize that the fortune teller is actually a centaur! The fortune has come true!
readings.hint: Imagine getting your fortune told by a centaur fortune teller. Do you trust this mythical beast to know your future? Watch him manipulate his crystal ball as his hooves stomp the ground beneath him.

meanings.mnemonic: Your ego ... it's totally out of control. I mean, my ego isn't out of control. I'm the greatest. That's me . But your ego? Ugh, so out of control unlike my amazing ego.
meanings.hint: Just keep going with this sentence about the ego. You can't stop, because really it's your ego that's out of control. That's I when you're saying it.

readings.mnemonic: I am the greatest in the world! I am no baby like you, that's why. A baby who says " ga ( が ) ga ga ga!"
readings.hint: You're just destroying the ego of that other guy. "Why?" you ask? "Because I am the boss here. And you are the baby. Ego things."

meanings.mnemonic: A loiter ing mountain king in the winter is an indication or sign of something to come.
meanings.hint: We don't want to focus too much on what the indication / sign actually is, though, because it will take away from the indication part (which we want to remember). So, focus on the fact that this is some kind of indication that's important, but you don't know what that indication means or is... because we'll use that with the reading.

readings.mnemonic: When you spot a loitering mountain king in the winter, you know it's an indication that soon Mrs. Chou ( ちょう ) will appear. It's like a Groundhog's Day kind of situation, but instead it means Mrs. Chou is about to come out, perhaps early for the season.
readings.hint: Imagine an announcer watching the loitering mountain king, "There he is... and it's still winter. We all know what that means. It's a sign that Mrs. Chou will be coming out of hibernation soon." Then, you can imagine her bursting forth from the mountain, running down her street.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a building standing in the way of the next chapter in your life, and it's hinder ing you. It's not like you couldn't find another way around, but it's definitely inconvenient.
meanings.hint: Picture this huge, tall building standing directly in the way. You peer around the other side and there is a book floating in the air that says "Chapter 5: Success and Happiness." Dang, you really want to get over there to that part of your life.

readings.mnemonic: Someone put this building here specifically to hinder you. They knew you were close to the next chapter of your life, and they wanted to hinder you really good. And there they are, sitting on a throne smirking. It's the Shou gun ( しょう ).
readings.hint: The Shougun wants to hinder you and your life. He wants you to have to work harder so you become better, faster, and stronger than anyone else. Hindering you is his way of making sure you don't take the easy way out in life.

meanings.mnemonic: There are fingers reaching towards you asking you to accept to instruct them. They're the fingers of students. And you're the teacher. See them reach out for knowledge. They want to accept this knowledge into their hearts and brains. Instruct them!
meanings.hint: Picture yourself instructing a class full of knowledge hungry little kids. They reach out with their fingers to accept the truth you bring to them.

readings.mnemonic: "Yes! I will instruct you, my dear students!" you cry, "I will instruct you on how to open a small ju ice ( じゅ ) box!" That's right. These little kids need help learning how to open small juice boxes (small because kids are small).
readings.hint: Picture yourself instructing little kids how to punch the straw into small juice boxes. Hold it right-side-up! Don't squeeze too hard! Don't punch it through the other side! No! Don't bite the box! Instructing is hard!

meanings.mnemonic: You hold gold fins in your mouth while shopping for pencil lead at the store. This pencil lead store only accepts gold fins as payment, but your fins don’t fit in your pockets and you need your hands to carry the lead so the gold fins go in your mouth.
meanings.hint: Imagine having these gold fins in your mouth. Taste the metal as your tongue runs over them.

readings.mnemonic: You pay for your lead but the cashier puts it in your mouth where the gold fins were and... everything en ds ( えん ).
readings.hint: Lead poisoning, huh? What a way for everything to end...

meanings.mnemonic: You loiter around your master until he depart s on a journey . You have a lot more freedom to do things after your master has departed, so until that time you just loiter about looking busy and staying out of his way.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself loitering around your master’s fancy mansion. You’re pretending to dust things off, but you’re actually just waiting for him to depart on his journey.

readings.mnemonic: Finally, it's time for your master's journey and he calls to you and says, "You are the 王 ( おう ) of the house now. Take care of everything while I'm gone."
readings.hint: Your master has departed! You are the 王 now!

meanings.mnemonic: A broken car sits in a building . You need to get rid of it, so you put it in the mail . Or, depending on where you live, maybe you put it in the post .
meanings.hint: You just put some postage on it and watch the mail carrier take it away like any other piece of mail.

readings.mnemonic: Who did you address this broken car mail (or post ) to? You sent it to the you ths ( ゆう ) of the world.
readings.hint: You figure youths don't have cars anymore, so they could use one. Ugh, youths these days and their ride share apps.

meanings.mnemonic: In a gold cross , you see a needle sticking out of it. The needle is right in the middle of the gold cross. If Jesus were on there, the needle would be sticking him right in the chest.
meanings.hint: Picture this golden cross in your head. Why do you suppose there’s this needle stuck in it? You try to pull it out but you can’t. Envision yourself straining, trying to get the thing out.

readings.mnemonic: You're about to give up on the needle when it pops out and flies directly into your shin ( しん ). Ouch! Now you're desperately trying to pull the needle out of your shin, but it really won't budge this time. Maybe you should have minded your own business.
readings.hint: Imagine a needle sticking into your shin. It hurts a ton. How would you try to get it out?

meanings.mnemonic: If you see someone loiter ing around with horns on their head you'd be correct to obey and accompany them.
meanings.hint: First of all, this could be really fun. Second of all, if this person is a demon or something, you should really just obey them or they could curse you. What's the worst that could happen?!

readings.mnemonic: If you obey the horned man you'll end up with a ton of jew els ( じゅう )!
readings.hint: You stole those jewels together and now you're an accomplice for accompanying him! You better hide those jewels!!

meanings.mnemonic: You loiter around a black hole , waiting for it to finish destroying and switch to restore mode. You were told all black holes finish eventually, and when they do you can press a button to restore everything back to the way it was.
meanings.hint: Imagine loitering around near (but not too near!) the black hole waiting for it to finish so you can restore the galaxy.

readings.mnemonic: Finally there's nothing else for the black hole to eat so you hit the restore button.... but nothing happens. "Oh f**k ( ふく ) you!" you scream as you realize you were lied to.
readings.hint: Maybe if you yell f**k you enough things will be restored. Ha. No. Everything's gone and it's your fault for being gullible.

meanings.mnemonic: During a tsunami , your lip ring is ripped off while you’re trying to cross a river . There was no other path to safety, you had to cross the raging river, but you lost your footing and smashed your face on a rock and lost your lip ring.
meanings.hint: Imagine how painful this would be, having your lip ring ripped from your lip. It would hurt a lot. Touch your lip and imagine missing half of it. Yikes.

readings.mnemonic: But why were you trying to cross this river anyway? Well your ca r ( か ) was on the other side of it, and you figured if you could get to your car you could outrun the tsunami and be safe. Too bad you fell and ripped out your lip ring though.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself fording the river, all wet and cold from the water. Keeping your eyes on your car, you move slowly across. Look at the car. Look at it!

meanings.mnemonic: You take this comb , break the handle off, and use it to stamp your official seal of approval onto a document. It's kind of a messy mark ing process, but it gets the job done.
meanings.hint: Imagine breaking and bending a comb so you can use it to stamp your seal onto things.

readings.mnemonic: You need this seal to stay at an inn ( いん ). All inns require official paperwork, so you had to improvise and make a seal on the fly.
readings.hint: Hopefully the inn accepts this seal... or you aren't going to have a place to sleep tonight. And it kind of just looks like you dipped a comb in ink and pressed it around on some paper.

meanings.mnemonic: You keep all of your shellfish under a roof down the street and it's your savings . Shellfish used to be used as money way back, and these aren't shellfish you're spending, they're ones you're saving up for later.
meanings.hint: Imagine walking down the street to deposit more shellfish into your special savings and gaze happily at the huge pile you keep far enough away that you aren't tempted to spend it.

readings.mnemonic: When you want to withdraw from your savings you force yourself to use cho psticks ( ちょ ) to pick up the shellfish. They're especially slippery, hard to use chopsticks too, that way it's really hard to take too much out of your savings.
readings.hint: Imagine trying to lift up a shellfish with a pair of smooth chopsticks. It slips right out. Ugh! I guess that's good though, because it's a savings for a reason (you shouldn't be spending it)!

meanings.mnemonic: You're trying to use your fingers to cause a landslide so you have to shake the earth. You probably won't succeed, but the best you can do is to shake your hands on the dirt as hard as you can. It looks like you're trying to wave at the ground now.
meanings.hint: Imagine shaking your fingers at the dirt. Do you think you can shake the dirt hard enough to cause a landslide (spoilers, no).

readings.mnemonic: You think you finally got the earth to shake but when you look down it's just your shin s ( しん ). Darn you shins, I thought I made some progress. Hey wait... why won't you stop?!
readings.hint: Imagine your shins shaking, your hands are waving, and they won't stop. No! You shouldn't have tried to start a landslide. This was a bad idea!

meanings.mnemonic: When you hold out your fingers , a person — and then a second person — stomps them into the dirt , leaving you with a serious finger sprain . That wasn't very nice of either person, and now you feel awfully discourage d about things.
meanings.hint: Feel the throbbing pain in your sprained, dirt-covered fingers, and imagine how intensely discouraged you are knowing that neither person who hurt you is going to face any consequences.

readings.mnemonic: You show your sprain to a doctor, and he tells you to go see the C za r ( ざ ). In your country, only the Czar has the authority to treat a sprain of this severity.
readings.hint: Once again, you face a crushing sense of discouragement — you'll never be able to afford the services of the Czar! It looks you're just going to have to live with this finger sprain for the rest of your life.

meanings.mnemonic: You see a soul floating around a tombstone acting suspicious . To be fair, if a soul is going to be anywhere, a tombstone is probably high up on the list, but this one seems like it's doing something weird.
meanings.hint: Imagine inching closer to this suspicious soul floating around a tombstone. What do you think it's doing?

readings.mnemonic:
You sneak around the tombstone to see what this suspicious soul is doing, and you realize it's pretending to kay ak ( かい ). The soul is sitting behind the tombstone, in a small kayak, moving its arms like it's rowing through water.

Even more suspicious, the soul seems to be holding a ke ttle ( け ). Considering the soul is already dead, this doesn't seem like something it will need.
readings.hint: Picture a suspicious, kettle holding, kayaking soul. The afterlife may be more interesting than you thought.

meanings.mnemonic: The tsunami going down your dry face is just sweat .
meanings.hint: Sweat really does feel like a tsunami though. Wow, it's dry and hot out right now!

readings.mnemonic: The sweat isn't just on your face, it's also dripping down your ass ( あせ ).
readings.hint: The one place you wish sweat wouldn't go, but somehow it always does: the ass.

meanings.mnemonic: "Take your fingers . Now , put them on your foe's heart and twist ." These are the words of your martial arts master as he teaches you his final, ultimate technique: the "finger-heart twister."
meanings.hint: No one can stop you now. Imagine how powerful you feel as you conquer dojo after dojo, using your bare fingers to twist the heart of any challenger who dares face you. What a twisted fiend you are!

readings.mnemonic: The last challenger to appear is… your own master, who taught you the twist technique. Turns out he's a nen doroid ( ねん ), so he doesn't even have a heart for you to twist. Welp, you're screwed.
readings.hint: Imagine the helplessness you'd feel facing him. Your master, who all along was a kind of plastic figurine called a nendoroid, is impervious to the twist technique he himself taught you! What a twist.

meanings.mnemonic: You're using your fingers to do something important for this ceremony 's construction . What's really important for this ceremony's construction, using your fingers? Well… you wipe the floors.
meanings.hint: Hey, somebody has to do it! Wiping the floors is as important for any ceremony as all the other work that goes into it… right?!

readings.mnemonic: Now wipe these floors, you foo l ( ふ )!
readings.hint: Oh wow, sorry, not sure where that just came from… Look, wiping floors for a living doesn't make anyone a fool, they're people just like you and me. Now hurry up, I want a nice sheen on these floors before the ceremony.

meanings.mnemonic: Out of bamboo , you make a brush – a writing brush . This is no ordinary brush, mind you. It is a brush specifically designed for writing elegant characters. And it’s made from bamboo!
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself using this bamboo writing brush. Do you know many kanji? Of course you do! Imagine yourself writing them all with this nice writing brush. It’s actually fun to write things with it!

readings.mnemonic: Once the writing brush hits ( ひつ ) that paper, it’s like you transform into a totally different person. All of a sudden you’re a master calligrapher, writing anything and everything in a most beautiful fashion. Once that brush hits the paper, it’s on!
readings.hint: Imagine the sound the brush makes when it hits the paper. It’s a bit of a plop, and then kind of a scratching sound as the brush bristles move around. Really focus on this sound that the brush makes when it hits the paper.

meanings.mnemonic: A woman sitting on a stool with a broken heart is very angry . She had her heart broken by some jerk and now she’s sitting on the stool, just stewing over the guy and how angry she is with him.
meanings.hint: This woman is so angry! Pretend you are the woman. Sit on your stool and feel your heart, painful in your chest, as you fume and think about how angry you are.

readings.mnemonic: As you're sitting on that stool feeling angry , your do g ( ど ) comes up to console you. He nuzzles his head into your lap, and you start to feel the anger melt away.
readings.hint: Imagine your dog (or somebody else's dog) putting his furry, warm head in your lap. Feel how comforting it is. Are you still feeling angry?

meanings.mnemonic: You want to go to the sun so you set up a slide twenty stories tall so you can ascend into the heavens.
meanings.hint: Feel the air whoosh past your face as you shoot off the end of the long slide and ascend into the air. It's starting to get hot and the sun is getting closer!

readings.mnemonic: You need to ascend into the sky and toward the sun because the Shou gun ( しょう ) said so. He's testing out some space travel technology (the twenty story slide) and you're his guinea pig.
readings.hint: As you ascend, look back through tear-filled eyes and see the Shougun staring up at you. He nods slightly. You've pleased him.

meanings.mnemonic: If you're riding your scooter through some rice , it's likely you've gone astray . Also you might be lost .
meanings.hint: Usually there aren't paved areas inside areas of rice. You must be really lost if you ended up here with your scooter.

readings.mnemonic: You got so lost and went so astray you ended up on the May flower  ( めい ), a ship that eventually took you across the Atlantic Ocean. Now you've really, really gone astray.
readings.hint: How you got so lost that you ended up on an old ship from 1620 is beyond my understanding. But you did it.

meanings.mnemonic: Someone is reaching out with their fingers to call for you. They beckon you closer, this way.
meanings.hint: Something about the way they're beckoning you with their fingers is really enticing. You can't help but walk toward them.

readings.mnemonic: The person beckon ing you is the Shou gun ( しょう ). You can't help but obey the Shougun's beckoning call.
readings.hint: Even if the Shougun didn't have an army that would crush you if you didn't come when he called, you'd go anyway. Those beckoning fingers are just so... weirdly enticing.

meanings.mnemonic: Inside this moon is a black hole ... haha just kidding, it's your belly ! It's beautiful and round like the moon and it eats up everything you give it like a black hole. What a good stomach you have!
meanings.hint: Look down at your perfect belly. It's looking back up at you.

readings.mnemonic: If you don't treat your belly with respect, when you look down at it, it'll yell, " F**k ( ふく ) you!" Be nice to your stomach!
readings.hint: Forget to eat lunch? Belly grumbles f**k you. Tell someone you're fat and need to lose weight? Belly cries f**k you. Be nice to your belly.

meanings.mnemonic: After a tsunami hit your farming land, you're gonna see a lot of dense silt left behind. This thick layer really enriches the soil, and your crops will come out tasting extra thick and dense in flavor.
meanings.hint: Imagine your farming land absolutely covered with this dense layer of silt from the tsunami, but then seeing your crops sprouting up one after another, thicker than ever. It’s all very thick and dense, we think you get the point.

readings.mnemonic: Know who’s likely responsible for this dense , thick layer of silt? No stradamus ( のう ). He’s the one who urged you to buy this farmland in the first place, likely because he predicted the tsunami would bring this thick layer of silt.
readings.hint: You can always rely on Nostradamus’ predictions hitting the mark, even when it comes to thickly-layered-silt-bringing tsunamis. Good ol’ reliable Nostradamus.

meanings.mnemonic: When your eye s get droopy , it means you're getting drowsy . Go get some shut-eye, you droopy-eyed drowsy person.
meanings.hint: You're so drowsy that your eyelids have drooped all the way down to the floor. Be careful not to step on them as you drowsily make your way to bed.

readings.mnemonic: The reason you're so drowsy is because you're on a swing ( すい ). A swing works like a baby cradle — it rocks you and makes you feel drowsy right away!
readings.hint: Feel yourself getting drowsier and drowsier as the swing gently rocks you to sleep. Hopefully it's one of those swings with the leg holes so you don't fall out as you're drifting off.

meanings.mnemonic: Setting up a stand on the top of a mountain , you rake all the rocks to the edge of it. You want to clear the area around your stand, so you get your rake out and you rake all the stones and debris away from the stand and toward the edge of the mountain top. It also creates a small barrier around the edge as well.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself at the top of the mountain, holding the rake in your hands. Look over the edge. Feel the sinking feeling in your stomach as you realize how high up you really are.

readings.mnemonic: You set up this stand at the top of the mountain so you could give a speech to the world. But you need to look your best, so you decide to get a tan ( たん ). You lie next to the edge of the mountain, and prepare to tan. Everyone likes a person with a healthy tan.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself at the edge of this mountain getting your tan on. Feel the sun beating down on you and feel the fresh mountain breeze blowing over your body. It’s very relaxing.

meanings.mnemonic: You see a canopy under which someone has left a brush (in its 肀 form) next to the water , creating a scene of such ease and peace !
meanings.hint: Imagine the sense of peace you'd feel in a place like this. The sound of the water, the shade of the canopy… Whoever was here must have felt at ease as they wrote or drew. But will they come back for their brush?

readings.mnemonic: It turns out this place of ease and peace was created by こう いち , who suddenly comes in looking for the brush he left.
readings.hint: Finding you there, こういち invites you spend some time under the canopy with him, practicing kanji together with his brush. Why not? Seems peaceful.

meanings.mnemonic: There aren't many tree s in your under ground prison , so you've been given a task: using only your mouth and a stool , plant as many in the ground as you can. It's an extreme challenge!
meanings.hint: How are you going to approach this weirdly specific, extreme task? Dig a hole using the stool and then plant the trees with your mouth? Good luck figuring this one out! You're in a weird underground prison for a reason, after all.

readings.mnemonic: This extreme challenge could only be solved by one person: Goku ( ごく ) from Dragon Ball Z! He has a special cloak that'll allow you to plant trees in record time, so he quickly cloaks you ( きょく ) to help with your extreme task.
readings.hint: Goku barely manages to cloak you before the prison guards realize what's going on. With this cloak, you'll be planting trees with your mouth and stool like no one else before! Hooray!

meanings.mnemonic: In the capital , even a broken dog can get a position . That's because the capital has so many jobs available that even a poor, broken dog can find a place to work.
meanings.hint: Imagine this dog going to the capital and getting a position. The poor dog looks pretty messed up though. The radical for dog in this kanji is a bit modified, so just remember that the dog in this story is all messed up.

readings.mnemonic: In order to help the dog get a position , the capital cobbler made him a pair of nice shoe s ( しゅう ). The shoes help him walk on his hurt legs, but more importantly, they boost his confidence and make him feel ready for any position.
readings.hint: Watch the broken dog settle in to his new shoes in his new home in the capital. The old dog can finally enjoy life!

meanings.mnemonic: Inside the " Good Building " there's a single guy .
meanings.hint: Feel free to walk around the Good Building. There are no things or people, except for that one guy. Who is that guy.

readings.mnemonic: You talk to that guy . The only thing he does is offer you roe ( ろう ).
readings.hint: You try to talk to the guy, poke him, and even yell at him. But, all he does is hand you some fish roe. When you take it, he offers you more fish roe. What's up with this guy?

meanings.mnemonic: The leader of your yoga class uses a special brush to stay healthy . She rubs the brush on her long arms and legs, and she never ever gets sick.
meanings.hint: If you want to be healthy too, just do yoga and rub this healthy brush all over your bod.

readings.mnemonic: Not everyone wants to be healthy , but Ken ( けん ) the samurai sure does. He needs to be ready at a moment's notice so he can do tasks for other people. He's at every class, rubbing himself down with the brush.
readings.hint: Imagine Ken, the picture of samurai health, doing yoga and getting that brush all up in those bits.

meanings.mnemonic: You say you've left some grain on the stairs . This is how you invite people into your place.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself saying out loud how you've left grain on the stairs, trying to lure people in. You're surprised people are actually falling for this.

readings.mnemonic: You've invite d people in by leaving grain on the stairs but some you ths ( ゆう ) have ended up eating all the grain.
readings.hint: Imagine the youths just loitering in the stairs having eaten all the grain and taking up all the space making it impossible for anyone else to come in.

meanings.mnemonic: Your soul stand s in the village you long for . It's the village you're from, and no matter how far you go, no matter how old you get, your soul is still there, and you yearn for it.
meanings.hint: Have you ever been away from your village? Maybe you're far away right now, studying Japanese alone. Can't you feel your body longing for your soul back home?

readings.mnemonic: No matter how much you long for it, you can't go back because a cougar ( あこが ) is in the way. Whenever you try to return to your village to be reunited with the soul you yearn for, a cougar is back, and won't let you in.
readings.hint: You long for a cougar to leave you alone! You yearn for a cougar to bother someone else!

meanings.mnemonic: You substitute some shellfish for collateral so your friend will lend you some money. Normally he would ask for something more valuable, but you’ve convinced him to substitute these shellfish for what he would normally take as collateral when lending to people.
meanings.hint: "Shellfish used to be money! People used to lend them to each other all the time!" you say to your friend. "Hmm... okay, I guess so," they say skeptically.

readings.mnemonic: You want your friend to lend you some money so you can buy yourself a ca r ( か ). You need money and a car a lot more than you need this pile of shellfish, so you’re happy to put them up as collateral for the loan.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself in this new car that you got with the money your friend lent you. Hear the roar of the engine and feel the wind in your hair.

meanings.mnemonic: A drunkard who has his mouth on the ground , has a heart that is misguided . First of all, he tries to drink his problems away, but worse, he's so drunk he's trying to drink out of the ground.
meanings.hint: Picture this misguided drunkard. His whole deal is messed up.

readings.mnemonic: Nowadays we don't say misguided , we say wack ( わく ). Like, damn that drunkard is so messed up, he's wack.
readings.hint: Did I do it? Did I figure out the kid lingo? Am I cool? Or am I just another wack, misguided adult?

meanings.mnemonic: When your sick mama tries to do a task she's filled with pain !
meanings.hint: Sick people shouldn't have to do things, of course they're in pain. You shouldn't let your mama do anything if she's in pain, do it for her!

readings.mnemonic: Your mama's pain is too ( つう ) much for her to handle, so now you have to do all of her tasks too.
readings.hint: You finish doing your tasks and hers too, and you ask her, "How is the pain now, Mama?" "THE PAIN IS TOO MUCH!" she cries. Oh mama.

meanings.mnemonic: If you're on your scooter and you run over a root , you'll need to retreat . The roots have obviously reject ed you, they want you to leave, so you should.
meanings.hint: Picture the roots raising up a little bit from the ground and tripping up your scooter. They reject you, so you should just retreat.

readings.mnemonic: You're trying to retreat by scooting backwards, but the roots grab onto your tie ( たい ). You try to pull your tie off but it's just getting tighter and tighter around your neck.
readings.hint: You didn't retreat fast enough, the roots are rejecting your life, and strangling you with your own tie.

meanings.mnemonic: When you ride a scooter you'll have a surplus of possible route s. Not only can you drive on any road, but you can ride on the sidewalk too!
meanings.hint: Okay, so it might not be legal to take the sidewalk route depending on where you live, but physically, you can do whatever you want because scooters are small.

readings.mnemonic: You decide to take the fastest route , since you have so many options, and drive on the sidewalk. However, in doing so you run over every single toe ( と ) on every single person trying to walk there.
readings.hint: Psh, whatever. They aren't your toes, and this is the fastest route! You'll name it the toe route and do whatever you want.

meanings.mnemonic: Take your leather and change it into shoes .
meanings.hint: You start with a sheet of leather. Now imagine yourself going through the process of changing them to shoes. Smell the leather and feel it, too.

readings.mnemonic: Some shoes aren’t made from leather anymore these days, but you know who always wears shoes made of leather? Old coots ( くつ ). Old coots are so old that the only shoes they wore growing up were made from leather so they refuse to wear any shoes that aren’t made from leather. Crazy old coots.
readings.hint: Imagine some of these old coots busting into a modern shoe store and kicking over all the display stands and setting everything on fire because they hate non leather shoes so much. Hear the old coots screaming and babbling nonsense as they destroy the store.

meanings.mnemonic: In the sun while holding a comb in a cage and sitting on a stool , you comb your hair in your spare time . Since you’re in a cage, you actually fashioned this comb yourself, so it’s a little messed up, missing a prong. You’re working on improving it in your spare time, and you also use it to style your mop.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself in your spare time, sitting on this stool in a cage in the sun. Feel the warmth of the sun on your body and feel the metal bars of the cage holding you in. You try to forget about your situation by combing your hair with the comb. The comb radical is a little modified, so be sure to remember that you made this comb yourself and it’s a little wonky.

readings.mnemonic: So how did you get in this cage? Well, before you used to comb your hair in your spare time , you used to steal ca rs ( か ). You’d stolen many a car in your day, but you finally got caught in the act of stealing a car, and now you’re spending all of your spare time in that cage, just combing your hair.
readings.hint: Picture yourself in this cage. You’re going to be there for life because you’d stolen too many cars. Feel the tears run down your face as you continue to comb your hair. You’re going to die in this cage.

meanings.mnemonic: Cut this comb up with your weapon and turn it into the steps for some stairs .
meanings.hint: When you think about it, a comb is like a ladder. So if you cut all the rungs off with your weapon and put them on the ground, they turn into stairs!

readings.mnemonic: You slowly work to create these stairs , you have to slice up a lot of combs, and at dawn ( だん ) they are done!
readings.hint: Imagine standing at the top of the stairs you made yourself, looking down at the entire town below you, as the light of dawn breaks and fills everything before you with light. And now more people can see this beautiful sight if they climb your steps.

meanings.mnemonic: Closing each eye is something that the peoples do before they sleep . The peoples can’t sleep with both eyes open and they can’t sleep with just one eye open either. Peoples have to close each eye before they can sleep.
meanings.hint: You are one of these peoples, so imagine yourself closing your eyes and sleeping. Go ahead and do it right now. Close one eye, and then the other. Now imagine yourself sleeping.

readings.mnemonic: The reason you want to sleep is because all the other peoples were mean ( みん ) to you. They were so mean that the only way you could get over it was to close your eyes and sleep. You just want to sleep.
readings.hint: Those other peoples were really mean to you. Imagine all the mean things they were saying to you. What did they say? Think about all these mean things as you close your eyes and drift off to sleep. Also keep in mind that this kanji has the same reading as 民 , which is included in this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: If you eat a rice paddy the size of the moon , your stomach is going to hurt. All the rice in a rice paddy the size of the moon is way too much rice for anyone to consume. If you do, your stomach is going to ache like crazy.
meanings.hint: Imagine having a whole rice paddy in your stomach. A rice paddy the size of the moon. Your stomach hurts a lot. You feel like you’re going to throw up.

readings.mnemonic: From the sky, an eagle swoops down and flies right into your distended stomach . Bam! The ea gle ( い ) hits your stomach and you vomit up the entire moon sized rice paddy.
readings.hint: This eagle saw your stomach distress and decided to help by making you barf. Hear the screech of the eagle as it swoops down and rams you in the stomach.

meanings.mnemonic: When someone is sick in order to diagnose the correct cause and treatment, you need to check their symptom s. You have to be especially careful too, because if you get a symptom wrong, you could kill them!
meanings.hint: If you know all of the symptoms for each and every sickness you'd be able to cure them all!

readings.mnemonic: You need to figure out the symptom s the Shou gun ( しょう ) is having. This is really serious too, so you better be correct about which sickness he has. If something bad happens to the Shougun it will be off with your head.
readings.hint: You get all up close and personal with the Shougun. Hmm, his symptoms are watery eyes, stuffy nose, angry shouting (wait no, that's not a symptom, is it?). Don't mess up!

meanings.mnemonic: You pull on a thread as you stand with a towel wrapped around you. This allows you to tighten the towel.
meanings.hint: Imagine pulling this thread and feeling your towel tighten around you. It's getting so tight that you can barely stand properly, which is probably why the stand radical looks a little weird here.

readings.mnemonic: You're tighten ing your towel because you've been eating a lot of ta ter ( てい ) tots lately. You want to keep a slim figure, and rather than give up your beloved tater tots, you've just opted to constrict yourself with this towel, corset-style.
readings.hint: You really don't need to do this, you know — eating tater tots isn't a sin. Tighten that towel just enough to be snug and comfy, and keep enjoying your taters. No one's judging you.

meanings.mnemonic: The scooter is white . This color will urge and compel you to use it. Scooter companies use colors to get you to do what they want. Some would say they are trying to coerce you, even.
meanings.hint: Look at how white the scooter is. It urges you to use it. It compels you. You feel coerced...

readings.mnemonic: When they urge , compel and coerce you like this, they are hack ing ( はく ) your brain.
readings.hint: Really all marketing, advertising, and the like is just a hack of your brain.

meanings.mnemonic: Someone just asked you to say which direction they should visit .
meanings.hint: You better think long and hard about where you think they should visit before you say so. They're relying on you.

readings.mnemonic: You end up saying, "You should visit my ho me ( ほう )." How kind of you!
readings.hint: Your home is just over that direction, and it's your favorite place to visit, so why wouldn't this stranger want to visit your home too?! So come and visit my home, random person!

meanings.mnemonic: Taking a thread and moving to the sound of the music, a drunkard makes a weave through some fabric. He’s using the thread to weave his clothes back together after he damaged them in a fall down the stairs. You know, because he’s a drunkard.
meanings.hint: Imagine this drunkard bipping and bopping to the sound of the music as he weaves this thread through his clothes to repair them.

readings.mnemonic: This drunkard is really chic ( しき ), but he's just fallen on hard times and has been hitting the bottle pretty hard lately. The piece of clothing he's using the thread to weave back together is one of his fancy chic outfits.
readings.hint: Imagine this drunkard, and his chic but shabby appearance. Let's hope he can repair that chic outfit and go back to being his usual chic self.

meanings.mnemonic: If your soul is lying in the grass in a treasure chest , you're going to worry about it. It's just sitting there, out in the open, waiting for anyone to come and open it.
meanings.hint: Picture your soul curled up inside this treasure chest that's just sitting out on the grass. Anyone could open it up and look inside, and this worries you quite a bit!

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, you also worry about opening it yourself. You stand in front of the chest and think, "Should I open it? Nah, yah ( なや ). Wait. Nah. Yah. Dang, you can't make up your mind.
readings.hint: You worry about what you'll find if you look directly at your soul. But you also worry about what will happen if someone else looks. So you're stuck in a constant state of worrying: nah or yah?!

meanings.mnemonic: You see a flag by the exit letting you know to yield . There’s cross traffic just outside the exit, and the flag lets you know that you need to yield to them.
meanings.hint: Look at this flag telling you to yield. Now look through the exit right by the flag. You can see a bunch of people out there walking. Imagine standing by the exit, yielding to the other people until you have an opening. Hear the hustle and bustle of the people as you wait.

readings.mnemonic: Even though the flag told you to yield , you’re starting to lose your patience. The people walking outside the exit are all a bunch of crazy old coots ( くつ )! These crazy coots keep walking right in front of the door, getting in the way. The old coots aren’t even going anywhere, they just keep looping around to obstruct the way!
readings.hint: Darn coots! Forget the yield flag, it’s time to exit. Imagine busting out of the exit, punching and pushing your way through the old coots. Hear them scream and shout “Augh! My hip!” as you plow through them.

meanings.mnemonic: Construction in the winter leads to aggression . Just think about it. It's cold, it's unpleasant, and you don't want to be there. So, you start to develop an aggression towards other people working with you on this winter project.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself walking through the winter construction site, taking out your aggression on anyone you see. You hate this winter construction!

readings.mnemonic: You're taking your aggression out on everyone. Then, the sexy construction worker from WaniKani Level 1, こう いち , walks by without a shirt on. You take out all your aggression on him.
readings.hint: "Aggression" is going to depend on you, so you should imagine a scene where you take your aggression out on こういち , whatever that entails.

meanings.mnemonic: If someone is coming toward you in a car , holding a weapon in their hand , they're going to attack you!
meanings.hint: Picture the car, weapon out the window, preparing to attack you full force!

readings.mnemonic: Right before the attack begins, you recognize the hand holding this weapon and driving this car. It's Gecki ( げき ), your long-lost gecko who you may or may not have let fly off into a really fierce tsunami. Oops. I think he may be here attacking you out of revenge.
readings.hint: Gecki is attacking you now. He's hitting you over and over with his tiny car. He's yelling and smacking your ankles with his weapon. And all you can do is wait and let the attack happen, because you know you deserve it.

meanings.mnemonic: After a tsunami , you find an axe on the ground next to some fins on the beach . The tsunami was pretty violent, and it looks like this axe got blown into some fish and cut off their fins and now those things are just on the ground by the beach.
meanings.hint: See these things on the beach. It makes sense to see fins, as fins are usually in the ocean, so seeing them on the beach is logical. Pick up the stuff you find as you feel the beach sand between your toes.

readings.mnemonic: Walking down the beach a bit more, you also find a hammer ( はま ). Gosh, looks like the tsunami hit a workshop or a hardware store or something. First the axe, now a hammer.
readings.hint: Pick up the hammer in your hand. It’s pretty heavy. The tsunami must have been very strong to pick up such a heavy hammer.

meanings.mnemonic: This thread is odd , but that's what makes it beautiful . It's a little frayed, it's a little miscolored, but that's what makes it appealing to you.
meanings.hint: If something is a bit odd, it's usually beautiful in its own way, just like this beautiful thread.

readings.mnemonic: You take this beautiful thread and lock it up with a key ( き ). You want to keep it all to yourself, you want its beauty to be for your eyes only!
readings.hint: To remember that there's a beautiful thread hidden away, you tie a not-as-beautiful thread to the end of the key.

meanings.mnemonic: There are some horns on the ground next to fins on a plate that are meant to benefit everyone. If you're a member of this town, one of your benefits is this little set up.
meanings.hint: You pick up the horns off the ground and put them on your head, then eat some fins off the plate. This is just one of many benefits to living here. You'll never go hungry!

readings.mnemonic: Another benefit is free 駅 ( えき ) usage! Whenever you come back home from places on the train, this 駅 is completely free.
readings.hint: Okay, so using the 駅 is probably a better benefit than eating cold fins off a plate on the ground, but you take what you can get. They're all free!

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami can't resist the call of a bog . Whenever a tsunami is headed toward land, a bog will always call out to it, "Hey tsunami, don't you want to hit me with your water? Come this way~"
meanings.hint: Close your eyes and hear the bog's tempting call.

readings.mnemonic: As you listen to the bog 's call you realize it looks just like the Numa ( ぬま ) Numa guy in your mind. You open your eyes and it's just a bog. You close your eyes, and there he is. The Numa Numa guy.
readings.hint: So that's what happened to him! If you're a youngun, just do a little Google and you'll be pretty pleased, I'm sure.

meanings.mnemonic: Pointing your magic solar powered wishing stick at the sun , you attain the legs of a child . You’re old and want young legs to carry you around, so you took your wishing stick that’s powered by the sun, and you wished for the legs of a child.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself getting new legs. Legs that belong to a child. They’re limber and young and full of energy! Feel your legs of a child underneath you!

readings.mnemonic: As you delight in your new legs, Je sus ( じ ) descends from the sky. “My child ,” he says, “why have you pointed the stick at the sun and wished for the legs of a child? You were already perfect, just as all of God’s children are perfect.”
readings.hint: Say back to Jesus, “I’m not your child Jesus! And you’re just jealous of my new legs! You wish you had the legs of a child, Jesus! Your legs are like super old!” Actually say this out loud, and remember how Jesus wishes he could be a child again too.

meanings.mnemonic: Your fingers before were complete .
meanings.hint: Imagine one of your fingers is missing, and they're no longer complete like before. When everyone told you not to join that yakuza gang, you just didn't listen.

readings.mnemonic: To have your complete fingers back, you gotta run to the hospital solo ( そろ ).
readings.hint: You can't be seen with anyone because your former yakuza mates might be watching you. Seriously, you don't want anyone else involved in this business.

meanings.mnemonic: The roof protecting your life and the net protecting your heart are both protected by the constitution .
meanings.hint: The constitution makes it so that everything you hold dear is locked away, nice and safe on a piece of paper, and no one can change the rules or hurt you without your say so.

readings.mnemonic: Your country's constitution is being protected by none other than Ken ( けん ) the samurai, hero for hire.
readings.hint: Picture Ken standing in front of your constitution. But keep a close watch, cause wily ol' Nic Cage is in the business of thievin' on constitutions.

meanings.mnemonic: A winter table would be an odd thing to deal with . Winter can be hard to deal with on its own, but turn your table into winter? Just dealing with your food getting cold would be the worst!
meanings.hint: Look at this winter table. How are you ever going to deal with it? Just think of your favorite warm food and then imagine it getting cold. Deal with it, dog.

readings.mnemonic: This winter table is something you have to deal with , sure, but what if you just gave it to a circus show ( しょ )? They like weird stuff like this.
readings.hint: Feel the relief of getting rid of this table. No more having to deal with it anymore. The circus show can have it.

meanings.mnemonic: You got your ice orders . Of course, your ice orders are to make things cool and cold .
meanings.hint: Making things cool and cold are pretty much the only orders you'll hear from ice. Ice is very simple in that regard.

readings.mnemonic: How will you make things cool and cold ? You'll use a special ray ( れい ) gun that shoots a cold ray.
readings.hint: Imagine a cold ray coming out of your ray gun. Anything it touches becomes cold. "Stay cool, bird boy," you say.

meanings.mnemonic: A grain , from one evening to the next evening , experiences a shift . After two evenings, the grain has shifted from one location to another.
meanings.hint: Picture the grain in your head with a time-lapse of one evening after another happening in the background. Slowly, the grain shifts.

readings.mnemonic: This shift is caused by the ea gle ( い ) that wants to eat the grains. Every other evening, the eagle swoops down, shifts the grains (eating a few) and then flies off.
readings.hint: Picture the eagle flying down on the second evening, scattering the grains it doesn't eat, shifting their location.

meanings.mnemonic: Your scooter takes you to the barracks , drop s you off, and then goes in reverse all the way back home in the opposite direction.
meanings.hint: Imagine riding your scooter to the barracks. It literally drops you on the ground when you get there, and then drives in reverse back the opposite way you came.

readings.mnemonic: Your scooter going in reverse and leaving you here is opposite of what you want. You want to ride it back home when you're done, so you stuff the wheels with Gak ( ぎゃく ). If you don’t know what Gak is, check out this Gak commercial .
readings.hint: You Gak up the wheels so when the scooter tries to switch to reverse it gets stuck. Success! But now it can't go forward either. Darn you Gak, you tricked me again.

meanings.mnemonic: This building features horns on a pig to represent our squad .
meanings.hint: Our squad's symbol is horns on a pig, and we put it on everything to show the whole world our squad owns this turf.

readings.mnemonic: Our squad also wears special tie s ( たい ) with our horned pig on it. And we don't care if you think that's lame, because we're A SQUAD.
readings.hint: Picture our squad. It's Koichi, Viet, the Crabigator and Tofugu. We're all wearing matching horned pig ties and walking toward you in slow motion, our squad building towering behind us with the pig smirking down at you.

meanings.mnemonic: Rice that is blue has a strong spirit . Our spirits are blue, so if you ever see rice (or other food, or even people) that's blue, you know that's their spirit radiating out!
meanings.hint: Picture this rice, the pulsing blue spirit of each grain is glowing so bright!

readings.mnemonic:
You want to see the spirit up close, so you take your sa ber ( せい ), throw the blue rice in the air, and slice it in half! The rice explodes and blue light fills everything!

Then it's gone.
readings.hint: As you release the spirits in the rice, your saber begins to glow blue too. Their spirits are going inside your saber, making it stronger!

meanings.mnemonic: You wear a great big cross with a drop on it, and head to a specialty exhibition for gambling . The big cross and drop on your chest is your badge for being admitted into the exhibition. Everyone at the specialty exhibition has a cross and drop, and everyone is gambling.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself at this specialty exhibition. Feel the weight of the cross shaped badge on your chest as you continue gambling at the exhibition.

readings.mnemonic: You’re not very good at gambling , but you are pretty good at cheating. You hack ( はく ) the specialty exhibition gambling machines to pay out lots of cash just for you. You hack quickly and quietly, so luckily no one notices that you’ve hacked the system.
readings.hint: Hack the gambling machines. Imagine the sounds of the exhibition around you as you work to hack the machines. Look at the computer parts and cables as you work.

meanings.mnemonic: You loiter on the mountain , reading off the digits of pi on this winter morning. The cold mountain air is so delicate , and changes only in very tiny ways — just like pi when you add another digit to it.
meanings.hint: What other delicate things and tiny changes would you notice on the mountain? A little bird alighting in a tree, or the snowflakes it shakes loose from a branch? That's for you to decide, but imagine it vividly whatever it is.

readings.mnemonic: Ah! Of course! The delicate bumble be e ( び ). Slightly out of place in winter, but this tiny creature is your idea of delicate. It lands on your finger, and you look at every tiny fiber of its being.
readings.hint: Picture yourself looking at the bee, thinking how each hair on its body is so delicate and tiny. Maybe it has a delicate aroma to go with it. A slight smell of honey?

meanings.mnemonic: The Grain Mouth King pours grain from his mouth for you and his other subjects. You go to him and pull his finger. When you do this, grain pours from his mouth. But we were greedy. We didn't understand the extent to which we were harvesting his mouth wheat.
meanings.hint: Of course, you need to imagine pulling the Grain Mouth King's finger, to some extent. You can't just read what we write for you, without going to the imagination zone. Pull that finger real hard a couple times, and watch an extra large purge of grain pour forth.

readings.mnemonic: The extent to which we were harvesting grain from his mouth was too great. The Grain Mouth King has run out of grain. He worries he will lose power if he has no grain to offer, so he switches to barfing out ta ter ( てい ) tots instead.
readings.hint: Imagine hearing the rumors that the Grain Mouth King ran out of grain, so you go check the extent of his failure yourself. But tater tots taste pretty good too. Imagine eating one of his mouth tater tots and go ahead and comment about it to him.

meanings.mnemonic: Ice from the east is frozen . Well, of course, all ice is frozen, but ice from the east is like, really super frozen.
meanings.hint: Imagine this super frozen ice from the east. You’ve never seen anything quite like it before. It’s so frozen, it’s like it was frozen once and then frozen again. Twice frozen. Feel the ice and how freezing cold it is. It’s super cold, guys.

readings.mnemonic: Japan is a country in the east, and this ice was frozen in とう きょう . とうきょう is really good at freezing frozen things, so it makes sense that this frozen ice came from there.
readings.hint: Imagine being in とうきょう and seeing all this frozen ice. Hear people speaking Japanese all around you as you touch and feel the freezing twice-frozen ice.

meanings.mnemonic: A canopy is over your car because your car is in storage in a large storehouse .
meanings.hint: The canopy just protects your car from the elements as it sits in storage for long periods of time.

readings.mnemonic: Your car is in this storage warehouse , just sitting there. A 子 ( こ ) notices this, and decides to take your car on a joyride. You won't notice, right? You never drive it.
readings.hint: Imagine a kid sneaking into your canopy to steal your car that's in storage. Maybe it's two 子 s, one stacked on the other with a trench coat. One 子 works the pedals while the other 子 works the steering.

meanings.mnemonic: The gladiator in front of you draws his sword . He points the sword at your chest and right when you think everything is about to end, you realize he's pointing the sword at the ticket in your hand.
meanings.hint: Right! You needed to buy a ticket to see the gladiator! He doesn't want to kill you, he wants to check to make sure you bought a ticket.

readings.mnemonic: The gladiator happily marks your ticket with his blade, when Ken ( けん ) the samurai pushes past you. He holds up his own sword, challenging the gladiator, who just shakes his head and points at your ticket again.
readings.hint: Ken takes a long time to examine your ticket. I don't think he understands that he needs a ticket to fight the gladiator. Maybe you should give him yours.

meanings.mnemonic: The giant radical and the giant kanji are exactly the same! Massive!

readings.mnemonic: Just recently, a giant rampaged through Little Kyo to ( きょ ), so now it's even smaller than before. Poor Little Kyoto.
readings.hint: See Little Kyoto after the giant rampaged through it. It looks even smaller and more pathetic than ever. Look at all the smoldering rubble and smell the smoke with your nose.

meanings.mnemonic: See that woman with the broom ? That's the lady of the house. She's getting ready to hand that broom to you — her butler — because you missed a spot over there.
