readings.mnemonic: What's the most dirty are the prisons' o bi ( お ) collections. You can hear the prisoners crying, "Oh, not the obi. Our obi are all dirty, oh no!"
readings.hint: The obi were the only nice thing the prisoners had left, and now they're all dirty. Even as you leave the prisons, you can still hear their echoing cries of "Oh, not our obi!"

meanings.mnemonic: You eat part of a director to domesticate him. This director has been going around kicking cameramen and punching actors and it’s really ruining his reputation. You need to domesticate him. The only way to do this is to shock him into submission. His ego is so big from two blockbusters that you have to eat part of him to get him to calm down.
meanings.hint: Which part of the director did you choose to eat in order to domesticate him? Think about eating that part of the director and imagine its taste in your mouth as you chew. Eww.

readings.mnemonic: While you did succeed in your attempt to domesticate the director, he is now bleeding profusely from the bite wound. All over the script! You gotta get your ca r ( か ) and drive him to the hospital like right away. Get to the car! Hurry!
readings.hint: You’re proud that you managed to domesticate the director, but now you feel kind of bad for having injured him. Okay, you really just feel bad because he’s probably going to sue you and he definitely has a lot of money. Oh, and he’s bleeding all over your car.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami runs into nine tree s and produces a dye the color of the forest. The tsunami water mixes with each tree, nine of them actually, and the resulting liquid is a forest green dye.
meanings.hint: Imagine this happening in front of you. See the tsunami turn from the color of water to the color of a forest. The dye washes over you and now you’re forest green as well.

readings.mnemonic: The dye continues to pass through the forest and hits a group of cen taurs ( せん ). Now each centaur is dyed with the forest green dye. They don’t look happy.
readings.hint: Hear each centaur shouting about how he hates that dye for changing his color. They look kind of funny, so you laugh at them a bit, but not too loud – they might hear you and get even more angry.

meanings.mnemonic: If your outfit gets sucked into a black hole , you'll need to get a duplicate version of the same outfit, 'cause you're not getting the original back. Hopefully you had multiple s…
meanings.hint: Always pick up a few duplicate outfits if you're planning on venturing out to a black hole.

readings.mnemonic: As you're shopping for duplicate outfits, some guy runs up and starts screaming " f*** ( ふく ) you!" at you. "F*** you, man! You're out here buying multiple outfits while kids in other countries are starving!"
readings.hint: In response, you shout "F*** you too, bro! I need these duplicate outfits for my black hole mission!" For the next few minutes, the two of you just scream "f*** you" at each other multiple times, over and over.

meanings.mnemonic: Two pool s, both altered and not quite like a pool you’ve seen before are put together and flipped around – this is an egg . One pool is the egg white, the other pool is the egg yolk. They’re different, but similar.
meanings.hint: Separate the two pools from one another. Now the pools look like the kanji. The kanji is one white pool and one yellow pool. Neat, huh?

readings.mnemonic: You remember Tamago tchi ( たまご ), right? Well, Tamagotchi is an egg , and the toy looks like an egg, so it’s pretty easy to remember that tamago means egg.
readings.hint: Just think Tamagotchi whenever you see this kanji. Tamagotchi = tamago = egg. Easy!

meanings.mnemonic: The eye 's root is the eyeball .
meanings.hint: Go ahead and feel your eyeballs moving around in your head. Your eyes aren't just the part on the surface, they're the whole balls in there, attached at the root.

readings.mnemonic: You know who has some pretty good eyeball s? Gan dhi ( がん ). Gandhi used his eyes to see the oppression around him and decided to do something about it. But it's all thanks to his eyeballs.
readings.hint: Look at Gandhi’s eyeball with your eyeball. It's pretty remarkable.

meanings.mnemonic: The eternity radical and the eternity kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: How long is an eternity ? Just look at this little a corn ( えい ) and ask. The acorn starts small but it grows into a huge tree and can live for all eternity by dropping more acorns to create more trees. Its acorn line will live on forever!
readings.hint: It's the miracle of eternal life!

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami that hits at night leaves even more fluid than normal. It's dark at night, so the sun isn't there to help evaporate all that tsunami liquid .
meanings.hint: Picture the dark fluids from the tsunami flowing by your window at night. You look out and the liquid is shining in the moonlight. It looks like an entire sea is out there!

readings.mnemonic: This fluid stays in the 駅 ( えき ) longer than anywhere else. All of those tsunami liquids pour deep inside the 駅 late at night and the sun still can't reach them to help evaporate them during the day. It's a real *night*mare.
readings.hint: Picture the 駅 filled to the brim with fluids and liquids. Maybe if you swim, you'll be able to catch your train.

meanings.mnemonic: Take your fingers to this cleat to dig out bits of tree and gather them. Taking your fingers to this cleat is the only way to gather the tree bits stuck in it. Gather them in a big pile… as evidence to examine later.
meanings.hint: Picture your fingers really getting in there to gather the tree pieces from out of this cleat. Some of the tree bits are stabbing under your fingernails, but you have to keep gathering them, don't stop now!

readings.mnemonic: You gather them all together and realize there are cy borg ( さい ) parts mixed in with the tree bits. Uh oh, it's as you feared. Your cyborg definitely got into trouble somewhere around here and you walked all over it with your cleats.
readings.hint: Gather all of these cyborg bits so you can put the cyborg back together later.

meanings.mnemonic: If you look inside a samurai 's heart , you'll discover their true intention s and ambition s.
meanings.hint: If you're facing down a samurai, it's pretty important for you to be able to understand their intentions. Not all samurai are good, you know.

readings.mnemonic: You try to read the intention s of the samurai in front of you and they're dark, evil, scary. Oh no! These are the intentions of the shee p ( し ) samurai!
readings.hint: The sheep samurai only has violent intentions for other samurai and sheep. Its heart is dark and surprisingly easy to read.

meanings.mnemonic: If you get on your scooter and enter somewhere, you’re going into that place.
meanings.hint: Really focus on going "into" wherever this is. Into the place you go. Right into it.

readings.mnemonic: Once you’ve gone into this place, what do you see? Your 子 ( こ )!
readings.hint: How did your 子 get into here? Your 子 really gets into everywhere. They get into all kinds of places, and they get into all kinds of trouble!

meanings.mnemonic: There’s a comb and what looks like the same comb , and you found them on the ground next to some fins you showed interest in. At first, the shiny fins on the ground caught your interest, but once you got closer to the fins you also noticed a pair of combs that are interesting too.
meanings.hint: Pick up these fins and the two combs. Feel their weight in each hand as you examine them with interest. Smell the fish smell and feel the teeth on the combs.

readings.mnemonic: Since the combs interest you more than the fins, you examine the combs more closely. Taking interest in where they were made, you discover they were made in きょう と . Dang, きょうと sure does make some nice combs.
readings.hint: You've always had an interest in きょうと , so it makes sense that you'd be interested in things from きょうと too.

meanings.mnemonic: If your mouth is big but your heart is in the right place, people will recognize your kindness . Even if you blabber on with your big mouth, as long as everyone knows that your heart is filled with kindness, they will love and accept you.
meanings.hint: Share your kindness with the world. Use your big mouth to let people know what you're doing. "I'M TAKING OUT THE TRASH AND NOBODY EVEN ASKED ME!" Y'know, that sort of thing. Go ahead and say that out loud a few times.

readings.mnemonic: To further show your kindness , you chop up some on ions ( おん ) for a friend who always cries when they chop onions. The onions make you cry too, so it's a nice act of kindness that you're toughing it out for your pal.
readings.hint: Imagine chopping up these onions. Smell the onion smell in the air as your eyes begin to sting from the onions. Your friend had better appreciate this kindness.

meanings.mnemonic: If you use a slide to enter a building, you'll be sliding for a long time .
meanings.hint: Imagine a never-ending slide that goes deep down inside a building. You can't see anything in the dark around you, you just plunge down and slide forever. And forever is a long time, my friend.

readings.mnemonic: You slide for such a long time that you get hungry. Luckily, once in a while a cu cumber ( きゅう ) comes tumbling down the slide next to you.
readings.hint: Cucumbers are tasty, but not when you've been eating them for a long time.

meanings.mnemonic: A Viking and his older brother join a party . They can raid and pillage better in a group like this, so it makes sense that the Viking and his older brother would join a group with other Vikings.
meanings.hint: Look at this big group that contains the Viking and his older brother. Hear them shouting as a group and imagine their Viking battle cry.

readings.mnemonic: This party has decided to raid とう きょう . It’s a large undertaking, but they have a large group of Vikings on the job. とうきょう doesn’t stand a chance.
readings.hint: Imagine this group of Vikings rampaging through とうきょう . See all the Japanese people running away and screaming as the group of Vikings comes busting in, swinging their axes and shouting loudly.

meanings.mnemonic: If the canopy comes beforehand , it's a preface for the rest of the canopy.
meanings.hint: Although not necessarily a book preface, you can imagine a book made out of canvas pages. The canvas pages that came beforehand (i.e., in the beginning, before the book started) is the preface.

readings.mnemonic: You read the preface . It just goes on and on and on about Jo -Anne ( じょ ), a short lady the author loves very much.
readings.hint: Actually imagine reading through the preface of your favorite book. Except, there's a preface that just goes into great detail about Jo-Anne and doesn't seem related at all to the book.

meanings.mnemonic: You use your mouth when you see a Chinese person and you sigh . You sigh so loud that it’s practically a roar coming out of your mouth. Chinese people and Chinese things make you sigh because it reminds you of when you stayed in China and it brings up memories. The sigh is a sigh of nostalgia.
meanings.hint: Let out a long sigh as you recall memories from China and all the Chinese things you saw and did. Oh, sigh.

readings.mnemonic: Your favorite Chinese thing to do is tan ( たん ). You can wander around without your shirt on and tan all day. No one will sigh or scoff at you, it’s perfectly acceptable. The Chinese just love to get their tan on.
readings.hint: Sigh again as you remember how nice it was to tan everywhere. Picture yourself wandering around some Chinese streets with your shirt off as you soak in the rays and get a tan.

meanings.mnemonic: Flowers growing out of a rice paddy into the shape of a cross are really showy flower s.
meanings.hint: Picture these showy flowers growing straight up and out of the middle of the rice paddy, into the shape of a giant cross.

readings.mnemonic: These flower s are too showy . You hate them. You hate these showy flowers so much you run them over with your ca r ( か )!
readings.hint: Imagine how the car feels as you ram into the showy flowers. The petals explode and rain everywhere. You turn on your windshield wipers to push them away.

meanings.mnemonic: Your leader sticks his toe into a shellfish . You ask him why and he says he wants to spy on it.
meanings.hint: There’s a hidden camera on his toe and the leader wants to have plenty of secret spy footage to review later.

readings.mnemonic: You and the leader review the spy footage and find a single ta ter ( てい ) tot hidden within the shellfish. Together you uncover a huge smuggling ring. Clams, oysters, mussels, you name it! Shellfish of all kinds are using their bodies to sneak tater tots to the undersea world.
readings.hint: If it wasn't for your toe spy network, these egregious tater tot crimes would have never reached the light of day.

meanings.mnemonic: If you can write an interesting poem about winter while riding on a horse , I’ll be pretty surprised . It’s hard enough to write a poem as it is, let alone an interesting one. And then you’re riding on a horse and the poem has to be about winter? Heck, I’d be surprised if you didn’t fall off the horse and injure yourself.
meanings.hint: Imagine trying to write a poem while riding a horse. It’d be really hard to do. I bet you’d be surprised if you managed to do it as well.

readings.mnemonic: You might also be surprised to learn that you are to ride this horse all across きょう と . That gives you a time limit. You have until you traverse きょうと to write this poem about winter.
readings.hint: Imagine riding through きょうと on a horse. As if that wasn’t difficult enough for poem writing, now you have all the sights and sounds of きょうと to distract you as well.

meanings.mnemonic: Wrap your fingers around this broom , because it's time to sweep !
meanings.hint: Keep your fingers tightly wrapped around the broom — if you drop it mid-sweep, all that dirt and dust will go flying everywhere and you'll have to start over from scratch.

readings.mnemonic: Sweep ing is good for the sou l ( そう ). Every sweep of the broom makes your soul a little more pure. Cleanliness is next to godliness, after all.
readings.hint: Feel your soul getting more and more purified as you sweep every inch of your house. Your soul's getting squeaky-clean, and it honestly feels amazing.

meanings.mnemonic: You want some flowers so you take your cleat to the side of a tree hoping they'll fall down, but you get a vegetable instead. What? You kick the tree again and another, different vegetable falls out.
meanings.hint: Now you have two vegetables and no flowers. Do vegetables even grow on trees?

readings.mnemonic: You feed these vegetable s (that you didn't even want) to your cy borg ( さい ). It only needs a little though, since its stomach is so thin, so you just make it hold the rest.
readings.hint: Picture your cyborg nibbling on the vegetables you cleat-kicked out of this tree. You give all of the things you don't like to your cyborg nowadays. Which is probably why there are so many weird things all over it...

meanings.mnemonic: The warehouse radical and the warehouse kanji are the same! You can call it a storehouse too.
meanings.hint: Know your radicals, yo!

readings.mnemonic: You have a warehouse to store all of your sou ls ( そう ). When people or animals you know pass away, you take their souls and store them in your dry warehouse for safekeeping. It's a public service, really.
readings.hint: Imagine if you kept a bunch of souls in a warehouse. This way they can't haunt anyone. And they can't get lost out in the spirit world. The warehouse is best for them.

meanings.mnemonic: You count nine of them in the tree , each turkey holding something random or miscellaneous . There are nine turkeys in the tree and they’re all holding random, miscellaneous things. How odd.
meanings.hint: Look at the turkeys in the tree. There are nine of them and they are all gobbling up a storm. Hear them gobble as you imagine what random, miscellaneous things they are holding.

readings.mnemonic: As if things couldn’t get any more random , Zazu ( ざつ ) from the Lion King flies into the tree and starts asking the nine turkeys miscellaneous trivia. Zazu always did have a thirst for knowledge, but this is kind of strange.
readings.hint: Look at Zazu asking the turkeys random questions about miscellaneous trivia. You’ve seen the Lion King, right? Zazu should be pretty easy to visualize here.

meanings.mnemonic: Tie a thread to yourself whenever you take the blame for someone. This marks your achievement . Your achievement in blame-taking, I guess. Isn't it a great achievement to selflessly shield others from blame? Of course it is! So these little blame-threads are symbols of your achievement.
meanings.hint: Pretty soon your fingers, toes, and whole body are covered in threads, for all your innumerable achievements. Think back on those achievements, on all those times you took the blame for someone. You're just so, so selfless. What a good person you are. Ahh, it feels good to be good.

readings.mnemonic: But your latest achievement is covering yourself in these threads and everyone thinking it's sexy ( せき ). The newspapers and fashion mags can't get enough of your sexy style. See? If you're a good person, people will think your achievements are sexy.
readings.hint: Imagine setting this sexy thread-based fashion trend. You're named the sexiest person alive and everyone dresses like spools of thread to copy you. But the achievements to match? Well, only you have those.

meanings.mnemonic: Under the moon while you sit on a cliff , you inject a syringe of tofu right into your vein . Ahh, tofu. You're addicted to it. You sneak out to the cliff at night to get your tofu fix, injecting it straight into your vein.
meanings.hint: Feel the tofu running through your veins. Feel the tofu goodness spreading all over your body. It's so intense you can even taste a little tofu in your mouth.

readings.mnemonic: Eventually, your friends and family find you at the cliff, tripping on tofu. Each and every vein on your body is engorged, pulsating with the effects of tofu. Your friends try and take you home. "You cyan't myake ( みゃく ) me!" you slur, suffering from the effects of the tofu. "None of you cyan myake me go home! You cyan’t myake me! Nyah!"
readings.hint: Say this out loud, it'll help you remember. Really focus on the "myake" be sure to say it like you're all bugged out from tofu overdose.

meanings.mnemonic: Under a tree sitting on the grass is a woman who loves sakura , or cherry tree , if you’re an English speaker. This woman really likes to just chill out in the grass under these cherry trees and watch the sakura blossoms. It’s very pretty.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself sitting under the tree in the grass with the woman. Smell the cherries and see the sakura blossoms falling down all around you. How whimsical.

readings.mnemonic: How do you say cherry tree in Japanese? Sakura ( さくら ). Sakura is Japanese for cherry tree. I’m sure you’ve probably heard this before, so it shouldn’t be too hard to remember.
readings.hint: Sakura means cherry tree and sakura is the way to pronounce this kanji. Easy!

meanings.mnemonic: Standing on your roof , you're certain that the nearby mountain is shrouded in secrecy . But you can't do much about it, stuck on your roof as you are. So much secrecy, but so far away.
meanings.hint: Really feel the frustration of wanting to uncover all the secrecy around this mountain but being stuck on your stupid roof.

readings.mnemonic: This secrecy is so secret that the people who know about it wear magical mitts ( みつ ) of secrecy that make it so we can't tell anyone, no matter what.
readings.hint: You can imagine trying to break one of the mitts-wearing members of this group, but they can't tell you anything because of their magical mitts.

meanings.mnemonic: You're loiter ing, staring down at the cross that got caught in the net of your heart , because it's trying to teach you to act with virtue .
meanings.hint: People who keep the cross in their hearts are supposed to be full of virtue, always doing the right thing, always helping others. But you aren't sure if you want to be virtuous, it's a lot of responsibility. So you look down at this symbolic cross and loiter instead of making a decision.

readings.mnemonic: As you try to decide if you want to live a life of virtue the ghost of Toku gawa ( とく ) Ieyasu appears before you. He points to your heart and the cross, then points to his own and you see a glowing cross in his own.
readings.hint: I don't know about you, but I'm pretty sure Tokugawa Ieyasu is telling you to choose a life of virtue.

meanings.mnemonic: A husband and his husband find a shellfish and agree to share it.
meanings.hint: Picture the husbands pulling a different side of the shellfish, it pops in two, and they each get a shell. They smile and high five.

readings.mnemonic: Since the husbands were able to agree , San ta-san ( さん ) believes they’re both good husbands and belong on the good list. Therefore, Santa-san gives them each lots and lots of shellfish for Christmas.
readings.hint: Imagine Santa-san flying over the husbands in his sleigh, raining shellfish down upon them from the sky. It warms the heart, wouldn’t you agree?

meanings.mnemonic: Take some alcohol and put a spider in it to make acid . Spiders have a little acid in them already, but if you want something really potent, smash one up in some whiskey to make a deadly acid that'll rip through anything.
meanings.hint: Imagine crushing up some spiders into alcohol. Watch it turn green. It's going to eat through the beaker! Quick, do something!

readings.mnemonic: You're trying to find a container for this acid when San ta-san ( さん ) bursts through your wall. He frowns down at you. You're on the naughty list now. You killed spiders AND made dangerous acid? Ooooh you're in trouble.
readings.hint: It's Christmas morning. Instead of gifts, Santa-san just throws a piece of charcoal at your face. Feel the charcoal smash into your face and explode into acid, burning you. Santa-san runs away laughing. Ho ho ho!

meanings.mnemonic: You come across a spirit wearing a top hat and it claims to be your ancestor . Well of course, if you remember correctly, all of your ancestors wore top hats when they were alive. This spirit must be your long lost, great great... uh... somebody.
meanings.hint: Imagine coming across your long dead top hatted ancestor in spirit form. What would you do? What would you say?

readings.mnemonic: You ask your ancestor what it wants and it points to the so ap ( そ ) by the sink. Soap? It nods at you. I guess your ancestor is here to see the wonders of soap.
readings.hint: Your ancestor didn't come here to see you, it just wanted to learn about soap. Something that they didn't have back when it was alive. That's actually kinda sad... and stinky. Your ancestor needs to use this soap, if anything.

meanings.mnemonic: A grain is certain to keep a secret .
meanings.hint: Imagine telling a piece of grain your deepest, darkest secret.

readings.mnemonic: If someone finds out your secret (not from the grain though, it will certainly keep it) then your entire face will fill with hea t ( ひ ).
readings.hint: This works both ways too. If someone tells you a secret, that same heat fills you up. Now whether this heat is from embarrassment or the rush of getting to be part of a secret is up to you to figure out.

meanings.mnemonic: To use your gold at the bar you need to turn it into a coin . Well, more than one coin, probably. But you know for sure that you can make at least one coin out of the gold you have.
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself strenuously pushing a lever to flatten your gold and punch a coin out it. You sweat as you hurry to make enough coins so you can go to the bar before it closes.

readings.mnemonic: You use the coin to pay a cen taur ( せん ) at the bar. The centaur is the bartender and he makes special drinks for those who pay in gold coins. Good for you!
readings.hint: Imagine handing this centaur bartender your gold coins. Hear him thank you in his booming voice and make you a mysterious gold-colored drink. Look in wonder at how the centaur has managed to fit in the narrow space behind the bar counter.

meanings.mnemonic: If you see a grass y cliff with an elf under it in winter , it's because their parents are so strict . When elf kids misbehave they're supposed to go stand under grassy cliffs to think about what they've done.
meanings.hint: Only really, really strict parents would make their kids do that in the middle of winter! It's cold!

readings.mnemonic: One of the strict parents of this elf kid is... Gen ji ( げん )?! Looks like he had another illegitimate kid... this time with an elf.
readings.hint: Oh Genji, if only you were as strict about using protection as you were on your elf child.

meanings.mnemonic: Your fingers have nothing to do when you're at your cottage except throw away things. Your cottage is for vacation. And vacation means being as lazy as possible. But you certainly don't want to live in filth, so you have to throw away your trash at least.
meanings.hint: Imagine sitting in your cottage. You throw away a banana peel. You throw away some used suntan lotion. You throw away all your cares in the world.

readings.mnemonic: The number one thing you throw away while in your cottage is your su it ( す ). Suits just don't fit the cottage vibe, man.
readings.hint: Just throw away all your suits when you want to relax. Don't worry about needing one someday, throw it awaaaay!

meanings.mnemonic: They say a shrimp who is bad with translation has no reason to live. Most shrimp make a living with their translation skills. They are the translators of the ocean. If they don’t know how to say something in all the languages of the sea, they have no reason to live. They might as well be dead.
meanings.hint: Imagine this one shrimp who is bad at translation. He’s trying to find a reason to live, but he’s having trouble. Look at him all alone, being shunned by the other shrimp.

readings.mnemonic: The translation this shrimp failed to make was for the word “ yak ” ( やく ). The shrimp found no reason to translate yak because there are no yak in the sea and then the shrimp just started questioning everything in his life and fell apart.
readings.hint: The translation of the word yak was the reason this shrimp fell into despair and gave up the translation profession. Now everyone hates him. See this poor shrimp being harassed by others while they shout at him, “Yak! Yak! Yak!”

meanings.mnemonic: In the valley you feel a lack of want . You want for nothing in this valley. It is the valley of plenty.
meanings.hint: Think of how much you want to be in this valley. They have everything in there. You lack nothing. You want for nothing. Man, you wanna be in that valley so badly.

readings.mnemonic: Once you get to the valley, you want some protein. Egg yolk ( よく ) falls from the sky! It’s a miracle! You wanted protein, and egg yolk was presented. It falls right into your open mouth.
readings.hint: This was the yolk you wanted. Feel it slosh into your mouth from the sky. Taste the yolk on your tongue before swallowing it. Mmm, egg yolk.

meanings.mnemonic: You're in the sun playing football and you get very warm .
meanings.hint: Feel how warm the sun is, as you try to throw and catch the football.

readings.mnemonic: It's warm like this because it's dawn ( だん ). That's when the sun comes up.
readings.hint: Dang, you knew you should have played night football so it wouldn't get so warm. Now the dawn sun is making you uncomfortable under all those layers of football protection.

meanings.mnemonic: In its scooter , a cat pirate scoots up to welcome you!
meanings.hint: "Nya! Welcome to my ship, grab a scooter and scoot on up here, we're about to start the welcome ceremonya!"

readings.mnemonic: The cat pirate then launches into a speech about how they welcome all kinds of people. Especially gay ( げい ) people. He puts a certain emphasis on gay, so you figure that the cat pirate either thinks you're gay or wants you to be.
readings.hint: You're really happy this cat pirate welcomes gay people aboard. Whether that's because you're gay or just because you're a baseline decent human being is up to you.

meanings.mnemonic: If a leader has a gun it's easy to wound people. When you have a gun and a lot of responsibility it's easy to get carried away and mess up, wounding the people you were supposed to be protecting. You can even wound yourself.
meanings.hint: Picture how easy it would be for the leader of your country to wound someone if they had a gun. Scary, right?

readings.mnemonic: Which leader wounded the Japanese people? The Shou gun ( しょう ). The Shougun is a military leader. The Shougun even has gun in the name. And they wounded hundreds of thousands of samurai and soldiers.
readings.hint: Picture the Shougun you've slowly grown to know over the last thirty-ish levels. But he's holding a gun wounding everyone around him. He's screaming and shooting and wounding everyone in sight. Even his mom, even his dog, even his farmers.

meanings.mnemonic: On a cliff , a fire burns to ashes . The ashes then blow off the cliff, and follow the wind wherever it may go.
meanings.hint: See the light from the fire as the ashes fly off the cliff. The wind carries some of the ashes over to you. Feel the ashes as they strike your face and wipe the black ash with your hands.

readings.mnemonic: The ashes inspire you to write a hai ku ( はい ). Using the ashes you write a haiku on a rock for everyone to enjoy.
readings.hint: Ashes on my face. Falling from the cliff of fire. Gives my life meaning. Or just come up with your own haiku. Whatever works for you.

meanings.mnemonic: If you see icicle s hanging off of a samurai 's clothes , don't worry, those are just part of his winter attire . Samurai have to travel and fight in all sorts of conditions, so it only makes sense that they have attire for every season.
meanings.hint: Picture a samurai in his winter attire. His helmet is white and fuzzy and he has decorative icicles hanging down off of his armor.

readings.mnemonic: Attire like this is important because it reflects your soul ( そう ). The samurai is wearing clothing with icicles because that's how his heart feels too: frozen over because he has to fight in this horrible winter weather.
readings.hint: Picture the kind of attire you'd wear as a samurai to reflect your soul. Mine would be bright pink and covered in horses and bananas and daisies to show that I have a happy, kind soul and that I don't want to be killed by anyone else.

meanings.mnemonic: If you want to give flowers to someone , give them to an author . Authors really love flowers, because they help them think of flowery language they can use to sound smart in their books.
meanings.hint: Imagine a time when a book you were reading used flowery language and you couldn't understand. Imagine the annoyance with the author for being so overwrought and flowery.

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, the author is allergic to touching flowers, so they hold them with a pair of cho psticks ( ちょ ). They hold them real close to their face with the chopsticks, so they can still use them to think of fancy, author-ly words.
readings.hint: Picture the author trying to use chopsticks to hold some little flowers. It looks like they're having a hard time, but people will do anything to be a great author, I suppose.

meanings.mnemonic: There's a lid on your village , so you kick it over on its backside to see what's on its underside .
meanings.hint: You've been curious about what's on the lid (and your village's) backside for years, and you finally can't take it anymore so you kick the lid as hard as you can!

readings.mnemonic: It turns out your village's own backside is covered in ura nium ( うら ). Eureka! You're all gonna be rich!
readings.hint: Wait, isn't uranium bad for people? This explains why all of the villagers' backsides are covered in weird glowing, green lumps.

meanings.mnemonic: This kanji comes straight from the droopy radical. What do droopy things do? They droop . Oh, and sometimes they dangle too.
meanings.hint: Don't let your spirits droop — kanji can be tough to learn, but you're doing great!

readings.mnemonic: You know what's better when it droop s a little? A swi ng ( すい ). Those swings with the droopy rubber seats are so comfortable to sit on and dangle your legs off of — way better than the rigid wooden ones.
readings.hint: Imagine setting your bum in a droopy rubber swing seat. Feel it droop down a little lower as you put your weight into it and let your legs dangle. Ah, such comfort.

meanings.mnemonic: You keep your moon weapon in your crotch . Where else would you keep it?
meanings.hint: Your moon weapon is a moon that you keep in your crotch. We'll let you imagine how this one works...

readings.mnemonic: Your crotch has a moon weapon, and it's served you well, so you hope that if you have a 子 ( こ ) one day, they'll have one too.
readings.hint: Your mother had a moon weapon, and her father before her. It's been in the family for generations, and you hope to pass it on to your 子 as well.

meanings.mnemonic: Social commentary aside, the white king is the king that wears all white. Traditionally, this is someone better than a king. The emperor !
meanings.hint: See the emperor, garbed all in white. There are other kings, but the emperor's white clothing shows his status above the rest.

readings.mnemonic: Who is the emperor ? It is こう いち . こういち the emperor stands over his subjects.
readings.hint: How did こういち become emperor?! His first decree as emperor is that everybody must learn kanji now.

meanings.mnemonic: Put a rice paddy together with other paddies and you'll see how much they differ . When you're driving or taking a train through the countryside it may seem like all of those rice paddies blurring together are all the same, but they can differ quite a bit!
meanings.hint: Try to picture how each rice paddy differs from the next. Can you think of any differences in the ones you've seen?

readings.mnemonic: To see how rice paddies differ , you need an ea gle's ( い ) eye view. It can be hard to see from the ground, so soar up into the sky like an eagle and look down at the rice paddies together.
readings.hint: Aha! As an eagle you can easily see that all of the rice paddies have differing colors, shapes, and (if you fly low) smells. They differ a lot!

meanings.mnemonic: Use a flag to compare your flatulence .
meanings.hint: How do you compare your flatulence? I recommend a flag. You can see which fart is strongest by seeing how much the flag flutters.

readings.mnemonic:
How do you read flatulence ? Heh ( へ )...heh..heh..heheheh. You can't NOT laugh at the word flatulence.

Note that some dictionaries incorrectly list おなら as a reading for this kanji. While おなら also means "flatulence" (as in "fart"), you’ll only ever encounter this reading in hiragana, as the kanji can only be read as へ .
readings.hint: Picture yourself unable to stop snickering over the word "flatulence", like a little schoolchild. Heh… heh…

meanings.mnemonic: To make your fingers wide you extend them.
meanings.hint: Extend your fingers right now and see how wide you can make them.

readings.mnemonic: As you extend your fingers a cock ( かく ) comes running by and runs straight into them. Bu-COCK!
readings.hint: You poked the cock right in the eye with your extended fingers. Maybe you shouldn't be doing that in public. Innocent cocks could get hurt.

meanings.mnemonic: A greenhouse is already really good when it comes to a plant's livelihood. But add the sun into that equation and you have something really great for the plants. Their livelihood will be awesome, now!
meanings.hint: Just feel happy that your plants have such a nice livelihood now that the sun is out. They look so healthy and fresh!

readings.mnemonic: From out of nowhere, Bo bo ( ぼ ) the notorious clown thief starts picking the plants, ending their livelihood . Bobo needs the flowers for his clown acts, so he uses the livelihood of the flowers to support his own livelihood.
readings.hint: Look at Bobo do what he needs to do to maintain his own livelihood. Hear the flowers screaming as they are plucked from their homes.

meanings.mnemonic: Those that are in the middle of your heart are the ones you have the most loyalty towards. The middle of your heart is the most sacred, special place for people, so it makes sense that those you keep in the middle of your heart are those you are most loyal to.
meanings.hint: Who do you feel the most loyalty towards? Imagine them in the middle of your heart. Feel them in your chest. Imagine them, literally inside the middle of your heart.

readings.mnemonic: You pictured Chew bacca ( ちゅう ), didn’t you? No? Well you should have. Chewbacca deserves everyone’s loyalty . Chewbacca is super loyal to you, so he deserves your loyalty back.
readings.hint: Chewbacca is very loyal. Focus on your loyalty to Chewbacca. Picture him right in the middle of your heart and hear his Chewbacca yell. Also keep in mind that this kanji shares its reading with 中 which is also included in this kanji.

meanings.mnemonic: There is a moon and a city inside your lung . Inside of your lung there is a city and above that city lies a moon. It’s a pretty sight, but also very strange. Moons and cities don’t usually reside inside lungs.
meanings.hint: Imagine having a moon and a city inside your lung. Feel their presence as you breathe in and breathe out. Also, 月 often refers to internal organs as well, so remember that when you see this kanji.

readings.mnemonic: Having a moon and city inside your lung inspires you to write a hai ku ( はい ). Perhaps the city and moon are just metaphorical. Either way, a haiku is the best way to go about things here.
readings.hint: The city and moon Inside my lung, forgotten. Where is the answer?

meanings.mnemonic:
If someone has their fingers in your syrup , it's just another way of saying they manipulate d you. Cause only slimy people manipulate other people, so their fingers are slimy and sticky because they're covered in your syrup from mucking up your life.

This also has another meaning: chastity . Just imagine if someone wanted you to keep your chastity and they called s.e.x. when someone "puts their fingers in your syrup." That would be pretty effective, right? Cause it's like, super gross.
meanings.hint: "Ah golly gee wiz, he sure got his fingers all up in my syrup again, dagnabbit." You know, this saying we say all the time when someone manipulates us into doing something.

readings.mnemonic: If you want to manipulate someone else it's going to have a permanent effect on your sou l ( そう ). Being manipulative isn't cool. And your soul will suffer forever for it.
readings.hint: What happens to your soul, you ask? It gets covered in syrup. That's probably where the syrup fingers phrase came from in the first place.

meanings.mnemonic: To say what's in a samurai 's heart you start a magazine . You go around writing about and interviewing all the samurai so you can share what they say to everyone who wants to read!
meanings.hint: Why hadn't anyone thought of making a samurai magazine before?!

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, your magazine helps the shee p ( し ) samurai find other samurai... and kill them. None of the samurai want to share their feelings with your magazine anymore, oh no!
readings.hint: The sheep samurai is ruining your magazine! Maybe you should start a new magazine called, "All the places the sheep samurai will be, so stay away!" to make up for all the damage you've caused.

meanings.mnemonic: This bamboo absorbed the moon 's power , and you want that power in your body, so you surgically insert them into your body to replace your muscle s and tendon s.
meanings.hint: Picture your new bamboo muscles glowing under your skin with the moon's power. You flex and feel the power coursing through you!

readings.mnemonic: Your special muscle s and tendon s have become famous and the Kin g ( きん ) of England has requested that you appear in his court! It looks like he wants you to teach his barber surgeons how you got your new muscles.
readings.hint: Imagine standing before the King, explaining in detail how you cut yourself open and replaced your weak human meat with the special muscles you have now. He's staring intently at you, but more so at your shining body than your mouth as you talk.

meanings.mnemonic: The Not Mouth says “ no ”. What else would you expect from something named the “Not Mouth”? The Not Mouth will always say “no”, regardless of the question.
meanings.hint: Imagine encountering this Not Mouth. Ask it a question. It answers, “No”. Imagine doing this over and over until you believe that the Not Mouth always says “no”.

readings.mnemonic: You ask the Not Mouth if it is afraid of he at ( ひ ). The Not Mouth says, “ no ”. So you set the Not Mouth on fire. The Not Mouth then screams, “NONONONONONONONONONOOOOOO~!” Looks like someone can’t handle the heat.
readings.hint: Listen as the Not Mouth continues to scream in bloodcurdling agony. Hear the flames flicker as they lick across the Not Mouth’s being. Seems as though the heat was too much for the Not Mouth.

meanings.mnemonic: If you become a plate , I will pile food up upon you. With such a huge pile, everyone will think you're prosperous .
meanings.hint: If you have piles of something, you're prosperous. If you become a plate, that's the best thing that can happen to you.

readings.mnemonic: You're so prosperous you use your sa ber ( せい ) to cut the food pile and shove the bits into your plate mouth. In fact, your entire family is using your special saber to cut and eat off of you. A normal knife wouldn't be able to get through all this food!
readings.hint: You know a family is seriously prosperous when they don't use forks, they use a magical saber to cut their food. Wowee wow wow.

meanings.mnemonic: Someone climbs onto the roof from the ground when dawn comes to proclaim that a new day has begun! They proclaim this at the top of their lungs!
meanings.hint: Picture a shadowy shape on top of the roof, proclaiming to the entire town that the new day is here! It's come! And it's also time to get up.

readings.mnemonic: The thing that climbs up on the roof to proclaim that the new day is here is a cen taur ( せん ). Ohoho, did you think I was going to say it was a rooster? Wrong! Centaurs are taller than humans and roosters, so when they climb onto the roof with their horse legs they can see dawn starting before anyone else.
readings.hint: Picture that shadow on the roof, proclaiming it's a new day, but this time make sure you picture the outline of this centaur. He has a human top half and a horse bottom half. And he's yelling down at you, "HEY THE SUN IS UP IT'S DAWN GET OUT OF BED!"

meanings.mnemonic: The leader who is king of every shellfish sets the rent for everyone. If you rent a home in King Shellfish's kingdom, the rent will be set by the great shellfish leader himself.
meanings.hint: King Shellfish loves when people rent from him. Imagine him rolling in all of the rent money (shellfish) he collected from all the people who rent from him. Hear his shell clattering as he frolics on all the dead shells from the shellfish.

readings.mnemonic: If you don't have enough shellfish to pay your rent , King Shellfish will punch you in the chin ( ちん ).
readings.hint: Feel your entire body shaking, but especially your chin, as you tell him you're two shellfish short on rent this month. The last thing you see is his big fist coming right for your chin.

meanings.mnemonic: Writing a poem about winter is the only way to get respect from other poets.
meanings.hint: Even better, write a poem about winter IN winter. Then they'll really respect you.

readings.mnemonic: Once you have their respect you get ca ke ( けい )!
readings.hint: The cake has the word RESPECT in fancy, cursive letters. Imagine eating that cake and taste the respect of your poet peers. Mm mm!

meanings.mnemonic: If you receive something edible, arrange it in a circle and boil it. That way, it’ll ripen more quickly.
meanings.hint: Picture yourself receiving something, arranging it in a circle in the pan, and leaving it to boil. See how quickly that stuff ripens? Now it’s all ripe and yummy.

readings.mnemonic: Once everything you received has ripen ed, you kick back near your juke box ( じゅく ) and eat it. The jukebox sets the mood, making the ripened treat feels twice as sweet.
readings.hint: Imagine the jukebox playing music to accompany your ripened treat. Ahh, jukebox music and ripened goodies. Can life get any sweeter?

meanings.mnemonic: Next to your mouth there are fins which react to alcohol . If you exceed a certain alcohol measurement , they start flapping about, which makes you spread rumors and gossip uncontrollably.
meanings.hint: Just imagine these fins forcing your mouth to move with all their flapping. This is what happens when you have too much alcohol, you can't control yourself.

readings.mnemonic: The flapping fins are making you slur your words, but that doesn't stop you from spreading rumors and gossip : " Ooo whassa t ( うわさ ) I hear about so and so having an affair?!"
readings.hint: Just imagine trying to talk eloquently and clearly when you have fins flapping around your mouth. And if you've had a few drinks on top of that, it stands to reason that you'd start slurring your words.

meanings.mnemonic: The horns of the creatures who drink alcohol have a measurement so large that they are revered the world over. The horns are revered both for their size and potency. The horns can be used in medicine, so they are very valuable, those alcoholic monster horns.
meanings.hint: Look at one of these revered horns. Pick it up and feel its weight. It measures very large. Your arms are getting tired from the weight.

readings.mnemonic: The reason you are interested in these revered horns so much is that your son ( そん ) is sick and you need one of these revered horns to make a medicine for your son. Only with the revered power of the horns will you be able to save your son’s life.
readings.hint: Good thing you already have one of these revered horns. Now imagine yourself grinding it up into a powder to mix into a potion for your son to drink. Hear the horn grinding away as you physically break the horn up into millions of tiny pieces.

meanings.mnemonic: You line up stalks of bamboo with an equal interval between each one. This pattern creates a sense of simplicity .
meanings.hint: Bamboo, interval, bamboo, interval, bamboo… Almost like a binary rhythm: 1, 0, 1, 0, 1. Now that's simplicity. It doesn't get simpler than that!

readings.mnemonic: That kind of simplicity is what Genghis Khan ( かん ) always demanded. Everything he decided was based on simplicity.
readings.hint: Simplicity is how he took over the world. Attack or don't attack? Boom, simple. If he hadn't stuck to simplicity, he wouldn't have been able to do what he did. Complex thoughts just get in the way of expansionist ambitions, you know?

meanings.mnemonic: If you grind a stone down to more than just a few pieces, it will become sand . Stones that get broken up enough will become sand, and you pick up a few grains in your hand.
meanings.hint: Feel these few grains of sand between your fingertips. Imagine the stones that they originally came from. Nature is crazy.

readings.mnemonic: After breaking up your stone into sand , you fetch your special sand sa w ( さ ). A few bits are still too chunky, so you'll have to use a sand saw now.
readings.hint: You may wonder what a sand saw is. Well, it's a saw for cutting sand into even finer little pieces. How does it work? It's a mystery!

meanings.mnemonic: If you take the flowers off some coral and boil them, steam comes out.
meanings.hint: Steam comes out of the coral flowers because they hold a lot of water. They're flowers, but they're coral flowers, so they came from the ocean. Of course there's water in them, and if you boil that water steam will come out.

readings.mnemonic: As you watch the steam coming off the coral flowers, someone appears behind you. It’s Joe ( じょう ), the big farmhand. "Are you steaming coral?" he asks. "Smelled it a mile away. I love steaming me some coral.”
readings.hint: It's weird that Joe is so sensitive to the smell of steaming coral. He's lived as a farmhand nowhere near the sea all his life... at least that's what he told you. And now he's in your house breathing in the steam really deeply with his nose practically in the pot.

meanings.mnemonic: The storehouse radical and the storehouse kanji meanings are the same. If you know the radical, you know the kanji!
meanings.hint: Your brain is like a storehouse, you know? Hopefully that's where you kept your storehouse knowledge.

readings.mnemonic: You have a storehouse . Inside is where you keep all your zo mbies ( ぞう ).
readings.hint: Be really proud of your storehouse. You've collected a lot of zombies and put them in there, where they'll keep fresh a long time!

meanings.mnemonic: Bring some rice under a canopy and stuff it in Wolverine 's mouth . Make him chew it a bit, scrape it out, and voila — you've made sugar !
meanings.hint: Taste that sweet, sweet sugar. Not many people know, but this is how it's made. The canopy is needed for temperature control, and the wolverine… well, it's got special enzymes in its mouth or something.

readings.mnemonic: You learned how to make this kind of sugar in とう きょう . You found a special とうきょう sugar sensei and they introduced you to all the secret methods you know now.
readings.hint: As you eat the sugar, you picture とうきょう . The place where you fell in love with these sugar-making techniques.

meanings.mnemonic: The thread you have inside is your whole supply . When you're outside sewing and you run out, you just go inside, look through your supply, and pick a new one.
meanings.hint: Go inside your house or shed, it doesn't matter where, and look happily at your thread supply. You have thread in all sizes, shapes, and especially colors.

readings.mnemonic: You have this supply because No stradamus ( のう ) had a prediction, saying you would. And that if you didn't keep a good supply of thread inside, you'd run out.
readings.hint: Nostradamus says you should have a large supply of everything you use regularly. Thread, water, canned foods. You can't see the future, but he can, and if he's telling you to stock up on your inside supplies I think you should listen.

meanings.mnemonic: They say someone with various talents is important. Various talents can include speaking various languages so if you are someone who knows various languages you can say things that various peoples will comprehend.
meanings.hint: You are someone who can say things in various languages. Speak some of these various languages now. You are someone who is good at saying things. Nice job.

readings.mnemonic: You’re so good at speaking various languages that you decide to put on a sho w ( しょ ). At the show, you say a lot of things in various languages and everyone is impressed.
readings.hint: Imagine yourself at this show. The spotlight is on and all eyes are watching. You feel kind of nervous, but you speak the various languages with ease.

meanings.mnemonic: The hole leading to the private heart of your house is the window . Or maybe it's just the window to your soul, and not your house. Like the poetry people say.
meanings.hint: Imagine someone looking into a hole that shows your private heart. That's a window. A window you probably should have kept closed. Now everyone is looking in.

readings.mnemonic: You realize that a ton of people are looking into your window and seeing all your private things. This makes you mad ( まど )! What right do they have to know your private thoughts and desires?!
readings.hint: Look out your window back at the faces. Try to express how mad you are at them with your face through the glass. Make as many mad faces that you can think of.

meanings.mnemonic: The beans radical and the beans kanji are exactly the same!
meanings.hint: *toots*

readings.mnemonic: These beans are from とう きょう . Everybody knows とうきょう has the best beans. What?! You didn’t know that? Tokyo’s beans are the best beans!
readings.hint: Imagine eating these beans from とうきょう . They’re the best beans you’ve ever had. Even if you don’t like beans, you love とうきょう beans. Mmm… beans, beans, とうきょう beans.

meanings.mnemonic: A tree branch is exactly what it sounds like: the branch of a tree.
meanings.hint: Picture a tree branch in your hands. Feel it. Smell it. It smells and feels like a tree.

readings.mnemonic: You're smelling and feeling the branch when, suddenly, it starts screaming at you: " Eh, da d?" ( えだ ). The branch won't leave you alone. It follows you around screaming "Eh, dad?", thinking you're its long-lost tree branch father. Poor thing.
readings.hint: Picture the branch suddenly crying out, "Eh, Dad?" in a squeaky voice. You'd better find its real dad, otherwise this branch is never going to leave you alone.

meanings.mnemonic: Take your fingers , step onto the forehead of your car , and brandish them in the air. Wave them around for everyone to see.
meanings.hint: Imagine standing on top of your car, brandishing your fingers in the air while all of the cars stuck behind you honk angrily.

readings.mnemonic: What are you brandish ing in your fingers? Your car's key ( き )! You want every driver and every car to know that you aren't going to be driving ever again!
readings.hint: You brandish the key in the air and then throw it into the woods. No more cars for you! Everyone should follow your example! Brandish your car keys in the air and throw them away!

meanings.mnemonic: A slice with a knife is called a carve .
meanings.hint: You carve meat, you carve wood, all with a slicing motion with a knife.

readings.mnemonic: You work up a sweat trying to carve meat and wood all day, so you grab yourself a nice Coke ( こく ). You like Coke, and it goes well with meat and sawdust.
readings.hint: Imagine washing down all your hard carving work with an ice cold Coke.

meanings.mnemonic: The claw radical and the claw kanji are exactly the same! Ravishing!
meanings.hint: Lucky for you, this kanji has already etched its claw marks into your memory!

readings.mnemonic: You sink your claw s into anything your two 目 ( つめ ) see, because you're such a fierce predator. If your two 目 can see it, your claws will get to it. Your claws are basically an extension of your two eyes, that's how skilled you are.
readings.hint: Imagine your claws darting out, almost automatically, as soon as your two 目 have seen your prey. Imagine catching your dinner that way.

meanings.mnemonic: A child will ask if they want two water s because they need a grown-up's consent . Children aren't allowed to have whatever they want, they need to make sure it's okay and get consent first.
meanings.hint: Picture the child finishing their first water. They look at a big stack of bottled waters, but they know better than to just take two more, they need consent first. "Excuse me, sir, could I please have two more waters?" the child asks.

readings.mnemonic: "Of course my child, you have my consent ," comes the voice of the Shou gun ( しょう ). The Shougun is very pleased by this child, because he expects everyone to ask for consent around him.
readings.hint: You need to make sure to get express consent from the Shougun before you do most things. Especially if it's something like taking more than you've been given.

meanings.mnemonic: Your mouth was slice d open by a terrible cough .
meanings.hint: You've heard of a hacking cough, right? Well, this is worse. It's a slicing cough, and it's sliced right through your mouth, too. Imagine it flapping around while you cough.

readings.mnemonic: Oddly enough, some people find your cough sexy ( せき ).
readings.hint: That slicing cough opened up a nice gravelly, sexy voice. Between coughs, you start noticing all the looks you're getting. You realize your cough is making you really sexy to all the people around you. Cough cough cough.

meanings.mnemonic: In a greenhouse , a towel becomes a curtain as soon as you hang it. Greenhouses are made of windows, it's all glass, there are no walls. So when you put a towel in there it turns into a curtain. It has no choice but to cover a window, after all.
meanings.hint: Imagine hanging towels over all the windows in the greenhouse. They're really fuzzy, thick curtains now.

readings.mnemonic: You're hanging these towel curtain s because you need a quiet place to eat a Big Mac ( まく ). But you're a little ashamed. Big Macs aren't really good for you, but this stupid greenhouse has too many windows and everyone can see you stuff your face. So that's where the towel curtains come in.
readings.hint: Imagine hiding in a greenhouse blocked off with towel curtains, stuffing your face with Big Macs. No one can shame you in here. You can eat as many as you want. And hey, you can use the curtains as napkins too.

meanings.mnemonic: You use thread in your construction project to hold everything together. The color of the thread is a deep red , and it gives everything a crimson look.
meanings.hint: Because thread isn't that strong, you have to use A LOT (to hold things like beams in place). So, the deep red or crimson thread actually stands out a lot in this construction project.

readings.mnemonic: You're done, and the whole building is a deep red crimson color (it takes a lot of thread to hold a whole building in place). It's time to show the client, Emperor こう いち , the finished product.
readings.hint: こういち walks up with a big smile on his face. "I love the color crimson!" he exclaims. "It's the same deep red color as my enemies."

meanings.mnemonic: The woman sitting on that stool is actually a dude . Oh oops! You thought he was a woman because of his long hair and the way he was sitting. Maybe you shouldn't be judging people based on their looks!
meanings.hint: Dude, I can't believe you called that dude a woman. What's wrong with you?!

readings.mnemonic: You only found out this dude wasn't a woman when a do g ( ど ) ran up and the dude stood up. He started rubbing the dog's face saying, "Who's a good boy? Who's a good boy? Not you! Me! I'm the best boy of them all. Because I am a dude, the best of all boys! HAHAHAHA!"
readings.hint: Look at the poor dog. He's not a dude and now he doesn't think he's a good boy, all because of this other dude. Was he sitting on the stool this whole time, waiting to make this dog feel bad? I guess you'll never know...

meanings.mnemonic: An arrow is what this turkey lack s. For a turkey, that is a delight .
meanings.hint: A turkey that lacks an arrow is a delighted turkey.

readings.mnemonic: Delight is what Genghis Khan ( かん ) felt when he saw his turkey friend, sans an arrow.
readings.hint: He loves his turkey friend, and every time he sees it he squeals with delight.

meanings.mnemonic: The ear and the mouth of a king are holy . The king has holy hearing and holy eating abilities. This king is divine.
meanings.hint: See the holy ear and mouth of this king. They glisten and glitter and people write hymnals about their holy prowess. Imagine a song playing as you witness their holy majesty.

readings.mnemonic: This holy king has heard of you and your special sa ber ( せい ) that glows with pure, blue light. Specifically, the holy king wants to use it on his food!
readings.hint: The holy king heard you turned into a prosperous plate and used the saber to cut your food and now he wants to try it too. As he holds your saber, a holy circle forms around it, making it sparkle and shimmer! Your saber is holy now too!!

meanings.mnemonic: If the fingers on a turkey are pointing right at you, you can infer that it wants revenge. This turkey knows you killed his brother for dinner last night, and now it's going to make you pay. You infer that this turkey is going to take its fingers and wrap them around your neck.
meanings.hint: Imagine a turkey is pointing at you with its fingers. The gears in your brain begin to slowly turn. From looking at the turkey to its fingers and back to the turkey, you can infer that something bad is probably about to happen to you.

readings.mnemonic: You were able to infer the turkey's plan, so now it's your turn. You look around and see the only way out... is to swi ng ( すい ) to safety! How did I infer that, dear Watson? Why, isn't it obvious! There are vines here. Turkeys can't fly. So I shall swing my way to freedom!
readings.hint: Picture yourself as Sherlock Holmes, you inferred your way to freedom, swinging away on a conveniently-placed vine.

meanings.mnemonic: Your moon storehouse is another storehouse you own. Remember how your regular storehouse holds zombies (from the kanji 蔵 ?). Your moon storehouse is on the moon, and it is filled with internal organs .
meanings.hint: Your moon storehouse has internal organs because 1) you have to keep them far away from the zombies or they'll go berserk, and 2) you think it's funny to see them bounce around in low gravity. Boing, boing, boing, haha look at that internal organ.

readings.mnemonic: Where did you get all these internal organ s from? You actually harvested them from the zo mbies ( ぞう ) from your other storehouse.
readings.hint: Basically, you store zombies in your regular storehouse, harvest their organs, then launch them into space where they are put into a storehouse on the moon where the zombies will never be able to find them again.

meanings.mnemonic: Raise your fingers to another member and you'll receive a loss in return. You should never hurt a member of your team, group, or society. It's just wrong. And you'll incur a loss as punishment for your bad deeds.
meanings.hint: Imagine raising your fingers to slap, hurt, or point angrily at a member of your own team. How dare you! I hope you're ready for some deep, personal loss.

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately for you, payment is in the form of the loss of your son ( そん ). The loss of any family member is horrible, but this was your son. He was your favorite child. You loved him. But that's the loss you must bear for raising your fingers to a team member.
readings.hint: Picture your son. Your future son, maybe even your animal son, if you don't want to have real kids. Now imagine the loss of that son. Nooooo!

meanings.mnemonic: Seeing a stone and some horns on the ground next to a piece of poop and another piece of poop , you try and pick each of them up with a magnet . The magnet fails to pick up either piece of poop. The magnet doesn’t pick up the horns either. But wait! The magnet picks up the stone!
meanings.hint: Imagine yourself trying to pick each object up off the ground with your magnet. Hear the clink of the stone against metal as the magnet lifts it off the ground. Must be some iron in it or something.

readings.mnemonic: You’re pretty excited that you lifted something up with your magnet . Science is cool! But then you hear someone laughing behind you. It’s Je sus ( じ )! Jesus was using his magic to levitate the stone all along, it wasn’t your magnet. Darn it Jesus! You’ve fooled me for the last time!
readings.hint: Imagine Jesus snickering behind you as the stone falls to the ground once again. Imagine running after Jesus so you can catch him and give him a piece of your mind. He’s always playing tricks on you.

meanings.mnemonic: They say accidentally firing a catapult is an unforgivable mistake . They say you shot the catapult at your own castle. Even though it was an accident, it’s still a mistake you will never be forgiven for.
meanings.hint: Imagine shooting the catapult on your own people by mistake. Hear the catapult whoosh as your heart drops into your stomach. Oh, what a terrible mistake you’ve just made.

readings.mnemonic: This mistake alerts Go dzilla ( ご ), who thinks it's town destroying time. He barrels over the hills, sees where you mistakenly catapulted your own people, and proceeds to stomp on the rest of it for you.
readings.hint: Picture Godzilla jumping happily on the castle and surrounding town you hit by mistake. This is probably the worst mistake you've ever made.

meanings.mnemonic: A tsunami was the original event to mark the origin of Japan. A big tsunami came and uncovered the islands of Japan. It's the origin of all life, as far as Japanese people are concerned.
meanings.hint: Picture the original tsunami used in the origin story of Japan (never mind that we just made this up). It was huge. Bigger than anything you've ever seen. And without it you wouldn't have your precious anime and manga.

readings.mnemonic: This tsunami was also the origin of Gen ji ( げん ), from "The Tale of Genji." Just think, without this origin, Japan wouldn't exist and neither would the first Japanese heartthrob, Genji!
readings.hint: The origin of Genji was the origin of all Japan: a big ol' tsunami.

meanings.mnemonic: If it's made of thread , your barracks will be pure . If you build it out of clay or bricks or wood, it will inevitably have some dirt in it. Build it out of thread to keep it pure of... well, impurities.
meanings.hint: Imagine a barracks made of pure thread. No brick or clay barracks can compete with yours, as long as you make it out of pure, unadulterated thread.

readings.mnemonic: Unfortunately, this pure thread barracks can only be used in June ( じゅん ). Any other time of year there will be snow and rain and mud that will get tracked into the pure barracks and ruin them. But June is the cleanest, most pure month, so you can use it then.
readings.hint: Okay, so maybe building a pure barracks that you can only use in June was a bad idea, but it's there now and I don't want you getting it dirty, so just use it when I say so, okay?

meanings.mnemonic: You want some flowers to put on the canopy above your helicopter to keep the bird s away, so you ask people what they would recommend .
meanings.hint: You've bought flowers before, but you really don't know which ones would work best to stop birds from landing on the canopy and pooping on your helicopter.

readings.mnemonic: You ask for a recommend ation from the cen taur ( せん ) that owns the local flower shop. Now he's walking around your helicopter canopy and staring at the birds, nodding.
readings.hint: I hope the centaur recommends a cheap flower... but with your luck flowers that keep birds away will cost a fortune.

meanings.mnemonic: Into a well , a drop of rice falls from your rice bowl . You were eating out of your rice bowl on the side of a well, and some rice fell out of your rice bowl and this drop of rice proceeded to fall all the way down.
meanings.hint: You lost a drop of rice from your rice bowl. Feel sorrow for the lost rice as you watch it fall down the well and hear it splat into the water below.

readings.mnemonic: Just then, the don ( どん ) of your local mafia shows up, steals your rice bowl , and shoves you into the well. You’d gotten on the don’s bad side, so he came to teach you a lesson. The don steals your rice bowl and shoves you into the well.
readings.hint: Hear the don shout, “See ya, sucker!” as he pushes you into the well. You’ve just lost a drop of rice, now your rice bowl, and now you’re all wet in a well. Worst day ever.

meanings.mnemonic: Set up your canopy , attach some meat to it, and the meat will eventually rot .
meanings.hint: Imagine the smell of rotten meat hanging from your canopy. Now no one wants to come near here.

readings.mnemonic: You let meat rot . You're a foo l ( ふ ). That's really all I can say at this point.
readings.hint: What, did you think attaching this meat to the canopy would make it turn into jerky or something? Fool...

meanings.mnemonic: There's a tsunami coming, so you put your fins on, open your mouth wide, and try to run alongside it.
meanings.hint: Your mouth is open so you can scream, because this is probably a bad idea.

readings.mnemonic: You're able to run alongside the tsunami for about ten seconds before everything en ds ( えん ).
readings.hint: Okay, so maybe trying to run alongside a tsunami with just some fins and a mouth full of screams and terror wasn't the best idea. But at least everything ended while you did... uh... something unique?

meanings.mnemonic: You need to take a body measurement before you can shoot . Whether it's for a photo shoot, you're gonna shoot them with a gun, or if you're about to shoot them full of radiation for X-rays, you need the right measurements of that body first.
meanings.hint: Imagine someone measuring your body before they shoot you. You hope it's the good kind of shoot and that you're not going to die.

readings.mnemonic: This shoot is being done by a sha man ( しゃ ). Not only is he doing the body measurements for the shoot, but he's also going to be shooting you with a shot so you don't get the flu.
readings.hint: Shamans are just a different kind of doctor. And this shot is going to save you from getting the flu, which could kill you. Make sure you always go to the local shaman and schedule a shooting.

meanings.mnemonic: A magic thread fell from the roof onto the leader of a hundred people, causing him to shrink !
meanings.hint: Picture one hundred people under the same roof watching in horror as their leader suddenly shrinks to nothingness before them.

readings.mnemonic: As the leader shrink s, the hundred people start screaming and running around the entire place. As a result, everything is shook ( しゅく ) up. Especially the poor, shrunken leader, who gets knocked around the most.
readings.hint: The leader gets shook so badly, he can't even call out to his people. They're going to trample his tiny, shrinking body!

meanings.mnemonic: A samurai handling a jackhammer , cleaning brush es (in their 肀 form), and carrying water for someone?! All that should tell you that he's not just working, he's been made into a slave .
meanings.hint: Traditionally, samurai don't work with jackhammers or do menial tasks like cleaning brushes and hauling water. This samurai is doing these things for someone else, meaning he's somebody's slave.

readings.mnemonic: You don't like seeing slave s. As you walk by, you hand this slave a ray ( れい ) gun so he can free himself.
readings.hint: The samurai can use the ray gun to shoot his keeper and get away. Imagine watching him holding up the master and getting away. A ray gun is a truly powerful weapon in the hands of a slave.

meanings.mnemonic: There's some rice in the shape of a nine on a cross and this is stylish . The rice grains are arranged to form the number nine. This number nine is prominently displayed on the cross, and it's stylish to carry it around and be seen with it.
meanings.hint: Imagine hefting this cross with the rice in the shape of a nine on it. You don't feel stylish, but society tells you that you are. You're stylish.

readings.mnemonic: It's also stylish to swi ng ( すい ) the cross around while you wait for things. You're waiting at a bus stop so you swing the cross around violently. You swing it so violently that the rice comes flying off!
readings.hint: See the rice fly through the air from you swinging the cross. Hear it as it clatters to the ground. This cross just isn't as stylish anymore now.

meanings.mnemonic: You are so sick that you've come to look like the humble stool : thin . Eerily thin, in fact.
meanings.hint: Imagine standing behind a stool and being able to disappear behind it completely. That's how thin this sickness has made you.

readings.mnemonic: When you're this thin , your sou l ( そう ) is visible from outside of your body too.
readings.hint: Picture your soul pulsing through your thin body. You have no body fat to cover it up, so everyone can see it pulsing and lurking in there. This is why you're hiding behind one of the humble stools in your house.

meanings.mnemonic: If someone fills your mouth with dirt , you're going to throw up . That's just a fact.
meanings.hint: That's pretty much what throwing up feels like too, right? Like a bunch of dirt coming out of your mouth. Barf.

readings.mnemonic: When you throw up it sounds like you're shouting, " HA ( は )!" Then all the dirt-puke comes out in a wave. It's less of a laugh ha and more of an oh no I'm throwing up ha.
readings.hint: Imagine the sound you made the last time you threw up. It sounded like you were saying HA from the bottom of your tummy.

meanings.mnemonic: The valuable radical and the valuable kanji are the same!
meanings.hint: The most valuable kanji are the ones you don't have to learn from scratch.

readings.mnemonic: Most valuable things require a key ( き ) to be accessed. I mean, you've gotta keep your valuable stuff safe, right? Best to keep it under lock and key.
readings.hint: Just be careful not to lose track of the key. Otherwise, your valuable stuff is going to be safe from everyone… including you.

meanings.mnemonic: You see a thread that wants to loiter from some horns and you would be correct to assume that it would hang vertical . The thread wants to loiter and hang from one of the horns. When things hang from things, they usually hang vertical, so that’s what this thread is going to do.
meanings.hint: Look at this vertical thread as it hangs from the horns. Take hold of it and pull on the vertical thread. Feel it in your hand.

readings.mnemonic: For some reason, Channing Tat um ( たて ) is strangely attracted to vertical pieces of hanging thread. Channing Tatum starts batting at the vertical thread like a cat.
readings.hint: Look at Channing Tatum playing with this thread. He’s like a cat playing with a piece of yarn. Look at Mr. Tatum bouncing around batting at the thread like some silly feline.

meanings.mnemonic: Boot s give you the power to work ! That's why so many professions require you to have work boots.
meanings.hint: Imagine putting your boots on and suddenly being filled with the power and energy to work hard and efficiently.

readings.mnemonic: You need boots to give you the power to work for the Kin g ( きん ) of England. Really, all work is for the King, since everything is for him. But even he wears boots. And they give him the power to uh... king over things.
readings.hint: Picture yourself in work boots. Now picture the King in work boots. Yours are worn and beaten up and his are clean and look brand new. But at least you both have them, which means you both work at least a little!

meanings.mnemonic: Take your fingers and dry them. Then dry them again. This is what you do when you are in worship . Once you go into your place of worship, and again before you leave. You have really sweaty fingers, and you can't worship anyone or anything if you have wet hands. So you dry them!
meanings.hint: Picture your sweaty, sweaty fingers. Dry them off before you enter a place of worship! No god wants to deal with your weird, damp hands.

readings.mnemonic: Once you're dry and in wherever your worship is, you begin with a hai ku ( はい ). Really all places of worship have their own version of the haiku, and heck, the 5-7-5 rule is just a guideline, so everyone is doing it pretty close to right anyway.
readings.hint: Imagine being in your place of worship. Close your eyes and say a haiku:

meanings.mnemonic: Your mouth is wearing a mask so people won't realize you're the one being so boisterous as you eat your miso . If you cover your mouth with this mask, no one will know you're such a boisterous eater of miso.
meanings.hint: Okay, this kanji doesn't actually mean "miso," it just means "boisterous." But you'll pretty much only ever see it in the word for miso, so I figured I'd sneak it in here to prepare you for that… Thank me later.

readings.mnemonic: You're being boisterous and having your miso outside when suddenly someone shoves so ap ( そ ) in your mouth. "Even your mask can't hide that boisterous miso-eating. Quiet down and wash your mouth out with that soap!" they yell.
readings.hint: Using your boisterous mouth, taste the disgusting soapy soap. The soap bubbles are already filling your mouth and stinging your tongue. This is the punishment for those who eat boisterously.

meanings.mnemonic: The ability to boil is one of the main abilities of a bear .
meanings.hint: I bet you didn't know this, but a bear can boil whenever it wants. Imagine a bear heating itself up and boiling, its fur and skin bubbling and letting off steam.

readings.mnemonic:
A bear boiling in front of you should fill you with great honor. Or, as they say in Latin: Magna cum ( くま ) laude.

Get it? A little hot bear humor for you.
readings.hint: You can also imagine a bear graduating in the top 5-10% of its class. Or, just imagine a bear saying "Cuma here!" to you.

meanings.mnemonic: There is a circumference around someone's hair , as in, you can see a circle drawn around the top of their head. As you examine it closer, and pull at it a bit, you realize that someone has carve d the hair off someone's head!
meanings.hint: Their hair has literally been carved out of their head, and you discovered it. Of course, the nastiness, the smell, and the feel should be applied to your imagination to make the mnemonic stronger.

readings.mnemonic: When you think of all the people who could have done this... all the people who could have carve d out someone's hair, only one person comes to mind, and that is Mrs. Chou ( ちょう ). She's the only person evil enough to do something like this.
readings.hint: You can also imagine Mrs. Chou doing the act of carving someone's hair out, but that may just be too much.

meanings.mnemonic:
There’s a tree in your hair , I think it’s a cedar . How did that cedar tree get in your hair?

(Certain types of cedar trees have bark that comes off in fine threads, making them very hairy trees. So this kanji makes perfect sense!)
meanings.hint: Imagine having a cedar tree in your hair. It’s just a little tree, a sapling, but it’s pretty strange and awkward. It does smell nice though. Mmm… cedar.

readings.mnemonic: Once the cedar is fully grown, you use the cedar wood to make a guitar for Sue. A Sue gui tar ( すぎ ), if you will. This Sue guitar was made just for Sue. She’ll love it.
readings.hint: Give Sue her Sue guitar. Hear Sue squeal with glee as she receives her Sue guitar.

meanings.mnemonic: The mouth of the World Tree , all it does is chat and talk .
meanings.hint: You'd think the World Tree's mouth would talk wisdom, but all it does is chat, chat, chat.

readings.mnemonic: To chat or talk with the World Tree, just head over to Sha man Bay ( しゃべ ).
readings.hint: The locals shorten Shaman Bay to "Sha Bay," because they always go there to 喋る with the World Tree. Imagine all the shamans hanging about in the bay.

meanings.mnemonic: You toss a pair of gold mustache s into your cooking pot and begin simmering them. Some people use bay leaves and whatnot, but where you're from, there's no seasoning you'd rather have in your cooking pot than a couple of gold mustaches.
meanings.hint: Smell the aromatic vapors as you simmer these gold mustaches in your cooking pot. You can see the gold whiskers slowly melting and turning into a delicious, shimmering broth.

readings.mnemonic: Your spouse asks you if they can have a taste of what's in your cooking pot , but you say, " Nah ba be ( なべ ), this is all for me."
readings.hint: Undeterred, your spouse keeps trying to sneak a spoon into your cooking pot, but you relentlessly box them out, shouting "Nah, babe" over and over.
